THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES:
OFFICIAL REPORT.
IN THE
SECOND SESSION OF THE FIRST PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
APPOINTED TO MEET THE FOURTEENTH DAY OF MARCH, IN THE
TWELFTH YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS MAJESTY KING GEORGE V.
FIRST SERIES-VOLUME II.
THE SENATE.

---

Tuesday, 14th March, 1922.
The Senate met at Twelve noon, Mr. Speaker
in the Chair.
THE KING'S SPEECH.
The SPEAKER: The Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod will now inform the
Commons that the Speech of His Excellency
the Lord Lieutenant General and General
Governor of Ireland is to be communicated
in the Senate forthwith and will invite
them to attend in order to hear the said
Speech.
The Commons having attended with their
Speaker at the Bar of the Senate.
The Speaker read His Majesty's Speech
as follows:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons-
Your Parliament has been established for
some months, and the legal process of
transferring services to the control of the
Government of Northern Ireland has now
been completed. I regret that the transfer
of staff and the due allocation of property
and records to the Departments of Government have not proceeded as expeditiously
as might have been desired, and I trust
that means may shortly be devised for removing the causes of delay and enabling
your Departments to cope with the difficult
administrative problems which necessarily
confront a new Government.
I congratulate you on the manner in
which, in spite of these obstacles, the Departments of Government have been
brought so rapidly into smooth working
order.
I regret that Northern Ireland is still
faced with the serious problem of unemployment, but am confident that the
Ministry of Labour is giving the matter
constant and sympathetic consideration.
The spirit of conciliation shown in Northern Ireland in the settlement of industrial disputes is tending towards a more
satisfactory state of affairs, and I trust
that spirit will continue to animate all
classes of the community.
I am glad to know that, acting in conformity with a decision arrived at by the
British Cabinet, the Cabinet of Northern
Ireland has decided to grant an additional
six weeks' benefit during the present
period of special distress.
Members of the House of Commons-
The estimates of expenditure on the
Services for the ensuing financial year will
be laid before you in due course, but owing
to the fact that your finance is largely
dependent upon that of the Imperial Par-
A
Debate on Address.
King's Speech.
THE SENATE.
liament, it will only be possible to make
partial and tentative estimates until the
Imperial Budget for the coming year is
available. Every effort has been made by
the Government to reduce expenditure to
the lowest possible limit consistent with
the security of the Province and the efficiency of its public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons-
It is hoped to lay before you measures
dealing with the following matters:-
The conferring of such additional powers
on the Government of Northern Ireland
as are necessary for the prevention of
crime, preservation of peace, and the
maintenance of order.
The continuance of the issue of Savings
Certificates.
The amalgamation of the General Nursing Council with the Central Midwives'
Board for Northern Ireland.
The amendment of the Intermediate
Education (Ireland) Acts.
The Breeding of Cattle.
The Imperial Government is introducing
legislation for making permanent the
principle of Daylight Saving. It will be
for you to consider whether in this matter
you will remain in conformity with the
Mother Country.
It is hoped that the Committees dealing
with the improvement of Education, the
the
establishment of the Constabulary,
system of Trade Boards and the Employment Exchanges in Northern Ireland will
shortly complete their labours. In that
event it may be found possible to introduce measures for giving effect to their
recommendations.
may
I pray that Almighty God bestow
His abundant blessing upon your deliberations.
Senate adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed at Half-past Two of
the Clock.
MINISTRY OF COMMERCE.
The minute of the Minister of Commerce
assigning Powers and Duties of Departmental
Officers under the Ministries of Northern
Ireland Act, 1921, was laid before the House
(pursuant to Act), and ordered to lie on the
Table.
LOCAL BILL.
Mr. Speaker acquainted the House that the
Clerk of the Senate had laid upon the Table
the certificate from the Examiner that the
Standing Orders applicable to the following
Bill have been complied with:-
Belfast Harbour.
Mr. Speaker having read the King's
Speech,
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move that a humble
Address be presented to His Excellency in
the following words:-
Your Excellency, we, His Majesty's most
dutiful and loyal subjects, the Senate of
the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to your
Excellency for the Speech which your
Excellency has addressed to both Houses
of Parliament.
His Majesty, in the beginning of his
Speech, congratulated the Government of
Northern Ireland on the manner in which,
in spite of many obstacles, the departments
of Government had been brought so rapidly
into smooth working order. Everyone who
knows, as we Members of both Houses do,
the serious and almost insuperable difficulties which the Prime Minister and his
Government encountered on proceeding to
take up the reins of Government of Northern
Ireland under the Act of 1920, will join
heartily in these congratulations and will
have admiration as well for their courage
in facing the difficulties in the way they
have done, as for the patience and pertinacity with which they have already
accomplished so much of their task, unfortunately not yet completed. It is to
be fervently hoped that some means may
be found of removing those obstacles that
still remain, so that our Parliament may
have a fair chance of carrying out successfully the important and responsible duties
that have been imposed upon it by the
Imperial Parliament. Under any ordinary
manufacturing undertaking it would be
taken for granted that, before any working
or manufacturing is begun, there would at
any rate be a factory built and equipped
and all the necessary machinery completed
down to the very last bolt, but when the
Northern Parliament was set in being our
Prime Minister and his colleagues had to
set to work first to provide suitable buildings
in which to hold meetings and accommodate
Members of Parliament. They had also to
create the various departments of State and
group these departments under several Ministers. They then had to arrange for the
transfer of staff to these departments and
I gather from the reference in the King's
Speech to that matter that it has been found
to be full of difficulty. I may say I am
not surprised, because the success of a department depends very largely on the kind
5 Debate on Address.

14 MARCH 1922.
Debate on Address.
of staff they have got to work it. And when
one wants to take away some members of
the staff from another department, the other
department is not likely to give them the
best men or with any great gush of welcome.
So I can see quite well how difficulties may
have arisen. The staff themselves may have
wished to have some say in the matter
whether they should come to Northern Ireland or stay where they were. Therefore,
we can all see how difficult the matter is
to arrange. For my own part, I think the
selections of the staffs have been most
fortunate up to the present time. In
addition to the matter of staff they have
had to arrange for the transfer to Northern
Ireland of the documents and records which
place. All
have been kept in another
For
that must take time. And then they had to
provide for the departments, such as the
establishment of Courts of Justice and so
forth, and other matters too numerous to
mention. But at the same time they
had to steer the ship of State safely
and securely through more dangerous seas
than have ever been encountered in all the
previous history of this province.
those achievements I certainly think the
Government deserves to get our most profound congratulations. The serious problem
of industrial unrest consequent on unemployment which is looming so largely throughout
the world at the present time, and from
which, unfortunately, Ulster is not exempt,
is also referred to in the gracious Speech,
and we have every confidence that the
Members of our Cabinet who are intimately
acquainted with the situation as it exists
here among us, are approaching the
matter already in a spirit of conciliation
and fair play all round. I have every hope
that they may discover some satisfactory
and permanent solution of the question in
Ulster. I say satisfactory and permanent,
but I think unless it is permanent it would
not really be satisfactory. Dangers and
difficulties arising every two or three years
would be a very bad thing indeed. So I
hope we may be able to discover some permanent and satisfactory solution of the
question. I am glad to say that in the
meantime, as stated in the gracious Speech,
they have arranged to extend the period of
benefit grant for six weeks owing to the
special distress which is being experienced
at the present time. I observe that the
gracious Speech holds out the prospect of a
wide field of legislation during the coming
Session or in the near future, and by far the
most important measure to come before us
is one to confer additional powers on the
Government of Northern Ireland for the
prevention of crime. Ulster can never
prosper until respect for law and order,
security of person and property are absolutely established throughout the entire area.
To ensure this, it must be definitely brought
home to every malefactor that punishment,
sure, swift, and adequate, will inevitably
follow the commission of crime-that there
will be no longer divided responsibility in
carrying out the law, and the punishment
of criminals, and that the taking of human
life unjustifiably is a crime that can in no
case be condoned. In my opinion then, and
then only, will crime cease in Ulster, and
will she be able to go forward on her way
with confidence and self respect.
I observe that the Daylight Saving Bill is
mentioned in the King's Speech. I gather
from what is stated here that an Act of
Parliament will be passed by the Imperial
Government in England which will deal
with this question, and will settle the question for Ulster unless the Parliament of
Ulster chooses to make some other Act of
Parliament altering or amending it. This
is a question on which there will, of
course, be a sharp divergence of opinion between agriculturalists and persons engaged
in other industries, or to put it more briefly,
between town and country. For my part, I
hope that if the Imperial Parliament should
bring in a Daylight Saving Bill on the lines
of previous Bills it will be accepted in
Ulster as well as in England. It would mean
a very serious dislocation if the time in England and Scotland differed from that in Ulster.
I hope the result will be that the town will
take a deeper interest in the country, to the
great advantage of both. There is mention
made in the King's Speech of the continuance of the system of Savings Certificates. I
do not quite know what is meant by that,
but I presume that what used to be known
as War Savings Certificates are now to be
issued in slightly different form and to be
just Savings Certificates, and I think it is
an excellent idea. From a considerable experience of those Saving Certificates I know
they were an excellent means of inducing
the working classes and small shopkeepers
to save.
In connection with a centre in
Kensington I noticed a very large number
of people were very glad to come and purchase their 15s 6d certificates. A considerable amount of revenue was raised in that
way. It was then used for war purposes,
but I understand the revenue will now be
used
for the general public services of
Ulster. I would hail with great satisfaction
the passing of that measure.
The last subject to which I wish to refer
is that of educational reform, one of the
most important in the Government's programme. Many changes, chiefly in administration, are urgently required, and we are
much gratified to and that the matter is being taken in hand by the Noble Marquess,
the Leader of the House, who will, I am
quite certain, go into it in a statesmanlike
way. A man of his experience is sure to find
out what is needed and to conceive the best
way of supplying it, and if I may be allowed
to say so, I think he has approached the
question in an admirable manner, desiring
to get the fullest information before embarking on legislation. For example, he has
personally visited the various educational
centres, and I happen to know he has given
great satisfaction to those centres by taking
so much interest in them, and also by the
appointment of the Committee under the
chairmanship of Mr. R. J. Lynn, M.P. That
Committee is composed of the most competent, representative, and painstaking men.
They have already held a great many meetings during the past few months and have
acquired a vast amount of information and
searched many of the difficult questions that
are bound to arise. I know there has been
universal appreciation of the spirit in which
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE.
Mr. Greer]
they have approached this difficult question.
They have given evidence of a sympathetic
and broad-minded spirit in trying to find the
best solution of the problem, and I have every
hope that their report will soon be published.
When it is published it will be read with very
great interest indeed, and will be very illuminating, I am sure. I hope and trust it
will lead to legislation that will put our
Province in an educational position at least
equal to that which obtains in any other
country. I beg to move.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I have the honour to
second the Address of Thanks from this
Senate in reply to the gracious Speech from
the Throne, which His Excellency, the Lord
Lieutenant, has been pleased to employ in
opening the present session of the Northern
Parliament. This Address has been moved
by the honourable Member who has just
spoken in such eloquent and exhaustive
terms expressive of the sentiments of unswerving loyalty and devotion to the Throne,
which inspire the Members of this Senatethat it will only be necessary for me to
dwell for a few moments upon some of the
points in the gracious Speech merely by way
of emphasis. The acute and widespread
anxiety felt throughout the Province in regard to the control and punishment of crime
will be to some extent relieved by His
Excellency's promise that immediate steps
will be taken to strengthen the powers of
the Northern Parliament in dealing with the
alarming state of disorder and crime that
prevails in our midst. His allusion to the
increase and re-organisation of the Constabulary to this end is also reassuring; and it
is to be hoped the extreme urgency of this
measure will be recognised and translated
into action. It is essential that complete
direction of the Military as well as the Constabulary should be entrusted to the
Northern Parliament to enable them to coordinate these forces if consistent and
resolute government is expected from them.
We all desire most earnestly a revival in
trade and the disappearance of unemployment with its attendant evils. It would be
impossible for trade revival and the consequent reduction of unemployment in this
Province to take place until cecurity of life
and property is assured, and some adequate
guarantee given of permanence that would
encourage new business enterprises and
stimulate individual effort in restoring the
prosperity of our Province.
The Northern Parliament has not had a
fair chance of establishing law and order,
owing to the trying delay in the transfer of
services, and the sort of dual control
exercised by the Imperial and local
Cabinets, which has enfeebled or frustrated
the administration of justice, and destroyed
that unity of purpose and endeavour required to establish law and order in this
Province. The greatest credit must be given
to our Premier and the Members of his
Cabinet who, in spite of this handicap, have
displayed energy, sagacity and celerity in
bringing their departments into action. No
part of the gracious Speech will be more
appreciated than His Excellency's generous
congratulations to the Cabinet for their
admirable efforts in a time of unexampled
difficulty and embarrassment. We earnestly
trust that any remaining obstacles may be
speedily removed and a freer and independent hand given to our trusted rulers.
We cordially welcome the references to
Educational Reform, and the conduct of
Intermediate Examinations in Ulster. This
country is quite thirty years behind England
and Scotland in the matter of education. It
is high time we had a radical change before
the lessons of the great war have been forgotten. We have to deplore that the
financial stringency in the Empire has led
to a reduction in grants for education just
at a time when we wanted to develop a
generous scheme to give our Ulster youth a
reasonable chance in the competition of life.
We have reason to congratulate ourselves in
this Chamber that the Noble Marquess, the
Leader of this House, is responsible for the
future of education. We may assure him
measure.
of our wholehearted support and assistance
in his efforts to supply us with a first-class
system of education when he introduces this
No finer monument to His Lordship's foresight and statesmanship could be
imagined than the carrying through of a
broadminded and liberal scheme of education
in all its grades. We shall eagerly await
the report of Mr. Lynn's Committee which
the Noble Marquess has called into being as
a prelude to legislation. It is earnestly
hoped they may be able to reconcile conflicting interests and evolve a model system
worthy of public approval, and in the attainment of which our people may sanction such
a measure of financial support that will ensure the highest standards of efficiency in
this most important national service.
arrest or cripple education would indeed be
false economy. I beg to second the Address
of thanks to the Throne.
Το
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
At the opening of a new Session it is always
customary for the Leader of the House to
make a general statement upon what has occurred, and to foreshadow, as far as he is
able, the plan of the Government for the
coming Session. Before I do that to-day
there are one or two matters to which I
should refer, and in doing this I am sure I
shall receive the approbation and sanction
of this House. I should not like this occasion to pass without making some mention
of the singularly happy event connected with
the Royal Family, to whom we all owe allegiance and for whom we all have such undoubted affection, and that is the marriage
of Princess Mary, which was solemnised
some short time ago. I am sure we desire
to convey to her and to her illustrious
parents our congratulations and our best
wishes for her future happiness. I would
now take this opportunity of congratulating
the mover and seconder of the humble Address to be presented to his Excellency, and
I feel we have been indeed fortunate in the
selection of the two eminent members who
undertook this task. Both are well known
to you. The mover, as we know, comes of a
family which bears an honoured name in
this country. Mr. Greer has an honourable
9 Debate on Address.

14 MARCH 1922.
Debate on Address.
career behind him, and we are indeed glad
to have him with us helping us in our deliberations here and to listen to such an eloquent
and interesting speech. The hon. Gentleman's sacrifice in the great war through
which we have lately come, and which carried with it so many private disasters, entitle him to the sympathy which he so justly
receives from everyone.
I should like to
congratulate him on his speech and to say
that I hope we shall be favoured with his
eloquent contributions to cur debates in the
future. The seconder of the motion is no
less distinguished. He occupies a very eminent position in the medical world, and I
feel we are indeed fortunate to find him as a
Senator. His speech was a very eloquent one,
and he undoubtedly showed the grasp he has
of public affairs and the great interest he
takes in the promotion of all measures for
the social welfare. We are all aware of the
meritorious career which was his during the
late war, and we were all glad when his
Majesty honoured his services by conferring
upon him the Commandership of the Bath
in 1917. May I also say how much we hope
that, despite the many other duties he has
to perform, he will find time to attend our
meetings here and give us the benefit of his
knowledge and advice.
My Lord, I had hoped on this occasion to
be in a position to take a more optimistic and
encouraging view of the situation than I was
able to do on the last occasion of the opening of a Session of this Parliament, but I
regret to say that we are still surrounded
by heavy clouds through which at this moment it is almost impossible to see any sunlight. No one can contemplate the present
state of affairs in the South and West with
feelings other than those of grave anxiety.
The finality which is invariably claimed by
British statesmen as the outcome of whatever they propose for Ireland seems as far
off as ever it has been in the past. The
Treaty ratified in both Houses of the British
Parliament seems to have produced, if possible, a greater state of chaos in Ireland than
existed before. We find Mr. Collins on one
side, and we find Mr. De Valera on the
other, and it will be within the recollection
of hon. Members that Mr. Collins was the
delegate sent over by Mr. De Valera for the
purpose of negotiating something or other
with the British Government. As soon as
something is, with great difficulty, negotiated,
we find that Mr. Collins and Mr. De Valera
are apparently holding divided opinions.
Mr. De Valera openly proclaims that his
aim and object is the setting up of a Republic in Ireland, and Mr. Collins, while
appearing to dissociate himself from Mr. De
Valera, practically makes the same
tions. I do not propose on this occasion to
say much about the state of affairs in the
South and West, except in so far as they
affect us in the North. I would, however,
venture to make an appeal to every loyal
citizen in the Six Counties to realise the vital
importance of self-control at this moment.
We are not only surrounded by dangers and
difficulties, but both exist in their acutest
form in our very midst, and unless the
majority of our citizens are ready and able
to take a calm view I say at once with all
sense of responsibility that the difficulties
asserof your Government will be immeasurably
increased. I think the saving point that has
enabled us to carry on is that the great
majority of our citizens do take a calm view.
Otherwise it is impossible to say what might
have happened amid all these difficulties.
The outrages which have occurred in Belfast
naturally fill every right-minded person with
horror. The death roll is indeed a long one,
and there appear to exist in the city of Belfast itself people so unmindful of all those
principles on which the maintenance of law
and order is based, that at different periods
crime and outrage proceed almost unchecked
and undetected. This is a state of affairs
which must not be allowed to continue.
Moreover, it is a state of affairs which can
only be checked by public opinion. It is
quite true that Belfast and the Six Counties
could be handed over to the military, and
Martial Law proclaimed, but I am bound to
say that I should regret very much to see
this done. Your Government has been
established with the support of the great
majority of the Six Counties, and I sincerely
hope that the time will never come when we
shall have to admit that owing to the lawlessness existing within the area under our
jurisdiction the powers at our disposal were
insufficient for the maintenance of law and
order. I do not anticipate that this time
will come. We have been labouring under
very serious difficulties. The mover of the
humble Address to His Majesty referred to
what he termed "dual control," and I am
bound to agree that there have been a great
many difficulties in this respect. I think
hon. Members are well aware of the situation as it exists at the present moment, and
that is that the military are called out under
the King's Regulations when the situation
is such that the recognised authorities,
which are the police, are not able to cope
with it. That is what has actually occurred
up to now, and consequently outrages of a
most terrible character have taken place.
What we desire to have and what we feel
will be enacted, is that we shall be able to
call upon the military for preventive
measures rather than for coming in afterwards when crime has been committed.
a
In this connection-and I am speaking of
the stamping out of outrage and crime-our
task would be a comparatively easy one if
crime and outrage proceeded from one
quarter alone, but I regret to say that this
is by no means the case. The fact that a
section of those who see eye to eye with us
on the present political situation are
implicated in outrages that are as reprehensible as those committed by Sinn Fein,
places your Government in well-nigh
impossible position in their negotiations
with British Ministers. As you probably
know, I have had many negotiations and
consultations with British Ministers, and I
should like to be able to appear before them
with absolutely clean hands. But that has
not been the case, and the knowledge of this
has not been lost either upon the British
Government or upon the rest of Ireland, or
upon our Colonies. I would therefore
venture to urge upon all those who see eye
to eye with us, and who realise that the
only solution of the difficulty is the stamping
11 Debate on Address.

THE SENATE.
Debate on Address.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
out of crime and outrage, to do their best to
use their influence with all those who are
inclined to lose their heads and take the
law into their own hands, and endeavour to
settle the matter with the recognised forces
that are usually made use of for the maintenance of law and order.
I should like to make this point very clear
indeed, because I have read speeches made
in the Six Counties in which the underlying
suggestion is that, as your Government
seems impotent, the Loyalists in the North
had better take the law in their own hands
and not rely for their safety on the forces
of military and police. This suggestion is
not only one of the most dangerous that
could possibly be made, but appears on the
face of it to be an attempt to create disunion in our ranks. It is a suggestion
which plays into the hands of Sinn Fein,
and I am bound to say that I have noticed
that as a corollary to speeches of this
description-it may of course be a coincidence, and I sincerely hope it is-crimes
have been immediately afterwards perpetrated in Belfast. I am not saying for one
moment that the provocation has not been
very severe, and probably that provocation
has been purposely brought about so as to
make Belfast appear in an unfavourable
light, not only in England, but before our
Colonies and before the whole world. Be
that as it may, I particularly urge on the
people of the Six Counties to keep their
heads in this crisis and, by maintaining the
law themselves, to strengthen the arm of
the Government, who are doing everything
they can to protect life and property.
There are various other matters which I
should mention to the House. However, I will
endeavour not to occupy your time to any
undue extent. I should like to express our
thanks to many who have been of such
great assistance to us during the last few
months. First of all I think our thanks are
due to the military authorities, to the Royal
Irish Constabulary, and also to the Specials.
All have passed through a very strenuous
time indeed, and the circumstances with
which they have had to deal have been out
of the ordinary. I think the manner in
which they have comported themselves is
one upon which they have every reason to be
congratulated. General Cameron has been in
charge of the military. I know that he has
done every single thing in his power to mitigate the difficulties and remove the
obstacles with which we are surrounded.
Colonel Wickham also deserves our thanks.
I should like further to add the names of
Colonel M'Clintock and that of an hon.
Member of this House, one whom I am
sorry not to see here, and who, I understand with regret, is shortly to resign his
seat. I refer to Sir Basil Brooke. I would
also express our gratitude to the Ulster
Members of the Imperial Parliament. They
have fought our battles strongly from
beginning to end. In particular I might
mention the names of Captain Charles Craig,
Mr. Ronald M'Neill, Mr. Reid, and Mr.
Moles. Indeed I might well mention by
name every single member of that party, because every one of them has done yeoman
service. I think thanks are due to them.
In the House to which I have the honour
to belong, Lord Carson, as he would naturally
do, has rendered splendid service, as perhaps
my Lord, I may be allowed to say, you yourself have also done. You have been consistently in your place on
every occasion
possible. You have put forward our point
of view on these questions with a sincerity
that has won for you the ear of the House of
Lords. Now I should not like, in venturing
to express our gratitude to so many, to omit
the Press. 1 think our thanks are due to
Lady Bathurst for the splendid way in which
she has put our case before Great
Britain and before the world. And in that
special connection there is my old friend,
Mr. Gwynne, who, as you know, is the
Editor of the "Morning Post." Lord Rothermere has given us great assistance, and so
has Sir William Madge. It is impossible
for you not to realise how indebted we are
to these various individuals for the promotion of our cause. Everything has been
done that could be done by those whom I
have mentioned. Now I should like to say
one word about our Prime Minister, although it may, perhaps, be presumption on
my part, to offer him a tribute of praise.
I can assure you that no one has worked
harder in our interests than the Prime
Minister, Sir James Craig. He has shown
a high degree of courage, and his statesmanlike handling of these difficult problems has
impressed to the fullest extent and on all
occasions Ministers on the other side of the
water with whom he has had to deal. He has
never lost an opportunity of putting our
difficulties before them and of showing them
what they should do. Everything that he
has been able to gain from them has been
due to his persistence and to the knowledge
that they have that he is addressing himself to a subject which he knows from end
to end.
We have passed through a great many
difficulties-and when I say that I am not
suggesting that we have nearly come to the
end of them; a very great many more may
have to, be surmounted-but I am convinced
of this, that it has been the courage and
firmness of the people of Ulster themselves
which our Government has realised that they
have behind them which has strengthened
us in the handling of the very difficult situation with which we have had to deal.
I should like to take this opportunity of
expressing my sympathy with the unfortunate victims, and also with the relatives
of the victims, of the recent outrages. We
are longing to see some change from the
sequence of outrages and crimes.
As you
know, we have done our best to urge upon
the British Government to use some firmness
themselves for the purpose of bringing
finality to this question. At the present moment it seems to me that there are two paramount difficulties which have to be surmounted. These two difficulties are, first of
all, the Boundary Commission which is embodied in the so-called Treaty, and concerning the operation of which there is a certain amount of doubt. The second difficulty
to be surmounted is the general state of unrest in the Province. In regard to the
Boundary Commission we have every reason
13 Debate on Address.
Debate on Address. 14
14 MARCH 1922.
to feel ourselves aggrieved by the fact of
this question being reopened. We certainly
were under the impression that the 1920
Act entrenched us in our position in Ulster.
We accepted that Bill reluctantly. It was
in no sense a state of affairs to which we
attached any feelings other than a certain
amount of suspicion. However, we accepted
the 1920 Act, and I think we were entitled
to say that the territory which was vouchsafed to us under that Act should have been
left intact. However, the British Government did not take the same view. They
have allowed the question to be reopened.
They have indicated that a Commission will
operate so as to decide the Boundary of
Northern and Southern Ireland, and consequently there is a state of unrest in regard
to this question. The position as regards
Northern Ireland is unchanged from the
date when we sent the final ultimatum to
the British Government on 6th February.
The words which we used on that occasion
were:-
We cannot consent to any alteration of
our boundary except by mutual agreement, failing which, in respect of any territory in dispute, the boundary to stand
as defined in the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920.
That seems to be a very definite position
which we have taken up, and a position to
which we are determined to adhere. It
will be in your recollection that Sir James
Craig had met Mr. Collins on this question.
It was decided between them that the
boundary should be arranged by mutual
agreement-failing mutual agreement, the
words I have read out would stand. It will
also be within your recollection that Mr.
Collins almost immediately went back
on his statement and threw the whole
thing into the melting pot. Until
some finality is reached in regard to
this matter, until the British Government
will say what they believe to be the interpretation of the words of the Treaty, this unrest will still continue. I venture to urge
upon everyone not to allow that unrest to
influence them unduly, but to realise that
the Government of Northern Ireland are
determined to maintain their position as
outlined by the words in regard to the
boundary that I have ventured to read out
already. The Prime Minister, as you are
well aware, has personally inspected the
greater part of the boundary, and he has
asked me particularly to say in this House,
what I know he will say in another place,
that he was specially struck by the spirit
of the men and women who live on the
boundary in what we may almost call our
"front line." I would like to say one word
in regard to the prisoners who are held by
the Free State. I need not dwell on thaton the kidnapping in which the aggression
of the Free State was further emphasisedbecause in Northern Ireland, thanks to the
loyalists for the great restraint exercised by
them, the Prime Minister was enabled to
secure
the release of all the kidnapped
prisoners.
With regard to the affair at Clones, which
was an outrage on which it is really very
difficult to speak with moderation, the
Prime Minister was asked by the British
Government whether he had any objection
to an impartial inquiry by a judge of the
High Court as Special Commissioner, and I
know that we have all supported him in
the attitude which he took in agreeing that
an inquiry should be held. We have nothing
to hide, and although we did not ask for
the inquiry, it would have been misunderstood if we had taken a different attitude.
The Prime Minister has made it clear that
as a preliminary to any inquiry there must
be the release of those specials who were
captured at Clones Station. The state of
unrest which exists at the present moment
we are proposing to deal with by a Bill for
the prevention of crime, which will have the
effect of strengthening the hands of the
Home Office, and will be introduced to-day
in another place, and read a first time. The
Second Reading takes place on Tuesday, so
that will enable Members to become
acquainted with the terms. The Bill proposes to give the civil authorities of the
Government of Northern Ireland power to
take in respect of matters within the jurisdiction of the Government all such steps as
may be necessary for preserving the peace
and maintaining order. The civil authority
for the purpose of the Bill will be in
general the Minister of Home Affairs, but
that Minister will have power to delegate
wide executive powers to officers of police
who are under his control.
The powers for the exercise of which it
is proposed in the first instance to provide
authority are those which have been hitherto
exercised by the Competent Military Au
thority under the Restoration of Order
Regulations. Accordingly there will be
scheduled to the Bill in adapted form such
of those regulations as in the opinion of the
Government are most useful for dealing with
the present state of disturbance. The Bill
will also give power to the Minister of Home
Affairs to amend the scheduled regulations
or to revoke or add to them, and provision
will be made for regulations made by the
Minister being laid before both Houses for
discussion. Offences against the regulations
will be prosecuted under the authority of
the Attorney-General before courts of summary jurisdiction, consisting in each case
of two Resident Magistrates. There will be
an appeal from these courts to the county
court, consisting of the county court judge
sitting alone. The court of summary jurisdiction will have power to inflict severe
sentences and heavy fines.
am-
Special punishment will be authorised by
the Bill to be inflicted both by the ordinary
courts and the courts of summary jurisdiction in the case of breaches of the
ordinary law and offences against the regulations when connected with the illegal
possession or using of firearms and
munition. The proposed penalty will be
that authorised for cases of robbery with
violence by the Larceny Act, 1916, that is
to say, in addition to any punishment prescribed by law, the court may order
convicted person, if a male, to be flogged.
The Bill will also contain such supplemental provisions as in the opinion of
the Government are necessary, whether
for the exercise of its powers under
a
15 Debate on Address.
Debate on Address. 16
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
the Bill, or to secure that the ordinary life of the community and the
enjoyment of property are protected from
disturbance. This is a wide extension of
powers, and I sincerely hope it places in
the hands of the Ministry a weapon for
combating the disturbances which are in
existence at the present moment.
There is one matter on which, I think,
we can congratulate ourselves. It brings
into service one who has shown himself so
thoroughly competent to discharge the duties
which have hitherto been allocated to him.
I would like to take this opportunity of
congratulating Sir Henry Wilson on becoming an Ulster Member, and also of congratulating the Ulster Members on having so
illustrious a colleague to assist them in
their work. The Prime Minister has had
many conversations with Sir Henry Wilson,
and he is proposing to call him in to discuss the general question of the Six Counties, their defence, and various matters connected with it. The Prime Minister has
asked me to read to you a statement with
regard to the position which he has established in connection with Sir Henry Wilson.
I will read it exactly :-
"Before leaving London I wrote asking
Field-Marshal Sir Henry Wilson whether
in view of the serious condition of affairs
he would consent to come over at an early
date and advise our Government as to the
best method of safeguarding our borders
and restoring order within our boundaries. I considered it only fair in asking
so much of so distinguished a soldier to
pledge my word that whatever scheme he
recommended would be carried out to the
full, regardless of cost and consequences.
My right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, welcomes the assistance it
will afford him in his difficult task, and
the Cabinet has unanimously endorsed the
undertaking I have given. I am now in a
position to inform the House that the
gallant Field-Marshal has willingly consented. Immediately on his return from
France, which country has just paid him
a tribute as unusual as it is well-merited,
he wired "Yes." It is too early to thank
him. He will know that we will do that
at the right time. Meanwhile I may take
it that the House approves of this undertaking that I have entered into with him,
and will see us through."
That is the statement which the Prime
Minister is putting before Members in another place. I take the opportunity of appealing on his behalf, as well as on my
own, to every Ulster Loyalist to render
assistance to lawful authority, and to refrain from being provoked into illegal
action of any kind, which not only embarrasses the forces of the Crown, but also
encourages the worst elements in our midst.
I think the inhabitants in the disturbed
parts of our city require our deepest sympathy. It is comparatively easy for the
great majority of us who read of these outrages and crimes in the newspapers, and do
not actually come into contact with them,
to take a view which may not be an accurate
one of the situation. I think we should
realise the feelings of these inhabitants in
the disturbed areas, with the daily fear that
either they or their relatives, who may be
more to them in most cases than their own
lives, are being done to death by these
criminals, by whom parts of this great city
seem to be inhabited. In extending our
sympathy to them we should realise the
great courage which they are showing. We
do not hear of panics in Belfast. We do
not hear of the population being carried
away by their feelings or by their fears,
and the maintenance of law and order
becoming absolutely impossible. I do feel
that a great tribute is due to the inhabitants
of these disturbed areas who are living with
death surrounding them on every side.
There is a matter in regard to the new
Ulster Constabulary on which I feel you will
require me to say a few words. I think
you are aware that a Special Committee is
sitting to discuss all aspects of this case, and
it is hoped to raise a Royal Ulster Constabulary Force at an early date. The Government have had to refrain from doing so
until the British Government have settled
the terms of the disbandment of the existing
members of the Royal Irish Constabulary.
It is not our wish or desire to prejudice the
just claims of the men now in the Force.
Hon. Members will realise the difficulties
which surround that question. There is
now existing the Royal Irish Constabulary.
The British Government have not yet
settled the terms on which that force will
be disbanded. Consequently it is impossible
for us to set up the new Constabulary until
we have some information on this point.
I should not like to resume my seat without making some reference to the unemployment which exists at this present moment.
The causes of unemployment are manifold,
and naturally the present conditions of
unrest are not conducive to the restoration
of trade and consequently to the employment
of workers. I sincerely hope that there are
signs on the other side of the water that
trade is reviving, and I need hardly say that
I pray that that revival will extend to us
here, and that by the diminution-in fact
I would like to say entire disappearanceof unrest we shall see trade restored to its
pristine prosperity. We are faced with a
disaster, if I may use the expression, in
the engineering lock-out which exists in
Great Britain, because there is a danger of
its extending to this side. I hope this
difficulty and this danger will be surmounted
as quickly as possible.
In the remarks which I have ventured to
make to you to-day I have taken a somewhat
gloomy outlook. I feel that it is vitally
important that we should look facts in the
face, and that we should realise to the
fullest extent the burden which has been
placed not only on the shoulders of our
Government and ourselves, but on the
shoulders of the whole loyal population of
the Province. But it is not in my nature to
dwell entirely on the gloomy side of the
picture. After all, there is another side,
and we should be wanting in our duty to
those whom we represent if we failed to
count our assets and enumerate those things
for which we have every reason to be thankful. First of all-and in this I know 1 shall
receive the concurrence of those whom I am
Debate on Address. 18
Debate on Address.
14 MARCH 1922.
addressing-we are not under 2 Dublin
Parliament. I think this is something for
which we have every reason to be thankful,
because if the Dublin Parliament, when it
does operate, shows the same elements of
chaos which seem to characterise the actions
and dealings of Dail Eireann and those who
belong to that body, we can certainly say
that we showed a full measure of wisdom in
saying on every occasion that we are unwilling to go into a Dublin Parliament. We
have set up our Northern Parliament, which
it is our duty to fashion into success, and
I do not feel that anyone will say that our
beginning has been anything but a very
hopeful one, and as time goes on I have no
doubt that our Parliamentary duties here
will do credit not only to the Members but
to the people whon. they represent. Then
our Law Courts are firmly established. We
have a splendid force of Specials, in whom
we can place the fullest reliance. I think
we can claim that the British people are
taking a far more favourable view of Ulster
than perhaps they did some months ago.
All this is to the good. In spite of gloomy
forebodings there is no extra taxation, and
there will be no supplementary estimates
I
for this year. We have not had to borrow
up to the present, but as every Government
requires capital the Prime Minister has got
the British Government to consent to ask
Parliament to guarantee principal and
interest of any loan we may require up to
three and a half millions. Our balance is
on the right side this year, and I think that
is a great accomplishment, and the Minister
of Finance deserves every credit for it.
know that the Prime Minister is prepared
to pay a tribute to the departments themselves for having exercised such rigid
economy. When we think over these points
I believe you will agree that we have every
reason to be justly satisfied. In the middle
of all our difficulties we have been able to
show a balance on the right side in regard
to finance. Everybody will agree that the
success and prosperity of the Government,
and also of the country, denend to a very
large extent on a healthy attention to their
finances.
I may be permitted to refer to the impor
tant services rendered by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Labour, not only to
the Northern Government, but to the welfare
and prosperity of all Ireland, in the leading
part he took in the settlement of the grave
crisis on the Irish railways during February.
He gave proof, if that were needed, that this
Government intends with all its heart to
devote itself not only to the interests of
Northern Ireland but to the interests of the
peace and prosperity of the whole country
The transfer of the services of Education
and National Health in February and March
completed the taking over by the Northern
Government of the services contemplated by
the Government of Ireland Act, 1920. There
have been certain difficulties owing to the
fact of the Provisional Government being
established in Dublin prior to the transfer
of those services, but it is hoped that no
serious difficulty will arise, and no impairment of the public services will follow All
this is very satisfactory. I am not going
to say now that we have not had a certain
amount of difficulty in our dealings with
the administration of the South, but I am
not sure that they are altogether to blame.
We have been dealing with men who hitherto
have not had much experience in public
service. I naturally hope that their lack of
knowledge in dealing with public affairs
will not increase as time goes on, but that
they will shortly realise how these publi
services should be administered.
I have not as yet made any reference to
the remarks made by both the mover and
seconder of the Address concerning the
department over which I have the honour
to preside--the Department of Education.
I was very glad to listen to the remarks
which fell from both hon. Members. I
know they feel, as I do, that on a sound
syste.n of education depends so much of the
future prosperity of our Province, and I
can assure you it is my earnest hope that
I shall be able, before I lay down the
burden of office, to establish in our midst
a system of education which will be satisfactory from every point of view, which
will supply needs unsupplied at the present
moment, and give the youth of our great
Province the opportunities in life to which
everyone is justly entitled. I know that
in such an important matter as this it is
use hurrying, it is no use adopting
measures in haste, and although I would
have liked to have introduced a Bill at an
early date, I have had to control my impatience in this direction and wait until
such time as I feel justified in bringing in
a Bill on such a vital subject as this. I
should like to say how grateful I am to
Mr. Lynn and his Committee for the excellent services they are rendering to the
State. I have no doubt that in a short
time I shall receive a report from them,
and that on the recommendations which
they will put forward I shall be able to
base a comprehensive Bill for the purpose
of incorporating such a system as that
which I know you are all
impatiently
no
awaiting. I am glad to think the office of
the department is in working order, and I
am deeply indebted to all those officials who
belong to that office who have given me
ungrudgingly of their best to place on its
legs an administration of such an important
character. I do not really feel that I
should trespass further on your time
to-day. There is a great deal I should
have liked to have said about the work we
are endeavouring to do on the other side
of the water, but I am not sure you
are not almost as fully aware of our efforts
in that direction as we are ourselves. I
can assure you nothing has been left undone
to establish our position as it should be
established not only here in the Northern
counties themselves, but in the opinion and
estimation of the people of Great Britain.
While I see many difficulties which are
yet to be overcome, and while I long to see
something in the nature of firmness and a
grasp of the situation by the British Government, still I think that our position is fairly
secure, and that the further security of our
position will depend on the people of Northern Ireland themselves-their power to control the outrages and crimes which break
B
19 Debate on Address.
Debate on Address. 20
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
out in our midst, and their ability to show
that under these new Parliamentary institutions which have been set up, against our
will, it is true, one portion of Ireland can
be governed for the benefit of itself, Great
Britain, and the Empire.
Colonel MAXWELL: I should just like
to say a few words in support of what my
hon. Friend, the mover of the humble
Address, said with regard to the Prime
Minister and Government of Northern Ireland. They had great difficulties to meet,
and I think we will all admit that they met
those difficulties with courage, tact and
firmness. I think also that the same applies
to the Executive officers who were serving
under them. I only mention this because
I have in my pocket an anonymous attack
on an Executive officer of very high standing
in Northern Ireland. I have no particular
reason to defend him, and I am glad to say
he has the confidence of his superiors. The
point I wish to make is this: there may be
other right hon. and hon. Members who
have received the same sort of pamphlet,
and I hope they will not allow their minds
to be biassed. If there is any criticism to
be made of any Minister or Executive
officer under a Minister, the place to make
it is either in this House or in the other
House. 1 think all anonymous attacks of
that sort are very detrimental to good
public service. The Noble Marquess, the
Leader of the House, has referred to the
steps which the Government propose to take
to restore peace and order in Northern Ireland, and to safeguard our interests. I am
sure every Member of this House will
support them in any action they may take
in order to bring back peace to this Province.
There can be no prosperity in Ulster without
peace. It is the most urgent need at the
present moment. I think that however
much our liberties may be threatened we
are in one sense in a much better position
than we ever were before, because we have,
as the Noble Marquess mentioned, a Parliament and Ministry responsible to the people
of Northern Ireland. That, I think, entails,
first of all, a duty on us to support by every
means in our power, that Government, and
it also entails a duty on the Government
to see that impartial justice is carried out
within our borders and to take all the responsibility for any military forces, and to
see that they are properly controlled and are
under the responsible Government.
I am sure the Government will remember
that it would be detrimental to public order
if any force, no matter how sympathetic it
might be to the Government, was allowed
to exist that was not under Government
control and for which the Government was
not responsible. I was very glad to hear the
Noble Marquess say that this Government
would always endeavour, not only to look
after the interests of the 3ix Counties, but
would consider the interests of the whole
country. You cannot live very comfortably
in a drawing-room if the dining-room is on
fire. As far as my humble opinion goes, it
certainly is a great credit to the Prime
Minister and his Cabinet, that they have
endeavoured in every way to meet the responsible Government of the rest of Ireland.
It does not mean weakness. It really means
strength. We can have no strength and no
security when the other part of Ireland is
in turmoil. We may have two Governments
-and I am not certain if we have not got
three-but there is really one Irish question,
and that is the problem of how two races
and two religious thoughts can inhabit this
one small island. Is it too much to hope
that statesmanship-if it cannot make it
possible for us both to work in absolute
harmony-may, at any rate, make it possible
for us to be as little inconvenience to each
other as possible and make the country
prosperous and peaceful? I am very glad to
support all that my hon. Friend, the mover,
and my hon. and gallant Friend, the
seconder, said with regard to the gracious
Speech from the Throne.
Mr. WOODS: At the commencement of the
speech we have just heard from the Noble
Marquess, the Leader of the House, he congratulated Lord Carson and yourself, Mr.
Speaker, on the fight you had made in the
House of Lords against the Treaty Bill.
I think it would be unfair if the Members
of this Serate did not offer some words of
congratulation to the Noble Marquess himself for the splendid fight he made on that
occasion. I understand he intends to cross
to London to take part in the debate on
the Second Reading of the Bill, and I am
sure we thank him in anticipation of the
address he will deliver on that occasion.
Many of his remarks to-day will tend to
create a feeling of trust amongst the people
who see eye to eye with us, but there is
nothing that will create greater confidence
than that ultimatum issued on February 6th
by our Cabinet to the British Cabinet to
the effect that they do not intend to give
over one inch of the Six Counties without
our consent. Nothing will tend to create
a greater feeling of security than that ultimatum. That is all I intend to say, but
I did not think it right that no reference
should be made to the valuable services
rendered by the Noble Marquess himself.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
lengthy and certainly very important speech
that the Noble Marquess, the Leader of the
House, has made deserves note. He has
brought to us very forcibly the considerable
difficulties that we have to contend with,
and also the deplorable state of the country,
and particularly the state of unrest in the
Six Counties. He certainly has made a
splendid appeal to our own followers to have
patience and control themselves and I do
wish to associate myself. being one of the
working-men Members of the Senate, with
the appeal he makes to these people. While
he has brought to our notice the difficulties
we have to contend with, at the same time
it is very consoling to find that the Government are entirely in touch with all the
difficulties and doing all they possibly can
to mitigate them and if possible to remove
them. In the speech which the Noble
Marquess made he referred to the splendid
work done by the Minister of Labour, and
I think all credit is due to the Minister of
Labour for what he has done, the energetic
21 Debate on Address.
Debate on Address. 22
14 MARCH 1922.
way he has taken up the duties in connection with that office, and the success that
has attended him. At the same time I am
very sorry that unemployment is still very
prevalent in our midst. I am really sorry
that something could not be done to remove
that. It has been stated here that if the
unrest were to be abolished unemployment
would also go and that trade would revive. I
really do think that trade would revive if
the unrest were abolished, because people
would have something to occupy their minds
and would probably be better engaged at
work than having to hang about accepting
doles. It is satisfactory to learn that the
Northern Government has made some
provision for the extension of the unemployment benefit, and yet it is not quite
satisfactory. I was listening somewhat
attentively to the Leader of the House to
see if he would give any intimation that
the considerable amount of work that the
Government of Northern Ireland has in
hands would shortly be undertaken. That
would be some little relief to unemploy
ment. We have considerable buildings to
erect, and if that work were pushed along
it would certainly remove some of the unemployment and assist the city at large. We
are faced with the disturbed state of the
city. There is no doubt there is an amount
of criticism at the present time being unfairly and unjustly levelled at the head of
the Government. I believe that the people
will recognise the great difficulties that the
Minister of Home Affairs has to contend
with. As has been pointed ont here, dual
control has had a great deal to do with
hampering his work in connection with the
disturbed state of the city. I do hope that
the people will have a little patience, and
give the Minister of Home Affairs a fair
opportunity of showing that he is able and
determined to deal with these offenders.
The fact that a Bill is likely to be introduced shows that they are fully mindful of
what is necessary. It has also been stated
-I think the Noble Marquess referred to the
fact that on the other side of the Channel
there is also industrial unrest in the shape
of trade disputes. I am sorry to say that
that dispute has also extended to Belfast.
It is really a pity when we consider the
political unrest which exists in our midst
that the industrial disputes should not have
been avoided at the present time. It is
really dangerous. I, for one, being one of
the working-class men, anxious for the welfare of our city and Province, should be
sorry to say anything that would cause
people to lose control of themselves.
understand the Minister of Labour has tried
to confine that dispute to across the Channel,
and has done his best to settle any differences that exist in Belfast. But he has not
up to the present been successful. I think
a little more energy on the part of the
Minister of Labour-and I know he has
exerted himself to a considerable extent-
I
might be able to bring about some satisfactory conclusion. It has been said by
employers that although Belfast workmen
are not in any way concerned with the dispute they, the employers, are only carrying
out the instructions of the Executive on the
other side of the Channel. It is a pity that
this is so, because son.e time ago one of the
trades in Beifast was instructed by the
Executive on the other side to strike against
Harland & Wolff's, and that trade, a very
large and important trade, recognised that
they were not in any way responsible and
refused to strike, with the result that they
sacrificed their membership of the trade
society, sacrificed all their savings and
superannuation benefit, rather than plunge
the city into this unnecessary strife. I wish
the employers would act in as reasonable a
way towards the workers, and with as much
concern for the welfare of the city.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
DEPUTY SPEAKERS.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move: "That the following Senators
be named Deputy Speakers of the Senate
during the present Session of Parliament-
Sir William Coates, Bart.
The Right Hon. Sir James Johnston."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move: "That the House do now
adjourn." Perhaps I should make a brief
statement on the Motion. I think you,
my Lord, are proposing, with others of
this House, to go over to London to take
part in the debate which takes place tomorrow and next day in the House of Lords.
Wednesday and Thursday, I understand,
will be occupied with the Second Reading,
and I do not know when the Committee
Stage, the Report Stage, and the Third
Reading will be taken. As there is no
pressing business before this House at the
moment, I venture to suggest that we adjourn
until one day next week, and if we provisionally said Thursday next week it might
suit the convenience of hon. Members. There
is no Government business until that date,
when I expect we shall have the Bill conferring powers
the Minister of Home
Affairs for the maintenance of law and order.
on
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
Minutes after Four of the
Clock until Thursday, 23rd
March, at 2-30 p.m.
Kitchen and Refreshment Rooms. 24
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 23rd March, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter before Three
of the clock, the Deputy-Speaker (Right
Hon. Sir James Johnston) in the Chair.
KITCHEN AND REFRESHMENT ROOMS
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
At the commencement of every Session it is
customary to appoint a Joint Committee for
the kitchen and refreshment rooms of both
Houses of Parliament. I propose that the
following Senators be named of the Joint
Committee:-The Viscount Bangor, Mr. J.
Cunningham, the Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham, Mr. J. G Leslie, Colonel the
Right Hon. R. G. Sharman-Crawford. Such
Committee to have power to agree with the
Committee of the House of Commons in the
appointment of a Chairman.
Moved: "That the following Senators be
named of the Joint Committee:-The
Viscount Bangor, Mr. J. Cunningham, the
Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham, Mr. J. G.
Leslie, Colonel the Right Hon. R. G.
Sharman-Crawford."-(Viscount
Massereene
and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Superannuation Bill.-The Commons
have passed the Superannuation Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of the
Senate.
SUPERANNUATION BILL.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The Superannuation Bill is a Bill to amend
the Superannuation Act, 1921. I beg to
move that this Bill be read a First time.
Moved: "That the Superannuation Bill be
read a First time."-(Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard.)
On Question, Bill was read the First time.
Ordered to be printed and to be read the
Second time on Tuesday next.
ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
As hon. Members
are aware, the Noble
Leader of this House could not be present
to-day, owing to the important amendments
he had to move in the House of Lords yesterday evening, where, I am sorry to say,
I could not be in attendance. I think it
only right to let hon. Members know, and
in moving the Adjournment of the House I
should like to make a statement as to what
we propose should be the business of the
Senate for the ensuing week. We do not
propose that the House should meet before
Tuesday of next week, when we hope to
have the Civil Authorities Bill from the
House of Commons and to give it a First
reading on that day. Hon. Members know
that the Bill is a very important one. We
also propose to have on that day the Second
reading of the Superannuation Bill. On the
same day, Tuesday, it is hoped to have the
First reading of the Consolidated Fund Bill
and the Exchequer (Borrowing) Bill. Both
these Bills, as hon. Members are aware, are
purely money Bills. Then on Wednesday of
next week it is proposed to take the two
money Bills through all their stages, so that
the Royal Assent to these Bills may be received on the same day, and also to take the
Second reading of the Civil Authorities Bill.
Beyond that I am not in a position to
pledge myself, or the Government, as to
what other measures may come before us,
but I think it will meet the convenience of
hon. Members if this House do now adjourn
till Tuesday next. I beg to move.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn." (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes before Three of the
Clock until Tuesday, 28th
March.
Superannuation Bill.
Superannuation Bill.
28 MARCH 1922.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 28th March, 1922.
The Senate met at half-past Two o'clock,
Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
BELFAST HARBOUR BILL.
Ordered: That the Belfast Harbour Bill
be read a First time.
SUPERANNUATION ACT, 1921
(AMENDING BILL).
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): In rising to move the
second reading of the Superannuation Bill
I will ask the indulgence of the House while
I relate very shortly the purpose of the
measure and the circumstances that have
given rise to its introduction. Honourable
Members will recollect that one of the first
measures we were called upon to pass in
this House was a Bill applying to the Civil
Service of Northern Ireland generally the
rights conferred upon the Imperial Civil
Service by the series of Superannuation
Acts from as far back as 1834 to 1914.
By Section 2 of the Superannuation Act
of 1909 it was provided that when a male
Civil Servant died whilst in the permanent
Civil Service of the Crown, the Treasury
were empowered to grant to his legal personal representative a gratuity equal to the
yearly salary and emoluments of his office.
In this regard the Act of 1914 further provided that the amount of the gratuity payable under Section 2 of the Act of 1909
might be as provided therein, or alternatively, such a sum as the Treasury might
have granted had the deceased Civil Servant
retired from the Civil Service on the ground
of ill-health at the date of his death.
In accordance with the purpose of the
Government of Northern Ireland, by the
Superannuation Act passed last session, to
endow our Civil Servants with all the rights
and privileges enjoyed by the Civil Servants
of Great Britain, it was intended by that
Act that the provisions I have mentioned
should also be applicable to the Civil Service of Northern Ireland. We are now advised by our Law Officers that it is doubtful whether under the Act which we passed
last Session a gratuity is payable in case
of death before the completion of five years'
service in the Civil Service of Northern
Ireland to the legal personal representative
of a specially appointed officer that is, an
officer who transferred from the Imperial
Civil Service to the Civil Service of Northern Ireland. I am sure that hon. Members realise that these Bills are very intricate, and our desire at all times is that
there should be no misunderstanding or possible misconstruction of any of the proposals which it was intended to introduce
in these Bills. That is why we are bringing in what is practically an Amending Bill
at this moment. We feel-and I know that
in this I shall have the agreement of every
one of the Members of this House-that we
are under a considerable debt of gratitude
to the specially appointed Civil Servants,
and we gave them a pledge as a condition
of their consenting to enter our service that
they should find themselves, at least, no
worse off for giving us their assistance than
they would have been if they had taken
another course.
The passing of the short measure before
the House to-day will leave no fear as to
disability being incurred by these gentlemen and a consequent breach of our pledges
to them. I beg to move.
Moved: That the Superannuation Act,
1921 (Amending Bill), be read a Second
time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
STANDING ORDERS RELATIVE TO
LOCAL BILLS.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move the appointment of the Joint
Standing Orders Committee, and also to
move that the Senators following, with the
Chairman of Committees, be named of the
Committee:-Mr. James H. Dickson, Mr. T.
M. Greer, Mr. J. A. Woods.
Moved: The appointment of the Joint
and also
Standing Orders Committee,
that the Senators following, with the
Chairman of Committees, be named of
the Committee:-Mr. James H. Dickson,
Mr. T. M. Greer, Mr. J. A. Woods.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
SITTING SUSPENDED.
we
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: It
is suggested as are at this moment
waiting for Money Bills to come down from
another place, and as there seems likely to
be some little delay, that the House should
adjourn during pleasure, and that when the
Bills arrive here we should resume our deliberations for the purpose of passing the
First reading of these Bills.
Senate adjourned during pleasure
The Senate resumed.
Adjournment,
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now
adjourn. I do not know that there is very
much that I should say on the adjournment
except that I feel that perhaps it is
necessary to bring before you the events
of the past week, and the proposals of the
Prime Minister at the present moment.
As you are aware the so-called Treaty Bill
has passed through all its stages in the
British Parliament, and is now awaiting
His Majesty's signature before it is put
into force. You will remember that
your representatives in both Houses of the
British Parliament did their best to amend
the measure and to make it more in accordance with our wishes. However, the British
Government took up a very strong stand
in the matter and refused to accept any
Amendments, and the Bill passed in its
original form, with the exception of one
Amendment, which the Government put in
themselves. That Amendment dealt with
the time when it will be possible for our
Government to vote Northern Ireland out
of the ambit of the Bill. I need not go
into this matter at the present moment.
The British Government seem to have
taken one view; we seem to have taken
another; and the representatives of the
Provisional Government in a letter which
they wrote at the time appeared to have
no special interest as to whether we were
capable of voting ourselves out of the Bill
at the passing of this so-called Treaty Bill,
or at a later stage when the constitution
will be brought up in the form of a Bill
before the British Parliament.
I do not propose now to make any comment
on the extraordinary situation set up oy
the British Government. It appears at the
moment to be negotiating within the confines of the British Empire with an administration that has no legal constitution or
status.
It appears to me that the
time has gone by when an alternative
policy for the government of Ireland
could be suggested, and that at the
present moment we must merely hope for
the best, and make up our minds that we
will maintain, as far as we possibly can, the
integrity of the position of Northern Ireland. That is a matter which concerns us.
That is an attitude consistent with what we
have stated on more occasions than onethat the British Government were entitled
to make any arrangements they liked with
the South and West, and that we only
came into the matter when our interests
were challenged or endangered.
Well, the great question at issue is the
question of the Boundary, which was
brought up in the so-called Treaty Bill. I
am not in a position to say why the British
Government thought it necessary to raise
We
the question of the Boundary at all.
were firmly of opinion that our area was
established by the 1920 Act, when our Parliament was set up here, and that the
boundary of Northern Ireland was established for all time or at least until such
time as
we decided to take another view.
Well, the interpretation now is that the
boundary is not decided, and as far as the
British Government is concerned it will
not be decided for some time to come. It
is impossible not to feel that the British
Government are running great risks and
are allowing a state of uncertainty to remain, attended with the most disastrous
consequences. We know perfectly well that
there is great anxiety throughout the Six
Counties, and more especially on the Boundary itself, as to what the future of the
inhabitants of the Boundary will be, and
I feel that all I can do is to urge upon
every loyal citizen to realise that it is absolutely necessary that we must maintain
law and conduct ourselves in a manner
which will be for the maintenance of law
and order in our midst.
You are aware that the British Government, through the agency of Mr. Churchill,
has summoned a conference to meet in
London-a conference of the representatives
of the Northern Government and of the
Provisional Government. The Prime
Minister and myself are crossing to-night,
and I take this opportunity of saying that
it will not be possible for me to carry out
my duties in this House for this week
owing to my having to go to London with
the Prime Minister.
Now the attitude which I took up in the
House of Lords in regard to the conference
was one which I feel is supported by all
those whom I have the honour to address
to-day. It will be in the recollection of
everyone it was reported in the newspapers
-that the Lord Chancellor made the suggestion that if only a conference could be
brought about a great measure of agreement
would be forthcoming. I ventured to say
that I did not see the object of these conferences because, as there appeared to be
no stability in the Provisional Government,
it was impossible for them to carry out
undertakings which they made. This
conference is not quite on the lines of the
conference the Lord Chancellor was referring to and to which I referred last week
in the House of Lords. This conference,
I assume, is called by Mr. Charchill tur
the purpose of the two Governments meeting
in the hope that those disastrous occurrences and this apparently unchecked orgy
of crime which is in existence at this
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
28 MARCH 1922.
moment can be controlled, and that the
outcome of this conference will be the
setting up of some system or understanding
by which it will be possible to enable the
authorities of the North and South to
maintain the law.
conference
I need hardly say that any
that has as its object the removal of crime
from our midst and the maintaining of the
law has my fullest sympathy and fullest
concurrence. I hope that when the Prime
Minister and I return here we shall be
able to feel that we have advanced, and
that both Governments will have a strong
grip on the areas over which their jurisdiction exists for the purpose of maintaining
law.
I do not feel that I can comment very much
on what has been occurring lately in our
midst. I ventured when we were met here
at the opening of this session to urge upon
everyone the necessity of realising their
responsibility. I should like to make a
further appeal to the loyal citizens in our
counties. At the present moment there
seems to be an absence of public opinion
for checking these crimes which are of,
daily occurrence amongst us. I was given
to understand that the other day a crime
was committed. Two Specials were shot
at mid-day in a comparatively crowded
thoroughfare. The murderers, after perpetrating this odious crime, turned round,
ran down the street through the crowd, and
disappeared. Well, that is a state of affairs
which cannot be tolerated at all. At other
times of our history as far as we know
if a crime were committed in the middle of
a crowd the escape of the miscreant would
be impossible. That is really what I would
venture to suggest to the citizens of Belfast
who are complaining more or less and
criticising the Government for not taking
steps to check these crimes and to arrest
the criminals. It is impossible for the
authorities to check every crime. If you
will consider the matter in all its aspects
crimes are bound to be committed unless
there is a universal public opinion
determined to take every possible step
to put down crime. I venture to appeal to
the citizens of Belfast to realise that every
one of them may have some part to play in
the maintenance of law and order. I sincerely hope that they will realise the difficulty of the position and that the Northern
Government are doing everything in their
power with the instruments which they have
at their disposal. What they do require,
and must have, is the support of public
opinion in the efforts which they are endeavouring to put forward.
I do not know that I can make any statement about public business at the present
moment. I think before putting the motion
there are one or two questions that hon.
Members would like to ask. I beg to move
that this House do now adjourn.
Motion: That the House do now adjourn
(The Marquess of Londonderry).
in
Mr. DUFFIN: I am sure it was with
much satisfaction that every Member of this
House learned that the Noble Lord the
Leader of this House will accompany the
Prime Minister to this conference.
We perhaps will look forward with the hope, but
not, I am afraid, with any very
great expectation, that this conference
will have a very marked effect
producing the results that are hoped
for. I am quite sure that our Government
will be able to satisfy the Imperial Government, and I hope also those whom they meet
in conference, that our desire here is, if it
is possible, to live in peace and to assist in
every way in putting down these horrible
outrages which scandalised and horrified the
entire community. I am sure the Noble
Lord did not intend to convey that there
was any absence of strong public opinion.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRRY:
Hear, hear.
Mr. DUFFIN: Or of support for the
Government in their very great difficulties.
I am satisfied that among all well living
citizens here there is the strongest desire to
do everything in their power in support of
the Government taking immediate steps to
endeavour to put an end to the disorder and
lawlessness and to arrest and punish the
criminals. I think that has been publicly
expressed in a great many quarters already,
and it is the universal attitude of all decent
citizens.
Mr. LONG: I should like to ask the Noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, whether the
hour of meeting-half-past Two-is irrevocably fixed, or whether it is competent for
us to alter it, and if it is competent whether
an alteration would meet with the concurrence of the majority of the Members of
this House? For those travelling on the
Midland Railway the time is most unsuitable. The mid-day train arrives at the
station at a quarter to three, too late for the
meeting of this House. For those travelling
on the train leaving Derry at seven-and
travelling a distance to it-it means a very
