early start in the morning. As a matter of
fact, one Member of the Senate has to get
up at 4 o'clock in the morning in order to
reach here at half-past two. If it would suit
the convenience of the House, I am sure it
would be a great advantage to those travelling on the Midland Railway if we could
suggest some alteration.
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
THE
SENATE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This is a matter which I think we ought to
consider. I am sure the hon. Gentleman
will not expect me to give a definite answer
to-day. There are many considerations to
be thought of. There is the hour at which
the House rises. I admit what was in my
head was the train by which hon. Members
might want to leave Belfast. This is
a matter for arrangement. I am sure also
that the majority of the House have no desire that any hon. Member should have to
start at so early an hour for the purpose of
arriving in time for the meetings. This is
a matter for consideration of Mr. Speaker,
and perhaps if the hon. Gentleman will
allow me I will confer with him as to what
the wishes of the majority of hon. Members
are.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask if I understand
correctly that it is proposed that the House
should meet to-morrow and Thursday, and if
so at what time?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The proposal is that the House should meet
to-morrow at 2-30. The House will also sit on
Thursday and probably on Friday.
Mr. LONG: I wish to thank the Noble Lord
for his consideration of this matter, and for
the kind way he has looked at our position.
There is another matter which is perhaps not
quite in order. As one who has a very
extended experience of Local Government
-perhaps so extensive as to be unique-
I desire to congratulate the Government
on the change they have made in the method
of payment to Local Authorities of Government grants. I know we have nothing to do
with finance, but it is a step in the right
direction. The Government have adopted the
method of taking the average of the last three
years. This is a tremendous boon to those
preparing estimates for the Local Authorities-accountants and such like. It is also a
very considerable advantage to the ratepayers. In such a year as 1920, which was a
marvellously good year in the matter of
grants, the ratepayers stand to gain.
accountants stand to gain, and all those who
are preparing estimates. It is an immense
boon. I congratulate the Government on
their business-like way of dealing with this
matter, and I am very proud to have to say
So.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The
The Senate adjourned accordingly at five minutes before
Four of the Clock, until
Wednesday, 29th March, at
2-30 p.m.
Special Powers Bill.
Consolidated Fund Bill.
29 MARCH 1922.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 29th March, 1922.
The Senate met at half-past Two of the
Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That the Commons have passed the Consolidated Fund Bill, and the Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of the Senate.
CONSOLIDATED FUND BILL.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that the Consolidated Fund
Bill be read a First time.
Motion: That the Consolidated Fund
Bill be read First time.-(Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
a
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CIVIL AUTHORITIES (SPECIAL
POWERS) BILL.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this Bill be read a First
time.
Motion: That the Civil Authorities
(Special Powers) Bill be read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
SUSPENSION OF STANDING ORDERS.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In rising to move the suspension of Standing
Order No. 23, Sub-section 3, and Standing
Order No. 25, I should like to explain to the
House that we are taking this course in
order that the Consolidated Fund Bill,
which we have just received from another
place, should pass through all its stages this
afternoon with a view to its receiving the
Royal Assent to-day. This course is very
necessary owing to the nearness of the close
of the financial year. I am quite sure that
hon. Members will not object to taking this
course in the emergency. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CONSOLIDATED FUND BILL.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In moving the Second reading of the Consolidated Fund Bill I might say, as hon.
Members are aware, this is a Money Bill
which we cannot amend. The effect of this
Bill is to authorise the grant of money from
the Consolidated Fund; in other words, to
provide money to meet the grants which
have been made in another place. It consists of four clauses, and as far as Clause I.
is concerned its object is to provide money
to carry on the services pending the full consideration of the estimates. The present Bill
is needed to authorise issues up to the net
sum total of the Supplementary Estimates,
plus the amount of the vote on account,
namely, £2,025,418, which amount is the
sum total of the sums which
in
appear
Clauses I. and II. of the Bill. As regards
Clause II., the important clause in this Bill,
its object is this: The Budget of any year
should be so arranged that it would be
unnecessary under normal conditions to incur debt. But it may easily happen that
during certain periods of the year the expenditure may exceed the revenue owing to the
unequal rate at which the two sides of the
account mature. It is therefore necessary to
obtain power from Parliament to borrow to
meet a temporary deficiency. As hon. Members are aware, in the Imperial House of
Commons it is customary in similar Bills to
obtain power to borrow the whole sum, but
as there is obviously some revenue coming
in, this course is unjustifiable and is only
adopted by tradition. There appears to be
no second reason for following this prece-
Ident, and it is proposed in this Bill, as in
the case of the Consolidated Fund Appropriation (Northern Ireland) Act, 1921, to limit
the borrowing to a sum of one million. I
beg to move that this Bill be read a Second
time.
Motion: That the Consolidated Fund Bill
be read a Second time. (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this Bill be committed.
On Question, Motion negatived.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this Bill be read a Third
time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this Bill be now passed.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
THE SUPERANNUATION BILL.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this House do resolve
itself into Committee on the Superannuation
Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
(In Committee.)
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair.)
Clause I. (Amendment of S. 2 of 12 Geo. 5,
c. 3. 9 Edw. 7, c. 10. 4 and 5 Geo. 5, c. 86).
Ordered: That the Clause stand part of the
Bill,
Superannuation Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
Clause II. (Short Title and Construction).
Ordered: That the Clause stand part of
the Bill.
Ordered: That the Title and Preamble be
adopted.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without Amendment to the House.
The House resumed, and the Bill was reported accordingly.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that the Report be now received.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons have passed the Exchequer
Borrowing (Local Loans) Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of the Senate.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this Bill be read a First
time.
Motion: That the Exchequer Borrowing
(Local Loans) Bill be read a First time.--
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
ROYAL ASSENT.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I am authorised by the Prime Minister, on
his behalf, to inform this House that His
Excellency the Lord Lieutenant, in the name
and on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the Consolidated Fund Bill.
ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In moving the Adjournment of this House
I may say I understand that the hour at
which this House meets is not convenient to
several hon. Members of the House, and I
propose, with the concurrence of the House,
that we should meet for the future at 3-15
p.m. instead of 2-30 p.m., and that the House
should start meeting at 3-15 from Friday,
31st March. I believe it will meet the convenience of hon. Members who come from a
distance and will not inconvenience hon.
Members who live in the city.
Motion: That this House do now adjourn. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask the Noble Lord
the nature of the business? Is it proposed
to take the Civil Authorities (Special
Powers) Bill to-morrow?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In reply to the question of the hon. Member
it is not proposed to take the Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill to-morrow. I
understand that this Bill will not be in the
hands of hon. Members a sufficient time to
enable them to consider it. This Bill will
probably not be taken before Friday next at
the earliest.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a
quarter before Three of the
Clock.
Superannuation Bill.
Adjournment.
30 MARCH 1922.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 30th March, 1922.
The Senate met at half past Two of the
Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
SUPERANNUATION
BILL.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this Bill be now read
a Third Time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that this Bill do now pass.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
A message was sent to the House of Commons to acquaint them that the Senate have
agreed to the Superannuation Bill without
any amendment.
EXCHEQUER BORROWING AND LOCAL
LOANS BILL.
I
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
As this House will recognise, the Exchequer
Loans Bill comes
Borrowing and Local
within the category of Money Bills which
are not susceptible of amendment here.
will, therefore, not trespass long upon the
indulgence of the House in moving the
Second Reading. The Bill has two main
purposes, namely, to enable the Finance
Minister to borrow for the Exchequer Account and to issue loans to Local Authorities
out of the Consolidated Fund. With regard
to the exercise of borrowing powers, these
are required to enable my right hon. Friend
to make loans to Local Authorities, to make
advances to the Unemployment Fund, and
to supply any deficiency of revenue.
The issuing power is sought for under this
Bill only in the case of Loans to Local Authorities because this power is already
granted, by means of separate Bills, or
will be sought for in similar cases if any
further authority is required. Therefore,
a separate Bill will be introduced to extend
the power of issuing for the Unemployment
Fund, while the Consolidated Fund Bill
gives power to issue for deficiencies of revenue. In brief, the borrowing powers of
this Bill are embodied in the authority it
gives for the Ulster Savings Certificates.
The Bill will give the security of the Consolidated Fund of Northern Ireland to the
investor in these Ulster Certificates and this
security will be further backed by the guarantee of the United Kingdom, the Imperial
Government having pledged itself to pass
the necessary legislation for this purpose.
The Bill also includes power to invest any
proceeds of these issues of Certificates which
are not immediately required for specified
purposes in order that the moneys upon
which we have to pay interest to the investor
may earn their interest instead of lying idle.
In regard to the power to issue for loans
to Local Authorities which this Bill gives,
I may say that with our limited requirements
we do not need a separate Fund such as they
have in the United Kingdom for this purpose. We propose to issue direct from the
Consolidated Fund, and the receipts from
the issue of Certificates will be paid into the
Exchequer for the credit of the Consolidated
Fund in accordance with the plan of my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Finance,
by which he proposes to conduct all operations through that single principal fund.
I think the House will agree in supporting
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Finance, by passing this measure, for there
is to be found in it the encouragement of
public thrift and the
economy of
administration which invariably characterises measures associated with his
I beg to move:
same
name.
Motion: "That the Exchequer Borrowing
and Local Loans Bill be read a Second
Time." (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move the adjournment of this
House. We propose, to-morrow, to take
the Second Reading of the Civil Authorities
of Northern Ireland (Special Powers) Bill
and in addition the Belfast Harbour Bill
Second Reading. I think this will meet
the convenience of this House. I should like
to remind the House also that to-morrow we
meet at 3-15.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a
quarter before Three of
the Clock, until Friday,
31st March, at 3-15 p.m.
Belfast Harbour Bill.
Asylum Grants.
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Friday, 31st March, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter past Three
of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That the Commons have passed the Unemployment Fund Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of the Senate.
UNEMPLOYMENT FUND (NORTHERN
IRELAND) BILL.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be read a
First Time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
BELFAST HARBOUR BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. DUFFIN: I beg to move that this Bill
be read a Second Time. I need not trouble
the House with any details of the Bill. It is
promoted by the Belfast Harbour Commissioners, and is unopposed. I may, perhaps,
be allowed to make the observation that it is
satisfactory to see the powers and machinery
now exercised by Parliament with regard to
special Private Bill legislation are so early
brought to perfection.
Mr. GREER: In this Bill which I had an
opportunity of seeing some days ago, there
are two clauses which I considered objectionable. These were Clause II as to temporary
securities, and Clause VII as to making the
Harbour Stock a Trustee Security. I entered
into communication with the representatives
of the Harbour Commissioners, and went into
the matter with them fully. I am glad to
say that they have agreed now to add some
words to Clause II. which remove my objection to it, and to delete Clause VII altogether.
With these alterations, which will no doubt
be put into the Bill in Committee, I feel
myself in a position to support it. I understand that my hon. Friend is aware of the
alterations which it is proposed to make, and
which no doubt will be duly made in Committee.
Mr. DUFFIN: I can say on behalf of the
promoters of the Bill that they are prepared
to adopt the suggestions made by the hon.
Member.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Committed to a Joint Unopposed Committee.
DEFICIT IN ASYLUM GRANTS.
Mr. GREER: Mr. Long, who is unable to be
here to-day, has asked me to put the question
which tands in his name: To ask the Leader
of the House whether he is aware that the
deficit in the Asylum Grants in the County of
Londonderry in the last ten years amounted
to £8,756, and as the Imperial Government
are responsible for that deficit, whether the
Government of Northern Ireland will make
representations to the Imperial Government,
in order that this and other similar deficits
in Northern Ireland may be refunded.
Vi count MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The record of payments of Asylum Grants for
the last 10 years were kept in Dublin, and the
Government of Northern Ireland have no
official information in regard to them.
It is under tood, however, that the deficit in
the Asylum grants in the County Londonderry
for the period in question amounts to upwards
of £8,000.
These deficits are due to the abatements
which have had necessarily to be made in pursuance of Section 58 (1) (5) of the Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1898, owing to the
insufficiency of the sums payable into the
Local Taxation (Ireland) Account to meet the
sums payable under the Section.
The payments made out of the account under
that Section include grants for Medical and
Educational expenditure in Poor Law Unions,
Sanitary Salaries in Rural and Urban Districts, as well as grants to County Councils for
the maintenance of the Lunatic Poor, and
corresponding abatements were made throughout Ireland generally in all these grants.
Accordingly, the Asylum grants must be
treated in conjunction with the other grants
referred to, and as the abatements were made
in pursuance of Statute generally throughout
Ire.and, it is not considered that any useful
purpose would be served by the Government
of Northern Ireland approaching the Imperial Government in the matter.
We
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
have received the Unemployment Bill
from the Commons, and I beg to move that
Standing Orders Nos. xxii. (3) and xxv. be
considered in order to their being suspended
for the purpose of passing the Unemployment
Fund Bill through all its stages this afternoon. This is a financial measure, and my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Finance,
desires these powers without delay. I hope
that under the circumstances the House will
consent to the suspension of the Standing
Orders.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Special Powers Bill.
Local Loans Bill.
31 MARCH 1922.
CIVIL AUTHORITIES (SPECIAL
POWERS) BILL.
Order for Second reading having been read,
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I have to make an important announcement to this House with regard to this Bill
Having regard to the agreement which has
been arrived at in London between the British
Government, the Government of Northern
Ireland, and the Provisional Government and
the legal and technical points which it
raises, it is essential that an opportunity
should be afforded to the Government of Northern Ireland to consider to what extent, if
any, the agreement entered into affects the
provisions of this Bill. Accordingly it is
proposed that the debate on the Second reading of this Bill should be adjourned until
Tuesday next. I beg to move that the Second
reading be adjourned until Tuesday next.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to intervene, and
I only do so in order to say how glad we
all are to hear from the Noble Lord that
there is a prospect of a settlement of the
present state of unrest which exposes us all,
and particularly the city of Belfast to so
much inconvenience. I also wish to take the
opportunity of asking the Noble Lord if he
could have it arranged for this House to be
furnished with authentic copies of the arrangement or agreement or whatever it is
called. I make that suggestion because it is
not quite satisfactory that in a matter of this
kind we should have to rely entirely on the
newspaper reports. I venture to hope it will
be possible to meet my suggestion.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In reply to my hon. Friend, who, I understand, desires that authentic copies of the
articles of agreement should be available for
every Member of this House, I may say that
we have not as yet received copies of this
agreement. When we do so and as soon as
they are printed, I have no doubt they will
be laid on the Table of this House, and Members will have an opportunity of studying
them.
EXCHEQUER BORROWING AND LOCAL
LOANS BILL
Order for Committee read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that the House do resolve itself
into Committee on this Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly, the Marquess of
Dufferin and Ava in the Chair.
Clause I. (Power to Borrow for certain Purposes).
Clause II. (Charge in respect of Guarantee
by Treasury of United Kingdom).
Clause III. (Payments into and out of Exchequer and Temporary Investment).
Clause IV. (Local Loans).
and
Clause V. (Short Title).
Ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Ordered: That the Preamble and Title stand
part of the Bill.
Ordered: That the Chairman report this
Bill without Amendment to the House
The House resumed.
Mr. SPEAKER: The Committee has gone
through the Exchequer Borrowing and
Local Loans Bill, and directed me to report
same without amendment to the House.
Moved: That the report of the Committee
on the Exchequer and Local Loans Bill be
now received. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that Standing Order No. XXV.
be considered in order to its being dispensed
with in respect of the Exchequer Borrowing
and Local Loans Bill, so that this measure
may go through its remaining stages this
afternoon. This is a financial Bill, and I
trust the House will not object to the suspension of Standing Orders.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved That this Bill be read 3a.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill be now passed.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
have agreed to the said Bill without any
amendment.
UNEMPLOYMENT FUND BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Sure.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In rising to move the Second reading of the
Unemployment Fund Bill, I will make the
shortest possible statement to the House, giving the purpose and effect of this short mea-
Owing to the unfortunate trade depression under which we have been labouring
during the past twelve months the drain upon
the Unemployment Fund of the United Kingdom has been so heavy that this Fund has
become temporarily insolvent, a state which
we hope brighter days of renewed trading activity will relieve and ultimately remove altogether. It is therefore necessary that the
43 Unemployment Fund Bill.

THE SENATE.
Adjournment.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.]
Exchequer here, as it does over the water,
should make a subvention to the Unemployment Fund, and the present measure gives
power to my right hon. Friend, the Finance
Minister, to make advances out of the Con-
Eolidated Fund of a sum not exceeding one
million pounds. This amount is in the nature
of a loan, and we are determined not to be
found backward in giving to those of our
fellow-citizens who are suffering from the
evils of unemployment all the assistance in
our power. With these few words I beg to
move the Second reading of the Unemployment Fund Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill be now Committeed.
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That the Bill be read 3a.-(Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill be now passed.—
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Que Fion, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
have agreed to the said Bill without any
Amendment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.—
(Vi count Massereena and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive Minutes to Four of the
Clock until Tuesday next at 3-15
p.m.
Peace Negotiations.
Peace Negotiations.
4 APRIL 1922.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 4th April, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, The Speaker in the Chair.
BELFAST HARBOUR BILL.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move that the Senators following
be named of the Joint Unopposed Committee on the said Bill:-
Mr. J. G. Leslie.
Col.
The Right Honourable R. D.
Perceval-Maxwell.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Said Committee to meet to-morrow.
THE PEACE AGREEMENT.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
ask the Leader of the House if he can make
any statement in regard to the agreement
arrived at as a result of the recent conference in London. In asking this question
there are one or two points I would like
to particularly emphasise.
If this agreement is carried out trial by jury will be
done away with. I have no objection to
trial by jury being done away with to meet
an extreme emergency, but if we make it
permanent under the Treaty it will be very
difficult to alter it in future years, if it can
be altered at all. Therefore I will be glad
to hear that this will only be a temporary
arrangement until matters are righted in
this Province. The same thing applies to
the question of the Press Censorship. We
know the grievance that exists in another
country in connection with the control of
the Press, and we all feel it has caused
trouble which might have been avoided.
When we realise that newspapers in the
North of Ireland will be censored, and other
newspapers in the other parts of Ireland
will not be censored, and will be circulated
in the North and other parts, I think it will
be agreed that this censorship should be
limited to the necessities of the times. The
other question I would like to ask is with
regard to the signatures of the members of
the British Government. Appended to this
Treaty are the names of four members of
the British Government. In no part of the
Treaty cau I read that they agree to do anything. Did they merely sign as witnesses
to the signatures of the others, or do they
undertake to carry out anything in the
agreement? I can remember that in the
old days when birthday books were in
common use we all tried to get signatures,
and as far as I can see the signatures of the
four members of the British Government
are appended to the Treaty in the same way.
I shall be very pleased to hear that they
have undertaken to carry out the terms of
the Agreement.
STATEMENT BY MARQUESS OF
LONDONDERRY.
MARQUES'S OF
LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I should like to
thank the right hon. Gentleman for giving
me this opportunity of making a statement
in regard to the Agreement which has just
been entered into between the Northern
Government and the Provisional Government, and which is countersigned by the
British Government. He has asked me
three specific questions. The first relates to
the duration of the Bill of which I shall
shortly have the honour to move the Second
reading. Mention of the Bill appears in the
Agreement, and I take it that the right hon.
Gentleman does not understand whether
or not this is to be a permanent measure.
am sure no one will quarrel with
the right hon. Gentleman for his remarks in
this connection. We are all vigilant
custodians of the rights of democracy, and
none of us are anxious to have in force
measures of greater power and influence
than is absolutely necessary. But I think
he will find the answer to his question in
the final clause of the Bill which I am
shortly to introduce. It is
I
"This Act shall continue in force for
one year and no longer unless Parliament
otherwise determines."
That is certainly a very salutory provision, and one which I am sure is in accordance with the wishes of the right hon.
Gentleman. He has also mentioned the restraint of the Press, and that is another
matter in which I am sure all of us will
agree with him. No one is anxious that the
Press should be muzzled or restrained, but
I think everyone will agree that in these
anxious times when public opinion is inflamed to such a degree as it is at the
present moment communiqués given in
consultation with the Press will probably
have a more steadying effect on public
opinion than if the Press prints anything
which it may desire. I am in entire agree
ment with the right hon. Gentleman when
says that if you
restrain
one Press and not another, great
many difficulties can arise, but I
do sincerely hope that if there are difficulties
in that direction, the other Press
will see the position in which we are placed
and will do their best to meet a situation
he
a
Peace Negotiations.
Peace Negotiations.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
which is of general as well as public importance. The right hon. Gentleman mentioned the signatures of various Members
of the British Government. I am sure he
will recollect that this was a Conference
which was called by the British Government
to meet a particular public emergency and
that the Conference was conducted under the
presidency of Mr. Winston Churchill. Consequently my own opinion is that it was
necessary that the Agreement should be
counter-signed by the British Government,
and I think that if the right hon. Gentleman will consider that if the Agreement
fails, that if no good results follow
the Agreement come to by the Northern
Government and the Provisional Government on certain matters, he will realise
there is a necessity for the British Government to step in and reconsider the whole
position. However much certain opinion in
Ireland may desire to cut Ireland off entirely from England it certainly is not our
desire in the North, and I am sure we shall
always look to the British Government to
sympathise with us and to assist us in all
measures which we shall take for the benefit
of this Province and also indirectly for the
benefit of the whole of Ireland. That is the
reason it was necessary for the British
Government to be signatories to an agreement of this description.
The Conference which took place in London is naturally one of very great importance. I think hon. Members will recollect
that it was on the invitation of the British
Government that your representatives went
over to London to take part in that Conference, and the reason was, as I have already
said, that a great public emergency presented itself not only to us but to the people
of England which the Government believed
could be remedied, or rather could be mitigated, by negotiation at a round table by
responsible representatives in Ireland who
are capable, or who at least we hope are capable, of maintaining law and order. I can
inform you that the Agreement was arrived
at after very long and very earnest consideration, and I feel sure everyone will agree
that whatever criticisms of detail there may
be in connection with this Agreement it was
an honest attempt to grapple with a very
difficult and also an unparalleled situation.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I should like
to pay a tribute to our Prime Minister. I
had the honour to be associated with him
and to attach my signature to the Agreement
with him, and I should like to say that no
one took a more leading or a more notable
part in the Conference than did our Prime
Minister. During the long hours of those
two days no one fought harder in the interest of peace; no one could have been more
steadfast or more loyal to the people of the
Six Counties and to the interests and rights
of our Province, and I think he can look
back on his work in that Conference with
satisfaction and with the knowledge that he
did every single thing a man could do in
the interests of peace. In no way did he
sacrifice one single principle for which we
stand and in which we believe. In my judgment the Agreement was a great step forward. So far it has been a step forward on
paper, and it remains for every rightminded man and woman in all parts of Ireland to come forward now to translate into
action the undertakings which are embodied
in that Agreement. I have reason to believe
that the Sinn Fein signatories are sincere
in their desire for peace and are ready to do
their utmost to carry out their part of the
terms. But the question which we are
naturally asking ourselves at the moment
is,
be
"Are they capable of doing so?" That
is a matter which is exercising our minds
very considerably. We have seen from the
newspapers that their difficulties in the
South are even greater than the difficulties
with which we are faced. We know that
Mr. De Valera is endeavouring to enlist support and is doing his utmost to frustrate
the Provisional Government in the action
they are taking. But whatever their difficulties are it appears to me that our position
remains the same. Our duties are the same
and our responsibilities are the same; but
if there is to be chaos in the South of Ireland our difficulties will naturally
greater. I do not feel inclined at this moment to say anything of criticism of the
South. We are well aware of the intricacies
of the problem with which we have for so
long been faced. The British Government
have assumed-and I sincerely hope that
their assumption is correct-that Mr. Collins
is anxious to establish a Government and to
maintain law and order, and for that reason
they have made a Treaty with the South of
Ireland. It remains to be seen whether the
British Government are right and whether
the Provisional Government is able to carry
out the undertakings which they have entered into with the British Government.
The path of peace is beset with difficultiesdifficulties which must be faced and overcome, and this task will require both courage and patience.
I do not desire to go into the past. I feel
that no one will believe that my ideas have
changed at all. I know that everyone is
well aware of what I have had in my mind
all my life-the best method by which peace
and prosperity can come, not only to Ireland, but to Great Britain and the Empire.
I believe in Union, I believe in unity, and
I have had no reason to think that the wis-
Peace Negotiations.
Peace Negotiations.
4 APRIL 1922.
dom of Pitt and Castlereagh has been
shown to be wrong But this policy of the
Union has for a time at all events been discarded. Therefore in my opinion it would not
only be foolish, but it would also be wrong if
we stood aside and brooded over what might
have been. We are dealing now with the
present, and we must make the best of the
present and do what we can to ensure the
prosperity and happiness of the people
whom we have been called upon to govern.
But the future is vague and uncertain, and
we can only hope that the fears which may
be in our minds at the moment will prove
to be unfounded. But we have not stood
aside here; we have embarked on a new
venture. It is quite true we have embarked on
it reluctantly, but we have not been behindhand in our efforts to make it a success-in
fact we have done our best to make it a
success by the enthusiasm with which we
have translated into action the words with
which we inaugurated our Parliament, now
nearly a year ago.
we
When we consider this matter, we should
count our assets, and we should use the
instruments of Government such as they
are to ensure peace and progress in o'r
midst, and demonstrate that it is the
character of our people which really
counts. As to our assets, we have our
Parliament in existence, legislating for the
benefit of the people of the Six Counties.
We have our judiciary, which is the foundation of all government, and moreover
have a stout-hearted population behind us
of whom we have every reason to be justly
proud. We only require peace to enable
the whole wheels of the machinery of
Government to revolve smoothly and
regularly. But let us look at the facts as
they are, and not be led away by fears or
preindices. We cannot have real peace here
-and this is a point I should like to
emphasise unless there is pence in the
South of Ireland. We have been forced to
blame the British Government. I think
their culpable ineptitude in allowing Ireland to sink into a state of chaotic degradation is something for which we shall always
bitterly criticise the post-war administration
in England.
We now see in the South of Ireland what
is called a Provisional Government. It is
a very uncertain Government. It is
asailed on all sides, it is weighed down to
a great extent by its own former misdeeds,
but it seems to me to contain-and I hope
I am right-some elements of a desire to
govern, and to administer those forms of
Government for the benefit of the people
whom they control. I know that the
difficulties with which they are faced seem
almost insurmountable, but we must hope
for our own benefit that they will be
enabled to overcome those difficulties. I
have before me a notice of the boycott of
Belfast, which is to take place on and after
10th April. It is signed by an individual
who styles himself a Brigadier-General.
This Brigadier General is an official in Mr.
De Valera's army. I have never yet seen
any inclination on the part of Mr. De
Valera to do anything constructive; in fact,
all his actions appear to me to be
destructive and opposed to any ideals we
have of consolidating or bringing peace to
this country. These are matters which we
have got to watch. I have no doubt that
before very long we shall be in possession
of information as to whether or not the
Provisional Government is able to function
and to carry out the terms of the Agreement to which our Prime Minister, Mr,
Archdale, and myself have set our
signatures. I would like to draw your
attention to the Agreement and to two vital
points incorporated in that document. The
first one is Article VI., which reads
"I.R.A. activity to cease in the Six
Counties."
That is a matter which I think you will
agree is a step forward if it can be carried
out. Moreover, it proves that if there is any
activity on the part of the I.R.A., it is not
the activity of the Provisional Government,
and that our hands are free to deal with the
miscreants who style themselves the I.R.A.
and disturb the peace in our area. The
other point which is a corollary to that is in
Article X.-
"The two Governments shall in cases
agreed upon between the signatories
arrange for the release of political
prisoners in prison for offences before the
date hereof. No offences committed after
31st March, 1922, shall be open to consideration."
That is a very important point indeed,
and one to which I would like to direct the
attention of hon. Members. It means that
after the date mentioned all persons apprehended for indictable offences will not be
considered in a special light, and no representation will be made to this Government
on political grounds, as in the past, for the
release of such prisoners. Hon. Members
may recollect that certain persons were released on grounds of conciliation, but after
31st March last, any malefactor apprehended in the Six Counties will be dealt
with by the law of the Six Counties, and
shall be open, in the words of the Agreement, to no consideration. Then there is
the question of the Joint Police Force. I
feel that a provision of this sort must enlist
D
51 Peace Negotiations.
Peace Negotiations.
THE SENATE.

[Marquess of Londonderry]
COthe sympathy and the approbation of every
right-minded man. It is quite intelligible
that the Provisional Government should
express anxiety as to the safety, the weland
fare,
their
the protection of
religionists It is an attitude which we
should take up. We should do whatever
lies in our power, at all events by representation, to protect the interests of our coreligionists living in the South of Ireland,
and I feel we have made a great step forward if we are able to enlist the sympathy
and co-operation of the Roman Catholics in
Belfast and the Sik Counties in the maintenance of law and order.
There seems to be abroad a spirit of revenge and a spirit of vindictiveness which
really takes us back to the dark ages. One
almost feels, and it is true, that the elements and principles of civilisation have
been brought into existence for the purpose
of checking these primitive instincts, and of
and
allowing!
to enjoy civil
everyone
religious liberty, but what is being practised
all over Ireland is the old, antiquated,
and primitive principle of an eye for an
eye and a tooth for a tooth. That is a
system which must be stopped, at all events
in our midst. After all, these crimes are
committed by a very small minority of the
They
population.
in
committed
defiance of the great majority of the
citizens of the whole of Ireland, and we
cannot allow ourselves to be intimidated by
a criminal minority who are responsible for
these outrageous crimes perpetrated in Belfast at varying intervals. The Agreement
which we have signed involves a far larger
issue than the adjustment of the quarrel
between Protestant and Roman Catholic.
are
I should like the public to realise that
this is an Agreement in a great Imperial
spirit. Great Britain, as she has been for
many years past, is the dominant factor in
the world. At the present moment the
world is assailed with difficulties and
dangers which are the aftermath of the
Great War. I am not sure if I am guilty
of exaggeration when I say that on Great
Britain and the Empire alone depend the
re-adjustment of the world and the reinstatement of the countries of the world in
the position they should occupy to help to
promote the progress of civilisation. There
is a paramount duty on all citizens of Great
Britain and Ireland to do everything in
their power to strengthen the potentiality of
Great Britain as a world-factor, as a strong
and steady influence in the welter of problems with which we are surrounded at this
moment.
Ulster played a noble part in the Great
War. Her record is second to none, and 1
ous,
feel that Ulster, by playing a great, generand magnanimous part now can take
the lead in the United Kingdom in settling
those problems which are the aftermath of
the War. I would venture, as far as 1 am
able to do so, to urge upon everyone to have
done with suspicion and misconception. I
know there is a fear in the minds of a great
many people that some principle has been
the
abandoned, that in negotiating with
Provisional Government we have abandoned
principles which we should conserve. I desire to assert that no principle has been
abandoned. Our Prime Minister is not a
man who is likely to abandon principles.
We are not a bigoted race here, and we
must never become one. It has always been
an indictment against Roman Catholics
that they seem by their doctrines to make
for disintegration; they seem in their tenets
to sow the seed of segregation. As Minister
of Education, my desire is to get over this
difficulty and to bring, if I can, the children
of all religions and all faiths together, so
that they can grow up knowing each other
and knowing each other's difficulties and
characteristics, and thus do away with the
possibility of disunion. Our creed has always been the maintenance of civil and religious liberties, and now we have an opportunity of proving cur theories I think
hon. Members reading this Agreement will
say that as your representatives we have
shown our desire to co-operate with and enlist the sympathy of everyone, no matter
what religion they belong to or what faith
they may profess. The South of Ireland
seems to shout unity to us across the border
in threatening terms. Threats leave me
cold. I have far too deep a faith in our
Northern race to have any fears whatever.
We shall never be dominated by threats; in
fact we shall never be dominated by anyone, and we shall never have cause to diverge
from the principles we hold by any highsounding phrases or by any threats being
translated into action. We have held our
own for many years past. We
look
round on Ulster with pride and satisfaction
when we read history and realise what the
Province was a hundred years ago. Our
forefathers have achieved these results by
their independent spirit, by their enterprise
and by their courage, and it remains for us
to maintain those traditions and to show
that we are worthy to be the sons of such
fathers. I feel that at this moment we have
a great opportunity of showing what we can
do, and what we will show to the South is
the restraint and dignity of a self-respecting
people. We will show them our capacity to
govern; we will demonstrate to them a spirit
of tolerance and forbearance, and at the
same time we will pray for a similar attitude in the South. We will expect active
can
4 APRIL 1922.
Peace Negotiations.

Peace Negotiations.
making it clear what are the desires and
intentions of the Government of Northern
Ireland.
co-operation from the South, and will show
to the world that people differing in temperament, in religion, and in many ideals
can live side by side with each other in peace
and harmony and vie with each other in
good government. That is a situation I
want to see, a situation which I believe can
be achieved and in which we can be the
dominant factor. When time has healed
our wounds and quelled animosities, and
prejudices are lost in oblivion, we can then
discuss a closer union and unity with the
South of Ireland. I hope that union will
comprise not only the Governments of the
North and South of Ireland but also the
United Kingdom and the great Empire of
which we have every reason to be so justly
proud.
Mr. DUFFIN: I would like to take this
opportunity to express in very few words
my deep appreciation and entire concurrence with the course that has been
taken by our Government in coming to this
Agreement with the Provisional Governaent, who now rule, or attempt to rule, the
South of Ireland under the authority of the
Imperial Parliament. I should like also to
say with what appreciation and admiration
I listened to the Noble Marquess in his
eloquent exposition of the terms of the
Agreement, and of the principles and motives
that underly this Agreement. It seems to
me that the dominant note of this Agreement is peace-peace with some section of
the opposing elements that are disturbing
the peace of Ireland, and possibly holding
cut hope of a permanent peace between the
North and the South, and unity
--but it must be a unity which
is compatible with the maintenance
of the independence of the Government
which we have established in the North.
The Agreement asks for the co-operation of
all men of good will in the effort to maintain law and order, and to establish better
relations between the two sections of the
political and religious creeds amongst us.
I am sure that that will meet with a hearty
response from both sides, and we hope it
will go a long way to dispel that atmosphere
of distrust and suspicion and hatred which
has led up to the horrible condition we see
to-day, in which we have murder and outrage repeated day after day. As the Noble
Marquess has said, it remains to be seen
how far the Provisional Government, or
those representatives of it who signed this
Agreement, will be able to carry out its
terms. I am glad to know that the Noble
Lord has no fear of their bona-fides or of
their intention to carry it out. But I think
we must look forward to a period of
difficulty and doubt before we can reap any
substantial benefits from it. At all events,
a long step has been taken towards peace in
Lieutenant-Colonel MAXWELL: I would
like to emphasise what has been said by
my hon. Friend opposite (Mr. Duffin). I
would like to congratulate the Prime
Minister and the Noble Marquess, the
Leader of this House, and the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Agriculture, on
an act which I look upon as one of great
Courage, great patriotism, and great
commonsense. When you look into the details of the Agreement there are any
amount of difficulties. It bristles
with difficulties. There are difficulties which will present themselves to this
Government and to the Government over
which Mr. Collins presides. But I would
say to anyone who is inclined to look too
much at these difficulties: "Were there no
difficulties before this Agreement? Were we
not gradually getting to the verge of civil
war? Is it not worth while making an attempt to evade them?" Civil war may come
--I hope it will not-but if it does come it
will settle nothing. It would simply be the
prelude to another period-possibly a generation-of bitterness and strife and unsettled
conditions. The meeting which took place
between our leaders and the leaders of the
Provisional Government had one great effect.
It has sown the germs of peace and prosperity in this country, and it remains for
the citizens and the followers both of Sir
James Craig and of Mr. Collins to nourish
that and to make it a tree that will bear
fruit. While that is a matter for the future,
there are some immediate benefits which we
shall receive. At any rate, there are two
Governments in this country against crime
--two Governments which have signed an
Agreement against crime instead of one
Government. That, I think, is something.
Then we have two Governments that have
decided that our difficulties shall be settled
by the ballot and not by the bullet. That
is something more. We have also the
Southern Government, or the leaders of it.
acknowledging our law courts, because this
Agreement establishes law courts which they
have acknowledged. We are also to get
£500,000, which will be of great benefit to
the Minister of Labour, and to those numerous people who are out of employment. I
am very glad to see that the Protestant exsoldiers are going to get the first share of
the two-thirds that is to be given to the
Protestants of Belfast. I hope the Government will be able to assure us that out of
the one-third that is going to the Roman
Catholic population the ex-servicemen will
also receive prior consideration. I know that
in the battalion which I had the honour to
55 Peace Negotiations.

THE SENATE.
Peace Negotiations.
[Lt.-Col. Maxwell]
command I had many officers, non-commissioned officers and men who were Roman
Catholics. I think I have behind me every
officer, non-commissioned officer and man
who fought in that battalion when I say
that we had no cheerier, no more gallant,
and no more steadfast comrades than they
were. They fought, and many of them died,
for Ulster and the Empire, and I would like
to say here that they are deserving of the
first consideration in the allocation of any
funds to men of their persuasion.
were too
There are some duties, as the Noble
Marquess has said, that devolve upon us.
It devolves upon everybody to support the
Government which has made this great
and
endeavour towards bringing peace
prosperity to Ireland, and particularly to
this Province. If anybody is inclined to
cavil or to think that we have given something away, they have only to look back on
the life-history of the signatories to that
document on our side. I ventured at one
time to oppose the present Prime Minister
and the Noble Marquess, the Leader of this
House, and Mr. Archdale on a question
which was of great importance to the people
of Northern Ireland, because I thoughtand I still think-that they
solicitous of the interests of the Protestants
in this particular part of Ireland, and that
they wanted to make their position too safe
at the expense of the Protestants of other
parts of Ireland. I only mention this fact
to show that the whole political life of Sir
James Craig has been absolutely bound up
and devoted to the interests of the Protestant democracy in Belfast and throughout the Six Counties. I am only too glad to
say that I can support with my whole heart,
and as far as I can in any other way, the
policy that the Prime Minister is now
pursuing. I mention this to show that Sir
James Craig's whole mind has been concentrated on making Belfast and the district
absolutely safe.
The Noble Marquess has appealed to
everybody to have patience and reticence
in what they say. I feel certain that as far
as Members of this House are concerned
they will endorse that. Might I appeal to
the Press to do all they possibly can to
help. I do not think there is any agency in
the country that has greater power or that
uses it more discreetly than does the Press.
But they have a great responsibility, and I
hope they will not consider me
presumptuous if I ask them to use that
power towards furthering the peace and
prosperity of Ireland. There is one other
class who, I believe, have a great duty to
perform, and who can help in attaining the
object of this Agreement perhaps more
than anyone else, without sacrificing any of
their political or religious principles. I refer to the Roman Catholics in the Six
Counties. I would appeal to them to help
us by co-operating with us in trying to
bring about a better state of affairs. All of
them, I believe, have in their minds a
united Ireland. I would put it to them in
this
I believe
way.
all
we
have-and
certainly I have as our ideal the United
Kingdom. Neither of us have got exactly
what we want, but until the whole
question is settled we do want orderly government in that section of the United
Kingdom in which we live. Might I appeal
to them to help us to make this Province
peaceful and prosperous. They can do it
without in the slightest degree sacrificing
any of their religious beliefs or political
principles. They can acknowledge this Parliament, and those of them who are members of it can come and express their views
either in this or in the other House. I would
say to them that if they have any idea of
future unity in Ireland it is only by gaining
our confidence that they can ever expect to
get that unity. The way to begin gaining
our confidence is to commence at home. I
would ask them to go slow. Every ounce of
suggestion of physical force against Ulster
produces a stone of opposition. If you want
to get to the top of the Cave Hill you will
never do it if you try to run, especially if
you are wearing an overcoat and it happens
to be an ulster. But if you go slowly you
will get there, and when you get to the top
you will find your ulster very useful. I
thank the Noble Marquess for his eloquent
speech.
Mr. WOODS: On the last occasion I addressed the House I rose to compliment the
Noble Marquess, the Leader of the House,
for the part he played in the fight against
the Treaty Bill in the House of Lords. I
rise to-day to congratulate him and his colleagues on the pact they have signed between the Northern Government and the
Provisional Government. I am sure the
Noble Marquess will agree with me when I
say that there are a number of clauses in
that Agreement with which we would not
agree, but there are other clauses which, I
think, render the Agreement perfectly agreeable to the people of Northern Ireland. The
Provisional Government have recognised
that there is a Government in Northern Ireland, and that, to my mind, is a very important concession. They have also recognised that we intend to restore peace and
maintain law and order, and in that connection they have inserted in the Agreement a
clause to the effect that the I.R.A. will end
their activities within the Six Counties. I
think that is one of the most important
4 APRIL 1922.
Peace Negotiations.

Peace Negotiations.
suffered, the working men have very little
consolation. Some misguided individuals
in this city have been so unfair as to say
that the Northern Government is indifferent to the number of deaths provided
they are confined to the poorer classes. I
think that should be contradicted. Every
person in the Northern area must know
that everything that can be done has been
done by this Government and its Ministers.
The Prime Minister has given an assurance
in the other House that no matter what it
costs peace must be restored. The
Government have also increased the
constabulary, and have made application
to the military to send additional
troops, not only to safeguard property, but
to protect the lives of the poor people. I
hope those who have made this statement
will examine the position, and if they do
they will find that there is very little justification for their allegation. I think every
man in the Six Counties is most anxious to
see this Agreement carried out, but at the
same time I am rather doubtful whether the
persons who signed the Agreement on behalf
of the Provisional Government have the
power to carry it out.
clauses in the Agreement, because after the
31st March we can deal with criminals in
the proper way before our own courts. One
clause in the Agreement-I think it is No.
7-which refers to the boundary question,
has been very largely criticised by the man
in the street. It is to the effect that there
shall be a further meeting between the signatories to this Agreement with a view to
ascertaining (a) whether means can be devised to secure the unity of Ireland; (b)
failing this, whether an Agreement can be
arrived at on the boundary question otherwise than by recourse to the Boundary Commission outlined in Article XII. of the
Treaty. It has been freely stated outside
that that means that in order to bring about
the unity of Ireland we must go under a
Dublin Parliament. According to the way I
read the clause we do not intend to go under
a Dublin Parliament, even if the unity suggested in the Treaty is brought about. We
can have unity without being under a Dublin Parliament. We can have a unity of the
Governments of Southern and Northern
Ireland, and I hope that this will be made
quite plain to the man in the street, so that
there will be no more criticism on this
point. I am sure we will be able to come
the boundary questo some agreement on
tion. I congratulate those Members of the
Northern Government responsible for coming to such an Agreement, and I hope they
will be able to carry it out.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I think
the explanation that has been given by the
Noble Marquess will remove some of the unfavourable and unfair criticism that has
been launched against this Agreement.
Speaking as a working man, there is no one
in this Senate who could wish more for
peace than I do, and I am satisfied there is
no one more anxious for peace in Belfast
than the majority of the respectable working men of this city. Nor is there anyone in
the whole Six Counties who have suffered
more during this terrible turmoil than the
working classes and the poorer people. On
no morning when they leave home for their
work do any of them know whether or not
they will ever return to their wives and
families. The sniper has been at work, and
the bomb has been used, and I am sorry to
say that now it is even carried into the
homes, and wives and children are being
for
attacked. It is certainly necessary
something to be done. I do not mind what
criticism is launched at this Agreement, but
whether it turns out as anticipated or not,
I must admit it is a splendid attempt and
an honest attempt to arrive at some kind of
peace, and to
the possibilities of
civil war.
Unfortunately, in the wrongs they have
remove
I am very much afraid that they will be
unable to control the I.R.A. in the Ulster
Counties, but I am satisfied that if the
activities of the I.R.A. ceased peace would
be restored in Belfast in a very short time.
It is only the periodical attacks made on the
Crown forces which seem to bring about reprisals of such a dastardly and brutal character as have occurred. I fail to see where
any of our principles have been sacrificed in
the Agreement, nor has the cause of Ulster
been injured in the least. With regard to
the police force I understand that from the
very first the Special Constabulary has been
open to Roman Catholics, and before this
Agreement was arrived at the Committee on
Police Organisation had made provision for
a certain number of Roman Catholics to be
taken into the new force. The fact that
we are to get £500,000 from the Imperial
Government is something for which we must
be very thankful. I believe there is nothing
more likely to bring about peace than the
removal from our street corners of the unemployed. If work was found for them
peace would be restored more quickly than
otherwise. I think we should compliment
our representatives on being able to bring
about such an Agreement as has been
reached between the Northern Government,
the Southern Government, and the Imperial
Government.
CIVIL
AUTHORITIES (SPECIAL
POWERS) BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Special Powers Bill
-Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I desire to move the Second Reading of the
Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill. You
are
aware that this Bill has passed in
another place, and has now reached this
House for discussion, and I should like to
preface any remarks I make by saying that
naturally the Government is very reluctant
to pass a Bill containing the drastic provisions which this Bill contains. But everyone will realise that the state of the country
at this moment is such that it is impossible
to deal with the disturbances under the
normal process of the ordinary law.
The Bill proposes to give to the civil
authorities of the Government of Northern
Ireland power to take, in respect of matters
within the jurisdiction of that Government,
all such steps as may be necessary for
preserving the peace and maintaining order.
The Civil Authority for the purposes of the
Bill will be in general the Minister of
Home Affairs, but that Minister will have
a power to delegate wide executive powers
to officers of police who are under his
control.
The powers for the exercise of which
it is proposed, in the first instance,
to provide authority, are those which
have been hitherto exercised by the
Competent Military Authority by regulations under the Restoration of Order
in Ireland Act. Accordingly, there are
scheduled to the Bill in adapted form,
such of those Regulations as in the opinion
of the Government are most useful for
dealing with the present state of disturbances.
The Bill will also give power to the
Minister of Home Affairs, either to amend
the scheduled regulations or to revoke or
add to them, and provision is made for regulations so made by the Minister being
laid before both Houses for discussion.
The general provisions as to offences are
based upon R.O.I.R., numbers 47, 48, 49, 50,
48A and 58B. A new provision is introduced enabling a person charged with an
offence, and the wife or husband of a person
so charged, to be a competent witness for
the defence, in order to ensure adequate
trial in cases such as those dealt with by
the Regulations, or the preceding Sub-sections, of Clause 2.
Offences against the Regulations will be
the
prosecuted under the authority of
Attorney-General before Courts of Summary
Jurisdiction, consisting in each case of two
There will be an
resident magistrates.
appeal from these Courts to a County
Court, consisting of the County Court Judge
sitting alone. The Court of Summary Juris
diction will have power to inflict severe
sentences and heavy fines. Special punishment will be authorised by the Bill to be
inflicted both by the ordinary Courts and
by Courts of Summary Jurisdiction in the case
of breaches of the ordinary law and offences
against the regulations, where connected
with the illegal possession or using of fire-
The
or
ammunition explosives.
proposed penalty will be that authorised for
cases of robbery with violence by the Larceny
Act, 1916, that is to say, in addition to any
punishment prescribed by law, the Court
may order a convicted person, if a male, to
be flogged. Under Clause 6 the death penalty can be inflicted upon any person who
is convicted ofarms,
(a) causing an explosion likely to endanger life or property;
(b) attempting to cause such
plosion; or,
or

an

(c) making
substance with intent to endanger life
or property.
The Clause was added in Committee in
the House of Commons upon pressure by
the House. Clause 7 gives to all persons,
including the military, who may be authorised to enforce either the regulations or
the ordinary criminal law, the full common
law powers of effecting arrests as they exist
in cases where felonies have been committed. The Clause was added in Committee
Clause 8 makes
in the House of Commons.
it clear that any police force under the
management and control of the Government
of Northern Ireland holds the same legal
position for the enforcement or execution
of law as is held by the Royal Irish Constabulary. The Clause was added in Committee in the House of Commons.
Clause 9 follows R.O.I.R. 81 as amended
by an Order in Council of 5th April, 1921,
with certain minor modifications, which will
enable it to be put in force by the Minister
of Home Affairs.
Clause 10 makes provision for compensation as the Civil Authority will not be
able to rely upon the compensation provisions which apply where acts are done
by the military for the defence of the realm
or restoration of order. The provisions of
the Clause were carefully discussed in the
House of Commens and amendments were
made in the Clause as introduced, in order
to give effect to the points raised.
The jurisdiction given to the County
Court or an arbitrator is based upon Section
I., and the Second Schedule of the Workmen's Compensation Act, 1906.
Section 5 of the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, forbids the making of a law
taking property without compensation.
61 Special Powers Bill

4 APRIL 1922.
-Second Reading.
Regulations 25, 26, 27, 34 and 35 of
R.O.I.R. were added to the Schedule in the
House of Commons.
One of them refers to the point raised
by the right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Samuel
Cunningham). That is that the Act will
continue in force, in the first instance, for
one year only. I think that agrees with
the view of the right hon. Gentleman. The
Bill gives such great powers that it is
consistent with our ideas of justice that its
duration should be only of a temporary
character, and I sincerely hope that matters
will so improve as time goes on that we
shall not have the necessity of re-enacting
this as an Act for next year. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The first thing that must
occur to all of us is a feeling of deep
sympathy with the Government of Northern
Ireland in being imperatively required so
early in their career and by events over
which they have no control to come to
Parliament with a Bill of this description.
But the regrettable state of the country,
and particularly of Belfast, is such that
we can do nothing but welcome the Bill,
and I think we may fairly welcome it as
an instance of the continuity of policy,
which is one of the things for the lack of
which Ireland has grievously suffered for
many years. It is a
continuous policy
following the lines of the Local Government
Powers Act which we passed last Session.
Under that Act powers were taken to interfere with local bodies who were not doing
their duty, and in this Act power is taken
to interfere very seriously with malefactors. The Bill has been introduced, as
I understand, partly in order to give the
Civil Authorities powers at present possessed
by the Competent Military Authority, and
partly to prevent the imposition of
Martial Law. The Prime Minister stated
that he would be no party to the introduction of Martial Law, and I venture
to hope that we shall not hear any more
about it from misinformed critics outside.
I
would like these people to come
down from the clouds and tell us what
they mean by Martial Law and how they
propose to give it statutory effect. There
is a great deal of misunderstanding about
Martial Law. The whole Bill is a very
satisfactory indication that our Government
intends the Six Counties to be governed
not by the law of might but by the might
of law. The Bill will be attacked, and it
will be attacked possibly by extreme
humanitarians-probably those who do not
live in Belfast or Ulster. It will be attacked
by them as introducing the death penalty,
and as we well know the trend of modern
legislation has been towards the abolition
or restriction of the death penalty. The time
is scarcely within the memory of anyone now
living when men were hanged for such things
as sheep stealing, but I had occasion recently
to refer to the calendar of Lincolnshire
Assizes in 1818, where sentences of death
were inflicted for very light offences, and
it is a fact that the death sentence still
remains on our Statute Books in cases of
piracy with violence, arson of His Majesty's
vessels, and treason, in none of which cases
is it essential that life shall be taken.
Therefore, I think we have a precedent on
the Imperial Statute books for employing
the death penalty when necessary, even
though life has not been taken. However,
there will be a clamour on that point, and
I am afraid we can only leave it where
it is and act on our own opinion. I have
always been taught and I accept that the
laws of the realm may inflict death punishment for grievous and heinous offences, and
if throwing a bomb at a tramcar laden
with workers, and probably with women and
children, is not a grievous and heinous
offence I am quite unable to give
a definition of it. Flogging is also a punishment that is extremely repugnant to
everybody, but during the last few years
there has been a distinct step taken to have
it resurrected. It was only in 1912 that
that particular punishment was imposed for
certain offences under the Criminal Law
Amendment Act. As for the Imperial
Government having done away with it, it
is quite the
But it is the
necessary that when this Bill
approaches the Committee Stage we should
most carefully examine it in order to make
quite sure we are doing nothing which can
be construed as an attempt to use the powers
and punishments for anything but their
proper purpose, namely, the protection of
society and for securing a proper trial and
reasonable sentence for persons arrested. The
Bill itself seems to me to disclose anything
but evidence of a tyrannous disposition. When
you come to the actual flogging sections of
the Bill I think there is an anomaly to
which attention can be called later.
more
reverse.
I should like to ask for an assurance
from the Government with regard to the
section which deals with the power to prohibit the holding of inquests. It may
happen that it may be necessary for inquests
to be prohibited in some area where there
is one of His Majesty's prisons. If a death
should occur in a prison we ought to have
an assurance that there will be an inquest,
but it is not a matter on which it is necessary to move any amendment. The fact
that the Act is to continue for one year only
speaks for itself, and goes a very long way
to disarm any criticism that might possibly
otherwise arise. The regulations, of which
there are 32 or 33, have a singular advantage which those concerned with the
Special Powers Bill
-Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
Defence of the Realm Regulations can envy.
They will be enacted as part of the Statute.
They are as short as they could have been
made. I was particularly glad to see that
it has been found possible to put into a
very few lines the whole of Regulation 40
of the Restoration of Order in Ireland Act.
Therefore it only remains for me wholeheartedly to support the Second Reading of
this Bill, and to hope that the measure
when it becomes law may assist in the
great work the Government has in hand,
and may help to bring into effect the agreement made last week in London, of which
I heartily approve.
Mr. GREER: This Bill in my opinion is
the most important one that has come
before the Senate, and it is our bounden
duty to consider it very carefully before
we pass it. As the Noble Leader of this
House has said, we are all most reluctant
to pass a measure of this kind on account
of the severity of the new punishments
created by it, and when we consider that
the crimes to which it applies may be called
quasi-political crimes, I think that in these
modern days before a Bill of this description
can be justified it must be shown that the
ordinary law has broken down and that
the punishment imposed by the new law is
not too severe. To apply myself to the
first question, "has
the ordinary law
broken down, and is it necessary to pass
a Bill of this description," I think there
can be no doubt whatever that the answer
is in the affirmative. Outrageous crime is
rampant in Belfast and all over the Six
Counties, and, what is almost as bad, it
remains undetected and is therefore unpunished. If things are allowed to go on
much longer in their present state the whole
Northern Area will be reduced to a position
of chaos, and therefore it is our duty to
devise some plan by which this intolerable
state of affairs can be brought to an end.
Are these new punishments, including the
death penalty and flogging, too severe under
the circumstances? The hon. Member who
has just sat down has gone into that
question, and has satisfied us that they
are not too severe. It is a greater crime,
considering the degree of culpability, to
throw a bomb into a tramcar than it is
to kill one person, and that principle has
been running through the whole of Section
7. The punishment of whipping has already
in other cases been found to act as a very
excellent deterrent, and I believe if it is
tried it will have most excellent results.
I have to thank the hon. Member who has
just sat down for covering a good deal of
the ground I had intended to go over.
would like to call attention to the Bill
itself, with a view to criticising it, in order
I
to improve it if possible. I should like to
pay a compliment to those responsible for
the excellent manner in which it has been
drawn considering the short time at their
disposal.
In Clause 2 power is taken for the Civil
Authority, which is the Minister of Home
Affairs, to delegate his power to some officer
of police. That, of course, is a very necessary provision, and I do not quarrel with
it at all, but I think the Civil Authority
will have to be careful also to preserve his
own powers over that district as well, and to
see when he delegates powers to a police
officer that he retains the powers he
possesses because the police officer may be
away on duty, and it will be very desirable
that the Minister of Home Affairs should
be able to act. I also wish to call attention
to Sub-section 4 of Clause 1, but I merely
want to say that I intend to move an
amendment in Committee on
that subsection in order to make the meaning a
little more clear. In Sub-section 3, Clause
2, after the word argument," I think it
might be well to add the words "or of the
Acts of Parliament in Section 5 hereinafter
mentioned." It may not be necessary as
a matter of law to add those words, but
as I suppose these regulations will be published I think it well to have them inserted
in order that the public may know that
it is their duty to inform the Civil Authority when they know some crime is
intended. In Clause 3, Sub-section 4 it
provides that a Court of summary jurisdiction when trying a person charged with
an offence against the regulations—

Mr. SPEAKER: All these points will of
course be gone into in Committee and it is
really only repeating what you will say
when the Bill is in Committee.
Mr. GREER: The reason I mention these
points is that I want to suggest them to
the Government. I do not propose to move
any amendment except to the drafting
clause.
MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: On a
point of order, I think that in accordance
with your ruling all the points which the
hon. Member has raised can be raised and
dealt with in Committee.
Mr. SPEAKER: If the hon. Member has
any general remarks to make on the Bill
I do not wish to stop him, but it is quite
useless going into points that will come up
in Committee.
Mr. GREER: I am quite satisfied.
Mr. McDOWELL: I do not wish to
criticise the Bill at all, but I think the
Government would be well advised under
the circumstances to withdraw the Bill for
Special Powers Bill
Adjournment.
4 APRIL 1922.
a certain period. We have heard a great
deal about the peace agreement, which I
hope will bear fruit, but do you not think
that our action in passing this Bill will
throw a certain amount of discredit on our
professions for peace? I think the Government would be well advised to withdraw
the Bill for the present and let the people
see that so far as we are concerned we have
confidence in the Treaty which has been
signed by our Government. I quite appreciate that the Provisional Government
will have difficulties to meet, because you
must place yourself in the position of a
young Sinn Feiner. He has defeated the
British Government and has driven them
out of Ireland and he has the opinion that
he may be able to drive us out.
Would
it not be another step in the direction of
peace if the Government agreed to withdraw
the Bill for the present and thus give the
people an opportunity of seeing the faith
we have in the Treaty?
LONDONDERRY:
I
to
MARQUESS OF
should like first of all to say how gratifying
it is to the Government to have received
from more speakers than one expressions of
measure which the
approbation of the
Government have found it necessary
bring in. The points that have been raised
by the hon. Member (Mr. Greer) are all
very important and I should like to offer
him an apology if I ventured to take any
exception to them at this stage. Perhaps
my rigid red tape Parliamentary education
and the fact that I have been spoken to
severely on more occasions than one in the
House of Commons has taught me that these
points are better raised in Committee than
in the House. An hon. Member referred
to the question of inquests, and I feel
that the matter is quite sufficiently safeguarded by the Clause as it stands.
hon. Member who last spoke (Mr. McDowell)
made an appeal to the Government to delay
the passing into law of this Bill, and I am
not going to say that I am altogether out
of sympathy with the remarks he made.
It is quite true that a declaration of peace
has prefaced the agreement which we made
but I feel sure that the hon. Member will
recognise, as I know the Provisional Government recognises, that there is a minority
of people in this country who seem to be
The
so callous to all ordinary considerations
that it is necessary that the Government
should be equipped with powers of this kind
The
for the purpose of preventing crime.
very day after the passing of the Agreement
which was hailed with such delight in
Great Britain and in all parts of the world
there was committed in the very midst of
this great city two of the most appalling
of the crimes to which we have been witness
for so long, and I feel that in the interests
of the peace which we all desire to achieve
by the agreement it is necessary that this
measure should be passed into law. It in
no sense affects law-abiding citizens, but
it is a menace to those who are prepared
to do their utmost to prevent peace being
country. Therefore,
established in this
while I would do my best to accede to any
request made by any hon. Member, I venture
to suggest that on consideration the hon.
Member who made this appeal will feel that
this Bill is a
weapon with which the
Government must equip themselves if they
desire to maintain the principles laid down
in the agreement.
On Question, Motion: "That the Bill be
now read a Second time" agreed to.
Committed to a committee of the whole
House to-morrow.
ROYAL ASSENT.
MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: It is
my duty to report to the House as follows:-
I am authorised by the Prime Minister
on his behalf to inform the Senate that his
Excellency the Lord Lieutenant, in the
name and on behalf of his Majesty the
King has been pleased to give his assent
to the following Bills:
Superannuation Bill,
Exchequer Borrowing and Local Loans
Bill,
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn." (Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
five minutes after Five
of the Clock.

they had considered the said Bill and
examined the allegations thereof which
were found to be true, and that the Com-

Ordered: That the Report lie on the
