Civil Authorities (Special
Powers) Bill.
Order for Committee on the Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill having been read,
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move that
this House do now resolve itself into Committee.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.)
Clause I. (Powers for preserving peace and
maintaining order).

(2) For the purposes of this Act the
civil authority shall be the Minister of
Home Affairs for Northern Ireland, but
that Minister may delegate, either unconditionally or subject to such conditions as
he thinks fit, all or any of nis powers under
this Act to any officer of police, and any
such officer of police shall, to the extent
of such delegation, be the civil authority
as respects any part of Northern Ireland
specified in such delegation."
Mr GREER: I should like to call attention
to Sub-section 2, Clause I, where it provides
that the civil authority may delegate all or
any of its powers to any officer of police and
that any such officer of police shall to the
extent of such delegation be the civil
authority as respects any part of Northern
Ireland specified in such delegation. I think
it would be well to make it clear that in delegating part of his powers to the officer of
police, the Minister would not part
with his powers with regard to the
Act. I merely suggest that it should read in
this way 66
Any such officer of police shall to
the extent of such delegation and in addition to the Minister of Home Affairs be the
civil authority as respect any part of
Northern Ireland." I think it would be
very desirable that the Minister should retain
equal powers with the officer of police for
that area. Something might happen to him,
or he might be engaged in some other place,
and could not attend. If it were possible I
think it would be an improvement.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member has made a suggestion in
regard to Section 2 of Clause I. I do not
understand whether he moved it as a specified amendment or not.
Mr. GREER: I do not like to insist on it,
but merely wish to call the attention of the
framers of the Bill to it. I am not putting
it as a direct amendment, but I think it
would be an improvement.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I note what the hon. Gentleman says that he
thinks it would be an improvement. I am
given to understand that the words of the
Clause really fulfil the desire he has in his
mind. Unless he makes a special point to it
I would suggest to leave the Bill as it is.
Mr. GREER: I am quite willing. I merely
wished to call attention to it.

Clause I. Section 4.

(4) All regulations made as aforesaid
shall be laid before both Houses of Parliament as soon as may be after they are
made, and, if an address is presented to the
Lord Lieutenant by either House within
the next fourteen days during the session
of Parliament after any such regulation is
laid before it praying that the regulation
may be annulled, the Lord Lieutenant may
annul that regulation and it shall thenceforth be void, without prejudice to the
validity of anything done thereunder, or
to the power of making a new regulation;
and regulations made as aforesaid shall not
be deemed to be statutory rules within
the meaning of section one of the Rules
Publication Act, 1893."
Motion made and Question proposed-
Page 2, line 15, leave out "during the
Session of Parliament " ("within the next
fourteen days during the Session of Parliament after any such regulation "), and inert
on which such House shall be sitting."-
(Mr. Greer).
Mr. GREER: The other point I want to
make is in regard to Section 4. I have
given notice of this amendment, to leave out
the words in line 15, page 2, during the
Session of Parliament." There is rather an
Special Powers Bill

5 APRIL 1922.
ambiguity, because "during the Session of
Parliament may either refer to the Session
which may last weeks or months and which
is formally opened and closed, or it may refer
to the actual Sittings af the House or of one
House. These words " during the Session of
refer
Parliament" apparently
to both

and so on. That applies to the

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am quite willing to accept this Amendment.

Clause V. (Special Punishment for Certain
Offences).
Court
a
"Where after trial by any
person is convicted of any crime or offence
to which this Section applies, the Court
may, in addition to any other punishment
which may lawfully be imposed, order such
person, if a male, to be once privately
whipped, and the provisions of Sub-section
(6) of Section thirty-seven of the Larceny
Act, 1916, as to sentences of whipping shall
apply accordingly.
"The crimes and offences to which this
Section applies are as follows:-
"(1) Any crime under the Explosive
Substances Act, 1883, as extended by Section
eighteen of the Firearms Act, 1920.
"(2) Any offence against the Firearms Act,
1920, in relation to the having, keeping, or
using of firearms.
"(3) Any offence against the Regulations
in relation to the carrying, having, or keeping of firearms, military arms, ammunition.
or explosive substances.
"(4) Any offence against Section thirty of
the Larceny Act, 1916 (which relates to
demanding with menaces, with intent to
steal).
"Provided that this Section shall not be
deemed to apply to any crime or offence
committed before the passing of this Act."
Motion made and Question proposed: Page
4, lines 24 and 25, leave out paragraph (2) “(2)
Any offence against the Firearms Act. .")
-(Mr. Leslie).
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment which I rise
to move is designed to remove the anomaly to
which I referred yesterday when speaking in
support of the Second Reading of this Bill.
I hope that the views which I shall endeavour
to express will find favour in this House and
with the Government. In the first instance
I must crave indulgence for referring very
freely when discussing Sub-section 2 to Subsection No. 1, which we have just adopted,
and to No. 3, to which in the special circumstances surrounding us to-day I do not
propose to object. The difficulty I feel arises
not from any action of our own Government,
but from the Imperial Firearms Act of 1920,
which in its application to Ireland enables
offences such as that indicated in the Subsection under discussion-which offences
appear to be those dealt with in Section I
Sub-section (1) of the Firearms Act, 1920,
though the wording is not quite the sameto be tried before a Court of Summary
Jurisdiction, which in Ireland consists of one
or more Resident Magistrates, whose decision
shall be final. That is, the Firearms Act
gives in Ireland no right of appeal whatever.
And, further, the Act contains no provision
Special Fowers Bill
-Third Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
to enable the accused or his wife to give
evidence. But the Firearms Act is, of course,
an Imperial Act. It gives no power of
whipping. The whipping which it is suggested
to give power for inflicting is in the case of
an adult male person, fifty lashes with the
cat-o'-nine-tails. The cat-o'-nine-tails is a
very horrible instrument. I venture to doubt
whether very many Members of this House
have even seen one. I must submit that
before we sanction the imposition of such a
punishment it behoves us to consider very
carefully:
First, the offences for which it is proposed
to be inflicted;
Secondly, the necessity for it in the case of
these particular offences;
even probable
Thirdly, the possible and
result on the minds of the public;
Fourthly, the safeguards which ought to be
established.
First, then, as to the offence under the Subsection I desire to omit, that is, the having,
keeping, or using of firearms contrary to the
Act. The Sub-section gces no further; it does
not deal with the serious-I may say the atrocious crimes to which Sub-section (2) relates,
that is unlawfully and maliciously causing by
an explosive substance, and a firearm is an
explosive substance in this sense under the
Firearms Act, an explosion likely to endanger
life, or even property, whether the horrible
purpose is achieved cr not, or doing any act
or conspiring with intent to do so, or having
in possession or under control an explosive
substance with intent to commit or to enable
another to commit such crime. For such
offences under the Bill when it becomes an
Act the Court can order whipping, under Subsection (1) of Section 5. They can apparently
pass sentence of death under Section 6. I do
not object to that under the special circumstances, and for the reasons I stated yesterday. It seems to me impossible to object even
to the severest penalty for such a horrible
crime; but we must always remember that
before conviction for one of these very serious
crimes a man must be tried before a judge of
the High Court. And for the present at all
events a jury is a necessary antecedent to
conviction.

The offences dealt with in Sub-section 2 are
light offences. They are not wrong in themselves. They may be foolish and particularly
dangerous at the present time, but still
such offences may be comparatively venial.
The Senate will not misunderstand the word
comparatively." I am most anxicus to
avoid that, but I would like to see a sense of
proportion. Such offences as are indicated in
this Sub-section can surely be sufficiently
punished by a heavy term of imprisonment of
two years, with hard labour. It cannot be
suggested that a sentence of two years' hard
labour is easy. It is a very serious punishment. As a matter of fact, I think I am correct in stating that a term of two years' imfor
prisonment is rarely given even
slaughter. That punishment can be inflicted
to-day under the Firearms Act by one Resident Magistrate without any additional
power under this Bill.
man-
Passing to the necessity for the use of the
lash in adult cases, I rather draw a distinction
between the use of the lash on an adult and
the whipping of a boy, because in the latter
case only a birch rod is used. Has a case been
made out for flogging in these cases? Can we
say that the ordinary law dealing with the
carrying and using of firearms has broken
down, when we remember that for practical
purposes the Firearms Act has only been in
force in Northern Ireland since 1st March. It
was during the month of February that
attention was called to the Act in the newspapers, and we are now in the first week of
April. Up to 1st March last apparently the
Government had relied for the control of
firearms on the provisions of the Restoration
of Order in Ireland Act. Those Regulations,
so far as I can find out, limit the penalties
under summary jurisdiction to imprisonment
for six months, and they also give the right
of appeal for which I am asking. As to the
effect on the public mind of imposing such
punishment as flogging for these simple
offences-not murder or attempted murder-I
am reminded of the words of the late Lord
Brampton, better known as Sir Henry
Hawkins:-
"A sentence ought never to be so severe
as to create in the minds of reasonable
persons having knowledge of the circumstances a sympathy with the criminal, for
that tends to bring the administration of
the law into discredit."
The same learned Judge, by the way, was a
strong supporter of the Criminal Evidence
Act, 1898, which this Bill applies in the case
of offences against the Regulations, but which
it does not apply to offences under the Firearms Act. The imposing of very heavy
sentences which seem out of proportion may
have a serious effect on your obtaining that
assistance from the general public, which is
su desirable.
With regard to the safeguards which I say
ought to be set up I hold that the accused and
his wife ought to be given a chance of giving
evidence on oath, and that there ought to be
the right of appeal. No man need exercise the
right of appeal if he does not want to. Both
these safeguards will exist in cases under
Sub-section 3, but are absent from Sub-rection
2, to which I object. It must not be forgotten
that a man charged with an offence is not
tecessarily guilty, even if the firearm is found
on his person or in his howe. It may be
possible for a man to establish his innocence
5 APRIL 1922.
Special Powers Bill
-Third Reading.

Resident Magistrates. You would gain
that right, and you would gain, I think,
the inestimable advantage of knowing
that before રી. man is sentenced to
the horrible punishment of flogging, you take
every precaution of securing that he has the
fullest chance of proving his innocence.
You have done all in your power to keep
yourself right, and to preserve the principle
that punishment can only be inflicted for
the protection of Society and must never be
purely vindictive. I say all these things will
be gained. What would be lost? The only
loss that seems likely is the possibility of
loss of time. I submit that the adoption of
my proposal will not weaken the Bill. It
will strengthen it, and I submit it for the
consideration of the Government.
on a second trial before a County Court Judge,
which he could not do when he is fint tried
before a Resident Magistrate. I need scarcely
elaborate that point. What is in my mind
is the case of a boy in the country who has
never heard of the Firearms Act. He has not
got the means of adequately defending himself. The only professional aid he can obtain
is from the local solicitor, who has not any
great criminal experience. Such a person
might be unable to establish his innocence in
the Lower Court; but if he had the advantage
of appeal he might be able to e tablish it at
a later date. lt is a very terrible thing
indeed to inflict a flogging on an innocent
man. If he is sentenced to imprisonment the
term can be reduced, and if you imprison the
wrong man you can give compensation; but
you can do nothing for the man who has been
flogged.
Let me pass now to the very simple counse
which seems to me open to the Government,
by which they can avoid the difficulties I have
tried to indicate, without any weakening of
their Bill or the granting of any general
appeal under the Firearms Act, or any interference with that Statute. The end can be
attained by omitting Sub-section 2, as I am
moving, and adding now, or at the Report
Stage, a new Regulation, which will be
embodied in the Statute, not made under its
provisions, reproducing so much of the
wording of the Firearms Act as it is desired
to incorporate. If you do that, it seems to me
that nothing of any value is lost. You have
got your Regulation; it is not a Regulation
made under the Statute, but part of the
Statute itself. The Regulation can be drafted
in five minutes, passed into the Act, and
receive the Royal Assent. When you have
done that Sub-section 3 of this Clause begins
to operate. You can try offences under
summary jurisdiction, you can impose
flogging, but you will gain first of all the
supervision of the Attorney-General in the
matter in question.
As regards offences against the Regulations,
it is specially provided in Section 3 of the Bill
that everything is to be done under the
direction of the Attorney-General. I venture
to suggest that bringing the Attorney-General
into the matter will satisfy the public generally. This is one of the things you gain. The
second thing you gain is a Court consisting
or two or more Resident Magistrates. Thirdly,
you gain for the accused the right for himself
and his wife to give evidence. He has got
that right for offences under the Regulations.
It is one of the fine points of the Bill. It may
be in a prosecution of this sort a point of
vital importance that the accused and his wife
would have that right. Finally, you would
gain the right of appeal from a Court of
Summary Jurisdiction, consisting of two
Mr. GREER: I must confess I was rather
startled when I looked into this matter and
found that although offences against these
regulations which we are going to make today must be tried under sub-section 3 before
two Resident Magistrates with the right to
the prisoner to appeal, offences against the
Firearms Act, 1920, in relation to having,
keeping or using of firearms may be tried
before one Resident Magistrate, and the defendant has no right of appeal. There may
be some indirect way of appealing after
conviction, but, as I said yesterday, this is
a very severe punishment, which one
Resident Magistrate may give, including
whipping, and the prisoner has no right of
appeal. It appears to me that in view of
the very serious punishment which the
defendant may have to suffer, it is very
important in the light of the general public,
to make sure that the prisoner at any rate
gets every reasonable opportunity of proving
his innocence. He may fail in the first
hearing, but it is a very valuable thing to
him that if any additional evidence turns
up he should have the right of appeal, and
have the means of establishing his innocence
by that appeal. It rather grates upon me to
allow the serious punishment of this kind to
be inflicted by one Resident Magistrate. It
may even defeat the ends of justice, because
one magistrate trying the case would be very
loth to inflict the punishment of whipping.
It would work very much better if some
words could be added to sub-section 2 to
provide that in the offences referred to such
offences should be tried before the tribunal
constituted by section 3, that is, before two
Resident Magistrates. There is another
matter in regard to whipping. It does not
arise on the actual words of the amendment
I moved by my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie), but
perhaps I may refer to it.
The CHAIRMAN: Better keep it till the
actual amendment is moved.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am sure no one will quarrel with the two hon.
Special Powers Bill
--Third Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
Gentlemen for raising points which are naturally very serious ones at this moment. I
listened to the speech of the hon. Senator who
moved this amendment with great attention.
But I am afraid that perhaps he does not fully
realise the very extraordinary circumstances
in which we find ourselves. Nothwithstanding
that, I can assure him that the Government,
acting on the advice of those competent to
give that advice, have satisfied themselves that
the Bill is in accordance with all those principles which he has so eloquently put forward.
In his speech, he was inclined to suggest that
the ordinary law had not broken down. I wish
I could share his views on that point. No
extraordinary legislation of this kind would
have been resorted to by the Government
unless they had been convinced that it was
necessary for the maintenance of law and
order.
a
The effect of the inclusion of paragraph 2 in
Clause 5 will be to make whipping one of the
penalties for the unlawful having, keeping, or
using of firearms, as defined by the 1st Section
of the Firearms Act, 1920. The difficulty that
the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) finds is as
follows:-That the criminal who commits an
offence against the Regulations scheduled in
this Bill by the actual discharge of firearins
will be tried by two or more Resident
Magistrates. Amongst other penalties, he may
be sentenced to be whipped; but against any
sentence imposed by this Court he may always
appeal to the Recorder or County Court Judge.
But a man who is charged for an offence
against the Firearms Act of 1920-that is, the
unlicensed having or keeping of firearms
without actual discharge of the weapon-will
before Resident
be summarily tried
Magistrate, who may by this Bill inflict the
penalty of whipping for the offence. Against
the sentence of such a Court of Summary
Jurisdiction no appeal lies. Mr. Leslie finds
an anomaly here-that the man punished with
a whipping for what he calls a lighter offence
has no appeal; while a man similarly
punished for an offence under the Regulations
has an appeal. It must be remembered that
the Firearms Act of 1920 is not our legislation,
but an Act of the Imperial Parliament, and
we do not propose in the present state of
affairs to interfere with any existing legislation passed to repress crime. It is our
intention to render crime less easy and less
attractive, rather than to erase a single
statute against a commission of such offences
as have led to the present legislation.
Under Regulation 3, Sub-sections (c) and
(d) scheduled to this Act, my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, has
powers, of which I am sure he will take advantage, which will enable the Attorney-
General, under Section 3 (2) of the Act, in
cases where a charge might be brought
under Section 1 of the Firearms Act of 1920,
to decide whether, under all the circumstances of the case, justice would not be
better done by proceeding by indictment for
a breach of the Regulations scheduled to this
Act, in which case there would be an appeal.
The Attorney-General tells me that in every
case where firearms are involved, or offences
alleged in reference thereto, he gives directions as to the mode of trial, summary or
indictment, as he considers proper. I think
that undertaking or that definition of the
powers of the Attorney-General should go a
long way towards allaying the fears of the
hon. Gentleman (Mr. Leslie). I am sure the
House will be willing to leave it within the
discretion of the Attorney-General to decide
in such cases, and will be content to leave
it to him to send such cases as are likely.
owing to their serious nature, to be
punished summarily by a whipping as
breaches of these Regulations, rather than
as a breach of Firearms Act of 1920.
ought to go far to remove the hon. Gentleman's difficulties. The Attorney-General
That
has power to use his own discretion in this
matter, and we can with assurance and with
satisfaction leave it to him to decide.
My hon. Friend must not think that we
as a Government are anxious to
use the
lash.
I am sure he is aware that nothing
but the gravest possible circumstances would
have induced the Government to resort to
a penalty which has fallen into desuetude
for many reasons that I need not go into
now. Only the very serious situation in
which we find ourselves when drastic punishment and some adequate deterrent are
necessary for the re-establishment of law and
order would have induced us to do so. My
hon. Friend suggested that if the punishment were too severe it might excite a
certain amount of sympathy with the
criminal. Ι am sure there can be no
sympathy with these very obvious criminals
who have thrown bombs in this city and
whose bombs have been instrumental in
killing children and destroying life in
tramcars. No sympathy can be found for
them-certainly not in the North of Ireland
or, indeed, anywhere else. The lash is a
punishment that we naturally hate and we
shall be glad individually and in our corporate capacity as a Government if our
courts never find it necessary to inflict such
a punishment. But it has been evident by
the commission of crime and outrage in
this city lately that the ordinary penalties of
the law are insufficient. In fact the ordinary
law has not been capable of dealing with
the crimes that have been committed in our
midst.
The object of the infliction of penalty by
the law is to deter, to make crime unattractive, to meet terrorism with terror, rather
than to punish the individual offender. The
77 Special Powers Bill

5 APRIL 1922.
-Third Reading.
possibility of being whipped for an offence
under Section 1 of the Firearms Act undoubtedly will act as a considerable deterrent
to those persons who habitually carry arms
about this city, and when my hon. Friend
talks of the carrying of arms as a lighter
offence than the using of them, at least, he
will admit that, were there no unlawful
carrying of arms about this city, there
could be no discharge of them, and, therefore, none of those more serious offences
could take place which we, as well as he, so
earnestly deplore.
To abolish the use of the Court of
Summary Jurisdiction in dealing with
offences would rob the law of much of its
efficacy because one of the surest deterrents
is the swiftness with which the penalty may
follow on the crime.
My hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) raised the
point about the inadmissibility of evidence
being given by the accused. I may point out
as I think he is aware, that by the general
Irish Law of Evidence no accused person
may give evidence on his own behalf, except
in cases under the Criminal Law Amendment
He referred
Act and cases of this nature.
The
to the fact that in England this evidence

rendered inadmissible For many reasons
which there is no good in my going into it
was supposed by the Nationalist Party to be
However,
a protection for the prisoner.
that is not a matter I am discussing.
reason why an exception has been made in

of an offence depends so often upon proof of

or upon the existence of lawful authority or

motive is

For these reasons I hope that my hon.

the Government must oppose in the interests
of the efficacy of the deterrent legislation
which is embodied in the Bill now under
I would
consideration of the Committee.
suggest to my hon. Friend that we, the
Government, fully realise the object he has
in view which is to prevent the miscarriage
of justice or the inflicting of punishment on
But I feel sure that
innocent people.
adequate safeguards lie in the power of the
Attorney-General of using his discretion of
allowing the prisoner to be tried in such a
way as to give him the best opportunity of
defending himself. I do not feel that there
is any need for the hon. Member to have any

Mr. LESLIE: In rising to reply to the
speech of the noble Marquess, the Leader of
the House, I hope he will allow me, first of
all, to thank him for his courtesy in dealing
at such length with my observations. I also
hope that I will be permitted to point out
that my remarks as to the ordinary law
having broken down had relation solely to the
ordinary law introduced by the Firearms Act.
The point I wanted to make was that the
Firearms Act had only been running from
1st March last. I think I must point out
that you are really interfering with the
Firearms Act if you add punishment, just as
much as if you interferred by giving the right
to give evidence or the right to appeal. I do
not know whether it is possible to suggest
at this stage that the Government should
consider omitting the words "having, keeping," so as to limit the offences against the
Firearms Act to the using of firearms. You
may find in a man's house things which the
man did not put there. It is not conclusive
evidence against him that he is keeping firearms because they are found in his house. I
would like also to point out that I was careful
to disclaim any approach to sympathy with
the people who cast bombs. I tried to show
that I was dealing with the words as they
stood in the first Section of the Firearms
Act. I have certainly no sympathy whatever
with people who throw bombs. On the contrary, I am generally in support of the Bill
which
proposes very severe punishments
on persons who make any resort to that class
of crime. The noble Marquess and the
Government must necessarily possess a greater
wealth of information as to the actual state
of the country, and as to the means by which
it can be brought back to a better condition.
They must possess greater sources of information, and I do not think it would be agreeable
to this House-it would be disagreeable to
myself to press this matter. I can scarcely
imagine anything I shall dislike more than
voting against them. Therefore, I accept, and
thank the noble Marquess for his direct assurance that the Attorney-General will exercise
every discretion to put cases of the kind I am
dealing with in the position of being tried
under circumstances in which there can be an
appeal. Therefore, I beg leave to withdraw
the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

to move the Amendment to add in Clause 5,

"(5) Arson, whether by common law or
by statute, and any offence punishable on
indictment under the Malicious Damage
Act, 1861 "

These are additional offences which will
I think

is most
hon. Members will see that it

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clauses 6 (Death Penalty), 7 (Powers of

MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
desire, on behalf of the Government, to move
the insertion, after Clause 8, of the following
new Clause:-
"The provisions of Section fourteen of the
Petty Sessions (Ireland) Act, 1851, in relation to the reading of depositions as
evidence upon the trial of a person charged
with an indictable crime or offence, shall
extend so as to authorise the reading of
such depositions as evidence, where it is
proved that a witness is so ill as to be
unable to travel; or, where the attendance
of the witness cannot be procured, owing to
the prevalence of a state of disorder
otherwise."
Depositions
or
of
are written statements
evidence taken at preliminary investigations
of charges.
The Irish Petty Sessions Act enables these
depositions to be used at the trial only when
the witness is dead.
In England the Indictable Offences Act,
Section 17, allows the use of depositions, not
only in cases where the witness is dead, but
also in cases where the witness is so ill as to
be unable to travel.
The new Clause under consideration further
extends Section 17 of the Indictable Offences
Act of England so as to enable the use of
depositions where the witness is absent owing
to force majeure, whether by kidnapping or
otherwise.
The necessity of adding this Clause will not
need demonstration under the present wellknown and deplorable circumstances, where
the difficulty of obtaining evidence is the
chief obstacle in the way of bringing to
justice criminals whose guilt is in many cases
well-known to the authorities.
This Clause, besides being of great value in
preventing the enforced adjournment of trials
owing to the absence of material evidence for
the Crown, will also operate to prevent
kidnapping and similar crimes, as the absence
of a witness, while it would preclude his
cross-examination in the interests of the
accused, would be no bar to the acceptance of
his evidence by the Court. I think hon.
Senators will realise that this is a Clause
which can with advantage be added to the
Bill. I beg to move.
On Question, Amendment agreed to.
Clause IX. (Provisions as to Inquests),
Clause X. (Provisions as to Compensation),
Clause XI. (Duration of the Act), Clause XII.
(Short Title), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
The Schedule (Regulations for Peace and
Order in Northern Ireland.
5. (1) The Minister of Home Affairs
may, by Order, declare this Regulation to

area no person other than a member of the

the Order, practise, take part in, or be
concerned in any exercise, movement,
evolution, or drill of a military nature, or
be concerned in, or assist the promotion or
organisation of any such exercise, movement, evolution, or drill, by persons other
than members of the Police Forces."
Mr. GREER: The part of this Schedule
to which I wish to draw attention is Section
5. I wish to insert after the word "police"
("member of the Police Force") the words
"or military." I think the word "military”
ought to be added to make it clear that they
are included.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: On
behalf of the Government I have great
pleasure in accepting the Amendment.
Mr. DUFFIN: I would suggest the words
"Forces of the Crown," as being more extensive than military.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
note what the hon. Gentleman (Mr. Duffin)
has said, and this is not a matter in which
I should feel competent to argue with him.
I venture to suggest that after the word
"police" it would be more in keeping with
what is required to put in "military."
Mr. DUFFIN: I was thinking there might
be Naval forces not included in the word
"military."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
have not really considered the point the hon.
Member is making, but I do not think there
is any objection. If it can be done it shall
be done.
Mr. DUFFIN: After the words "other than
a member of the police" you could put "or
of any of the Forces of the Crown."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: If
I might appeal to the hon. Gentleman not to
press this Amendment, we will ascertain
exactly what word is necessary. The Amendment might be moved on Report.
Mr. GREER: May I refer to Sub-section 3?
I am quite willing to withdraw it,

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In Committee two hon. Members with great
courtesy withdrew their Amendments for the

Mr. DUFFIN: It quite covers the point.
My attention had not been called to that

Amendment of my hon. Friend opposite (Mr.

Mr. GREER: I considered that point before
I moved the Amendment. I think that the
meaning of that Section is that nothing put
in these Regulations in the way of offences
shall apply to members of His Majesty's
Forces. I thought where we were relieving
them of possible offences it would be well to
insert the words. If the Leader of the House
thinks it unnecessary I do not press for the
words.

The Special Constabulary.
Motion for Adjournment.
7 APRIL 1922.
ROYAL ASSENT.
THE SENATE.

Friday, 7th April, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
MESSAGE FROM THE LORD
LIEUTENANT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
have a message from the Lord Lieutenant to
communicate to the House:-
Answer from His Excellency, the Lord
Lieutenant General and General Governor
of Ireland to the Senate of the Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
"I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the Speech
with which I have opened the present Session
of Parliament. (Signed) FitzAlan of Derwent,
Lord Lieutenant General and General
Governor of Ireland."
CIVIL AUTHORITIES (SPECIAL POWERS)
BILL.
Message from the Commons.-The Commons
have agreed to the Amendments of the Senate
to the Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill
without Amendment.
THE SPECIAL CONSTABULARY.
Mr. DUFFIN: I beg to ask the following
Question, of which I have given private
notice:
In view of the rumours that are being
circulated, can the Noble Lord, the Leader
of this House, inform the House as to the
position of the Special Constabulary as
affected by the Agreement entered into by
the Government with Mr. Michael Collins.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The answer to the Question which the hon.
Senator has asked is:
Nothing in the Agreement affects the
Government decision to maintain at full
strength the present establishment of the
"A," "B" and "C" Special Constabulary
throughout the Six Counties until such
times as the Boundary Question has been
satisfactorily settled and permanent peace
is assured, which, unfortunately, cannot be
said to exist at this moment.
Business of the House.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
With regard to the business of the House,
we have to make a postponement as the
Commons have not yet dealt with the
Criminal Procedure Bill. In the Orders of
the Day it is noted that the Criminal Procedure Bill will be received from the Commons. That Bill has not yet reached us,
because it has not passed through all its
stages in another place.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY I
am authorised by the Prime Minister on his
behalf to inform the Senate that His
Excellency the Lord Lieutenant in the name
and on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
given his Assent to the following Bill:-

The Civil Authorities (Special Powers)
Bill.
Motion for Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move: "That this House do now
adjourn." I understand there will be no
business to put before hon. Members until
Wednesday next, so I will make my Motion,
That this House do now adjourn until
Wednesday." The question of the time of
the meeting of this House is one which has
already been mentioned on another occasion,
and I am not sure whether 3-15 is a time
which is altogether suitable to everyone. I
would suggest for the consideration of the
House that perhaps 3 o'clock would be a
better time than 3-15. It will be within the
recollection of hon. Senators that the question was raised by Mr. Senator Long, who
put forward as his reason for changing the
time to 3-15 the difficulty in connection with
the train service, which entailed his leaving
Derry at a very early hour. Therefore, the
time was fixed at 3-15, but I understand the
train from Derry does reach Belfast at
2-45 p.m., so I suggest, if it is in accordance
with the convenience of Senators, that the
time, at all events for the present, should
be fixed at 3 o'clock.
Mr. SPEAKER: Might I inform the Noble
Marquess that the House of Commons have
just fixed
the time of their Sittings at
3 o'clock, and there will be some difficulty
about prayers. The Standing Orders of the
House of Commons are to be altered on
Tuesday to give effect to their decision.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: If
that is the case I will withdraw my suggestion.
Mr. SPEAKER: Would you make the
time 2-45?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Under the circumstances I think it will be
for the general convenience of the House if
the time of meeting remains at 3-15.
On Question, Motion, "That this House do
now adjourn," agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at twentyfive minutes after Three of the
Clock until Wednesday next.

Criminal Procedure Bill.

quess of Londonderry).
Bill."-Mar-
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CRIMINAL PROCEDURE BILL.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I do not think I can better preface my remarks upon the Second Reading of the
Criminal Procedure Bill than by refreshing
the memory of this House, and by reading,
with your indulgence, Article 4 of the Agree
ment so lately signed in London by the representatives of this Government and the Provisional Government of the Free State, and
countersigned by the representatives of the
British Government which had issued the invitations to the Conference that took place
in London a fortnight agc. Article 4 of that
Agreement runs as follows:-
A Court to be constituted for the trial
without jury of persons charged with serious crime, the Court to consist of the Lord
Chief Justice and one of the Lord Justices
of Appeal of Northern Ireland. Any person
committed for trial for a serious crime, to
be tried by that court-
(a) If he so requests; or
(b) If the Attorney-General for Northern
Ireland so directs.
Serious crime should be taken to mean
any offence punishable with death.
penal servitude or imprisonment for
a term exceeding six months.
The Government of Northern Ireland
will take steps for passing the legislation necessary to give effect to this
Article.
The whole of this measure is founded, and
necessarily founded, upon that Article, and
a comparison of it with the first Clause of
the Bill before us this afternoon will reveal
how closely we have adhered in drafting to
the wording of the Agreement. That, I am
sure, honourable Members will have looked
for, as we know that they are as anxious as
the Government that we should fulfil our
bond to the letter.
I should like to draw the attention of hon.
Members to Sub-section 3 of Clause 1 of this
Bill, because we have interpreted by that
Section and the Schedule referred to in it,
the obvious intention of the signatories to the
Agreement rather than the exact wording.
89 Criminal Procedure Bill.
Criminal Procedure Bill. 90
12 APRIL 1922.
I
mean we have made this Bill, and
through this Bill the Agreement, refer only
to that class of serious crime, the commission
of which largely led to the summoning of
the Conference and the prevention of which
we sincerely hope will be one of its happy
results.
I am not one of those who find that political
motives are any excuse for the commission of
crime, nor do we intend that this Bill should
indicate that we regard so-called political
crimes as less detestable than crimes of another character. But the Agreement which
we are called upon to carry out by this Bill
refers only to crimes of a so-called political
character, and we have, therefore, confined
the activities of the special Court hereby set
up to dealing with such serious crimes.
I do not feel called upon this afternoon to
defend the Agreement at length, as the House
from all quarters gave so kind a reception to
it when we discussed it here a few days ago.
I should like, however, to tell those critics
outside who first accused the signatories of
having made concessions too great, and now
accuse this Bill as seeking to rob citizens of
their rights under Magna Charta to be tried
by their fellow citizens, that they cannot have
it both ways. In their opinion either we have
been too soft-hearted or we have been too
tyrannical. One charge or other must fall
to the ground, and the fact that both
charges are levelled against the Government
in respect of this Agreement and this measure appears to me to tend to support our
claim to have been judicious, to have surrendered no principle, to have made a reasonable
and necessary concession to the interests of
peace.
My Lord, when men go into conference as
we did, they should go prepared to make
some advance towards a common ground, to
contribute something to the common cause.
Let us examine very shortly what are to be
the effects of this Bill. They are certainly
not to rob any man of his rights under Magna
Charta. No one can maintain that after
reading the speech of my right hon. Friend
the Attorney-General for Northern Ireland.
He certainly does not intend to exercise his
option to proceed before this Special Court
rather than before judge and jury as heretofore, except in the most unlikely and unprecedented contingency of Ulstermen failing in
their duties as jurymen owing to intimidation
or some other unhappy cause. And, therefore this Bill is not tyrannical. It does not
aim at defeating justice by withholding from
any man the right-that most ancient rightof judgment by his fellow citizens. But it
does make a concession in the interests of
peace. The Prime Minister in another place
has stated the origin of this special court to
lie in the representation made to him by the
Roman Catholic minority amongst us. They
have asked that men of their creed should
be granted the opportunity of pleading before
this Special Court rather than before
a jury of their fellow citizens. They have
said that this will give confidence to
their fellow religionists and restore peace.
We, as a Government, have the utmost
confidence
the Ulster jurymen. We
in
know of no case where they have decided
against the weight of evidence and against
the direction of the presiding Judge. We do
not feel that in making this concession to
prejudice for it is prejudice, and only prejudice that we are giving one guilty man a
single further chance of escape or robbing
an innocent man of the certainty of acquittal.
We do most earnestly desire to win the confidence and trust of the minority, for peace
within a State is based not solely upon the
will of the majority, but upon the willing
consent of all the citizens to be ruled under
the same institutions. I am speaking broadly.
There may be a section desiring amendment
or change in this direction or that-but on
the whole the citizens must be content with
their political institutions.
I shall probably be asked what will occur
in the event of a disagreement between the
two judges. I shall be told that we make
no provision for this in our Bill. And
that is true, for we are leaving it to my
right hon. Friend the Attorney-General to
exercise his discretion in such an event. and
to decide upon the merits of each case
whether he shall enter a nolle prosequi or
proceed afresh.
I think I may leave this Bill with confidence to the judgment of the House. In
passing this measure hon. Members will
register the approval of the Agreement which
they have already expressed here. They will
greatly strengthen the hands of the Government by ratifying an arrangement which
has already won for us the good-will of
the Empire and, by shifting the onus of proof
of the will to peace and reasonableness upon
other shoulders than our own, has presented
the people of Northern Ireland in a new, and
may say, a true perspective. I beg to
I
move
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the Bill."-(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The Marquess of Dufferin and Ava in the
Chair.)

2 (Powers, Procedure, and rules of Special

UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE
BILL.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The first thing I ought to say in bringing
forward the Unemployment Insurance Bill
for a Second Reading is that in a great
part it is a measure of the Imperial Parliament, for we are applying by it to the
circumstances and needs of Northern Ireland
the Unemployment Insurance legislation
which was passed through the Imperial
Parliament the other day. I think I might
preface my remarks by informing the House
that there were on March 13th in Northern
Ireland 52,416 persons registered as wholly
unemployed and 16,523 as on short time.
The Cabinet of Northern Ireland concluded,
and I think you will agree with me, rightly
concluded, that the working man in Northern
Ireland who, through no fault of his own,
has lost his employment, should stand in
regard to the benefits receivable from Unemployment Insurance upon exactly the
same footing as his brother workman in
like circumstances across the water.
The Bill provides for two new special
periods, the first from the 5th April, 1922,
to 1st November, 1922, a total of 30 weeks,
during which benefits may be received for
3 periods of 5 weeks alternating with 3
periods of 5 weeks, during which benefits
are suspended; the second new period,
called "the 4th special period," extends
from the 2nd November, 1922 to the 1st
July, 1923, during which benefits are paid
for 12 weeks at similar rates. During this
4th special period the Minister is empowered
to extend the benefits for 2 further periods,
neither of which is to exceed in the aggregate 5 weeks. These provisions will be
found in Clause 4 of the Bill.
My right hon. Friend the Minister of
Labour, in his able and lucid speech upon
this Bill in another place yesterday, stated
that he expected that the cost to the State
during the 65 weeks over which this Bill
extends would be £420,000, and that as a result of the provisions of this Bill he would
have to draw upon the borrowing powers of a
million pounds which have already been
granted to him in respect of Unemployment
Insurance by another Act to the extent of
£420,000. He estimates that the total benefits to be paid will be £1,436,000, and that the
contributions of the three parties-the employee, the employer, and the State-will
amount to £1,177,000. The adverse balance
thus amounts to this £420,000, which he will
borrow for repayment when happier times
restore the Unemployment Fund to a state of
solvency.
There are two matters to which I should
like to draw the attention of the House before I sit down. The first is that this Bill is
not a measure of charitable legislation.
There is no 66
dole" here. The worker who
will draw benefits will have contributed, as
the Schedule shows, not only directly in
personal weekly payments, but indirectly in
the weekly contribution of the State. This
is no more of a dole than the payment of
a life insurance is a dole. The worker, each
one of those composing those great masses of
men who live by the labour of their handsthere are
no fewer than a quarter of a
million of them in Ulster-must meet with
unemployment during his career as surely
as he, and all other men, are faced by death
at the end of it, and if it is sound economy to
make provision against the latter-from
which, in this world at any rate, there can
be no recovery-it is surely equally
sound
economy to make provision
against unemployment, which mercifully
is but a temporary, and not a
permanent, affliction. Therefore, let us
hear no more of the word "dole" in regard
to Unemployment Insurance. It is unfair,
it is a slander upon a great body of our
fellow-citizens whose misfortunes we deplore
and with whom we are in fullest sympathy.
The second point to which I should like to
call the attention of the House is the provision in Clause 8 of the Bill which refers
to local Employment Committees. My right
hon. Friend was able to announce in another
place yesterday that his Committee of
Labour Exchanges and Unemployment Insurance had unanimously recommended the
setting up of Local Employment Committees
in association with Labour Exchanges as in
Great Britain, and Clause 8 of this Bill
93 Unemployment Insurance Bill. 12 APRIL 1922.

Adjournment.
gives my right hon. Friend's blessing to
the recommendation of his Committee and
foreshadows the setting up of Local Employment Committees in the Northern Area.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill."-
(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.

Chair

and on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the following Bills:-

weeks.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

for purposes connected therewith. I beg to

G
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 23rd May, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: It devolves upon me, in the
absence of the Noble Marquess the Leader
of this House, to announce that it is not our
intention to proceed to-day with the business
upon the Order Paper, but to move the adjournment forth with as a mark of respect to
the memory of the late Councillor W. J.
Twaddell, Member of Parliament for the
West Division of Belfast in this Parliament,
who was brutally assassinated outside his
business premises in Belfast yesterday morning.
It is our desire-and I feel sure I shall carry
the whole House with me in this-to mark
thus at once our sympathy with the sufferer
and his family, and our singular detestation
of the horrible crime which robbed this
Legislature of a distinguished Member and
the city of a trusted citizen.
We propose, therefore, to set down the
Second Readings of the two measures, which
appear on the Order Paper for to-day, for
to-morrow. It is not my intention to detain
this House for more than a few moments in
moving the adjournment, but I feel sure that
I shall be voicing the general feeling of this
House if I give expression to our recognition
of the high character of our late fellow-citizen
and of the ability and energy that he always
displayed in the execution of his public duties,
and our deepest sympathy with his widow and
relations in the loss they have sustained. I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn
till to-morrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
House adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes after Three of
the Clock, until Wednesday,
24th May, at 3-15.
Nurses and Midwives Bill-
Second Reading.
24 MAY 1922.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 24th May, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
JOINT NURSING AND MIDWIVES
COUNCIL BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The purpose of the Bill which I have the
honour to bring before this House to-day for
a Second Reading is to establish a Joint Council for the Registration of Nurses and Midwives for Northern Ireland in place of the
Registration Council for Nurses and the
Board for Midwives which would otherwise
have to be set up for Northern Ireland, with
the consequent duplication of the Secretary,
Registrar and Clerical Assistants and other
incidental expenses which would be too heavy
for either body to carry individually. The
effect of the adaptation of enactments to the
new circumstances established by the
Government of Ireland Act of 1920, is to
establish for Northern Ireland a Council for
Nurses and a Board for Midwives separate
from those in Dublin, which were originally
established for all-Ireland by the Midwives
(Ireland) Act of 1918, and the Nurses' Regittration (Ireland) Act of 1919.
Hon. Members are no doubt aware that the
bodies in Dublin have remained as originally
constituted, and have passed several Resolutions against the establishment of separate
Registration Authorities in Northern Ireland. However, the setting up of such
separate Authorities is the necessary legal
consequence of the Act of 1920, and the only
method by which all-Ireland Authorities
could be re-established would be by identical
legislation by the legislatures in both
Northern and Southern Ireland, which at
the moment is quite impossible. Whatever
the future may hold, it is necessary for us to
proceed with the establishment of these
separate Registration Authorities for
Northern Ireland.
These Authorities are self-supporting, and
receive no grants from the State, and the
amalgamation of the two bodies is necessary
if they are to remain self-supporting, and in
the interests of economy.
Taking the Bill in detail, Clause 1 provides
for the establishment of the Joint Council
which is to be a body Corporate with Seal.
Sub-Section 2 of Clause 1 provides for the
constitution of the body which is set out in
the Schedule. According to that Schedule the
Council comprises 10 persons in all, and on
its first constitution 4 of these 10 shall be
appointed by the Minister of Home Affairs,
one of the 4 being representative of County
and County Borough Councils, and 2 being
representatives of Medical organisations in
Northern Ireland after consultation with the
Councils and organisations concerned.
The remaining 6 are to be appointed by the
Minister of Home Affairs, and are to be nurses
with actual experience of nursing. Two of
these are to have special qualifications in
Midwifery, but all these are to be appointed
after consultation with the interests
concerned. The first Council will remain in
office for three years, and after that succeeding Councils will, with two exceptions to be
appointed by the Minister, be composed of
elected members. These elected members
will comprise two registered Medical
Practitioners elected by registered Medical
Practitioners' Organisations in Northern
Ireland, and 6 nurses and midwives elected
by the body of nurses and midwives who are
actually on the Register in Northern Ireland. Sub-clause 3 of Clause 1 makes the
general provision for authentication of the
Seal of the Council. Sub-clause 4 of Clause
1 simply provides that the separate and distinct Registering bodies which are technically in existence in Northern Ireland shall
cease to exist for the purpose of this amalgamation.
Clause 2 safeguards the interests of Nurses
and Midwives who have already been
registered. I beg to move.
Mr. BARBOUR: There is a point in this
Bill which I would venture to suggest to the
promoters might be changed in a way which,
I think, would strengthen it. There is no
doubt that if a Council such as this
were once established, and if in its deliberations it suggested legislation which would
come before us, I feel sure that every member of this House would be very much
influenced by any recommendations that a
body such as this would make. I venture
to say that these recommendations would
appeal more to us and would carry more conviction to us and to the public generally if
the Lay membership were somewhat
strengthened. It seems to me that in a Council of ten a membership of two representing
laymen almost suggests an ineffective
minority. While I would not suggest that
the laymen should be in a majority, I would
suggest that the number might be
strengthened in order that the laymen's
point of view might be adequately ventilated,
and consequently that the recommendations
that a body of this kind should make for
legislation would have the benefit of adequate
lay opinion before such legislation
introduced.
was
103 Motor Vehicle Races Bill-

THE
Second Reading.
SENATE.
Mr. GREER: Might I ask the Noble Viscount a question arising out of Clause 1. 1
should like to know if it is proposed that
members of the Council should be paid, or
are they giving their services voluntarily ?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In reply to the point raised by the hon.
Member, Mr. Barbour, with regard to the
inadequate representation of lay membership, I will consult my right hon. Friend,
the Secretary of State for Home Affairs, and
perhaps, if I may be allowed to bring up this
matter again on the Committee stage, I will
be able to answer him more fully. With regard to the question raised by the hon. Member, Mr. Greer, members of the Council are
not to be paid; it is purely voluntary.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly.
MOTOR VEHICLE RACES BILL.
Order for Second reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The Bill, which in the enforced absence of
the noble Marquess, the Leader of this
House, I venture to bring forward for a
Second Reading generally follows the lines of
the Act of 1903, which was passed by the
Imperial Government for the purpose of
enabling the Gordon-Bennett Race to be held
in Ireland. That Act was known as the
Light Locomotives (Ireland) Act, 1903, and
only remained in force for one year. The
present measure will have a permanent
application, and will make the closing of
roads for the purpose of motor racing, with
proper safeguards for the public interest by
order of any County Council in Northern
Ireland. It has been represented that many
people in Northern Ireland will benefit from
the organisation of races for motor vehicles,
not only the Motor Trade but also the hotel
keepers, farmers, railway and steamship
companies and in proof of this it is urged
that the Isle of Man benefits to the extent of
some £200,000 yearly from motor racing.
Taking the Bill in detail, it will be noted
that Clause 1 sets out the procedure to be
adopted in the cases where it is desired to
hold races in any particular county.
In
order to safeguard the interests of particular
sections of the community of particular areas
provision has been inserted similar to the
Act of 1903, providing that the County Council's Order closing the roads shall contain
such provisions as may be required by the
Ministry of Home Affairs, and shall also be
duly published in the Press for the information of all concerned. This provision enables
the Ministry of Home Affairs, if any objec
tions are raised locally to the proposals, to
consider such objections and to make such
stipulations in the County Council's Order
as will meet as far as possible the objections
raised.
Clause 2 of the Bill merely provides that
the County Councils shall not be at any
expense in the matter, all incidental expenses being defrayed by the club or organisation who make the application. This is a
very short Bill, and I do not think I can add
anything more to what I have said that
would interest the House. I beg to move.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move the motion
which stands in my name, that the Bill be
read a Second time this day three months.
As the Noble Viscount has said, this Bill
has been brought forward by the Government, as I understand, in the interests of the
motor industry, in order to make it possible
to have motor races in the Agnalee district,
part of the County Antrim, in the month of
June next. Now I personally highly approve
of anything that we can do that will foster
any, industry in our Six Counties, and I admit that the motor industry is a large and
very important one, and is deserving of the
very best treatment at our hands. And when
1 glanced through this Bill at first I thought
I would be able to support it, and support it
as strongly as possible, but on a little tuller
consideration I came to the conclusion that
there are certain things provided for in this
Bill that it would be very unwise for us to
pass. Now, what is wanted is to have motor
races in the month of June. Of course, as
you all know, the County Council, or the
promoters of these races have to go to Parliament to get an Order interfering with the
county roads in this way.
The functions of the County Council are to
make and to maintain the public roads, and
they have no power to close them for a
limited period such as is given by this Bill.
When, therefore, public roads have to be
interfered with by any project, the promoters
have to come to Parliament. As you all
know, it is done in many cases, and quite
properly done now. The question that is
raised by this Bill is a large and important
one that is, how far public rights should
be subordinated to private interests. There
are cases where it is right and proper and
very desirable that they should be. For
example, in the construction of railways and
canals or even waterworks, public roads have
to be interfered with, and it is right and
proper that they should. But the promoters
have always to go before Parliament to get
powers to interfere with the roads in that
way, and they only get these powers by
bringing forward an ad hoc Act of Parliament explaining what is contemplated to be
done under the Act, showing what roads
would be interfered with, and showing that
it would be of very greater benefit to the
public to let those roads be interfered with
and let the particular industry be carried
on than to leave matters as they were. And
105 Motor Vehicle Races Bill-
24 MAY 1922.
Second Reading.

so they get these powers, notwithstanding
that roads are interfered with.
Well, the Government was evidently asked
to bring forward a Bill of that kind to allow
roads to be interfered with on the present
occasion. What have they done? They have
brought forward a Bill giving all the County
Councils in the Six Counties power to make
an Order interfering in any way they like
for the purpose of motor racing in the whole
area of the Six Counties. That is, it will
take away
the check that we have upon
whether the liberty should be granted to
them or not. They propose to hand over
this question, which rests with Parliament,
to the County Councils to decide for themselves. There are some checks that are provided for. On the first section it is provided
that hey must insert such provisions as are
requred by the Minister of Home Affairs
for the temporary suspension and regulation
of other traffic, for the safety of the public,
for the restriction of speed in populous
places, and for other purposes incident to the
proper conduct of such races. Subject to
this provision being inserted it would be impossible to prevent any County Council making an Order to have races on roads in any
part of the county they please. I think it
would be safe enough to give County Antrim
these powers. I dare not say anything else
with two Members of the Council sitting behind me. I think I might also say it
would be safe to entrust County Londonderry for practically the same reason.
But there are councils within the Area of
the Northern Parliament to whom I do not
think it would be safe to give that power.
For example, there is a county council at the
present time that has got a Sinn Fein
majority. What is to prevent them, just to
irritate the inhabitants of a certain large
town, to hold motor cycle races? What is
to prevent them making an order to have
motor cycle races on one of the roads leading
into that large town on the fair day or the
market day, and diverting all the cattle and
all the men and the carts and cars and so on
that are going into the market of the townto divert them from their ordinary means of
access? I think that is a power that we
should not hand over to the county councils
without having it properly controlled. This
difficulty might, I think, be met by the introduction of a clause by the Government providing that no such powers be granted without the previous consent of the Minister of
Home Affairs, who would inquire into the
circumstances of the case, and decide whether
the race should be held, whether the part of
the county selected for the holding of the
race was suitable, and whether the date for
the race to be held was a desirable date to
hold it. If any clause of that kind were
brought forward by the Government, I
would have no objection to the Bill, except
for what I am going now to say.
There is another thing. I notice the races
are to be held in the month of June. Now,
turning from the general question of principle, the point which I am going to raise is
one which I consider of very great
importance, and I would ask the earnest consideration of the Senate to what I am going
to say. We know the present conditions of
the country. After the experience of last
week I think we all realise that we are
practically in a state of war, and with our
backs to the wall, and I think the Prime
Minister in his speech of yesterday made it
perfectly clear that we are now going to stand
by ourselves and yield nothing. What we
have we will hold. It seems to me that we
are just going to have a very critical time.
I hope and believe that we will be able to
pull through all right, but it may be possible
for us to invite our friends in England and
Scotland and other parts, and in the Colonies
who, to my personal knowledge, have offered
in large numbers to come over and help us.
A crisis may probably arise when we will
want their assistance, and it strikes me that
it is not a very wise thing to do after the
position has been stated so clearly to us by
the Prime Minister, we are to devote ourselves to the passing of a Bill to facilitate
motor racing on our roads throughout the
entire Province. It seems to me it is very
like a case of Nero fiddling while Rome is
burning. I would much rather you were
bringing forward a Bill to enable the county
councils to get men to guard the roads, the
bridges, the culverts, and to have the county
buildings, so far as the county council was
concerned, kept safe. It seems to me it
would be more in keeping with the times
than to bring in a Bill to facilitate motor
racing. My next point is that I do not think
this is a proper time to have motor racing.
Remember I am in favour of having motor
races in County Antrim when it can be done
reasonably safely.
The district is that of Aghalee, which
borders on Lough Neagh, and I understand
from men of experience whom I have consulted, and I know of my own knowledge,
that it is a very good place, and that they
can have very long, straight runs. It is a
very suitable place for holding races, but a
very unsuitable time. If these races are held
the promoters will be advertising them very
extensively and inviting motorists and the
general public to come from all parts of the
country. Numbers will no doubt come from
England and Scotland, and we will welcome
them here. But they will also come from the
South of Ireland, and how can you keep them
out if they do come? And for my own part
I do not want to see them here. Is it not just
inviting the Military Authorities in Southern
Motor Vehicle Races Bill--
THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
¡Mr. Greer]
Ireland to execute a coup, to come over in
their fastest and best appointed cars filled
with men armed to the teeth who would get
across the border by day or by night? They
would be welcomed to those races. And if
200 or 300 of them came in these cars and
began to give trouble, it would be a very
serious thing, and it would be a very difficult
thing to keep order and to provide for the
safety of the public. This particular place
is not very far distant-only a few miles
distant from the scene of the cowardly and
dastardly outrage that was perpetrated on
Friday night, the burning of Shane's Castle.
What is to prevent men who committed that
outrage from coming and undermining the
bridges and undermining the roads and
simply bringing disaster to the district? I
think it is little short of madness to talk of
holding motor races and attracting crowds of
people and valuable motor cars to the district
within a month of the time the outrage was
committed.
For that reason I do hope that Members
of the Senate will see before passing the Bill
that there will be some assurance given that
these races will not be held in June, or until
the country is in a quieter condition. Then,
and then only, I would be in favour or having
these races. I hope they will have them, and
that they will be a great success. In the
meantime, I feel constrained to move that the
Bill be read a Second time this lay three
months.
Mr. LESLIE: I am distinctly in agreement
with much that has been said by the hon.
Senator on my left (Mr. Greer). I venture to
think that it might be possible to secure
most of the safeguards that he desires without going to the somewhat extreme length
of rejecting the Bill. The Bill has been
asked for by a rather important section of
the community, and it has received the
approval of at least one of our County Councils. But at the same time I do think that
the object of the measure requires something
in the nature of clarifying and strengthening. But I think that these matters could
be attended to if the promoters of the Bill
take the same view. It is left, as it seems to
me, a little doubtful. I would suggest that
it be entrusted to the Minister of Home
Affairs. Sub-section 2 of the first clause requires that the order of the County Councils
is to contain provisions required by the
Ministry. It is not clear whether the
Minister is to be seized of the terms of the
order. I imagine the intention is that he is
to see the order in draft, but I do not think
it quite says so. It appears to me that the
clause might be varied so as to make the
position quite clear as to the terms of
getting the sanction of the Minister of Home
Affairs. Advantage might perhaps be taken
of that to give the Minister of Home Affairs
the direct power of prohibiting the proposed
meeting. I think that by this means you
could get over the dangers which my hon.
Friend (Mr. Greer) has indicated, and which,
indeed do present themselves very forcibly
to the mind at the present time.
There is yet another point as to which I am
not quite satisfied, but I speak without complete knowledge of the terms of the special
Motor Car Act of 1903, and that is the
position of the County Council that is promoting these races, as regards possible claims by
the competitors. I think that you want something to show that if the County Council sees
fit to allow these races to be held on its
roads the County Council and the ratepayers
would be protected against possible claims,
on such ground, for instance, as that the
roads are not fit for the purpose for which
they have been used. The intention, I am
quite sure, is that these races are to be held
at the risk of the competitors. I think you
should run no risk of landing the County
Councils into liability on this matter, which
I think would bear investigation, and could
easily be dealt with at a later stage. I would
suggest that the House should give the Bill
a Second Reading, and that the matters
which have been indicated should receive
attention at a later stage.
Mr. DICKSON: I would like to support the
Senator who has just sat down in asking
that the Government should read this Bill a
Second Time to-day, and that the suggestions
that have been made, and further Amendments, may be considered in Committee. The
question, I think, of leaving the power in
the hands of the County Councils of the
Northern area would be quite safe. The
suggestion of the hon. Senator, Mr. Greer,
who referred at length to the present state
of the country as the cause that this Bill
should be postponed for three months, is one
with which I entirely disagree. He has put
forward very strong arguments, but I think
the Northern Parliament are not the people
to show the white feather and retard indus
try or hold back owing to the menaces of
the gunmen of the South. I think if the
motor races are held these people will be
very chary of coming into the Northern
area with the intention of doing any damage.
With all these fast motors around they would
have very little chance of getting outside.
But the amendments that would be necessary, such as have been suggested, for keeping the power in the hands of the Minister
of Home Affairs could be made in the Committee stage-such amendment, for instance,
that the Home Office would have the power
of veto of preventing any races taking place
on Sundays, or things of that kind, could be
considered in Committee. I think if an
amendment of that kind were made we
could safely pass the Second Reading to-day.
24 MAY 1922.
Motor Vehicle Races Bill-

Second Reading.
County Council under this Bill shall not be
so exercised without the consent of my right
hon. Friend, the Minister for Home Affairs,
for a period of twelve months from the
passing of the Bill. I hope and trust that the
addition of some such amendment will
satisfy my hon. Friend, and that he will
withdraw his motion of postponement.
With reference to what my hon. Friend,
Mr. Leslie, said as regards the precise time
that the Home Office can interfere with
these races, I think that if he will look at
Sub-section 3, Clause 1, he will see that the
Minister must see the Order before it is promulgated. That is the intention of that
Sub-section. As regards the question of
claims by competitors, I am not quite sure
what the hon. Member means. Does he mean
claims by competitors if they lose their life
or break their limbs in a race?
Mr. BARBOUR: I should like to endorse
the Second Reading of the Bill with the proviso that any application for races must have
the approval of the Minister of Home Affairs.
There is one little thing in the Bill that
makes me rather sceptical about supporting
it. Having been a member of a County
Council and a Rural Council for some years
I have been greatly struck with the tendency
there has been in legislation to pass laws
which would add considerably to the burden
of people who are never appointed to carry
out the duties which this new legislation is
constantly heaping on their shoulders, and
the great argument against this Bill is that
the safety of the whole Six Counties is impunged, and according to this Bill the safeguarding of that area is put on the shoulders
of the County Councils. I believe that in
County Antrim the public safety would be
adequately safeguarded, but I do not think
that a matter of that kind ought ever to be
determined by a County Council. If the approval of the Minister of Home Affairs has
got to be obtained before the County Council
is called upon to give its decision, I would be
quite satisfied, but I do think that to put the
responsibility of saying yes or no to a question of that kind on the shoulders of the
County Council is not desirable in the public
interest
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I will try and answer the various points that
hon. Members have raised. With reference
to Mr. Greer's remarks, I can
assure him and assure this House that
the Government are fully cognisant of the
reasons which have led the hon. Member to
bring forward the motion for the postponement of the consideration of this Bill, and I
may say at once that we are fully in
sympathy with that motive, and we intend,
if the hon. Member will consent to the withdrawal of this motion, to make such amendment to the Bill in Committee as will give
him the assurance in regard to the exercise
of the powers conferred on County Councils
by this Measure during the present unfortunate state of affairs. At the same time,
it will ensure to the motor trade in
Northern Ireland the passing of a Measure
which, in the opinion of the trade, will be
highly beneficial to the Province, and will
enable those authorities which deal with the
organisation of motor races to proceed with
the preliminary stages of such organisation
with the assurance of my right hon. Friend,
the Minister for Home Affairs, that their
plans will come to fruition as soon as the
general state of the country will permit.
Without giving the exact wording of the
proposed amendment, I would inform the
House generally that we are considering the
addition of a new Sub-section to Clause 1,
whereby the powers exerciseable by a
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might be permitted to explain. What was in my mind was a
claim by a competitor either if he had been
injured in life or limb or if his machine had
been damaged owing to the state of the road;
that is, through the roads which by his own
choice he had elected to travel not being in
a state to withstand the heavy traffic at the
high rate of speed.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I think that any competitor who enters for
motor races on certain roads takes the risk
of the condition of those roads, and, as I
understand it, no claim by a competitor on
this ground would lie against any County
Council. The County Council may close the
roads, but it does not guarantee their state.
I trust that this House will not accept the
amendment of my hon. Friend, Mr. Greer,
and I would earnestly appeal to him not to
press his amendment, but to withdraw it. I
give him the assurance that between now and
the Committee stage I will try to draft, with
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, and with the assistance of my hon.
Friend, Mr. Greer, some such clause as will
meet the very serious objections he has
raised.
Mr. GREER: I think the objections I
raised have been very fairly met by the
Government, and I have much pleasure in
withdrawing the motion. At the same time,
I should like to see the clause that it is
proposed to insert in Committee. I have no
doubt whatever that we will be able to agree
upon a clause, and I do not want to make
any fractious opposition now.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
"
Motion: That the Bill be read 2a," put
and agreed to.
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move "That this House do now
adjourn." With reference to the Motor
Vehicle Races Bill, I would suggest that the
Committee stage be not taken until Friday,
and possibly until Tuesday next week, in
order to allow time to draft amendments
which will satisfy all parties and will not
have the effect of impeding the progress of
the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask if it is proposed
that the House should sit on Friday?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
It is proposed that the House should sit on
Friday to pass the Third Reading of the
Joint Nursing and Midwives Council Bill.
This Bill is required rather urgently by the
Northern Government, but if hon. Members
Mr. SPEAKER: I think this Bill must go
through next week, and therefore it will be
necessary to pass it through this House on
Friday.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
That answers the question raised by the hon.
Member.
Mr. DICKSON: Can the noble Lord tell us
what the business will be to-morrow?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The Committee Stage of the Joint Nursing
and Midwives Council Bill, and there are
also two questions put down by the hon.
Member on my right (Mr. Long) which have
to be dealt with. There is no other business
at present so far as I know.
Motion, "That this House do now adjourn,"
put and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes after Four of the
