113 Halting of Motor Vehicles.
Tax on Motor Vehicles. 114
25 MAY 1922.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 25th May, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
BELFAST HARBOUR BILL.
Mr. DUFFIN: This Bill now comes before
the Senate from the Joint Committee of both
Houses to whom it was referred under the
Standing Order dealing with procedure of
Private Bills. I do not think it is necessary
to do anything more at present than to propose that the Report be now received.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
HALTING OF MOTOR VEHICLES.
Mr. LONG: I wish it to be distinctly
understood that in this question I mean no
reflection on the Forces of the Crown. On
the contrary, I have the highest admiration
for those who are discharging their onerous
duties in these trying and difficult times in
a noble manner and to the satisfaction of the
entire law-abiding citizens. Many regrettable accidents have happened and many
narrow escapes have occurred, but I am sure
that no one is more sorry for these accidents
than the man who fired the shot. In fact,
I know the keenest distress has been experienced by at least one such. Motorists are
agreed that in a closed car or one with a
close fitting hood, and with perhaps a noisy
engine, it is practically impossible to hear a
verbal challenge. I am acquainted with
three loyal, law-abiding, peaceable men who
have been fatally injured in the way indicated by failure to hear the challenge, and
recently the wife of the Rev. Mr. Houston,
of Limavady, was shot through the lung
and a lady travelling in the motor was
shot through the neck. Both remained
in a critical condition for a considerable time so critical that vehicular
traffic was prohibited through the streets
where they resided. Both are now in
to the great
a
fair way to recovery,
some
joy of the entire community. In all
the circumstances I think it is the plain
duty of the authorities to devise
more excellent way. I beg to ask the
Marquess of Londonderry whether the
Government is
aware that numerous
motorists have within recent times been fired
on by members of the Constabulary Forces
in Northern Ireland for failing to stop their
motor cars when challenged; whether the
reason given for failing to stop in every case
has been that the vocal challenge was unheard; and whether in the circumstances
some more efficient method of stopping motor
vehicles entailing less risk to the persons
travelling therein will be devised and put
into operation, such as the waving of a flag
in the daytime or the use of coloured flash
lamps at night.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In reply to the question which has been
brought forward by my hon. Friend I need
hardly say that I fully sympathise with him
in many of the remarks which he has made.
It is quite true that there have been what I
may call regrettable incidents. Cases of this
kind have unfortunately occurred, and he
has told us of one with regard to which, I
am sure, we all extend our deepest sympathy
to those who have been the victims of a misunderstanding. I should like to say how
glad we are to hear from him that those who
were the unfortunate victims are on the high
road to recovery. I can assure the hon.
Gentleman that arrangements have already
been made to adopt the use of lamps which
it is hoped will be instrumental in preventing any further incidents of this nature. We
have no reports that any such cases have
occurred during the daytime. The signal to
halt is invariably given by the police in a
conspicuous manner, and it is highly improbable, as the hon. Gentleman will agree,
that the summons in daylight to halt can he
misunderstood or can pass unnoticed. The
police have distinct and definite orders to
stand in the middle of the road to hold up
their hands to stop cars, so that I think it
will be agreed that when these signals are
given-and I have every reason to know that
these signals are given-they cannot fail to
be seen by the occupants of the cars who are
stopped in this way.
TAX ON MOTOR VEHICLES.
Mr. LONG: I understand that many
Members of this House are anxions to get
away to another place. and I need not unnecessarily detain the House with lengthened
remarks. I think the question is sufficiently
explicit. The practically unanimous opinion
of motor users is that the present system
operates unjustly. The man who only uses
his car once a week, say. and consequently
with slight damage to the roads, pays as
much as the person who has his car in continuous use. I would very much like if the
Government could see their way to adopt
what I feel would be a very popular change
in the method of taxation. I beg to ask the
Marquess of Londonderry whether the
Government of Northern Ireland are not convinced that the imposition of a tax on
petrol or other motor spirit would be a more
equitable way of taxing motor owners than
the present system of motor taxation, as this
method would bear some relation to the use
of the motors: if so, whether having regard
to the anomalies in the present system of
H
115 Tax on Motor Vehicles.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Long].
taxation the Government will favourably
consider the advisability of altering the tax
in the ensuing financial year.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This question has been engaging the attention of the Government of Northern Ireland
as well as of the Imperial Government for
some time. The latter Government appointed a special Departmental Committee in
1920 to consider the whole question, and that
Committee, after careful consideration, recommended the adoption of a single tax
based on vehicles in substitution of a system
of motor fuel duties.
In arriving at this conclusion the Committee had before them all shades of opinion
on the subject, and their recommendations
were based on the grounds that, apart from
the difficulty of collection of a tax on fuel,
its obvious disadvantages are that users, for
traction purposes, of paraffin, benzol, and
other substitutes would escape taxation, that
fraudulent practice over rebates would be
difficult to detect, and that the Road Fund
would inevitably be deprived of an increasing
proportion of income that should accrue. I
feel sure the hon. Member is aware that the
decision arrived at was only arrived at after
very exhaustive examination of the subject and the examination of witnesses,
also the examination of the returns which
were derived from these taxes. But on the
other hand they pointed out that the principal advantages of a single tax system on
mechanically propelled vehicles are that the
tax would be clear in its incidence, it would
be applicable to all types of vehicles, and it
would be difficult of evasion. After all these
are the principles on which taxation is based.
I feel that this is one of the subjects in which
any other form of taxation would be capable
of methods of evasion being applied almost
ad infinitum. The Committee recognised
that the obvious disadvantage of a tax on
vehicles is that the lower the annual mileage
-this is the point which the hon. Gentleman
made the more the taxation cost per mile.
He mentioned that those who used their cars
very little would not be making such demands
on the roads of the district as those who use
their cars a great deal. I think that he has
forgotten, or perhaps he is not aware, that
to meet this disadvantage a scheme of
quarterly licences has been devised, which
means that the user of a vehicle can take
out a licence for the time that he will probably need to make more use of his car than
he would at other times in the year.
The Government of Northern Ireland are
keeping in close touch with this matter, and
in the event of the Imperial Government
making any alterations in the existing law
in this respect, the Government of Northern
Ireland will take into consideration the question of assimilating the law in so far as
Northern Ireland is concerned.
Professional Gentlemen's Cars. 116
MOTOR CARS OF
CLERGYMEN.
DOCTORS AND
Mr. LONG: Perhaps I might be permitted
to thank the Marquess of Londonderry for
the answers he has given to the two previous
questions. In this question-I really did
not intend it as a separate question, but
thought it was merely a clause of the preceding question-but with regard to it I recognise the difficulty of differentiating and
feel sure that if you made an exception in
any particular case numerous other professions and persons would apply for the like
advantage. The three professions named are
in my opinion insufficiently remunerated for
their valuable services, and it seems to me
fitting and right that some recognition of
their position in our midst should be given
and thus mark our sense of their value and
of their labours in relief of humanity. I
beg to ask the Marquess of Londonderry
whether the Government will favourably
consider the advisability of exempting from
taxation in any form motor vehicles owned
by ministers of religion and used for une
purposes of their calling, and motors used
by medical doctors and veterinary surgeons
in their respective practices.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Gentleman in asking this question
has really given the reply to it himself, that
it would be very difficult in cases where,
on the face of it, it seems to be most justifiable relief, not to include others in addition
to those which he has named in the question.
Careful consideration has been given to a
number of suggestions and applications that
have been made with regard to rebates to
certain classes of user, similar in character
to the rebate now granted to medical practitioners These included not only medical
practitioners and veterinary surgeons, but
clergymen road surveyors, commercial
travellers, and relieving officers. The Committee previously referred to were unable
to recommend any such rebates, and considered the principle involved an undesirable
one. The real ground of these applications
is, that the cars are used for professional
or business purposes, and admittedly they
are so used extensively. When owners of
commercial vehicles were entitled to a rebate from the Petrol Duty, there were
grounds for considering rebates for those
owners of private motor cars which were
largely used for professional and business
purposes, but not altogether. Now that the
principle of rebate has been abandoned by
the Committee in respect of commercial
vehicles, no argument can be advanced for
continuing rebates in respect of particular
classes of private motor cars, nor, under
the scale of duties set out in Part III.,
would the transference of such private motor
cars to the commercial class ordinarily afford
any advantage to their owners.
117 Nursing and Midwives Bill.
Adjournment.
25 MAY 1922.

The recommendations of the Committee
in this respect were accepted in full by the
Imperial Government, and the Government
of Northern Ireland sees no reason for any
differential legislation in this respect.

Order for Third reading read.
Mr. DUFFIN: The House has now received
the Report of the Joint Committee to which
this Bill was referred with the amendments
made therein. The Bill has also passed its
Third reading in the House of Commons. I
do not think I need trouble the House with

were of a purely formal and drafting character. It may be satisfactory to the House to
know that the amendments which were suggested in this House have been adopted by the
Committee, and are now incorporated in the
Bill. I beg to move that the Bill be now
read a Third time.

Clause I. (Authorisation of races with
motor vehicles).
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move the amendment which stands
in my name that a new sub-section be inserted, the words of which are on the Paper.
I had not the privilege of dealing with this
Bill when it first came before the Senate.
That duty was undertaken in a most efficient
manner by my colleague who sits on my left
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard). I
understand that this amendment deals with
a point which was made on the previous
discussion. I think that the amendment is
a satisfactory one and will satisfy those who
were inclined to think that the Bill was not
complete without the insertion of this subsection.
Moved:-
Page 2, Clause 1, line 10.-At end insert
as a new sub-section :-(5) The powers of a
County Council under this section shall not be
exercised except with the consent of the said
Ministry of Home Affairs. (The Marquess
of Londonderry.)
Mr. GREER: I am quite satisfied with the
wording of that amendment. It meets the
difficulty that I had in the original drafting
of the Bill. But I should like to call
attention to line 8 in this same sub-section
It says that-
4.
The CHAIRMAN: We have finished with
that. We cannot go back on any amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
On a point of order, I think the hon. Member
could raise this point on the Third reading,
and if he desires then I think it is possible
to insert it on the Third reading.
Mr. GREER: It is only a very small point
I wanted to raise.
The CHAIRMAN: The hon. Member
cannot raise it on this amendment, but he
could raise it on the question that this
clause stand part of the Bill, that is, he
could discuss it.
Mr. GREER: Could I raise the point I
wanted to make?
The CHAIRMAN: I will just put the
Question that this amendment be agreed to
LOW.
On Question. Motion agreed to.
On Question, That this Clause as amended
stand part of the Bill,
Mr. GREER: The point I want to raise is
a small one, but I think it is really necessary
that it should not be overlooked. I think
the words "while actually" should be inserted before the word "engaged," 8th line
sub-section 4. Sub-section 4 provides that
the restrictions as to speed contained in
various Acts of Parliament relating to
motors are not to apply to any motor vehicle
or driver thereof engaged in such race. It
might be held to be a description of the
persons or motor vehicles. I think it is
rather intended to apply to the time when
the restrictions do not apply to these persons.
I would therefore insert the words "while
actually" before the words "engaged in such
races." I think that when they are on the
ground and not actually engaged in the race
they should be subject to the general law,
that is to say, the Motor Act, 1903, which
provides that they cannot through recklessness or negligence go at a speed dangerous to
the public, having regard to all the circumstances of the case, when driving motor cars
on the public roads. I think that provision
should apply except when they are actually
engaged in the race.
121 Motor Vehicle Races Bill-

26 MAY 1922.
Committee.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I do not exactly know what our position is
with regard to the amendment which the
hon. Member presents.
I should like to
have your ruling.
The CHAIRMAN: The hon. Member could
move this amendment on the Third reading.
We cannot amend it now, but on the Third
reading.
Mr. GREER: Thank you.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I take it from your ruling that the hon.
Member is entitled on the Third reading
At
to move the amendment of this Clause.
the present moment we are engaged in the
discussion as to whether Clause 1., as
amended, stand part of the Bill. I venture
to ask the hon. Member (Mr. Greer) whether
he feels that this amendment is necessary.
I assume we can take the discussion at this
point, and if it is necessary the amendment
can be incorporated on the Third reading.
Naturally I do not feel that I am competent
to argue or discuss legal matters with the
hon. Member. He is an individual who is
well versed in legal matters-better perhaps
than most of the hon. Members whom I
have the honour of addressing. I am not
quite sure what the point is to which he
takes exception in the Bill. I did not gather
from his speech whether his reading of the
Bill is that the fact of a driver being engaged
in the race really places him for the time
being above the law, and that these words
which he has formulated, "while actually
engaged in such race," would only cause
him to be above the law while he is actually
engaged in the race in which he is driving.
I wonder really if there is a possibility
of this danger to which the hon. Member
refers. After all, Acts of Parliament are
for the guidance of those who have to administer the law, and they are expected to
administer the law in accordance with the
provisions which have been passed by Parliament itself, and my reading of this Clause
is that it really would be impossible for
anyone who had to interpret the law through
the instrumentality of this Clause to cause
any driver to escape from the penalties to
which he was justly entitled to be subject.
Certainly it is a matter for the non. Member
to consider. I should have thought, speaking
for myself, that the Bill was complete as it
is, and that these words were unnecessary,
but if the hon. Gentleman feels very strongly
about it there is no objection to these words
being inserted on the Third reading, and
if he does move the amendment on the Third
reading, I shall be quite agreeable to putting
it in, although speaking for myself and I
admit that the hon. Gentleman knows more
about the law-I think these words are not
actually necessary to be inserted in the Bill.
Mr. GREER: The position that I take up
in the matter is that it is open to doubt.
The construction of this Clause is open to
doubt. Where a man is brought into court,
of course, if there is any reasonable doubt
the case is always easier to defend. In my
opinion putting in these words would make
it rather clearer, but if the advisors of the
Government think it is unnecessary, I certainly should not propose the amendment
on the Third reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause II. (Expenses of County Council.)
Mr. LESLIE: As regards Clause 2, there

I thought that the Government were going

have done so. Therefore, if I may, I will

Government are satisfied that the county
council is protected from any action by the
competitors in the race on the score that the
road over which they have raced was not
fit for the purpose to which it was put. As.
a county councillor, one felt a little doubt
on the matter. I have no hesitation in
thinking that the matter has been looked
into, but I would like an assurance before
we part with the Bill.

arising it might be suggested that it was due
to the faulty condition of the roads.
road might be good enough for ordinary

the particular character that were used. I
only want to safeguard the interests of the

In
Mr. LESLIE: May I take the opportunity
to call the attention of the Noble Marquess
to the report which appears in this morning's
newspapers to the effect that the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Home Affairs,
has, in the exercise of the powers recently
conferred upon him made a regulation under
the Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Act,
which requires all the inhabitants of the
Six Counties to remain within doors during
the hours from 11 p.m. till 5 in the morning.
I have not seen the regulation itself.
fact I think it has not yet been laid upon
the Table. At all events no copy is obtainable upstairs. I do not call attention to the
matter in order to oppose the regulation at
all, because as one living in a country district I am bound to recognise that such a
regulation, if it can be enforced and if it
can obtain general support, might be a very
valuable weapon in the hands of the Minister
at the present juncture. I do not therefore
approach the question in any spirit of hostility. I would like to have more details as
to administration difficulties. Of course
conditions in the country districts are
altogether different in character to those in
the town. It may very well be that it would
be practicable to restrict in the country the
infringement of the liberty of the citizen
to a greater extent than can be done in the
towns, because it has been urged that the
extended Curfew hours, as they are called
in Belfast, operate in support of law and
order, and very materially in mitigating the
burden imposed on the physical endurance
of the police, which, in itself, is a very
desirable object. But the same condition
does not quite hold throughout the country,
and one is anxious to be assured that permits
will be granted somewhat more freely in the
country than in the city.
I was also anxious to know where such
permits may be obtained. The suggestion
in the newspaper is that the permit would
have to be applied for and obtained from

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Is the hon. Member asking another question?
Mr. LESLIE: I intended the whole thing
to go together, but if the noble Marquess

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I was sheltering myself under the recognised practice of this House in regard to
questions, which is that notice is givenprivate notice is given of the particular
question it is desired to raise.
Of course,
on the question of the adjournment, it is
possible to raise any question, but the only
thing is that the questioner, if he does not
give notice to the Minister, is liable to receive
the answer that notice must be given and
that the Minister is unable to answer the
question. The hon. Gentleman has given
me short notice of a question about imposing
further restrictions upon motor traffic, and
I shall be very happy to give him the answer
which I have had furnished in the few
moments that have elapsed. The hon. Member has the advantage over me of having
read the newspaper on this question, which
I have to confess I have not been able to see.
I do not know what nature of report has
been published, whether it is an authorised
statement or whether it is the impressions
of the editor. But in reply to the hon.
Member I can inform him that the question
of imposing a further measure of restrictions
upon motor traffic is most certainly engaging
the attention of the Home Office, but no
I
definite decision has yet been reached.
should like to add to that answer that I am
sure that any representations which the hon.
Member likes to make-representations which
he is fully entitled to make-will be received
with gratitude by the Minister of Home
Affairs.
There is one matter to which I should like
to make a brief allusion-the hon. Member
has given me the opportunity of doing soand that is in relation to the measures which
I am
curtail the liberties of the citizens.
sure that everybody will agree with me
that at this moment it is necessary for
us to place a certain amount of inconvenience
on ourselves by the events which are taking
Anyone
place around us in this country.
can see we are living in not altogether a
happy state of affairs. Nevertheless, we
are all optimists and we hope that the clouds
with which we are surrounded will shortly
pass. I venture to make this appeal to
public opinion to realise that these inconveniences are imposed in the interests of
bringing about that result. And while these
restrictions impose inconveniences on lawabiding citizens, if they have the effect of
controlling or apprehending the malefactors
I feel that everybody will realise that the
inconveniences are worth suffering for a
short time. It is really with the intention
of appealing to all those who are capable of
influencing public opinion that I suggest to
them that they should criticise in a friendly
spirit-I am not alluding to the speech the
hon. Member made, because all his criticisms
are in a friendly spirit-but these criticisms,
if they are to be made, should be made in a
friendly spirit, so that the Government
should not have the further difficulty of
having to prove to the people at large that
these restrictions which they are bringing
forward are brought in the interests of apprehending the malefactor and certainly not
of inconveniencing law-abiding people.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I am
very sorry, indeed, to hear that these regulations to which my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
referred have not already been passed by
the Home Office. As far as I can judge-
I have only been a day and a half in Ireland
-I have had people of all descriptionsfarmers, labourers, and business men coming
to say,
Why are not the Government doing
more to tackle this great evil in our midst?"
When I saw these regulations in the paper
to-day I hoped, and still hope, that they
""
Newspapers in the House. 128
Adjournment-
THE SENATE.
[Colonel Perceval-Maxwell]
will mature into actual regulations, because
I am perfectly certain the people in this
country are all behind the Government even
if there is considerable inconvenience to
themselves-in any measures which they take
for the protection of loyal people of all
classes and creeds. I feel sure that the
Government will have the support of the
people behind them in the most drastic
regulations that they may make. There may
be a certain inconvenience, which my hon.
Friend pointed out, and which I have no
doubt can be got over.
As far as I can
gather, people are most anxious that the
Government should press in every way the
vigorous application of the law.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before putting the motion
for the adjournment, perhaps I should point
out that it is an old custom at Westminster
not to bring newspapers into the House,
and I think we should do well to follow
this precedent. In future if hon. Members
desire to quote from a newspaper they should
cut out the quotation and bring it, but not
the newspaper.

29 MAY 1922.
THE SENATE.

Monday, 29th May, 1922.
The Senate met at Four of the Clock,
Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Constabulary Bill, the Referees and Arbitrators
(Procedure) Bill, and the Live Stock Breeding Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of the Senate.
The Constabulary Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be read
a First time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Referees and Arbitrators
(Procedure) Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be read
a First time.
On Question, Motion agreed to
The Live Stock Breeding Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be read

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move that
this House do now adjourn until to-morrow
at the usual time. I should like to take
this opportunity of acquainting hon. Members
with the course it is proposed to pursue in
connection with the business. To-morrow
it is proposed that we should take the Second
reading of the three Bills to which this
House has given a First reading to-day. On
Wednesday, in order that the Constabulary
Bill may be completed on that day, it will
be necessary for this House to sit at 12 o'clock
and pass the Bill through the remaining
stages. The Bill will then proceed to the
House of Commons where, if there are any
amendments, those amendments will either
be agreed to or the House of Commons may
differ from this House. It is hoped that
we shall be able to terminate our proceedings
on Wednesday. It is necessary that the
Constabulary Bill should be finished on that
day, because it is the last day of the month,
and the present Constabulary Forces, as Members are aware, come to an end on that day.
I am not aware whether the hour of 12 o'clock
is suitable to Members of this House, but
I feel sure that they will realise the urgency
of the business which necessitates the meeting
in the morning on Wednesday. I will assume,
therefore, that 12 o'clock is the best hour
to suit the convenience of all the Members.
I beg to move that this House do
adjourn.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
now
House adjourned accordingly at
Five Minutes after Four of the
Clock until Tuesday, 30th May,
at 3-15 p.m.
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday 30th May, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
MILITARY AND POLICE UNIFORMS.
Ministry of Home Affairs-Regulation,
dated 25th May, 1922, under the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act (Northern
Ireland), 1922, prohibiting the unauthorised
use of Military and Police Uniforms.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Constabulary Bill (Northern Ireland.)
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
My duty to-day is to move that the Constabulary Bill (Northern Ireland) be read a
Second time. I should like to say at once how
much I regret that it has not been possible
to give a longer time for the discussion of
this very important Measure. Hon. Members
are aware of the difficulties with which we
have been faced owing to the date at which
the Imperial Government has arranged that
the Royal Irish Constabulary as it now exists,
should be brought to an end. I may say this,
however, that this Bill, which I have the
honour of introducing, is based on recommendations in the report of the Committee
which was presided over by Mr. Lloyd Campbell, and therefore the provisions which are
included in this Bill have been before hon.
Members for some time.
The Bill itself deals with three specific
matters. The first is to establish in Northern
Ireland a Constabulary Force to take the place
of the Royal Irish Constabulary. The second
provision is a small revision of the law elating to the jurisdiction of resident magistrates. Hon. Members are aware that under
certain circumstances resident magistrates at
the present time occupy a somewhat anomalous position, and this is rectified in Clause
VI of the Bill. The third provision which is
included in this Bill is the continuance and
management of the Special Constabulary.
You are doubtless aware that the Government
of Ireland Act made certain provisions for
the Royal Irish Constabulary, as it existed
then, but recent events have necessitated a
modification of what was then proposed.
It was made in that Bill a reserved service,
which it was proposed to transfer after three
years to the Government of Northern Ireland and to the Government of Southern Ireland. It was contemplated to divide the
existing Force between those two Governments. But as I have said, for other reasons
which I need not go into, that enactment has had to be abrogated. The Royal
Irish Constabulary, as we have known it, and
now brought to an end, is resuscitated by this
Act in the Northern Area under the name of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary. The
necessity of this Bill, as I have already said,
is due to the fact that by an Act passed by
the Imperial Parliament the Royal Irish
Constabulary actually comes to an end on the
31st May.
Now, I feel that there is very little
necessity for me to say anything to hon.
Members here about the Royal Irish Constabulary. It is a Force of which we have every
reason to be justly proud. It has now been
established for something like one hundred
years, and owns indeed a splendid record.
The Force itself has been drawn from the
people of Ireland, and that Force has been
able continually to uphold its high traditions
in the face of immense difficulties, and, in
recent years, overwhelmed by an almost insupportable strain. We feel that the new
Force inherits the traditions of the old, and
I have every confidence that it will maintain
the splendid record which the present Royal
Irish Constabulary leaves behind it. The
establishment of the new Force, the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, has been, as I have
already said, the subject matter of a committee, which was presided over by Mr. Lloyd
Campbell, and this Committee has dealt with
all these questions regarding the organisation
of the new Force. I feel that we are deeply
indebted to the Committee for their labours.
I should like, on behalf of the Senate, to congratulate them on what they have done to
assist the Government in this matter. They
have laid down as their recommendations
that this Force should be under civil control,
and we are adopting the recommendations of
the Committee, all of which do not actually
appear in the Bill, but can and will be
carried out by orders which can be made
under the Bill itself. The Committee recommended, as hon. Members are doubtless
aware, that there should exist a single force
not exceeding 3,000 men. This number is
slightly in excess of the force of the Royal
Irish Constabulary which was maintained in
the Northern Area. Previously that force was
2,700 men
The Committee also dealt with
the recruitment of members of the Royal
Irish Constabulary, and also the recruitment
of the Special Constabulary, and we propose
under Clause II, sub-section 2, to provide for
the recognition of the services of the Royal
Irish Constabulary who will be in future incorporated in the new Force, and we have
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading.
30 MAY 1922.
made ourselves responsible for portion of the
payment of the pensions which belong to
that Force. Broadly speaking the plan of
the Bill is to apply the Acts and Regulations
which relate to the Royal Irish Constabulary,
with the necessary modifications to the new
Force which will shortly come into being.
His Majesty has been graciously pleased to
allow this new Force to be called the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, and as it is a Crown
Force, the appointment and removal of members of the force are vested in the Lord Lieutenant, but all other powers are vested in the
Ministry of Home Affairs, so that it will be
recognised that this Government has direct
and immediate control over the Force itself.
I have already stated that the Force itself
consists of 3,000 men of all ranks, and excluding officers and men, specifically set out in the
Schedule as belonging to the Headquarters and
Depot of Reserve forces, the balance, which
amounts to over 2,800 officers and men, can
be distributed amongst the six counties, and
the two county boroughs by the Minister as
he may think fit. If owing to any outbreak
or political demonstration the number in any
county is insufficient, extra police, either
from the Reserve Force, which is also
mentioned in the Bill, or from other counties,
can be sent to it, and half the cost of such
extra police can be recovered from the
county concerned. That deals with the
expenses which any county may be put to for
the purpose of obtaining extra protection.
Formerly the Acts relating to the charges for
extra police were very complicated. We have
replaced them by a simple, comprehensive
clause. It also sweeps away those special
enactments relating to night watchmen and
police in Belfast and in Derry. A moiety of
the cost of the police in these cities in
excess of the free quota assigned by the
Minister will be charged for in the usual way,
and will be equivalent to the special payments previously made.
We have taken power to continue for a
period of three months the existing members
This is
of the Royal Irish Constabulary.
merely to fill up a gap and to obviate the
necessity for having every member of the
new Force sworn in on 1st June, as on that
date they will have ceased to be members of
There are
the Royal Irish Constabulary.
really two points remaining in this connection which I would venture to bring before
the House. In the first place the Constabulary Act of 1919 requires the drafts of
certain orders to be submitted to
representative bodies of the police so as to
enable them to make representations on the
subject. As it will take some time to
establish these bodies in the new Force, and
as orders will have to be made immediately
the Act passes, we have been obliged to suspend for the moment the requirements that
these orders should be submitted to the representative bodies mentioned. The other
point which I must bring before the House
is that we have decided to do away with the
system of wet canteens.
The other sections of the Bill need not delay
us very long. The matter which I am referring to at this moment is the question of
resident magistrates, about which I have
already said a word. This is dealt with in
Clause VI. At present a resident magistrate
can only act in the county for which he is
As
appointed and in any adjoining county.
this is often a source of embarrassment where
a resident magistrate is sent to act as deputy
for another, or is sent to Petty Sessions for
the hearing of a case where it is necessary
that two resident magistrates should adjudicate, it is proposed to give to each resident
magistrate jurisdiction throughout the whole
of Northern Ireland, which means that he
will be able to act not only in his own county
and the counties adjoining, but in any
county where his services may be required.
The principal object of the provisions as to
the Special Constabulary is to enable that
Force to be continued beyond September next.
The Special Constables Act, 1914, as extended
by the
Laws
War Emergency
Continuance Act, 1920, enabled Special Constables to be appointed under the Act up till
September next, and as it might be a very
unfortunate thing that there should be any
difficulty regarding the continuance of the
Special Constabulary after that date, we have
taken power under the Bill to appoint them
for such period and for such area as the
Minister of Home Affairs may direct. I need
hardly say that I trust that eventually the
Royal Ulster Constabulary will be quite
sufficient for policing Northern Ireland, but
in the present state of affairs everyone will
recognise that we should be deserving of the
censure of all in the Northern Area if we
were to take upon ourselves the responsibility
of running any risk whatsoever of losing our
Special Constabulary, who have done such
admirable work and who were never more
needed than they are at present when we are
exposed to the attacks of a persistent and
cowardly enemy. I beg to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I note that
the Noble Marquess wishes us to know that
he is in no way responsible for the short
time placed at our disposal for the discussion
of this Bill. I am very sorry that there has
not been more time given. The Bill has only
been in the hands of Members since 2 o'clock
to-day, so that really we have had no opportunity of considering the contents of the Bill.
I am very pleased at the same time to hear
the Noble Marquess pay a tribute to the
Chairman of the Committee, who acted with
reference to the reorganisation of the Police
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Cunningham]
Force for Northern Ireland. There is no
one deserving of recognition more than
Mr. Lloyd Campbell. He worked as Chairman of the Committee very energetically
and anxiously to do all that he possibly could
to bring forward recommendations that would
be satisfactory to all concerned, and would
really be the means of setting up a splendid
Force in Northern Ireland. I may say that
there were two Members of the Senate un
that Committee, Mr. Hill Dickson and myself.
While there were some very valuable recommendations made, I have not had an oppor
tunity of seeing to what extent they have
been embodied in the Bill, but I learn from
the Noble Marquess that if they are not
embodied in the Bill the recommendations
will be carried out by the Department. The
Committee recommended, and I have no doubt
that there will be very little discussion on
that point, single control for the Six Counties.
It was thought advisable that there should
be one Force. It provides for the men a
better opportunity of promotion, and at the
same time it gives more satisfactory administration and control,
With reference to the Royal Irish Constabulary, I am sure we all agree with what
the Leader of the House has said about that
great Force, but at the same time it is
singular to remark that in Belfast it was not
as popular as it might have been. It is
somewhat remarkable that while the Force
was drawn considerably from the South of
Ireland and from the Roman Catholic religion
those people in particular seem to be more
opposed to it than any other section of the
community. I do hope that the new Force
that is being brought into existence will be
set up strictly for the purpose of carrying
on their duties efficiently and not considering
the religion of the men. At the same time
I do hope that the Ministry of Home Affairs
will not be inclined to defer to and pamper
any particular party by including
greater number of one religion than
they are entitled to. The Committee
recommended--I believe it is very fair-that
of the 3,000 one-third should be Roman
Catholic. That, I believe, bears very favourable comparison with the population of the
Six Counties, and would give them very fair
representation.
a
With reference to the remark of the Noble
Marquess regarding wet canteens this seems
to be a question that every one is threshing
out on the platforms and even at the street
corners. I do not think there should be so
much made of this matter. So far as wet
canteens are concerned I think it is much
better that there should be wet canteens in
the Depot where there are sensible men to
control them. I think if you had wet cauteens properly controlled it would be better
than seeing men going to the corner or
getting it in the back parlour of some house.
I certainly do not agree with the temperance
advocates placing such obstacles in the way of
men-particularly intelligent men-and interfering as to what they should drink and how
they should refresh themselves. I find that
the more obstacles you place in men's way
the more determined they are to have it. 1
have known and can give instances of it. I
can assure the Noble Lord that while I have
not abused it to any extent still I am not a
"teetotaller." I look upon every intelligent
man as being capable of controlling his appetite. I think it would be much better if there
were properly controlled canteens in these
depots for the Forces than to have the men
going out to some places not very respectable
to have this refreshment. I think that is all
I have to say. I do hope that out of this
Ulster Constabulary we will have a Force
that will be able to restore peace in the Six
Counties and maintain it. I know most of us
are depending on this Force actually doing
that, and I trust we will not be disappointedi.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to confess to
feeling extremely sorry at the passing of the
Royal Irish Constabulary. I feel that they
have established a tradition and built up a
history of which every Irishman can be
proud. One cannot help feeling a little
measure of regret to think that a thing which
has been a source of great pride to all of us
is to be brought to an end. I share with
the Leader of the House the hope that the
new Force, the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
may enjoy the full measure of the splenaid
tradition the Royal Irish Constabulary has
built up. I think on the particular question
of centralised control or otherwise I cannot
help feeling that every Member of this House
will agree with the Bill, namely, that we
should have a Police Force with centralised
control. Whatever may be urged on general
principles about other police forces, I think
in a Bill of this kind one has to pay a very
great measure of regard to the tradition
under which the people of the country live.
No doubt we are accustomed to centralised
control in the Royal Irish Constabulary.
think that that system will appeal to us as
the proper one for the new Royal Ulster Constabulary.
I
There is a point where, I think, a slight
amendment, or possibly in the administration of the Act, a new feature might be introduced which would be beneficial to all
concerned. On page 2, section 2, the Minister
is empowered to send additional Constabulary
to any point in the Six Counties where the
local conditions may call for this step. I
would suggest that it would be desirable that
the County Council or Borough Council
should be informed of the fact that it has
been necessary to send these Constabulary
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading.
30 MAY 1922.
there. After all, they are to be the paymasters as to half the cost at a later period.
I think it might call forth an effort on the
part of the offending County Council or
County Borough to try to allay the local
trouble. I know in the County Council of
which I have the honour to be a member
there has been irritation-almost quarrelswhen we have been served with the bill for
the Constabulary, and when the ordinary
members of the County Council have had no
intimation at all that extra constables had
been brought into the district. I think it
would make for the smooth working of the
Act if notification were sent to the local
Council of the fact that extra constables had
been sent into the district. As I say they are
to be made responsible for half the cost of
moving the constables, and I think it would
be only fair if some scheme could be arranged
to notify them of the fact of extra constables
having been drafted into the district.
Mr. LESLIE: Certanly all of us will join
both with the noble Marquess and the other
speakers in regretting the passing of the
Royal Irish Constabulary. I think that
when we look back on the work of that Force
in the past, we shall think not only of the
support it has given to law and order in
the months, and even years, immediately
preceding its disbandment, but we shall cast
our minds back to the troublous times of the
"eighties" when that Force obtained for itself
a
name throughout the civilised world as
being the very pattern of a semi-military
Police Force. We regret very much that the
Force has gone, and we all join in hoping
that the new Force, which we are pleased
that His Majesty has graciously endowed
with the title "Royal Ulster." will do
everything to emulate the glories of its
predecessor.
Passing to the Bill itself, it is, of course,
a matter for regret that we should have so
little time at our disposal for examining it,
but we recognise that it is not the fault of
our own Government that that state of things
occurred. It is one of those matters which
must be made the best of. We congratulate
the Government on the simplification referred to by the noble Marquess, but I think
it will occur to some of us that the Bill
contains a great deal too much legislation by
reference. Legislation by reference is always
troublesome, and one might venture to hope
that the Government will endeavour in future
Bills, wherever it is practicable, to codify the
old law, rather than thus refer to it so very
extensively. The Bill, of course, does not
tell us anything about where the men are
to be stationed in the respective counties. It
would be too much to expect the Bill to provide for the establishment of a barrack here
or a barrack there. The actual apportionment of men to barracks, and the re-opening
of barracks that have been closed, is a subject
that has engaged a good deal of attention
in country districts. I spoke a day or two
ago, on the adjournment, and mentioned that
the barracks at Mosside and Dervock had
both been closed and not reopened, the result
being that on the main road from Ballycastle,
an urban district, to Ballymoney, another
urban district, a distance of sixteen miles,
there is no police barrack at all. I hope
that matters of that kind will receive the
attention of the Ministry dealing with the
administration of the Bill. I feel satisfied
that their powers under the Bill are ample.
As regards the possible claim for the
expenses of extra forces from the local county
authority, some of us, I think, would be disposed to prefer the fixed free quota. We
were formerly able to ascertain what was
the free quota of a particular county. Now
that power rests entirely with the Minister.
Then, again, it arises out of the point made
by my Friend on the left (Mr. Barbour) as
to the possibility of payment having to be
made by the Council of the county in respect
of additional men provided. I am not quite
satisfied that there ought not to be some
provision requiring that before the Minister
decides whether the money is to be raised by
rate on the county at large or on the county
district or districts, the local authority
should have an opportunity of being heard.
I think it ought to be provided that the
local authority should have an opportunity
of being heard. It is quite understood that
the proposal to levy a small additional sum
over the county at large would be much more
popular in the district or electoral division
where the difficulty had arisen than in the
county at large. If the proposal were to
limit the rate to the district, the authority
of that particular area might wish to say
something about it. I imagine that the
figure of 3,000, given as the total of the Force,
is intended to cover the detective force.
The only other point of detail to which I
would like to draw attention is the number
of County Inspectors which the Force is to
have. I find it a little difficult to follow
the schedule as to that. Whether there is
any real ambiguity or whether it is perhaps
that my idea is due to the haste with which
I have read the Bill I am not quite sure,
but I make out that the intention is that
there should be, if necessary, two County
Inspectors at headquarters, possibly
officer of that rank at the Depot, and six
more County Inspectors. The allocation in
theory is one for each county. If the
Minister should so decide, two counties might
be joined together, but there should be six
The
County Inspectors for the counties.
wording is, I say, not too clear.
an
I do not know-that may be due to my
lack of acquaintance with some of the statutes
referred to what exactly is the precedent for
the new form of oath. The wording is
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.

[Mr. Leslie]
entirely unexceptionable, but the oath is to
do duty to our Soverign Lord the King and to
his Government of Northern Ireland.
imagine that there is a precedent for a form
of that description, but I must confess that
I am unaware of it. On the whole, I see
no reason to doubt that this Bill will be
found effective for the government of the new
Force, and that the new Force will very
shortly be one of which we may all be proud.
Mr. GREER: I think the Government
may be congratulated on the expedition they
have displayed in bringing forward this
highly technical and complicated Bill so soon.
They started in a very prudent manner, in
my opinion, by appointing a Committee to
go into the matter. We are all very much
indebted for the valuable assistance which
that Committee has given us, and for the
suggestions which they have made, which I
gather from the Leader of the House have
been largely incorporated in the present Bill.
The Bill is naturally a very technical one,
and I must confess I have not had sufficient
time to go into it as fully as I should have
liked. As my hon. Friend on my right (Mr.
Leslie) has said, a great deal of legislation
in this Bill is effected by incorporating Acts
relating to the R.I.C. I think that is right
and proper, because it is the same kind of
Force. There have been a very large number
of Acts relating to the R.I.C, and these Acts
were passed from time to time as necessity
arose, and I think it is a safe thing to go as
far as possible upon the legislation which has
already taken place and proved successful.
It does make it a little difficult for one to
understand the exact position. As to codifying the law relating to the Constabulary, it
would certainly be a very interesting thing
if it could be done. I do not know whether
the ordinary man in the street would trouble
himself much to read the codified law. He is
more interested in the Acts passed that
concern himself personally, and not alto-
It is no
gether in the Constabulary Force.
doubt a very difficult matter to arrange what
I might call the obsequies of the R.I.C. and
the birth of the R.U.C. in one Act of Parliament; but I think it has been done with very
great care and very successfully. I have
really no criticism to pass on the Bill as it
stands. I approve highly of Clause 6, which
extends the power of R.M.'s to every County
As to the number of
in Northern Ireland.
men, I should have thought at the present
time it would have been better to have a
larger number than 3,000; but the Committee
has gone into the question, and if they are
satisfied I am quite satisfied also. They know
the position far better than I do, and they
have the same interests and the same desire
to preserve the peace. I am quite content. I
do not think I need add anything to what has
been so well said by so many speakers as to
the fine traditions of the Force that is now
coming to an end. I can only hope and trust
that the new Force will carry out these
traditions successfully for many a long year.
The Marquess of LONDONDERRY:-I am
sure that I feel very grateful to hon. Members
for the manner in which they have received
this Bill, and for the very constructive suggestions they have made. I ventured at the
beginning of my remarks to apologise to the
House for the very short notice which they
received with respect to this Bill. It is quite
true that legislation has been hurried
through both Houses on more occasions than
one. I feel sure that hon. Members are aware
of the difficulties with which the Government
is faced at this moment, and that they will
realise that those who are responsible for the
well-being and the dignity of the Senate are
doing their utmost to prevent measures being
rushed through without hon. Members having
the opportunity, to which they are fully
entitled, of examining and also discussing
them. After all, we have not been in
existence for a very considerable period, and
we have had a great deal of work to do, and a
great deal of legislation which it has been
imperative to pass. While I fully recognise
and sympathise with the protests which have
come from hon. Members, still I do ask them
to be patient and realise the immense difficulties with which the Government is
confronted. I do not think that there are
many questions to which I need give an
answer. I feel that hon. Members are fully
aware of the importance of this Measure, and
also of the position surrounded by difficulties
which may have made some of its provisions
not quite as clear and explicit as they ought
to have been. The hon. Member who sits
opposite me (Mr. Barbour) and also Mr.
Senator Leslie, referred to the extra costs
upon what he (Mr. Barbour) described as
the offending County Councils. I am sure
there are no such bodies at this moment. I
do not think he need have any apprehension
as to any undue expenditure falling upon the
County Council without their having full
voice in the matter. After all, if he will envisage the situation himself, he will see that
the drafting of extra police into a county
would not be on the whim of the Minister,
but on the desire of the county itself, fully
realising the emergency with which it was
faced. These Measures are brought into
existence at a time of stress and a time of
difficulty, but we must naturally hope that
when normal times appear again the drafting
of extra police will be a very rare occurrence,
and that such drafting will only be brought
about with the full cognisance of the district
and county concerned. The hon. Gentleman
at the head of the Bench opposite (Mr. Leslie)
referred to the matter of legislation by refer-
141 Referees and Arbitrators Bill-

30 MAY 1922.
Second Reading.
ence. I think Mr. Senator Leslie displayed
the ingenuity and talents of the old Parliamentary hand, because I have recollections
that this is an argument which has often been
used to criticise a Government which legislates in this way. I am quite willing to
plead guilty. I hope that when further legislation is brought forward in this House the
practice of legislation by reference will be
eschewed as much as possible. The hon.
Gentleman went on to ask that it should be
laid down more definitely in regard to the
situation of the barrack accommodation of
the Force. I think that when he really considers the meaning of what he has said he
will realise that it is quite impossible at this
juncture to know how it will be possible in
future, when the Province is in a more
peaceful condition, to allocate the force of
3,000 Constabulary throughout the Six
Counties. That will largely depend on future
events. I do not feel that he need have any
apprehension that the distribution of this
Force will be on anything but equitable
terms, and terms satisfactory to the Province
itself, because each county must have a voice
in the matter. The hon. Member also referred
to the oath. It is one which has been carefully thought out after due consideration,
and it was believed that this oath would be
most satisfactory. As he is aware, the oath
was changed a short time ago for various
reasons, one of which deals with the power
of the police to belong to different societies.
As it was changed in that connection the
present oath has been established. Taking
all these matters into consideration I do not
feel that there is anv necessity to add anything to what I have already said. I hope
the House will give a Second reading to the
Bill.
On Question, motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a committee of the whole
House for Wednesday.
Referees and Arbitrators (Procedure)
Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move the Second reading
of this Bill. As hon. Members will see, it
is a Bill to amend two Acts, as its Title
proclaims. The object of the Bill is two-fold.
(1) Sections 33 and 34 of the Finance
(1909-10) Act, 1909, provide for the hearing
of appeals in regard to Mineral Rights Duty
and Estate Duty by Referees taken from
a panel of persons appointed by a Reference
Committee. Article 9 of the Government of
Ireland (Adaptation of Taxing Acts) Order,
1922, provides that there shall be a separate
Reference Committee and a separate panel
The
of Referees for Northern Ireland.
Reference Committee for Northern Ireland
shall consist of the Lord Chief Justice and
the President of the Surveyors Institution,
member of the Council of the
Institution nominated by the President,
having special knowledge of valuation in
Northern Ireland. The Referees appointed
need only be part-time officers and are paid
by fees.
or some
Difficulties have arisen in regard to one of
the qualifications of the representative of the
Surveyors' Institution, viz., that he should
be a member of the Council of the Surveyors'
Institution, and Section 1 (2) of the Bill
makes provision that the person appointed
need not necessarily be a member of the
Council. As steps have already been taken
by the Lord Chief Justice to appoint a panel
of Referees, the Bill makes provision so as
to give covering authority for these appointments without going to the inconvenience of
re-appointment
(2) Section 1 of the Acquisition of Land
(Assessment of Compensation) Act, 1919, provides for the appointment of official Arbitrators, to be taken from a panel of persons ap-
These
pointed by a Reference Committee.
Arbitrators arbitrate on questions in regard
to compensation for land taken over by
Public Departments and Local Authorities.
As in the case of the arrangements
under the Finance Act, a separate Reference
Committee and a separate panel of Arbitrators, similarly constituted and with the same
qualifications, are set up for Northern Ireland. The Arbitrators under the Acquisition
of Land Act must be whole-time officers, but
in Northern Ireland there is insufficient
work to occupy the time of one official, either
under this Act or combined with the duties
under the Finance Act.
The Bill, therefore, proposes to remove the
qualifications in regard to the whole-time,
and provides for remuneration by way of fees
instead of salaries. Moreover, the distinction
which exists in the original Acts and which
has been maintained in the Adaptation Orders
under the Government of Ireland Act between these two Reference Committees and
their subsidiary Arbitrators or Referees is
now abolished, one Committee and one set
of Referees being made to do duty for both.
I do not think I need say anything more in
explanation of this Bill. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
143 Live Stock Breeding Bill-

THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
Live Stock Breeding Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
desire to move the Second reading of the Live
Stock Breeding Bill. It may be of interest
to note that the introduction of this important and beneficial measure is due to the
fact that we in Northern Ireland have now a
Legislature of our own separate from that at
Westminster; and, strong Unionist though I
am, I must acknowledge that there is a certain advantage which we gain from the passing of the Government of Ireland Act of 1920
in that we can introduce legislation where it
is demanded by the people of this Province
without waiting upon either the conversion of
the rest of the United Kingdom to our views,
or upon the time and convenience of the
Government at Westminster.
I make this remark because so far back as
May, 1906, the Council of Agriculture for Ireland unanimously passed a resolution in the
following terms:-
"That, as experience has shown, so long
as inferior, ill-bred, deformed, diseased, or
closely inbred animals are allowed to propagate their breeds in Ireland, it is difficult
to obtain a general improvement of live
stock.
"We therefore recommend that measures
be taken to register every stallion, bull,
ram, and boar in Ireland, and that no
animal be permitted to serve except those
registered, which shall be up to a certain
standard of merit."
No-
The resolution was again adopted by the
Council at its meeting in November, 1907, and
in both instances the proposal was heartily
supported by Ulster representatives.
thing could be done by the Irish Department
of Agriculture alone, and it was necessary to
wait until the Departments of Agriculture in
England and Scotland gave their assistance,
and in 1918 the first great step in the improvement and preservation of the breeding industry was taken when the Horse Breeding Act
of that year was placed upon the Statute
Book.
This was a beginning, but the Council of
Agriculture for Ireland showed that it did
not mean to rest until the terms of the Horse
Breeding Act had been extended to cattle and
pigs, or similar legislation in regard to these
classes of live stock had been passed, because
in May, 1919, it unanimously passed the following resolution:-
"Now that the war is over, we call upon
the Vice-President to introduce the longlooked-for Measure for the licensing of bulls
and boars in Ireland."
The Council repeated its request in March,
1921, and the Department of Agriculture
agreed that the time was ripe for the introduction of a Bill. Owing to the difficulty of
obtaining the time of the Imperial Parliament for the discussion of a purely Irish Measure of this kind, and the failure of the Department of Agriculture in Dublin to obtain
the co-operation of the English Ministry and
the Scottish Board of Agriculture in the production of a joint comprehensive measure for
the whole of the United Kingdom, the proposed legislation has remained in abeyance
until the establishment of the Government of
Northern Ireland, and the transfer of the
functions of the Irish Department of Agriculture to the Ministry of Agriculture for Northern Ireland early in the present year has
given the opportunity of bringing forward a
Bill which has been long looked for and
ardently desired by every County Committee
and Farmers' and Stockbreeders' Association
in Ulster for the past 15 years.
Objection may be taken to the Measure
as infringing upon individual liberty, but I
would remind the House that the sole sanction of individual liberty is the common
weal, and that there is no lasting profit or
enjoyment in such liberty as results in the
impoverishment and hurt of the community,
and it may be observed that Switzerlandthe most democratic country in Europehas had a law of this kind in operation for
many years with obviously good results, and
without any apparent hardship. I do not
think it is necessary for me to go into the
provisions of this Measure. They are perfectly plain and straightforward. I would
simply draw the attention of hon. Members
to the fact that, in its present form. the
provisions of the Bill only extend to the
breeding of cattle; but, by Clause 13, the
Ministry is empowered by Order to extend
its terms, with such modifications as may
be necessary, to pigs and sheep. I can assure
the House that my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Agriculture, will not hesitate to
use those powers as and when he thinks fit,
and all who know my right hon. Friend will
be sure that he will not use his powers in
any arbitrary way.
In order to avoid the infliction of hardship,
the policy in connection with stallions has
been to raise the standard gradually, and
not to seek perfection at once. In this
manner hardship has been avoided, and it
is now proposed to administer the Live Stock
Breeding Act in a similar spirit of consideration and in close consultation with the
Advisory Committees to the Ministry. The
Bill, if passed, will not come into operation
until the begining of 1924. The object of
this long interval is to make the fullest
preparation for the smooth working of the
Measure by giving ample notice to all concerned in our great industry of stock-raising.
I beg to move.
30 MAY 1922.
Adjournment.

Adjournment.
propose to make to-morrow will not inconvenience Senators. The reason is the passing
through this House in its remaining stages of
the Bill of which I had the honour to move
the Second Reading at the beginning of our
proceedings this afternoon. I said then that
it is necessary to obtain the Royal Assent to
this Bill on 31st May, and I ventured to put
forward the reasons why the immediate
sanction of the Lord Lieutenant was required.
To achieve this it is necessary for us to take
the Committee Stage, the Report Stage, and
the Third Reading to-morrow morning, when
the Bill will then proceed to another place,
where the amendments which will be inserted
in this House will be discussed and either returned to us for further amendment or else
passed. I regret having to ask the House to
meet at 12 o'clock, but, after the explanation
I have given, I hope Senators will realise the
difficult position in which we are placed.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to say how
glad I am to see this Bill introduced. I think
it is a very happy augury for the future success of the Department of Agriculture and
the Six Northern Counties that, as far as
possible, the procedure which worked so well
in the Department of Agriculture for all Ireland has been followed. I understand that the
Agricultural Committees for the six different
Counties have been consulted about the subject matter of this Bill, and heartily approve
of it, and I understand the Agricultural
Societies, or at least the larger of them, which
werk in the Northern Area, have also been
corsulted, and that all of them approve of
this Bill. The position before this Act was
contemplated was rather an anomalous one.
On the one hand we were supporting the improvement of our cattle, and on the other
hand we were silently acquiescing in the maintenance of the old undesirable strain which
we were actually trying to improve. This Bill
will remove that anomaly, and for fear some
Members of this House may think farmers are
sceptical as to the ability of any legislation
to improve their industry, I think that, whatever be said about the Department of
Agriculture, if they press their inquiries
judiciously and tactfully they will get this
admission from our farmers generally all over
Ireland-that since the introduction of the
Department the value of cattle has increased
by at least £1 per head. I am not overestimating the figure, but any one who knows
anything about the value of the industry to
Ireland and to our Northern Area will be able
to form some appreciation of the tremendous
achievement which lies to the credit of the
Department of Agriculture. I feel glad that
our new Department has consulted the people
interested in this question before actually
introducing legislation, I believe the Bill
will have the hearty approval of those chiefly
concerned, and that it will be a great benefit
to all those who are interested in stock breeding.
On question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY; I
beg to move "That this House do now adjourn." In doing so I would venture to repeat, for the benefit of hon. Members what I
said yesterday, and that is that it is proposed
we should meet at 12 o'clock to-morrow. I
can assure hon. Members that it is my constant desire, as it is also my duty, to study
the convenience of hon. Members as to the
times of the sitting of this House, and I
sincerely hope that the exception which we
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask whether it is proposed to take the Committee Stage of the
Live Stock Bill before the adjournment for
the Rocess?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
There is no specific hurry with regard to the
Live Stock Bill. It was proposed to take the
Committee Stage to-morrow, but I am quite
willing to leave that in the hands of hon.
Members.
Col. MAXWELL: As this Bill does not come
into force until 1924, it would give us time to
aiscuss with our Agricultural Societies and
leading stock raisers any amendments that it
might be thought desirable to put in the Bil
if the Committee Stage was not taken until
after the Recess. I do not know of any
amendments so far, but, for the reason I have
mentioned, it might be an advantage to take
the Committee Stage after the Recess.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am quite willing to accept the suggestion made
by the hon. Gentleman.
Mr. BARBOUR: Is it proposed that the
House should sit to-morrow afternoon as well
as to-morrow morning?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
There is no further business after we have
completed the Constabulary Bill, unless the
other House return the amendments to us.
Therefore, I would suggest, with the concurrence of Mr. Speaker, that we adjourn, and if
necessary, meet again at the usual time.
On question, Motion, "That this House do
1ow adjourn," put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Thirty minutes after Four
of the Clock.

THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 31st May, 1922.
The Senate met at Twelve noon, Mr.
Speaker in the Chair.
Londonderry Bridge Commissioners
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. DUFFIN: I beg to move that thie Bill
be now read a Second time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Accommodation for Model School
Pupils.
Mr. WOODS (for Mr. Joseph Cunningham)
asked the Minister of Education whether any
provision had been made for the accommodation of the pupils who were being educated
at the Model Schools prior to the destruction
of the same.
The reason for asking this question is that
an examination was arranged to take place
for the Higher Grade on Saturday, and the
pupils who have made all arrangements for
it and have paid their fees have not, up to
the present time got any information as to
when the examination will take place. Therefore, the parents of the pupils are anxious to
know if any arrangements have been made by
the Government to carry out the examination.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the absence of the Noble Marquess, the Leader
and
of this House
Minister of Education, who has been detained
at a Cabinet meeting, I will answer the
question of the hon. Member.
Through the courtesy of the Belfast Technical Instruction Committee, arrangements
have been made to accommodate for the present the pupils of the boys' and girls'
departments of the Belfast Model Schools at
the Belfast College of Technology. A hall
has been kindly lent by the owners in the
vicinity of the Model Schools for the pupils
of the infants' department.
Constabulary Bill (Northern
Ireland)
Order for the House to go into Committee
on this Bill read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this House do
resolve itself into Committee on this Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair).
Ordered: "That Clauses 1 (Establishment of
Royal Ulster Constabulary) and 2 (Appointment, removal, pay and pensions of Constabulary) stand part of the Bill."
Clause 3 (Free Quota and Additional Constabulary for Counties and Boroughs.)
Moved: "That Clause 3 stand part of the
Bill."
Sir JAMES JOHNSTON: I want to draw
attention to Clause 3 at this juncture. No
provision has been made for the quota of free
police for the County Councils or the County
Boroughs, and owing to the trouble which
has been hitherto experienced in dealing with
this quota I think it would be well that this
should be set out in the schedule. Belfast
and the Northern Counties had to fight very
keenly on this matter in the past, when the
power was in the hands of the Lord Lieutenant. The Lord Lieutenant formerly had
the power to deal with this matter, and now
I see the same power is to be put in the hands
of the Minister of Home Affairs, who will say
what quota is to be given to the different
places. From the time of Lord Aberdeen
until Lord French came, no justice could be
done to the Northern districts, while Clare,
Kerry, and other Southern places could get
more than their fair share.
whether it is too late in the
I do not know
day to ask the
Noble Lord who has charge of this Bill if
he could now schedule the free quota for the
County Borough of Belfast and the different
County Councils. I am sorry there is not
more time to deal with this matter-I suppose
the Bill has to go through to-day-but if an
undertaking is given that this matter will be
considered I think it will satisfy my purpose.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This question has been rather sprung
upon me. The right hon. Gentleman spoke
to me a minute ago on the subject. I
understand his wish is that each County
Borough and County Council should have a
prescribed quota.
Sir JAMES JOHNSTON: Yes.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think it would be somewhat inadvisable to fix the free quota for the
following reason, that there is a question of
financial liability for each county. Under
Clause 4 it is provided that a County Council
or County Borough will only pay for the
149 Constabulary Bill-

31 MAY 1922.
Committee.
additional force by which the number of free
quota is exceeded. May I quote an example.
Suppose the free quota of a County Council
or County Borough were 400, and the force
If an additional
stationed in the county 350,
100 men were sent down the free quota is
exceeded by 50, and the Council would pay
half the cost of that 50, and not half the cost
of the additional force. The taking of these
powers to prescribe a rate and method of
the
of
views
the
enables
calculation
authorities to be
county
considered. I
understand that is what the county authorities desire. They complain that they
were not taken into the confidence of the late
Government. That state of things was no
doubt partly due to the intricate nature of
the laws which the present Bill will supersede.
The Government consider it is better to leave
the matter to be fixed by administrative
action than to rely upon the inflexible rules
of statutes which often cause dissatisfaction.
I venture to think it is better that the question
of free quota should be left to the Minister
of Home Affairs rather than that it should
be definitely fixed as the right hon. Gentleman
desires.
Sir JAMES JOHNSTON: I can scarcely say
that I am satisfied with the reply given by
the Noble Lord, I think the number could be
very well fixed, because the contribution from
each county and County Borough will be well
known. Suppose the quota of County Down
I do not see
is 400, and they have 350 men.
why they should be called upon to pay for the
other 50 until the free quota is made up.
I see the total in the Schedule is 2,718. I am
sorry there is not time, otherwise I would
ask the House to reconsider the whole question. It is not desirable that this should
be left unsettled. I would ask the Noble
Lord to take note, at any rate, of my view
that it should be settled. It would save a
great deal of difficulty and heart-burning
afterwards. We have seen so much of it
during the last twenty years. Lord Aberdeen
began wrongly here. He had the option and
dealt with it against the interests of Belfast.
Ho said the option was his, and not whatever was equitable and right so far as Belfast
was concerned, and he exercised it in that
way. His successor did the same thing
set
through the Under-Secretary, Sir J. B. Dougherty, and it was only when Lord French
came to Ireland that the matter was
right, and I am afraid that if the matter
is left where it rests at present, it will crop
up again and we will have the same old
trouble.
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I ask the Noble
Lord a question in connection with the financial arrangements under this new Bill?
We understand that the police rate hitherto
The CHAIRMAN: Are you dealing with
this Clause 3 ? I think the matter would
arise on Clause 4.
Colonel SINCLAIR: Very good.
Clause 4 (Financial Provisions).

of Finance for Northern Ireland, and shall
be administered in such manner as may be

members of the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
and there shall be paid into that fund the

(a) Any fines imposed on any officer or
constable of the said Constabulary;
(b) Any fines, penalties, proportions of
fines
damages
or penalties and
awarded by any justice to any such

information or otherwise; and
