measures, so far as those fees
applicable to the said fund
are
Moved: "In Clause 4, page 4, line 33, after
"otherwise" insert or directed by a Court
to be paid into the said fund." (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
The
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The amendment, which I move in the
absence of the Noble Marquess, is simply one
which proposes to insert after the word
"otherwise "the words "or directed by the
Court to be paid into the said fund."
reason that this is inserted is that the income
of the reward fund to be established under
sub-Section (5) of Clause 4 will be derived
in part on the analogy of the old Constabulary Force from penalties awarded by the
Courta under Section 66 of the Licensing Act
of 1872, which, for the information of hon
Members, I will read. It is as follows:-
"Any part not exceeding a moiety of any
penalty recovered under this Act may, if
the Court shall so direct, be paid to the
superannuation fund of the police establishment, within whose jurisdiction the
offence in respect of which such penalties
are imposed shall have occurred."
This fund follows on very similar lines to
that of the old Constabulary Force fund. I
ber to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I rise to ask if the
Noble Lord will be kind enough to explain
whether in connection with these financial
arrangements the police rate as struck by the
city vanishes, and whether the cost of the
Constabulary of the city merges into the
general provincial expenses or a grant for
hat purpose?
Committee.

Constabulary Bill -
THE SENATE.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With regard to the question raised
by the hon. and gallant Member behind me
(Colonel Sinclair), I do not like to answer
it definitely at the moment, but I will communcate with the Minister of Home Affairs
and let him have the information later.
Mr. DICKSON: In connection with the
financial clauses of this Bill, I wonder is this
House aware of the rate of pay-which the
Bill does not give to us-laid down for the
new Royal Ulster Constabulary as recommended by the Committee, of which I was a
member, and which we discussed. The Des.
borough rates of pay were agreed to, and I
take it that that has been passed and excepted by the other House. The rate of pay
is the same as that paid to the London
police, namely 70/- per week, with allowances
for lodgings and everything of that kind, and
an ordinary constable in the Northern area
will thus cost us £4 a week. I am convinced
that that is a panicky price to pay for the
duties of the police when the payment falls.
as it eventually will fall, upon the Northern
Government instead of being retained under
grants from the Imperial Parliament. I was
one of those members of the Committee
who recommended that the rate of pay
should be 60/- instead of 70/- for a start, but,
as I say, I think the Prime Minister, owing
to the present state of the country, felt that
it was necessary to give 70/-. I am absolutely
convinced that the Northern area would have
got 95 per cent. of exactly the same men
for 60/- as they are going to get for 70/-. We
may not look upon 10/- a week as a great
thing, but in a Force of 3,000 men, 10/- a
week runs into thousands of pounds. I made
my protest at the Committee, but the House
seems to have felt that the higher Desborough rates were those that should be
accepted.
One would not mind the very excessive
rates of pay if we were going to get better
results than we have in the past. When a
Member of the House of Commons is shot
down in broad daylight, and policemen standing at the corner let these fellows away, and
when another fellow comes down from the
top of a tram with a smoking revolver in
his hand, and with six thousand Special
Constabulary in the Northern area, independent of the military and everything else, and
nobody is ever brought to justice, it is time,
really, that this great eulogy of the police
should stop, unless we get better results. In
Ballynahinch the other night when Old Court
was burned and a man came to the barracks
to ask for assistance they would not open
the door to him. If it were not for our "B"
men, who are practically not paid at all, we
would be at the mercy of these gunmen.
During the great trouble in Ireland the
Force which was of service was the Auxiliaries, Colonels in the Army, men wearing
their V.C.'s, who did it for patriotism
They, of course, were paid, but they did
their duty, and they did it from a patriotic
point of view. The entire evidence we received at that Police Committee was of a
mercenary character; from beginning to eni
it was a question of what pay, what pensions,
and what allowances would be given; it was
not the question of how are we going to get
over this trouble. I made my protest, as I
said, at the Committee, and I am not going
to move any amendment, but I think that
exactly the same men could have been got
at 10/- a week less.
Mr. LESLIE: I wish only to remark that
the speech of my hon. Friend (Mr. Dickson)
seems to be a little unfair to the new Force.
It is a case, if one may say eo, of trying to
penalise the child unborn on account of some
deficiency in a collateral relation. Really
we are trying at the moment to get the best
men we can, and we are not offering them
a very attractive position. Any man who
enters the Royal Ulster Constabulary at the
moment to some extent takes his life in his
hand. He is embarking on something
very-
The CHAIRMAN: I am sorry to interrupt
the hon. Member, but I do not think the
speech he is making has anything to do with
the financial arrangements of this particular
clause.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Clause 5 (Temporary Provisions)
During a period of three months from the
appointed day the Inspector-General, or, by
direction of the Inspector-General, any
commissioner or county inspector of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary may authorise
any fit person, having been an officer or
constable of the Royal Irish Constabulary
and having retired from the latter Force
in pursuance of any Act of the Parliament
of the United Kingdom including provision
in the disbandment thereof, to discharge in
Northern Ireland the duties and exercise
therein the powers which were, prior to his
retirement, appropriate to such officer or
constable, and any such officer or constable
so authorised shall during the said period
be deemed to be an officer or constable of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary, having corresponding rank and functions, notwithstanding that he has not been appointed as
an officer or constable of the last-mentioned
Constabulary, or has not taken the oaths
required by this Act to be taken by an
officer or constable of the last-mentioned
Constabulary.

Moved: "In Clause 5, page 5, line 8, leave
out "provision in and insert "provision
for." (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
Constabulary Bill-

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This, I may inform the House, is
merely a verbal amendment. I beg to move.

stand part of the Bill.

Enactments relating to the Royal Irish Constabulary), 8 (Provisions as to Special Constables), 9 (Definitions), and 10 (Commencement and Short Title) stand part of the Bill.
Ordered: That the First and Second Schedules stand part of the Bill.
Ordered: That the Preamble and Title
stand part of the Bill.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported to the
House without amendment
Ordered: That the House do resume.

with amendment.
SUSPENSION OF STANDING ORDER.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that Standing Order
No. 25 be considered in order to its being dispensed with in order that not only this Bill
but the Bill that is standing in my name
may go through their remaining stages today.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Constabulary Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that the report of the
Constabulary Bill be now received.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be now
read a Third time.
Mr. WOODS: There are one or two matters
to which I wish to draw the attention of the
House in regard to this Bill. The first is
the appointment of the Inspector-General and
other important officers under him. I
am of the opinion that before these
positions are filled they should be
referred to a Select Committee of both
Houses. That is, I believe, the opinion
outside, and such an arrangement would be
well received by the people, because they
think the Houses should have something to
say in these particular appointments. Another
matter I wish to mention is the statement of
the Noble Marquess, the Leader of this House,
on the Second reading of the Bill with regard
to the Government deciding to do away with
wet canteens in the barracks. Now I am of
opinion that the wet canteen in the barrack
is a thing that should be supported, because
it would be better to have a well-controlled
canteen in the barrack than to have the men
going outside to public-houses to get drink.
I am speaking as a temperance man, and,
therefore, I expect I will have the wrath of
the fanatical temperance people down upon
me because I am advocating wet canteens. I
consider it would be better for the men to
have a wet canteen in the barracks. They
would be supplied with good drink-that is,
if any of it is good-and they would not have
the trouble of going to the house at the
corner. Therefore I think the Government
should see the advisibility of establishing a
wet canteen at the barracks.
Mr. LESLIE: Mr. Speaker, my Lord, I
must apologise to you for having said what I
wished to say in the wrong place, but I feel
rather strongly that it was very unfair to en-
Ideavour to reduce the emolument which we
are offering to the new Force, the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, on the ground that
principally during the transition period some
of the proceedings of the late Force may not
have been entirely up to the standard set by
their predecessors. I venture to hope that the
new Force will be in every way up to the old
standard of the R.I.C. I have every confidence that offering fair terms to recruits is
one of the best means we can adopt to ensure
that result. I hope that the Bill will pass
through the remaining stages without amendment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I must confess that I agree with the
hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) who has just spoken
in what he has said about emoluments with
regard to the new Force, the Royal Ulster
Constabulary. I think we must all recognise in fact we all do recognise that they
have got probably a very trying time ahead
of them. Speaking for myself-and I am certain that in this respect I am speaking for the
Government, I do not consider that their pay
is too high for the services they are called
upon--or may be called upon-to perform. I
think the hon. Member (Mr. Hill Dickson)
was a member of this Committee on the
Force, and as far as I know I think we
carried out the recommendations of that
Committee.
Mr. DICKSON: I moved an amendment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With regard to the selection Committee the point raised by the hon.
Member (Mr. Woods) to select the higher
this
appointments in
Force, I cannot
hold out any hope that the
ment will accept this. I think that
the appointment of the Inspector-Gene-
Govern-
155 Referees and Arbitrators Bill-

THE SENATE.
Adjournment.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
ral and of these high officers must be left to
the discretion of the responsible Minister.
With regard to the question of the wet canteens, I, like the hon. Member opposite (Mr.
Woods), am an advocate of temperance and
cannot hold out any hope that in the present
circumstances the statement made by the
Leader of the House yesterday on the Second
reading will be altered or that the Government will change their decision on this
matter, although, to a great extent, my
sympathies are with the hon. Member.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill do
now pass.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

20 JUNE 1922
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 20th June, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
Live Stock Breeding Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That the House do resolve itself
into Committee on said Bill. (Marquess of
Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AVA in the Chair.
AND
Ordered: That Clause I (Restriction on
keeping of bulls) stand part of the Bill.
Clause II. (Power to grant, suspend or revoke licences or permits).
(e) is of a breed or type unsuitable for
the district in which it is kept, and is not
a pure-bred bull belonging exclusively to
a single individual and kept for the service
of cows belonging exclusively to him.

Moved: Clause 2, page 2, line 20, to leave
out "a single individual" and insert "any
person." (The Marquess of Londonderry).
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The amendment which I desire to move in
this clause is to leave out the words "a single
individual" and insert "any person." This
amendment is merely a drafting one. I beg
to move.
On Question Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In line 22 there is another amendment of a
similar character, to leave out the word "him"
and insert "that person." I beg to move.
Moved: Line 22 to leave out "him" (ex
clusively to him) and insert "that person."
(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered
That Clause II as amended stand
part of the Bill.
Clause III. (Transfer and production of
licence or permit)
(2) A licence granted under this Act or
certified copy thereof shall be produced at
the time of or before the service of a cow
by the bull, if so required by the owner or
person in charge of the cow
Moved: Clause 3, page 2, line 40, to leave
out "be produced" (shall be produced at any
time). (The Marquess of Londonderry).
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This amendment proposes to leave out the
words "be produced." This is also a drafting amendment. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: Line 41 after "bull" (by the bull
if so required), insert "be produced.”—(The
Marquess of Londonderry).
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This also is a drafting amendment. I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That Clause III, as amended,
stand part of the Bill.
Ordered: That Clauses IV (Slaughtering or
castration of unsuitable bulls), V (Appeals),
VI (Duties of owners of licensed or permit
bulls), VII (Inspection and marking of
bulls), VIII (Forgery and fraudulent use of
licence or permit) stand part of the Bill.
Moved: After clause VIII to insert the
following new clause:-"Sections 1 to 4 inclusive of the Criminal Evidence Act, 1898,
shall apply in the case of a person charged
with an offence against this Act." (Mr.
Leslie).
a
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill we are considering
has been received with such a universal
chorus of acclamation and satisfaction that
I feel something in the nature of diffidence
in drawing atention to one of its lass
attractive aspects. But the fact is that the
Bill by reason of some of its clauses will be
a penal statute, and what is more, it will be
the first penal statute which has been
brought forward in this Parliament as
permanent addition to our Statute Book.
And consequently in dealing with this Bill
we are bound to make a precedent one way
or the other as regards the admission or exclusion of the evidence of the defendant. I
suggest that we should admit the evidence of
the defendant and his wife or her husband, as
the case may be, and the provision I propose
is that in accordance with the terms of the
Act which I have cited, the Criminal
Evidence Act, 1898, the defendant and his
wife should be competent, but not compelled
witnesses, and they shall have the protection
given to them under the Act of 1898, and
other details shall apply. I may mention
that the section in the original Act covers
about a page and a half, and therefore it is
Live Stock Breeding Bill—
THE SENATE.
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
better and simpler for us to deal with it in
three or four lines. I suggest that such a
provision might reasonably be required
under Section I of the Bill. Under Section
I of the Bill it is an offence to keep or have
control of a bull of prescribed age without a
licence. Now the age of a bull may very well
be a matter of doubt, although I freely admit
that a practical man could generally tell the
age of a bull without requiring a certificate.
Still if the matter comes into court, evidence
will have to be given, and it seems only fair
that the defendant should have an opportunity of telling his story in evidence. At
the moment the practice in Ireland as regards admiting or rejecting the evidence of
the accused person is in rather an unsatis
factory condition. In some cases the
defendant can give evidence, but in the
great majority of cases he cannot. This is
because Ireland was excluded from the provisions of the Criminal Evidence Act, 1898,
under the circumstances which the Noble
Marquess alluded to a few weeks ago. There
is no good reason why the North of Ireland
should remain bound by these conditions.
Our position to-day is practically the position
at it was in Great Britain during the quarter
of a century or so previous to Lard Halsbury's
Act of 1898. The position at that time is very
aptly illustrated in an article which was
published in 1902 under he signature of Sir
Courtenay Ilbert. Sir Courtenay wrote:
The
"From 1872 onwards numerous enactments were passed making persons charged
with particular offences and their husbands
and wives competent witnesses.
language and effect of these enactments
was not always the same, but the insertion
of some provision to this effect in an Act
creating a new offence, especially if it was
punishable by summary proceedings,
gradually became almost a common form in
legislation."
In examining this sub-section I found, as
I expected, a large number of instancesabout a score-in Acts of Parliament of the
greatest possible diversity, and I only found
one case in Acts between 1872 and 1896 which
did not contain such a provision; that was the
Motor Car Act of 1896. In examining these
statutes I was struck very much by the fact
that such provision as that which I am asking the House to insert was more usually
found in an Act where the burden of proof
was cast on the accused person. A provision throwing on the accused the burden of
proof is at first sight contrary to the usual
practice. Such a provision occurs in the Act
we are discussing in Clause 9, and also a
similar provision occurs in the Horse-breeding
Act of 1918, on which this Act is very much
modelled. I found, as I said, a large number
of precedents, but I do not propose to inflict
all of them on the House. I will instance, if
I may, one or two. There is the Licensing
Act of 1872, which in some cases throws the
burden of proof on the accused; the Food and
Drugs Act of 1875, and the Merchant Shipping
Act of 1876, Plimsoll's Load Line Act,
and another Act still in the 'seventies, to
which I want to draw particular attention.
This is the Contagious Diseases (Animals)
Act of 1878. With the exception of one section that statute was repealed and re-enacted.
As it stands at the moment the Act of 1878
is included in the later Act of 1894, which is
directed to be cited and construed as one
with the later Acts of 1896 and 1903. All
these Acts are administered by the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Agriculture.
Consequently if we introduce this provision
into this Bill it will not come altogether as
a stranger to the Department of Agriculture.
Later on, during the 'eighties and the 'nineties, there were other instances with which I
need not trouble the Committee. Since 1898 in
Ireland we have two instances, the Motor Car
Act of 1903, which is of great interest in this
matter, because it deliberately introduced in
Ireland the power to give evidence. We have
also the Children Act of 1908, on which I dd
not lay so much stress, because it was chiefly
an extension of power to give evidence where
power had already been given. Since then
there has been one precedent, which
seems to me the greatest of all, and
that is the precedent I ask the House
to follow the precedent we set ourselves.
In the Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Act
there is a provision contained that the con.
travention of the regulations is an offence
triable by Summary Jurisdiction, and in such
case our own Act provides that the defendant
shall be enabled to give evidence; in fact,
the provision inserted is practically in the
words which stand in my name. I venture
to suggest that we should do well to follow
that precedent, partly perhaps because it
is our own precedent, but still more because
the precedent is right in itself. If we adopt it,
it will keep us fully abreast of the trend of
the best of modern thought. It will be an
example of our desire to maintain unity with
the Sister Island, whose system in all matters
relating to the administration of jusice is the
best in the world.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I find myself once more engaged in a legal
and unequal contest with my hon. Friend
who sits opposite (Mr. Leslie). I need hardly
say I have listened with great interest to his
remarks on the Amendment which he has
proposed. I am sure he will forgive me if
I do not enter into argument with him in
connection with the Measures which he has
enumerated, of which I am convinced he
knows the substance, but of which I must
plead guilty to not knowing the substance.
Live Stock Breeding Bill-
Committee.
20 JUNE 1922
The suggestion made in the hon. Member's
Amendment is that Sections 1 to 4 inclusive of
the Criminal Evidence Act, 1898, shall apply
to the case of a person charged with an
offence against this Act. My hon. Friend
has told us that these provisions, while
applicable to cases in England, have not been
His
made applicable to cases in Ireland.
speech is all directed to making a considerable alteration in the law in Ireland as it
stands at the present moment. I feel bound
to say that there is a great deal in what he
says. I have no doubt that in the future
public opinion will probably move in that
direction, but I am not sure that the Bill
which he has selected is one which should be
the instrument for altering the procedure
which obtains at present. do not feel inclined on this occasion to argue the actual
merits of the change which he desires. I
think I should argue with him that his plan
is not one which operates altogether in
favour of the prisoner. It is supposed to
give the prisoner an additional chance and
additional assistance, but I am not sure that
it always has operated in that direction. I
think the hon. Member will agree with me
that the view which I am putting forward
is one which was put forward very strongly
by the Nationalist Members in 1898. That
is the reason Ireland is in a different
position from Great Britain as regards this
particular point. With regard to this
Amendment, it should be observed that the
Criminal Evidence Act, 1898, does not apply
to Ireland except in the case of a limited
number of enactments which involve the
delicate consideration of moods or states of
mind, and where the class of offence is of
such a character as to render trial
impossible unless the accused person be
admitted as a witness in his defence. If the
cases where the Act of 1898 applies in Ireland be examined, it will be found that this
consideration must have influenced the
Legislature. The Act of 1898 was recently
applied in connection with the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act to Northern
Ireland, and its effect will be understood when
illustrated by the following provisions:-
"Any person who harbours any person
whom he knows or has reasonable grounds
for supposing to have acted in contravention
of the Regulations shall be guilty of an
offence. (Section 11.2).
"It shall be the duty of any person who
knows or has good reasons for believing
that some other person has acted in contravention to the Regulations to inform the
Civil Authority of the fact, and if he fails
to do so he shall be guilty of an offence."
Where an offence is committed by a company every director and officer of the company shall be guilty of the like offence unless he proves that the act constituting the
offence took place without his knowledge
or consent."

Where any act if done without lawful
authority is an offence against the Regulations the burden of proving that the act
was done with lawful authority shall rest
on the person alleged to be guilty of the
offence."
The Act of 1898 is applied to Ireland by the
Motor Car Act, 1903. This Act contains
similar offences and defences, viz., the
person shall not be liable to a penalty under
Section 2 if he proves that he has had no
reasonable opportunity of registering the
car; the person driving the car shall be
guilty of an offence under this Act unless he
proves that he has taken all steps reasonably
practicable, etc.
In proceedings for crimes under Section 4
of the Explosive Substance Act, 1883, the defendant may be called as an ordinary witness
in the case. This Section deals with persons
who make or knowingly have in their possession explosive substances, and it enables
them in defence to prove that they had the
explosive in their possession for a lawful object. This Section is significant as showing
the trend of the law prior to the Act of
1898. Under Section 133 of the Children's Act,
1908, persons charged with certain offences are
made competent to give evidence.
The Bill now before the Senate is of an
entirely different character to the statutes
just mentioned, and to which the Criminal
Evidence Act of 1898 applies. There is nothing
in the Live Stock Breeding Bill involving the
delicate consideration of moods or states of
mind. In Clause 9, where the burden of proof
is placed upon the person charged, the only
evidence required would be the licence or
permit. There is nothing which could be said
on this Bill which might not more appropriately be urged in favour of the general application of the Criminal Evidence Act of 1898
to all criminal cases in Northern Ireland. If
and when the Legislature considers it desirable that the provisions of the Act of 1898
should be applied generally, this will be more
appropriately done by means of a separate
enactment providing for such general application. Brevity and simplicity are outstanding features of the present Bill, and no useful
purpose would be served by complicating the
Measure with additional clauses which would
not add to the efficiency of the instrument.
In the circumstances the Ministry feels bound
to oppose the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I very much regret the decision to which the Government has come. The
only criticism urged against the Amendment
is that it is of a somewhat differential nature.
I thought myself that the insertion of the
Clause might have reassured some persons
L
Live Stock Breeding Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
who felt dissatisfied. However, I suppose I
must put up with the fate of the reformers,
who, in England, again and again during
twenty-five years introduced a Measure
of this description, or at least endeavoured
to introduce words of this description. Two
or three Governments, I think, brought forward Bills. Some of the Measures I have
quoted were Government Measures. It is
certainly not worth while to ask the House to
divide on the Amendment, but I am sorry
that the Government has taken this line. I
ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Ordered: That Clauses IX (Burden of
Proof), X (Power to make Rules), XI (Expenses and Fines), and XII (Interpretation)
stand part of the Bill.
Clause XIII (Power to apply Act to Boars
and Rams).
The Ministry may, at any time after the
appointed day, by order, apply to the provisions of this Act, with such modifications
as may be specified in such order, to boars
and rams, or to either of those classes of
live stock, and any such order shall have
effect as if enacted in this Act, but may be
varied or revoked by a subsequent order.
Moved: In Clause 13, after "boars" (to
boars and rams) to insert "goats." (Mr.
Greer).
Mr. GREER: I have been asked to move the
Amendment which stands in my name by a
Member of the other House who is much interested in goat improvement, and who was
prevented by an unfortunate fatality from
moving it himself. He asked me to bring it
forward in this House. I would like to say a
few words in favour of that useful, if somewhat humble animal, the goat, because I
think it is really a very useful animal for
the poorer classes in the country. It has been
called the poor man's cow, and in some respects the milk of the goat is admittedly
better than the milk obtained from the cow.
It is practically free from the tuberculosis
germ, which unfortunately cannot be said of
the cow. The milk is very easy to digest and
very nourishing, and is extremely good for
children. From the statistics which have
been compiled or obtained by the Ministry
I find that in the year 1910 there were 40,390
goats in the Northern area, and ten years
later, in 1920, there were as many as 56,906.
That is to say, there has been an increase in
that period of some 16,516 goats. A few years
ago a very well-known lady in County Down,
in the interests of the children of the poorer
classes in her neighbourhood, decided to try
to improve the class of the common country
goat. She tried to improve it in two directions. is the direction of appearance, that is
to say, in form and coat, and also in the
direction of milk-producing capacity. She
effected a very great improvement in the
appearance of the goat by a mixture of
the Anglo-Nubian breed, and she then endeavoured to improve the milk-producing
quality by a mixture of the Toggenberg breed.
with the amazing result that she has now got
a valuable and well-known herd of goats at
Ballywalter, and instead of her goats producing a little under a quart of milk
per day, which was the average of the ord.
inary country goat before she took matters
in hand, her goats now produce a great deal
more milk. I would like to read you her
own words:
"I have now had goats for over 20 years,
and have worked up my herd to be really
good milkers. Last summer I had four giving over a gallon a day each and this year
I hope to have more. They are hardy,
being out day and night and getting only
a small feed of oats and drink at milking
time. They are out every day all through
the Winter and give milk all the year."
I think every Member of this House will
agree that it would be a splendid thing
if the ordinary country goats could be improved so as to yield anything like such a
large supply, and it is in order to try to effect
that improvement that I am moving this
amendment to have goats included in this
most valuable Act that has been brought for
ward on behalf of the Minister of Agriculture.
I see that Lady Dunleath has been making
this experiment for over 20 years, and so far
as I know she has not been advertising her
herd in any special way, but I speak from
what I see in going about the roads in North
Antrim when I say that this new class of
goat, which goes by the name of the Dunleath
goat, is making excellent progress in the
County of Antrim, and I think that in a
very short time, if the same provisions that
are applied to these other animals can be
applied to goats, they will make a very excellent, not to say useful animal throughout
Northern Ireland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I should be the last person to quarrel with
the hon. Member for having introduced this
subject, and I am sure he has by no means
over-emphasised the value of the goat to
many families in this area and elsewhere.
The Amendment which he proposed is one
which it was found impossible owing to various circumstances to introduce in another
place, and consequently I am very glad he has
introduced it here because it gives an opportunity to discuss a question of a certain
amount of importance. I venture to say that
165 Live Stock Breeding Bill— 20 JUNE 1922

Committee.
on this occasion it would be quite impractic
able to work a scheme for the licensing of
goats with any reasonable degree of efficiency.
Goat breeding is at the present moment almost unorganised. I say almost because we
are well aware of the activities in that dir
ection which have been mentioned already by
the hon. Member. These animals are scattered all over the country in ones and twos
and many of them are at the present time
in an almost entirely wild condition. It will
therefore be seen that goats are on an entirely different footing from sheep, which exist
in flocks, or from swine, the breeding of which
is, in the main, highly organised.
Comprehensive schemes for the improve
ment of cattle, sheep, and swine have been in
existence for many years, and the main object of this Bil! is to supplement and complete
the work of these schemes by preventing careless or indifferent people from keeping
inferior sires and thus neutralizing the
beneficial effects of the good blood already
introduced. The expenditure of money on the
licensing of bulls, boars, and rams is justified
from an economic point of view because of
the great value to Northern Ireland of the export trade in these animals. In the last year
for which figures are available the numbers of
animals exported from Northern Ireland were
-cattle, 200,361; sheep, 63,546; swine, 7,723.
The yearly value of the trade in these animals
has exceeded £8,000,000. If, as a result of the
Bill, the value of these animals is even
slightly increased, it is obvious that we add
very considerably to the wealth of the community, I think the hon. Member will see
that no such results could possibly follow from
a scheme for the licensing of goats, which
hardly count at all from a commercial standpoint. In the peculiar conditions associated
with goat-breeding, improvement schemes
such as those now being promoted by
Lady Dunleath, Miss Saunders and others
will be a far more effective and
less expensive means of raising the standard of goats than any licensing measure.
I think the hon. Member will agree with
me in that. It is for this reason that, on
behalf of the Minister I am unable to accept
the amendment which the hon. Member has
brought forward.
Mr. GREER: I have to thank the Noble
Marquess for the sympathetic manner in
which he has dealt with this subject. I was
afraid there must have been some special
reason why goats were not included in the
Bill, and I must confess that there is a good
deal in the reasons which the Noble
Marquess has given. I think it is worse than
useless for us to include in the Bill a clause
which would be mere window-dressing.
There is no use bringing forward legislation
unless we can carry it into effect, and I do
see a serious difficulty in the case of goats.
I have gone into the matter with Lady Dunleath to some extent, and she has suggested
a scheme which she thought would probably
in a short time effect a great improvement,
and with the permission of the House I
should like in a few words to state what it
is. There is in existence at the present
time an Irish Goat Society, the President of
which, I believe, is Lady Aberdeen. It has
been doing the work for some years, but at
present it seems to have fallen on evil days,
and for some reason is not flourishing. Lady
Dunleath thinks that if an Ulster Goat
Society were established and received a
small grant from the Government as other
Societies do, with the subscriptions from
members, it would be quite easy to purchase
male goats of good class and farm them out
at suitable places in country districts, and
let the poorer classes have the benefit of the
services of these animals at a moderate
charge. They see the very great improvement that has taken place in these goats, and
if a small supply, four or five, could be
obtained and kept by the Society and sent
round to the various agricultural shows, it
would bring home to the minds of the persons
attending those shows what a very great improvement in the animal could be effected.
I think that if the Government could see
their way to look into the matter from that
point of view, and either establish, or aid in
the establishment of, some such Society it
would be of very great advantage. In the
circumstances, I ask leave to withdraw my
amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Ordered: That Clauses 13 (Power to apply
Act to Boars and Rams), and 14 (Commencement and Short Title) stand part of the Bill.
Ordered: That the title stand part of the
Bill.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported with
amendment to the House.
Bill reported with amendment.
Royal Assent.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am authorised by the Prime Minister, on
his behalf, to inform the Senate that His
Excellency the Lord Lieutenant, in the name
and on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the following Bills:-
Referees and Arbitrators (Procedure).
Motor Vehicle Races.
Joint Nursing and Midwives Council.
The state of the Province.
Adjournment.

THE SENATE.
Adjournment.
now
MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, "That this House do
adjourn until Thursday at 3-15 p.m.
Mr. BARBOUR: Might I ask the Leader
of the House if he has anything to report
about his recent visit to London. I think I
would only be expressing the feelings of
every Member of this House and of the vast
majority of the inhabitants of this Northern
area if I expressed to him the very deep feeling of gratitude that animates us at the
readiness he so often displays in going to
London on our behalf. When men and
women feel that their all is at stake, they
are grateful for any assistance that may be
offered to them, but when assistance, which
is as effective as the Noble Marquess can
give is offered, and offered so freely, I think
it would be a very serious omission on our
part if we failed to let him know how deeply
every Member of this House, and indeed
every member of the community, feels the
debt they owe to him for the ever-ready way
he comes to our assistance when duty calls
him. I am sure that the Prime Minister
(Sir James Craig) must feel that in Lord
Londonderry he has a very valuable
Lieutenant, and I feel sure that our cause
is pleaded efficiently and gets admitted to
quarters that would be almost impossible to
any other Member of this House. We would
like to express in this way our gratitude
for the measure of assistance we are so constantly receiving from the Noble Marquess.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am sure I feel most grateful to the hon.
Member who has been kind enough to use far
too flattering expressions with regard to myself. I need hardly say that any service I
can render to the cause of Ulster is a privilege and an honour to myself, and I only hope
I shall never be behindhand in doing what I
can for the Province of which we are all so
proud and which we love so well.
I do not feel able at this moment to make
I
anything in the nature of a statement.
understand that the Prime Minister in an-
At
other place is adopting the same course.
the present moment we are in a position of
uncertainty, and I am sure it is the wish of
everyone here that that uncertainty should be
terminated. As we are all aware, elections
are proceeding in Southern Ireland, and I
think it would be improper for me to make
any comments of a political character on any
of the events which are passing before our
view at the moment. I feel we are deeply indebted to our Prime Minister for the work he
has done and is continually doing on our behalf. As you are all aware, he has kept in
close touch with the British Government,
who have done everything in their power to
assist us, and I should like to take this opportunity of paying a tribute to Mr. Winston
Churchill, who has been sympathetic and
helpful in every possible way. He realises
our position and is fully aware of our rights,
and I feel sure he is doing everything in his
power which he believes is consistent with
the policy of the British Government towards
Ulster and the policy which we ourselves
hold. Beyond that I do not think I should
be entitled to say anything to you to-day. The
negotiations which we have been carrying on
with the British Government are naturally
of a very delicate character. We have asked
the British Government for assistance on
more occasions than one, and they have acceded to our request on nearly every point.
The state of the Province at the present
moment is one which we cannot altogether
regard with satisfaction, but I feel that we
are entitled to look on the future with a certain amount of confidence. The Police Force
belonging to Northern Ireland is carrying out
its duties efficiently, and I certainly think
we owe the members of that Force a debt of
gratitude for what they are doing. On this
occasion I did not intend to make any remarks
at all in regard to the political situation, and
I feel sure hon. Members will excuse me
from making any fuller statement.
Col. PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I should like
to associate myself with the remarks of my
hon. Friend (Mr. Barbour) with regard to
the appreciation which I am sure we all feel
towards the Noble Marquess, the Leader of
this House, who has always shown himself
anxious to do everything he can for the benefit of this Province. I can quite understand
that at the present moment the negotiations
recently conducted in London are too delicate
to be brought before this House, and I do
not propose to say anything on that point.
But I do not quite agree with the Noble
Marquess when he says that we can look to
the future with confidence. I do not
apologise for bringing this matter before the
House. In my district alone within the last
fortnight there have been either seven or
eight raids, and these in a district which was
the most peaceful in Ireland. That district
is now subject to a form of intimidation by
nightly raids which I do not think the
Government can regard with complacence.
The noble Marquess has on several
occasions appealed to this House for
(patience and trust in the Government, and
constructive criticism. I am sure he will
admit that so far the Government have not
had much to complain of in the way of
criticism. The noble Marquess has undoubtedly had a great deal more experience
than any of us, but I cannot imagine a
Government that has received less criticism
in times of great stress than this Government, or an assembly that has shown more
patience in the pathetic and terrible conditions that exist than this assembly, and the
The state of the Province. 170
Adjournment-
20 JUNE 1922
Having
reason of it all is that we have entirely
trusted the Government. But we have also
a duty to the country, and we cannot discharge that unless we express candidly and
fairly our opinions on such a vital and
important matter as the means by which
law and order are to be restored and maintained in this country. We all know that the
Government inherited from its predecessors
in government, or misgovernment, a condition
of unrest and crime for which this Government was in no sense responsible.
come into power, it was for our Government
to deal with this condition of things, and
they were entitled to choose their methods
and their weapons. They chose the Civil
Authorities Bill, and they got it, I think the
Noble Marquess will admit, practically as
they had drafted it. Although I wish it had,
it has not yet produced that law and order
which we all hoped would come, and which we
still hope will come. Since that Bill became
an Act, murder, reprisals, and burnings have
become more extended and more numerous
than they ever were before.
I think it is perfectly safe to say that the
whole moral atmosphere of this country has
become less sensitive, and we are fast degenerating into the condition of a South
American Republic or a Balkan State. If
order is to be restored I would suggest that
the Government will have to act vigorously
and promptly, or they along with the rest of
the country will meet the fate which hesitation and vacillation deserve. The conditions
which exist now are those of a state of war,
and the enemy is not confined to one front or
to any front. We have law-breakers and sympathisers with law-breakers in every creed
and in every corner of the Six Counties. I
take it that the law-breaker is the enemy of
the State, the enemy of his religion, whatever
it is, and the enemy of society generally, 1
think this House will admit that innocent men
of all creeds and of practically all classes have
been done to death and much suffering has
been caused to people of all creeds and classes
who themselves have never hurt or wished to
hurt men of any creed. I am convinced that
the only way to deal with this situation is by
placing it in the hands of a single authority
who will control all the forces of the Crown.
and possibly the local forces too. I do not
know what the position of our Government
is in relation to the Imperial Government,
whether it is possible to get the Imperial
Government to give us or our Government
control of the military forces in this country,
and to put all under the military authorities.
I do not know whether it is possible, but at
any rate I am quite certain, if it is not, that
it would be better to have Martial Law than
to continue the state of things in existence at
present. The reason given by the Government on former occasions against Martial
Law was that the fair name of Belfast would
suffer
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Who gave that reason?
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I have
heard that reason given.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Could you quote it?
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I think
the right hon. Gentleman, the Lord Mayor,
said it at one time. Another reason given
was that the reputation of our Government
would suffer, and another reason I have seen
quoted was that the Imperial Government
might take away the troops, and thus we
should be left without any protection. I am
prepared, so far as I am concerned, to risk
all these possibilities sooner than hear of one
week-end at Newry, and many week-ends that
have happened in this great city of Belfast.
I think no one who saw law and order restored would think less of Belfast, and its
reputation will never improve till order is
restored. I feel certain that the Government
which had the courage to put the right
authority in the saddle to deal with a purely
military situation would receive not only the
support of this House, but of the whole
British Empire, and especially of the people
of Ulster. The fact of our gallant and splendid Specials being associated with His
Majesty's Army even for a short time would
add immensely to their efficiency and
discipline, and they would be in a much
better position to take the Army's place even
if the worst should happen and the Army had
to be withdrawn. We should have the local
Force, and we should have it very much improved. One thing I am certain of, and that
is this, whether you proclaim Martial Law
now or later, you will not deal finally,
effectively or economically, with the present
conditions unless you employ welldisciplined, well-paid and well-trained troops
controlled by a single commander, and commanded by well-trained officers and noncommissioned officers.
In the Specials you have splendid material,
both in officers and men, but it is a job that
cannot be done-I was going to say cheaply,
but I know that does not come into it-it
cannot be done in a half way and it cannot be done with divided command. I heard
a story the other day-of course there is not
a word of truth in it, but it illustrates what
might easily happen. A man was going down
the streets of Belfast when, to use an ex.
pression that is common in some parts of
the country, he met a bomb with a time
fuse on it. He called a soldier and said to
him: "Take tway that bomb," but the
The state of the Province.
Adjournment-
THE SENATE.
[Col. Perceval Maxwell]
soldier said, "It is against my orders." He
called for a policeman of one sort who also
said it was against his orders. The man
called another policeman who said: "I will
go and fetch the Fire Brigade." Of course
the story, I have no doubt, is only a story,
but it illustrates the troubles of divided command. I am perfectly certain that you must
have a paid and all-time Force. These partvoluntary forces no matter how gallant
or how keen they may be will never put down
this organisation which we are up against. I
would also add this that the system of having
men serving in their own districts is unsound.
or how efficient
no matter how impartial
they may be. It is really adding fuel to
the fires of prejudice, and placing a ready
weapon in the hands of the agitator, besides
being grossly unfair to the men themselves,
even if they desired it, and to their relatives,
and building up a future in ths country
which will cost many a life, and add untold
difficulties to those who are responsible for
the maintenance of law and order. I do hope
the Government will consider the possibility
of trying to unify the command, and to bring
the military into it and Martial Law, but I
can understand their difficulties.
On Question Motion agreed to.
House adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes after four
o'clock until Thursday next
at 3-15
Live Stock Breeding Bill.
Adjournment.
22 JUNE 1922
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 22nd June, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
Live Stock Breeding Bill.
Order for receiving report of Amendments
read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move that
this report be now received.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that Standing Order No. XXV
be considered in order to its being dispensed
with in respect to said Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill be now read a
Third time.
Mr. LESLIE: I think we should scarcely
wish to part from this Bill without a word
of congratulation to the right hon. Gentleman
the Minister of Agriculture on the success
which has attended his maiden effort at legislation. We all wish the Bill the utmost possible success and look forward to the realisation of the anticipations he made in his
speech of an improvement in the character
and quality of the stock which will be produced in the future. We discussed one or
two aspects of the Bill yesterday with a view
to its improvement and its efficiency. We may
congratulate ourselves that we have improved
the Bill, because the Amendment which was
carried by the Noble Marquess removed one
serious objection to the Bill which I had
heard. I am quite sure that all of us-particularly those of us from the country-will
return to our homes satisfied, and we shall
all take every opportunity of impressing on
those engaged in cattle-rearing the importance
and the merits of the Measure which we are
passing to-day.
On question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill do now pass.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the Commons to
acquaint them that the Senate have agreed to
the said Bill, with some Amendments, to
which they desire the concurrence of the Commons.
The Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving the adjournment, I think, perhaps,
the House will wish me to say a few words
as to the Government having given no answer
to the speech made by my right hon. Friend
opposite (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell) at our
last meeting. I am under the impression that
the right hon. Gentleman imagines that his
speech was out of order. I should like to say
at once that his speech was in no
sense out of order. It is open to any Member
of this House to raise any question that
he wishes on the motion for the adjournment.
The only thing which I should like to
point out, as much for the convenience
of the House as for my Own convenience as Leader of this House, is that
if any Member desires to bring forward any
subject on the adjournment it is easier for the
Leader of the House if some sort of notice is
given to him that it is proposed by a Member
to raise a certain question. If hon. Members
will cast their minds back to Tuesday they will
realise what I mean. The hon. Gentleman
sitting opposite, Mr. Milne Barbour rose at
the adjournment to utter certain remarks
which he was good enough to make eulogistic
of myself, and resumed his seat. As Leader
of the House I took all the steps I could to
ascertain if anybody else was proposing to raise
anything on the adjournment. I then rose in
my place, thanked the hon. Member for the
kind words he had used in regard to myself,
and said at the same time that I thought at
the moment it was not necessary or altogether
proper for me to give a complete review of the
situation; and I was strengthened in my
opinion on that matter by the fact that the
Prime Minister in another place was proposing
to adopt exactly the same attitude. After I
had resumed my seat and exhausted my right
to speak, my right hon. and gallant Friend
(Colonel Perceval-Maxwell) rose in his place
and raised some very important points, and
delivered a speech which was not altogether
unprepared. The only thing I would like to
say is that if the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman had given me private notice, which
was quite in his power to do, that he was proposing on the adjournment to raise very important matters, I should have been only too
happy to have given a reply and perhaps to
have assisted him in the course he was pursuing. I was precluded from doing that. It
is quite true that I might have made a reply
by leave of the House; but in a matter of
such great importance as the speech
made by him, which constituted a real
indictment of the Government, I was
not ready to give him the answer which
he should on that occasion have had. I do
not know whether the right hon. Gentleman
THE SENATE.

Adjournment.

Adjournment.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
a
realises that, from the position he occupies
and the record which he holds in the war, and
the fact
also from
that he is
Privy Councillor, a great deal of importance
is attached to his remarks. If he took occasion to read the papers next day he will have
seen that his speech was fully reported, not
only in those organs which support the Government and his views and ideas, but also in
those other organs of the Press which never
lose any opportunity to publish anything
which may be to the detriment of the Six
Counties. I am sorry for that reason that the
speech which he made went without
answer.
an
I have only risen in my place to-day, not to
refer to the speech made by him (Colonel
Perceval-Maxwell), because I am not sure that
he will require an answer to that speech, but
to say that as we are a new Assembly, and as
only a small number of us have any experience of what I may call the amenities of
assemblies of this description, I suggest to
hon. Members that not only for my own convenience-after all, that is a small matterbut for their own satisfaction in receiving an
answer to the question which they may like
to bring forward, if they could see their way
to give me a certain amount of private notice
that they are proposing to raise a certain
question, and, if they like, give the nature
of this question, I can assure them I shall be
ready to give the answer I am capable of
giving, or else take steps in order to ascertain
from the various Departments concerned the
answer which they may think necessary to put
forward. I have only risen for the purpose of
making these few remarks because I feel, in
the interests of hon. Members themselves, it
is most undesirable that any question should
not be replied to by a representative of the
Government.
I do not think it will be necessary for this
House to meet next week at all. We may be
called together on one day, and I venture to
suggest, Mr. Speaker, that you should use
your jurisdiction in giving us notice as to
what day. In the week after it may be necessary for the House to sit on Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday. I beg to move that this
House do now adjourn.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: Perhaps
I might be allowed to say a few words by way
of personal explanation of what the Noble
Marquess, the Leader of this House, has
brought before us. I must thank him for the
very nice words he has used. I can sympathise
with his position. I can assure him it was
the last thought in my mind to show any discourtesy towards him or towards the House.
To be perfectly candid, I had considered this
question. It was a question I wished to bring
before the House and before the country, My
hon. Friend, Mr. Barbour, rose to ask the
Noble Marquess a question as to the negotiations in London, and he gave him an answer.
I was not aware at any rate I did not mean
the slightest discourtesy to him in getting
up and bringing to his attention and to the
attention of the Government a different
question altogether, the state of this country
at home and my views on it. I only make
these remarks to show him and to show the
House I had no intention of any discourtesy.
I can assure him if I have at any time anything to bring before this House I will on
another occasion acquaint him of my intention.
I think I should have done so on Tuesday if
I had not been rather late, but I really did
not think it was necessary to do so. I am very
sorry for the apparent discourtesy.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
ac-
The House adjourned
cordingly at half-past Three
o'clock.
Adjournment.
New Member.
30 JUNE 1922
THE SENATE.

Friday, 30th June, 1922.
The Senate met at Four of the Clock, Mr.
Speaker in the Chair.
New Member Sworn.
John Clements Glendinning, Esquire,
Sworn, and took his seat in the room of the
late hon. Hugh Thom Barrie, deceased.
Message from the Commons.
That they have passed the Church Temporalities Fund Bill, the Consolidated Fund
Appropriation Bill, the National Health
Insurance Bill and the Local Government
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Church Temporalities Fund Bill.
Moved: That the Church Temporalities
Fund Bill be now read a First time.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Consolidated Fund (Appropriation)
Bill.
Moved: That the Consolidated Fund Appropriation Bill be now read a First time.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Moved: That the National Health Insurance Bill be now read a First time.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Local Government (Northern Ireland)
Bill.
Moved: That the Local Government (Northern Ireland) Bill be now read a First time.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
ASSASSINATION OF SIR HENRY
WILSON.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving that this House do now adjourn,
I feel that hon. Members would wish me to
take this, the first, opportunity which has
presented itself of making a reference to the
tragic event of last Thursday-I mean the
assassination of Field-Marshal Sir Henry
Wilson on the door-step of his house in
London.
It will be in the recollection of all hon.
Members that this House had adjourned
before the news of this catastrophe reached
Belfast, and, consquently, we were neither
able to express our heart-felt sorrow, nor
our indignation at, nor condemnation of so
atrocious a crime.
The subject was mentioned in both Houses
of Parliament in London, and also in the
House of Commons here, and I know that
I shall be voicing the general opinion of all
hon. Members of this House in giving expression to our feeling that the official Report
of Debates in this House should not be
without a mention of so tragic and momentous an occurrence. Tragic and momentous
it indeed is, when the Empire has lost one
of its most illustrious soldiers and Ulster a
warm-hearted and courageous friend.
I should like to say on the adjournment
that the Second reading of the four Bills
which have just been read a First time will
be taken on Monday and the Committee
stages on Tuesday. The remaining stages
of these Bills will be taken on Wednesday.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten minutes after Four
of the Clock.
M
Standing Orders-
Amendments.
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Monday, 3rd July, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
Standing Orders Relating to Public
Business.
Moved: That Standing Order No. XVIII be
amended as follows:
(Note-The additions are shown in Italics).
XVIII. (1) At a Sitting of the Senate
business relating to Private Bills appearing
on the Orders of the day shall be taken at
the commencement of the sitting and before
Public business.
(2) Notices relating to Private Bill business
and Public business respectively shall be
taken in the order in which they appear on
the Order Paper, and these notices shall be
set down on the Order Paper in the order in
which the notices are received, subject as
follows:-
(a) Questions must appear at latest on the
Order Paper circulated on the day before
that on which an answer is desired.
(b) Questions, to which a star is attached,
will be put down on the Order Paper before
other Public business, and no debate shall
be allowed on any such question.
(c) Motions for the suspension of any
Standing Order shall be entered on the Order
Paper after questions but before other Public
business.
(d) Business of which no notice need be
given shall be taken either after questions
and before other Public business, or at the
conclusion of Public business.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
There are several amendments which our
practice has shown to be necessary in the
Standing Orders. The first amendment I beg
to move is to insert a new paragraph to
sub-Section 2 of Standing Order No. XVIII,
as follows:-
(a) Questions must appear at latest on
the Order Paper circulated on the day
before that on which the answer is desired.
The object of this amendment is to meet
the convenience of hon. Members who live at
a distance.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That Standing Order No. XXII (3)
be amended as follows:-
(Note-The addition is shown in Italics).
STAGES OF A BILL.
XXII. The following are the stages through
which a Public Bill must pass:-
(1) First Reading. The Bill must be read
a First time on a motion for the purpose.
Amendment of a Motion to read the Bil
a First time, or debate on the motion, shall
not be allowed except in cases where notice
has been given of the First Reading of the
Bill.
(2) Second Reading. The Bill must be read
a Second time on a motion for the purpose.
(3) Committee stage. Unless the House
orders that a Bill shall not be committed,
on a motion for the purpose, the Bill must
be referred to a Committee, being either a
Committee of the whole House or a Select
or Standing Committee, on a motion for
the purpose.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The next amendment is to insert certain
words in Standing Order No. XXII (3). The
reason for this amendment is that it avoids
the necessity for committing a Money Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That Standing Order No. XXXIV
be amended by adding-
(a) An elected Senator may resign his seat
by giving notice in writing to the Speaker,
and his seat shall become vacant on such
notice being given.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the reason for this amendment will
be apparent. By the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, a Senator who wishes to resign
his seat must hand his resignation to the
Lord Lieutenant unless the House appoint
another person to receive such resignation.
By Standing Order the Speaker must notify
the Lord Lieutenant of a vacancy. Therefore, the Speaker should receive the notices
himself and thus avoid the necessity of
waiting on the notice from the Lord Lieutenant. I think the purpose of this amendment will be clear to hon. Members.
I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Northern Government Forces.
Mr. GREER: I beg to ask the Leader of
the House:-
Whether he can inform the House in
regard to the Forces which have been set
up under the Northern Government for
Oral Answers-
Northern Government Forces.
3 JULY 1922
the maintenance of law and order, of the
exact division of responsibilities amongst
the various classes of officials concerned in
the performance of these duties.
Few persons outside the actual Members
of the Government can realise the extraordinary difficulties which they have had to
encounter on taking up the reins of Government. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) They
were opposed--actively opposed-by persons
whom they were entitled to expect opposition
from. They were also thwarted in every
direction by persons in high position from
whom they were entitled to expect all possible
help and assistance, and I should think that
we Members of Parliament who know perhaps
more of the difficulties than the outside
public must be full of admiration, not to
say wonder, at the success which has attended
their efforts. It is really little short of
marvellous that they have been able to
get on so very well in the difficult circumstances. One of the first problems which
they encountered was the maintenance of law
and order in perhaps the most difficult time
that any of us have ever experienced. They
set about doing this by organising and
This was by
creating a new police force.
no means an easy task, especially as it had
to be done in a hurry. It turned out to
be an easy matter to get men of the right
stamp. But we must all be aware that it
is absolutely impossible to turn men who have
not been accustomed to drilling into well disciplined, trained soldiers at once by word of
command or by wavng some fairy wand over
them. It takes time-a good deal of time-to
bring men to proper ideas of discipline. Now
they have raised five different classes of
police, that is to say, the R.U.C., the "A"
Specials, the "B" Specials, the "C" Specials,
and then the C1 Force. Of course all these
different Forces have to be provided with
officers from the highest down to the lowest.
Now, speaking generally, in these forces, I
think they have managed to get together a
most excellent, body of men who are full of
courage, full of enthusiasm, full of determination to do the very best they can to
preserve the Six Counties intact, and 1 think
the Government is to be congratulated on
having found so many men of this stamp.
No doubt we have heard of some complaints,
but anyone can make complaints, and,
speaking generally, I think the new Force
There
is most admirable.

the sun
as we know, and we
be surprised if in new body of men
number
of this kind and of this large
two people perthere should be on or
haps that we had rather had not
their way to join. As I said before, speaking
generally, we have a most excellent force.

I
have not a word to say against them.
Criticism is of two kinds. There is criticism
that is intended to injure and in my opinion
does not injure so very much as the critics
hoped it might. There is also criticism that
is intended to be anything but harmful to
do good, and in my opinion we had a specimen of that kind of criticism the other day
from the hon, and gallant Gentleman who
is now present (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell).
I am quite sure that the authorities welcome
criticism that is intended to help the force.
My contribution in the way of criticism is,
I may say, very small. I do not intend to
say a word against the large number of
different forces that have been created in
varying conditions and for varying terms of

years. That is not my intention.
doubt the Government were advised by persons who were quite competent to give advice,
and we have got these five forces. Here is
where my criticism comes in. They have all
separate officers, and there has been a little
difficulty in knowing to whom you should
apply in case, say, one of the Specials or one
of the in the first instance it is not easy
to know to which force the man belongs by
looking at him-and then if he has done some
act that you think is deserving of special
commendation and you want to bring it before his officer, or if he has done an act that
deserves reprobation that you might also
want to bring before his officer, the difficulty
is to know who is the officer in absolute control. I know that difficulty has arisen in
various cases. We have heard a good deal
about dual control, and it appears to me that
there is something like dual or quadruple
control existing at present. The question I
have put on the paper is designed to abolish,
if possible, that state of things, and to let
the public generally know who is in special,
absolute control in every district.

in the slightest degree, but I am sure that
