Oral Answers-
THE SENATE.
[Colonel Perceval-Maxwell]
it is so powerful that really a little criticism
of any method, I think, cannot do it
much harm. The very fact that after the
Special Constabulary have been in existence
for nearly two years because they came into
existence before the Government-and after
the Government has been in existence for
one year, the question my hon. Friend (Mr.
Greer) has asked as to who is responsible in
a County for the control of the different
Forces of Police, is a very strong indictment
against the whole system of the Special
Constabulary and Police Forces in this
country. I believe that the House will agree
that in order to restore peace certain things
are necessary. The Executive must be efficient and strong enough to defeat the
criminal, and disciplined enough to ensure
the confidence of all law-abiding citizens.
You cannot have efficiency and you cannot
have discipline without a direct chain of
responsibility, and that is what it appears
to me you have not got in the present system.
I understand after the Inspector-General the
Police Force is divided under County Inspec
tors, County Commandants, and so on. The
consequence is that when you have a raid
or anything of that sort or any sudden call
there is a doubt about whose job it is.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Not at all.
I think
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
sometimes there is confusion in the lower
ranks. It was for this reason and to have
single control that I ventured to suggest
the only means by which you could speedily
obtain the services of a highly-disciplined
and trained force was that the Government
should adopt martial law, because then you
would get at the top the military forces.
However, I understand the Government will
not entertain that idea; in fact, I saw that
the Prime Minister has described martial
law as the negation of all law. I believe it
has been described that way before. That
may be a correct description, but I would
humbly submit that martial law has been
used-
Mr. SPEAKER: I might remind the hon.
Member that we are not discussing martial
law. The original question is as to the exact
division of responsibility amongst the various
classes of officials concerned in the performance of these duties. You are not in order
in discussing whether we should have martial
law in force or not.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I only
wish to point out that I thought we were
discussing the Police Forces generally, but
I shall say no more about martial law. The
chief business, the important thing, is to
have a strong administration able to enforce
law and order. What the public really want
is order. They are tired of laws which are
not obeyed. I maintain that the present
police system creates a great deal of irritation
and does not provide us with the amount of
protection that the splendid material we have
at our command is capable of. I would suggest to the Government that we want a simplified system. We want a Headquarters'
Staff, and a chain of responsibility right down
through each police district. There are two
staffs, I believe, in this city. There is the
enormous staff of the Military Adviser,
General Solly-Flood, and then there is the
staff of the Inspector-General. There should
be a more simple system in which whatever
officer you put at the head of affairs has
his staff, and after him you have the officers
in charge of all the police in the county, and
of all the police in each district. I think
that would simplify it and would make it
very much easier for the people. They would
know whom to go to, and also it would
bring the responsibility either for success
or failure in any district down to a particular officer. It may be said that my criticism may be too critical and not constructive.
Instead of having half the alphabet of
categories, instead of having two large staffs,
and instead of covering the roads with
Crossleys, which appear always to be driven
as if they were going through an ambush,
I would suggest that you have one
staff and two categories, the Royal Ulster
Constabulary and the Royal Ulster Special
Constabulary, that these men should be permanent men enlisted for the period of twelve
months and that they should be paid and
disciplined. You cannot discipline a man
who can walk away when you speak to him,
and it is most important, and nowhere more
important than in the North of Ireland,
that the people who are responsible for the
maintenance of law and order should be
beyond criticism in the point of discipline.
I know you have got a splendid Force, I
know you have got efficient officers, but you
must give the officers the power to make
their Force as efficient as it is worthy of
being. You have got fine material, and I do
hope the Government will look into this
question and try to simplify the system and
put the Force on a basis on which discipline
can be properly enforced.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I should like to thank the hon. Gentleman
(Mr. Greer) who has initiated this debate
by means of a question for the recognition
which he has paid to the difficulties with
which we
are assailed at this moment. I
should also like to thank him for the tribute
which he has paid to our Police Forces
throughout the Six Counties. I think under
the circumstances we can claim to have been
successful. That is certainly due to the cooperation we have received from all rightminded people in this Province. He has
referred to the difficulties and anomalies
with which we are faced at this present
185 Oral Answers
3 JULY 1922
Northern Government Forces. 186
that Martial Law is a panacea which our
enemies are always putting forward, and I
think the hon. and gallant Gentleman realises that on other occasions when he has put
forward this suggestion the sense of those he
has addressed has been of an entirely opposite
character, and I venture to think the opinions
given on those occasions have been more or
less correct, if they have not been altogether
correct. The hon. and gallant Gentleman
speaks of staffs as if we were overwhelmed
and over-weighted with staffs. I do not
know whether he speaks from an economical
or an administrative point of view, but there
is no overwhelming staff administering anything in the Six Counties. We have rigidly
considered the question of economy, and we
have to, because a bankrupt State will in
no sense be able to carry out the views the
hon. and gallant Member has in his mind.
I do not know even now that a lavish expenditure of money in having an additional
staff would do what he thinks and bring
about co-operation. Co-operation is what
we want or is what we ought to have. My
view-and I am quite prepared to give itis this: that we have been suddenly faced
with a very difficult situation indeed. We
have had to build up a force, we have had
to build up discipline, and we have had
to establish this chain of responsibility
which it is obviously the desire and the
duty of all Governments to build up.
moment. The difficulties are innumerable,
but there are also anomalies owing to
the situation-the warlike conditions, and yet
not warlike conditions, the desire to govern
the country in the ordinary way, an intense
desire to create public opinion and public
spirit, and not to bring the whole country to
a standstill by determining that martial law
is the only way by which the trouble can
be overcome. We are gradually moving
forward, and in a time, perhaps shorter than
one can say, we shall see law and order
reasserted, backed by public opinion and the
Six Counties, and the whole of Ireland in
that condition in which we wish to see it.
I have no reason to complain of the manner
in which the hon. Members of this Senate
have treated me; they have one and all
shown a desire to co-operate, and I can
assure them that my task has been considerably lightened by the obvious efforts which
have been put forward to assist me. The
hon. Senator Greer says criticism may be
of two kinds, one intended to injure, and
the other intended to help. I have been
fortunate inasmuch as the criticisms I have
received have been with the intention of
helping, and I can only hope that those
who read our debates realise that those
criticisms are by people who are desirous
of not bringing this Government down, but
of building it up. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
I think that probably I shall be able to
satisfy the hon. Gentleman in regard to the
question he has asked to-day. I am also
grateful to the hon. and gallant Gentleman
(Col. Maxwell) for the remarks which he has
made and for the consideration which he is
obviously anxious to show.
He made one
or two statements on which I do not know
that I have the time to dilate. I think I
should be infringing on your ruling, Mr.
Speaker, if I followed him into some of the
arguments he has made. He assumes that
the Government is all-powerful. I wish they
were all-powerful, because then we could
perhaps deal more efficiently with the situation than we can at the present moment,
but I have never heard of any Government
which is all-powerful. I have heard of
magicians and I sincerely wish we had in
our midst a magician who with a wave of
his wand could touch the forces of disorder
and dispel them in one moment. But I am
sorry to realise there is no such magician
and all we can do is to follow the policy
we believe to be right and which we are
convinced will in the end be successful.
The hon. and gallant Gentleman (Col.
Maxwell) has gone off on a subject on which
I should be very happy to answer him but
which you have ruled out of order, and I
should only like to refer to it in this respect;
Col. PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: There is no
chain.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think that if the hon. and gallant
gentleman will read the Schedule of the
Constabulary Bill he will see there is a
consecutive chain of responsibility and it is
an extraordinary thing that the Senator
should make such a statement. Personally, I
am very glad that he has, because I am
able to give him an answer. I might have
turned the tables on him and also on the
other hon. Member (Mr. Greer) and said
they ought to have studied the question
themselves. They would have seen there is
a consecutive chain of responsibility which
carries out the object we have in view. I
would ask the hon. Member to read the first
Schedule before he comes here and makes a
speech which may be intended to be
helpful, but which is not helping me
at this moment and appears as
critical force in our midst which is
doing everything in its power to say that
what the Government are doing is wrong.
The Forces we have are three in numbera
firstly, the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
which is the regular police, and so the
regular and established arm of Civil authority:
Northern Government Forces. 188
Oral Answers-
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
secondly, the Special Police Force, which,
as its name implies, is an emergency Force
to meet the special circumstances of Civil
commotion which these unhappy times have
produced.
These two forces are those to which my
hon. Friend's question particularly refers,
for it is over them alone that the Government of Northern Ireland has direct authority. Incidentally, I may say with regard
to what the hon. and gallant Gentleman
said about Martial Law-
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: May I
rite to a point of order. I was not allowed
to finish my argument on that point.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The question of Martial Law has nothing
to do with us at all. The third force is
the military, and this does not come within
the scope of my hon. Friend's enquiry, inasmuch as it cannot be said to have been
set up under this Government for the purposes
of law and order. But I may be forgiven
for alluding to this third force now because
it has been of incalculable importance and
assistance to us although it is not under
our direct control. The frontiers of Northern Ireland are the frontiers of the United
Kingdom, and we are assured that any invasion of those frontiers from without will
be treated by the British Government as an
invasion of the frontiers of Great Britain,
and the whole forces of the United Kingdom
will be mobilised, if necessary, to repel such
invasion. That is what we all fully understand, and what, we are confident, will occur
if the eventuality arises.
But to return to the first two forces which
I have mentioned, namely, the regular Royal
Ulster Constabulary and the Special Constabulary. The Royal Ulster Constabulary
is organised on the model of the Royal Irish
Constabulary whose place it has taken. That
is to say-and I would refer my hon. Friend
here to the First Schedule to the Constabulary Bill which we passed last Sessionthe whole of the Constabulary Force is
divided amongst the counties and county
boroughs, with the exception of the Reserve
Force, which is kept directly under Headquarters. The responsible officer for each
county is the County Inspector, with District
Inspectors under him in command of each
district into which the county is divided.
It is all perfectly clearly set forth in the
First Schedule to the Act to which I have
referred.
I now come to the organisation of the
second Police Force, namely, the Specials,
made up of the various categories, A, B,
C, and C1, with corresponding differences in
the terms of enrolment, etc. This Force
again is divided into county divisions, at
the head of each county division being a
County Commandant. The Force is further
sub-divided into platoons under Platoon
Commanders. The duties of this Force are
to assist the Regular Constabulary in the
maintenance of law and order. At the head
of the regular Constabulary is the Inspector-
General, with General Headquarters in
Belfast, and to secure full co-ordination, the
Military Adviser to the Government, with
his headquarters also in Belfast, was appointed as temporarily in control of both
Forces of Constabulary. I might remark in
passing that, as is also the case with the
regular Constabulary, when occasion arises
extra Forces of Special Constabulary are
drafted into a county where the Forces at
the disposal of the County Commandant are
not sufficient to deal with the situation.
I will now proceed to deal with the proper
method of communication between the
various Police Forces and the general public.
If an individual wishes to furnish a report,
or to ask for special protection, his proper
procedure is to apply to the nearest police
barracks of the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
when, if necessary, the report will be
forwarded to headquarters.
In cases of complaint against the conduct
of a member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, or of the Special Constabulary,
acting as barrack reinforcements, that is,
in the capacity temporarily of regular Constabulary, report should be made to the
District-Inspector. Where complaint lies
against a member of the Special Constabulary, when that Constabulary Force is
acting in platoons on special duties, report
should be made to the County Commandant.
If the individual have any cause of doubt
as to the exact quarter, whether to the
District-Inspector or local Commandant,
to which his complaint should be addressed,
he should address himself direct to headquarters in Belfast. I think this is the
answer which the hon. Gentleman has asked
me to give and I hope that what I have said
will be satisfactory to him.
Mr. GREER rose.
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member has
already spoken.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
On a point of order, I think the hon. Member
is entitled to speak by leave of the House.
If he moves for papers, he is fully entitled
to an answer.
Mr. SPEAKER: In this case the hon. Member only asked a question, but, by leave of
the House, he can speak.

Church Temporalities Fund Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The object of this Bill is set out in
the preamble, but I think that before explaining the details of the measure, it might
be of interest to the House if I were to give
a short history of what is known as the
Church Temporalities Fund. During the
time of the establishment of the Church of
Ireland contributions out of the produce of
lands were paid to the incumbents who for
the time being were in charge of the spiritual
welfare of the various parishes in Ireland.
Up to the year, I think, 1820 many of these
contributions were paid in kind. After that
time they were commuted into equivalent
payments of money known as Tithe Rent
Charges. This was the position in the year
1869, when it was decided to disestablish the
Church. In order to carry out this diseatab
lishment effectively the British Government
found it necessary to purchase from the
Church their interests in the lands. That
interest at the time yielded an income of
£600,000 per year. The purchase money to
do this came to a total sum of £9,000,000.
This sum was handed over to the Church
authorities, and the British Government took
over the income of £600,000 per annum; but
the Government, instead of appropriating this
annual income for general purposes, kept the
Fund, as we may call it now, intact, and regarded it as a purely Irish asset. Since the
year 1869 the income of that fund has been
somewhat transformed by the fact that many
of the rent charges have been commuted into
terminable annuities, and also that by the
operation of the Land Purchase Acts, under
which rent charges on land being sold are
redeemed in lump sums, and these sums were
invested in Government securities, so that
the income of the Church Temporalities Fund
at the present moment can be said to consist
roughly of three types, that is to say the
original tithe rent charge, the terminable
annuities, and the yield of investments.
This income, however, has never been allowed
to accumulate, but has been used ever since
the year 1869 in some Irish services, and sometimes even the capital has to a certain extent
been eaten up, with the result that the income of this fund is considerably decreased.
The fund is now used for the relief of distress
in bad years and grants in aid, and so on.
It so happened that when the appointed day
under the Government of Ireland Act was
established, the charges upon the fund were
as follows:-There was a terminable charge
of £70,000 per annum to the Department of
Agriculture, to form part of the endowment
grant for agricultural purposes; this however
lapses in the year 1930. There was a charge
of £39,000 per annum in aid of teachers'
pensions; there was a yearly charge of
£41,250 for the Congested Districts Board;
there was an annual charge of £10,000 a year
for the Queen's University, and an annual
charge of £10,000 a year for the National
University, and other small charges, making
in all a total of somewhere about £180,000
a year. The income of this fund in the period
from the year 1869 to the present time came
down from £600,000 a year to about £180,000
a year. Hon. Members will perhaps remember
that Section 31 of the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, declared that this fund belonged
to the two Governments to be set up in Ireland, and that the necessary apportionment
of this fund was to be made by the Joint
Exchequer Board, and the apportioned parts
were to be disposed of according to Acts of
the appropriate Parliaments. The proposals
of the Government as to the disposal of this
fund are set down in this Bill before the
House. The Northern Government are receiving a portion of this fund by provisional
order of the Joint Exchequer Board from the
Irish Land Commission, who have hitherto
been the administrators. Briefly the intention of the Government in this Bill is that
the assets of the Northern share of the fund
shall be completely divorced from the lia
bilities, which remain after the operation of
the Government of Ireland Act. I may tell
hon. Members that the assets of this fund,
the portion at any rate which is transferable
to the Northern Parliament, represent an
income of, roughly, £34,000 per annum, and
the assessable charges on this amount are expected to be somewhere about £24,000. This
Bill has been rendered necessary owing to
the fact that this money is now being apportioned to the Northern Government, and
under the Bill the assets and income of the
fund shall belong to the Exchequer, and an
estimate of the income has already been in-
191 Church Temporalities Fund Bill—
THE SENATE.

Second Reading.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
cluded in the miscellaneous revenue in the
Budget statement of my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Finance. The liabilities of the
Fund are to be met by voted moneys, and I
am sure hon. Members will agree with me that
it is a matter of sound principle to bring
under Parliamentary control in this way the
expenditure of public money where hitherto
the real cost of services has been obscured
from Parliamentary oversight by the application of income not evidenced in the yearly
vote for those services. The real object of
this Bill is to enable the Government to carry
out this scheme by appropriating the assets
and income to the Exchequer, while payment
of liabilities is authorised out of moneys provided by Parliament.
Moved: That the Bill be now read the
Second Time."

that the Joint Exchequer Board has not yet

seems to me to be rather obscurely indicated

Consolidated Fund (Appropriation)
Bill.
Order of the day for the Second Reading
read.
The
It
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move: "That this Bill be
now read a Second time." As hon. Members
will realise, this is also a Money Bill.
object of this Bill is to authorise the issue
from the Consolidated Fund of the balance
of grants made in Committee of Supply
which have not previously been authorised
by the Consolidated Fund Bill of 1922.
will be remembered that in March a Vote
on Account was taken for £2,000,000 odd for
the services of the financial year 1922-23,
and the issue of this sum, together with a
sum of £9,618 from the Exchequer granted
in Committee of Supply for the service of
the year 1921-22, is set down in Schedule A.
The total grants made in Committee of
Supply, now that all the Estimates of the
year have been voted, amount in the aggregate to a sum of £9,346,000 odd, as shown
in Schedule A. The House of Commons, by
means of a Ways and Means Resolution,
gave legal sanction to a sum of £7,321,000
193 Confolidated Fund Bill.

3 JULY 1922
Health Insurance Bill.
odd, £2,000,000 odd having already been the
subject of a separate Ways and Means
Resolution in March of last year. This Bill
is not only a Bill to authorise the issue
from the Consolidated Fund, but, as hon.
Members will see, it is also an Appropriation
Bill. The object of Clause 3 of the Bill is
to appropriate the grants of money to the
definite objects on which Parliament by Votes
in Supply has agreed that money may properly
be spent.
A Consolidated Fund Bill has
only one function, that is to authorise the
issue from the Consolidated Fund. An
Appropriation Bill, however, has a double
function, namely that of appropriation, and
it becomes necessary to appropriate to the
various services by reference to a schedule.
This Bill in Schedule B shows the money
appropriated to the various services, not only
the grant of £7,000,000 odd, which I have
already mentioned, but also the sum of
£2,000,000 odd which was voted last year, and
the sum of £9,000 odd which was voted in
the previous year. The total grants come
to £9,000,000 odd. This figure represents the
net supply grants, but hon. Members will
be aware that when the House of Commons
passed these Estimates that House approved
of the gross expenditure shown in these
Estimates, and therefore it sanctioned not
only the sums granted for the various services as shown in Schedule B, but also the
various Appropriations in Aid which are also
shown in Schedule B. By the inclusion of
these two-the Appropriations in Aid and
the sums granted-the gross expenditure in
the Estimates was authorised by the House
of Commons. Clause 2 of the Bill refers to
the question of borrowing money. Hon.
Members will see that the Ministry of
Finance may borrow any sums, not exceeding
in the whole £4,000,000. I think this House
will agree with me that the Budget of the
Government for any year should be
arranged that it is unnecessary in normal
conditions to incur debt, but it frequently
happens that during certain periods of the
year the expenditure may exceed the revenue,
owing to the unequal rate at which the two
sides of the account mature. The flow of
revenue into the Exchequer does not necessarily keep pace with the flow of money out
of the Exchequer, and while the expenditure
for the year may balance the income there
are periods during the year when the expenditure exceeds the income. Therefore
it is necessary to borow temporarily. Consequently in Clause 2 power is taken to
borrow to meet any temporary deficiency.
The Northern Government has departed from
the usual principle which obtains in the
Imperial House of Commons. In the Imperial House of Commons it is customary
in Consolidated Fund Bills and Appropriation Bills to obtain power to borrow the
whole sum, that is to say, the entire income
SO
of the year, but the Northern Government
have not followed that example, on the
ground that there is obviously some revenue
coming in which makes it unlikely that it
will ever be necessary to borrow the total
sum in this Bill, that is to say the entire
income accruing for the year. Consequently
in this Bill they have taken power to borrow
to the extent of practically half-that is to
say £4,000,000 out of a total of £7,321,000
odd. I beg to move that the Bill be now
read a Second time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be committed to
a Committee of the whole House." (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I will ask the indulgence of the House
while I move the Second Reading of the Bill
amending the National Health Insurance
Act. When the Health Insurance Scheme
was originally put into operation it was
proposed that the Government grants should
consist of two-ninths of the cost of benefits
and administration, and it was not the
intention that Health Insurance Funds
should be subsidised to any greater extent
out of public moneys.
After the Scheme had been in operation
for some little time it was found that there
were certain expenses which would have to
be incurred and which were not originally
foreseen. In Ireland, owing to the absence
of medical benefit, some arrangements became
necessary for the provision of medical
evidence of incapacity in connection with
claims for sickness benefit, etc. The Imperial
Government undertook to shoulder this responsibility, and the cost of medical certification in Ireland has been a recurring
charge upon the State. In Northern Ireland
this expense amounts at present to about
£28,000 per annum.
Owing to the necessity which has arisen
for economy in the public service it has been
necessary to consider the attitude of the
State towards this supplementary grant. In
Great Britain, at the instance of the Geddes
Committee on national expenditure, it was
proposed that the contributions by employers
and employees under the Health Insurance
Statutes should be increased with a view
to removing the supplementary charges in
that area from the State to the Health
Insurance Funds. The approved Societies in
Great Britain were opposed to this increase
of contributions and suggested voluntarily
that they themselves should, out of their
reserves, accept the burden for a limited
period. Legislation to effect this was laid
before the Imperial Houses of Parliament.
N
195 Health Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
The matter was placed before the Consultative Committee of Approved Societies
in Northern Ireland. This Committee, recognising the desirability of keeping in dine
with the United Kingdom Scheme, advised
the Ministry that the lead of the English
Approved Societies should be followed. This
action was subsequently endorsed by the
North of Ireland Association of Approved
Societies representing the
of
interests
Societies which
Approved
operate in
Northern Ireland.
During this Committee stage of the Bill in
the Imperial Parliament some opposition was
met with from members representing the
interests of Approved Societies. As a result
of negotiations which followed a compromise
was effected in accordance with which onethird of the funds required is to be drawn
from the Central Fund and two-thirds from
the funds of Societies.
and
The Central Fund is a fund set up in the
early days of the Health Insurance Scheme
for the purpose of helping societies which
might be found to be in deficiency on valua
tion. It was formed of deductions from the
weekly contributions of employers
employees, supplemented by an Annual State
Grant. It is under the control of the Joint
Committee. After the results of the valua
tion at the 31st December, 1918, were made
known, and it was found that there was no
appreciable call on the Central Fund, the
transfer of moneys to it out of contributions
and the Exchequer was discontinued.
The result in the end of the arrangement
is that the cost of certification will be met
as follows:-Two-ninths, being the usual
State Grant towards the cost of benefits and
administration, will be provided by the
Exchequer. The remaining seven-ninths will
be divided between the funds of Societies
and the Central Fund, two-thirds being
borne by the former and one-third by the
latter. The following table summarises the
arrangement:-
...
Exchequer
Approved Societies, 2/3rds of
...
2/9ths
7/9ths
7/9ths
Central Fund, 1/3rd of
Owing to the fact that if the moneys were
allowed to remain in the Societies' Funds,
they would not be spent on additional benefits until after the next valuation, and that
the State grant would not thus be payable
until a later date, it is necessary to take the
State grant as well as the Societies' share
out of the Societies' funds in the first instance. When, however, the State grant
comes to be paid to Societies interest will
be reckoned on the sums borrowed and State
grant will be allowed on such interest.
The valuations of Approved Society assets
and liabilities carried out by the Government Actuary, as at the 31st December, 1918.
showed that the Approved Societies are in
a better financial position than was anticipated when the Imperial Government agreed
to the making of supplementary grants.
Considerable surpluses have been revealed
in the case of most societies. These surpluses are believed to be in a large measure
due to influences arising out of the war and
therefore to be of an exceptional nature.
A proportion only of the surpluses has
been certified by the valuers as disposable
and the remainder has been carried forward
as a reserve against losses of an exceptional
kind which may follow as an after consequence of the war. The present trade depression with its high rate of unemployment,
shortage of contributions, and possibly lowered vitality on the part of the workers may
occasion serious encroachments on the funds
of societies. The Government Actuary
advises, however, that the Societies have
sufficient reserves to meet the charge for a
limited period without in any way affecting
the schemes of additional benefits which
most Approved Societies have adopted as a
result of the valuation reports.
Clause 1 (1) of the Bill proposes that the
cost of Medical Certification should fall
partly on Approved Societies and partly on
the Central Fund as explained above. This
will relieve the taxpayer for the present to
the extent of about £28,000 per annum.
Clause 1 (2) makes provision by which
Societies will not lose the State grant which
they would have earned if the moneys which
are now proposed to be taken out of their
funds had been retained by them and expended on additional benefits after the next
valuation. It is proposed that the moneys to
be contributed by the Societies to the cost of
Medical Certification should be regarded for
the purpose of the two-ninths State grant,
as continuing in their funds, and bearing
interest at the usual rate, and available for
expenditure on additional benefits in the
ordinary way.
Clause 1 (3) gives effect to the proposals
from the 1st April, 1922, the beginning of the
financial year, and limits its operation to the
31st December, 1923. The latter date is that
on which the arrangement with the Doctors
in Great Britain terminates. In Northern
Ireland it is desired to work on parallel lines
with Great Britain.
In Clause 2 of the Bill opportunity has
been taken to deal with a matter which has
arisen out of the present conditions of severe
unemployment. Ordinarily in Ireland an
insured person who has less than 17 weekly
contributions to his credit in any contribu-
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading.
3 JULY 1922
benefits
This,
tion year is not entitled to any
during the succeeding benefit year.
when insured persons are unemployed
through no fault of their own for considerable periods, constitutes a serious hardship
which it is desired to avoid.
Last year
Societies regarded all members who were
unemployed for prolonged periods, owing to
trade depression, as having 26 contributions
to their credit, and paid them benefits
accordingly. The cost of this was met
out of the Societies' own funds. It is
desired to treat insured persons in a
similar manner during the present year,
but owing to the expenses which the
Societies are undertaking under Clause 1
it is thought that they should not be asked to
continue to bear the expenses involved.
Certain moneys are available in the Health
Insurance Fund which it is proposed to apply
for this purpose. These moneys are contained
in the Unclaimed Stamps Account and represent the difference between receipts from the
sale of stamps and the contributions represented by cards surrendered by insured
persons. Clause 2 of the Bill gives power to
effect this and no expense falls upon the
State. I beg to move.
Mr. GREER: I rise with very great diffidence indeed to say a word on this Bill. It
seems to me the most difficult bit of reading
I have undertaken for some time. I confess
I do not understand all the ramifications
of it. I must thank the Noble Marquess for
the help that he has given me to understand
a good deal of it. A large fund, the Central
Fund, has been accumulated. The Government is going to take money from that Fund
and apply it to reduce the contribution
that it would otherwise have to pay into the
Fund. It proposes to pay interest on the
money, 80 that So far as that is
concerned, it is a quite reasonable
and proper Bill. This Central Fund
of course, is the Fund raised, not only in
Ireland but in England and Scotland, and is
administered by this Joint Insurance Committee, and I presume that any action that
we take will be followed or preceded by similar action on the part of the Legislature in
England so that we shall all be dealing with
it in the same way. I am quite satisfied that
that is right. One substantial fact that
merges from this Bill to my mind is that
huge sums have been accumulated, not only
by the Central Fund, but by the various
approved societies. Some of these Societies
have £50,000, £60,000 and £100,000 accumulated in a short space of time. The Act of
Parliament was passed in 1911, I believe,
under which these payments can be made.
When that Act was passed the contributions
to be paid by the employers and the employed
were settled at certain figures, and I suppose
they were made purposely high so that there
would be no deficiency-so that there might
be a surplus instead of a deficiency in the
accounts of these societies. But the result
has shown that these sums were very much
over calculated, and for the last few years
when it has been so very difficult for employers and employed to pay these sums they
have had to contribute to swell these
surpluses which you have at the present time.
It seems to me a very great pity that when
the Minister of Labour in approaching
this House with a Bill to deal with
section of the question, he should
not have gone on to deal with the
matter in its entirety. I am very sorry
indeed that he has not seen his way
to introduce a clause to provide that it shall
be referred to the actuaries to deal with the
figures in the light of what has happened
since the year 1911 with a view to decreasing
the contributions made by the employers and
employed during that period. I do not think
I have any other observations to make.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Local Government (Northern Ireland)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I must further ask the indulgence of the
House for moving the Second Reading of
another Bill which has come up to us from
another place and in moving it I think it
well to lay particular stress on the reasons
which have moved the Government of
Northern Ireland to introduce such a Bill
as this at this early stage of their adminis
tration. Hon. Members will no doubt realise
that the Government anticipate that hostile
criticism will be levelled against their action
in repealing a measure so recently put in
force by the Imperial Government as 1919,
but we are satisfied that in asking this House
to accept the Bill for repealing the principle
of Proportional Representation in Local Government elections we are giving effect to a
long-desired wish on the part of the people of
Northern Ireland, a wish that has been
forcibly expressed to us and represented to us
by practically every organization in the Sixcounty Area.
The Local Government Act of 1919 which
applies the principle of Proportional Representation to Ireland was not asked for by us;
it was not desired by us; it was not required
by us. It was forced on us to meet circumstances and conditions that obtained elsewhere, and its results, as far as Northern
Ireland is concerned were not satisfactory
to any section of the community. The
people of Great Britain have steadfastly set
their faces against the principle of Proportional Representation, and although some
enthusiasts still cry for it, it was clearly
manifest from the discussion which took
Local Government Bill-
THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
place in the Imperial House of Commons
some weeks ago that what was considered
good enough for Ireland was not considered
good enough for Great Britain.
Hon. Members
are familiar with the
principle at least I take it that they are
familiar with the principle of Proportional
Representation They are, however, familiar
with the method of working and the results,
and perhaps I should weary them by recount-
But I
ing its merits or rather demerits.
think it necessary for me to explain why we
consider it desirable to revert to the old
electoral areas and the old block system of
voting. In the first place the new system of
As a
elections proved exceedingly costly.
case in point, I may take the cost of conducting municipal elections in the City of Belfast. Under the new system the bill that
the ratepayers had to meet for their munici
pal elections in 1919 amounted to twice as
much as compared with the expenses incurred under the old arrangement. A still
greater difficulty on the ground of expense
occurs in the case of the small towns like
and
Aughnacloy,
Antrim,
Downpatrick,
several small towns of that description.
As hon. Members are aware, these small communities cannot rate themselves for Local
Government purposes more than one shilling
in the pound. This leaves a very small
margin for incurring expenses
description.
the
of this
adminion the
We found on taking over
the Local
stration of local services from
Government Board that several of these
small communities were nearly bankrupt
after the last elections owing to the high
cost of conducting their elections
Proportional Representation principle. Apart
from that actual cost there is the great
trouble and complication which such a highly
involved system of election brings with it.
We have in Northern Ireland some of the
ablest local officials in the United Kingdom,
and I venture to say that even they found
unnecessary difficulties in dealing with the
system.
So complicated was the system
that the Local Government Board was compelled to detail a considerable portion of
their staff to go round the country and
lecture the local officials on the subject and
conduct model elections. Local Government
administration at the best of times is not an
easy matter, but it should be the object of the
Legislature to make it as smooth and easyrunning as possible, and we feel sure that
every local official interested or concerned in
elections will welcome our proposals to return to the old and comparatively easy
Now let me
method of straight voting.
take the case of the elector. If the local
officials and local election agents found some
difficulties in administering the Act and
Orders relating to Proportional Representation what was the case of the unfortunate
elector who, being accustomed for years to
place a cross against the name of his favoured
candidate, now found himself compelled to
pick out three or four from a group and give
them consecutive numbers? The people of
Northern Ireland certainly can hold their
own with the rest of the United Kingdom
for intelligence, but even in Northern Ireland there are some people whose habits
are difficult to change and who cannot understand why they should not put a cross on
the voting paper against their nominee.
When I looked up the reports of the Dublin
Local Government Board on the municipal
elections held in January, 1920, I found
that there were nearly 10,000 spoiled ballot
papers in connection with elections in county
boroughs, urban districts, and towns. There
is no record of the number of spoiled papers
for county councils and rural district
Councils, but
but it would be reasonable to
assume the number was a very much higher
one. Another point in connection with Proportional Representation, although not of
importance to the community as a whole,
which rather detracts in a sense from the
interest of the candidates and of the voters
is the delay in counting the votes. I am sure
hon. Members in this House have very vivid
recollections of waiting for the declaration of
the poll on the night of the election. All the
pleasurable excitement of those days was
swept away by the Act of 1919, which entailed three, or four, or five days' count, and
meant a certain amount of staleness in
interest.
Now I come to another point which is, perhaps, the most important of all, and on
Under the old
which we lay most stress.
system of elections a Rural District Councillor was elected for a District Electoral
Division a County Councillor was elected
for a County Electoral Division,
was elected for

and
or
a Ward. The man elected was generally
a prominent person in the Division
Ward, as the case may be, well-known to
the electors of that ward, and taking a deep
interest in the affairs of the area both before
and after his election What was the result
of the new system? The old electoral areas,
that is, the Divisions and the Wards, were
amalgamated, and new fictitious areas were
created areas that were of no local interest
Candidates for election
or significance.
found themselves compelled to go into districts
in which they were not known, and for
which they had no concern, and there endeavour to secure a certain amount of
support. Under the group system of election
no one person considered himself as representing any particular area or, indeed, any
3 JULY 1922
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading.

new scales of election expenses, and for
matters incidental to Proportional Representation. Section 10 of the Act enabled the Local
Government Board to make certain Orders
prescribing the method of voting and transferring votes under the Proportional
Representation Scheme, while Section II of
the Act of 1919, repcaled certain provisions
in the former Acts relating to local elections.
Sub-Section 3 of Clause 1 of the Bill must be
read with Part 1 of the Schedule to the Bill,
and these two together are substituted for
Sub-sections 2 and 3 of Section 4 of the Act
of 1919. The effect of this substitution is to
provide an improved method of electing
aldermen and councillors in County
Boroughs, which is briefly as follows:-
particular interest. Under the old regime if
were required in a particular
a pump
Electoral Divison, a representative of that
Division brought the case before the Council
concerned, and fought it on behalf of his
constituents. Now, when a pump is required
the ratepayers have to go to three or four
members of the council and endeavour to
interest them in the proposal. The whole
essence of Local Government administration
is contained in the word "local," and in order
that it may be satisfactory to all interests
and to all sections of the community, the
areas of representation should not be unduly
large. For poor relief purposes your local
Guardian should know the circumstances of
every person in his area who applies for relief; for road purposes your county councillor should know every road in his County
Electoral Division, and unless there is that
local and specialised interest on the part of
the councillor and the ratepayers of the area
he represents, your system is not working as
it should. I submit to this House that in our
system of local elections we should aim at certain essential features, and of these the most
important are simplicity, economy, and due
representation of local interests (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) These features were more
apparent in our former system of elections
than they are under the new system introduced by the Act of 1919, and it is with confidence, therefore, that I ask this House
to pass this Bill which has for its object
the abolition of the system of Proportional
Representation in so far as Local Government Elections are concerned in Northern
Ireland. Having briefly indicated in general
terms what our proposals are and our reasons
for them I would now ask hon. Members to
bear with me while I explain more or less
generally the salient features of the Bill.
The whole essence of the Bill is contained
in Sub-section 1 of Clause 1, which provides
that on and after the ordinary day of election
in the year 1923 Local Government elections
shall be conducted by the method of voting
which was in force prior to 1919, and that the
members of the various classes of local
authorities shall be elected for Electoral
Divisions, Wards, Urban Districts and
Towns instead of for the Local Electoral Areas
constituted under the Act of 1919, in other
words under this Bill we shall revert to the
old block system of elections and to the
old electoral units; and the new electoral
areas promulgated by the Local Government
Board, Dublin, in their various orders shall
no longer be applicable for Local Government
elections. Sub-Section 2 of the first Clause
is merely consequential and affects the repeal of the provisions of the Act of 1919,
which are to be superseded. Section 1 of
the Act provided for Proportional Representation. It provided for the constituelectoral areas which
tion of the new
abolishing, provided
are now
we
for
Instead of having an election for aldermen
separate from that for councillors we shall
have one election in each ward for councillors
only. The councillor or councillors, as the
case may be, who receive the highest
number of votes in each ward will be alderman or aldermen, for example, supposing
that one alderman and three councillors are
allotted to a particular ward, there shall be
an election for four councillors, and the
councillor who is clected by the greatest
number of votes will be the alderman for the
ward. Similarly, if two aldermen and three
councillors are allotted to a particular ward,
there shall be an election for five councillors,
and the two councillors who obtain the
highest number of votes will be the two
aldermen for the ward, the remaining three
being councillors. The object of this Clause
and Schedule was to introduce into Northern
Ireland a simplified mode of electing aldermen and councillors in County Boroughs, but
it was found during the Second reading of
the Bill in another place that the Belfast
Corporation wished to go back completely to
the old system of election of aldermen.
and Councillors that prevailed in Ireland
prior to the Proportional Representation Act
of 1919, and the Government agceded to
their request and introduced a new Clause
in the Bill giving effect to it as far
Belfast is concerned.
as
Sub-Section 4 of Clause 1 must be taken
with Part 2 of the Schedule, and these two
read together make provision for re-enacting
the necessary provisions of the Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1898, which were
repealed by the Act of 1919. These provisions
generally have relation to the election of a
County Councillor for each County Electoral
Division, for the election of a Rural District
Councillor for each District Electoral Division
and for the assignment of Guardians to
Urban Districts.
In this connection I think it necessary to
lay stress on the fact that we are retaining
203 Local Government Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
one important feature contained in the Act
of 1919, and that is in connection with the
reduced number of Rural District Councillors.
In the Local Government Act of 1898 each
District Electoral Division sent forward 2
Rural District Councillors and the result was
in some cases, huge unweildy Councils of 60
or 70. The Act of 1919 reduced the total
number of each Council by one-half, and that
is practically the only feature of that Act
which we consider is very valuable. In order
to preserve this feature we are providing in
the Bill that there shall be only one Rural
District Councillor for each District Electoral
Division.
Another feature of the Act of 1919 which
we are retaining is the system of triennial
elections in municipal areas, but the annual
elections will be held in Belfast owing to
special representations by the Corporation.
Hon. Members will recollect that under the
laws relating to municipal towns and boroughs
there were annual elections in many of the
urban areas in Ireland, one-third of the local
authority retiring each year. This arrangement may have possessed certain advantages
in that it maintained a degree of continuity
in administration, but, on the other hand,
it involved considerable disadvantages both
on the ground of expense and of uncertainty.
The constitution of the local authority changed
each year, and, on the whole, the Government of Northern Ireland consider that
the provisions which have been applied to
County and Rural District Councils should
also obtain in the case of municipal authorities, and that the municipal elections should
be held triennially also.
Clause 2 of the Bill was inserted during
Committee Stage in the other House owing to
the representations which have been made
in regard to irregularities at elections. It
has been strongly urged that the community
has been put to considerable expense and that
bona-fide candidates had been put to considerable inconvenience owing to bogus
nomination papers being sent in to Returning Officers. The Government has recognized
the force of these representations and has
inserted in this clause provisions requiring
consent of candidates to nomination for office,
also a provision requiring a deposit of £10
in the case of subordinate authorities, and
£25 for election for County and County Borough Councillors. These deposits are repayable to the candidates on election, or for
any other satisfactory cause as determined
by regulation of the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Clause 3 empowers the Ministry of Home
Affairs on the application of any municipal
Authority to apply by Order provisions enabling elections to be held annually instead
of triennially.
Clause 4 is a special Clause framed to meet
circumstances that obtain in the City of
Belfast.
I have alluded to this matter already in
regard to the holding of annual elections,
and I may add to that now, by indicating
that this Clause reimposes the conditions
that obtained prior to 1919 in regard to separate election for Aldermen and Councillors.
The Corporation of Belfast have represented to the Government with considerable
force the expediency and desirability of having annual elections in order to preserve
continuity of administration, and also of
having separate elections for Aldermen, in
order to preserve the dignity of that office.
The Bill specially provides for Belfast in
this respect.
Sub-section 2 of this Clause is consequential
on Sub-section 1, and has been inserted at
the request of the Belfast Water Commissioners in order to co-ordinate their
Statutes with the present Bill.
Clause 5 introduces a new principle in
Local Government administration in requiring all members and officers of Local
Authorities in Northern Ireland to make a
declaration of allegiance to His Majesty the
King and the Government of Northern
Ireland.
Clause 6 is a necessary Clause, empowering
the Ministry of Home Affairs to make such
Orders as may be necessary for giving full
effect to the Act.
I may add that since the Bill was taken
in the Lower House the Government have
had under consideration the difficulties which
will be encountered by members of the Constabulary in regard to their votes, owing to
residence in barracks, and I propose, with
the leave of the House, to bring forward a
short Clause during Committee stage which
will meet the situation. I beg to move.
Mr. BARBOUR: It is with no small measure of diffidence that I venture to rise
to say a word on behalf of principles which
we seem about to abrogate now. I think it
is always well to consider what one has when
considering what one might have, and it
seems to me that I almost hear the echo ringing in this room of the praise of the work
that has been done by the present Government. It occurs to me that when we are
listening to that echo we should do well
to remember that the present Government
has been given to us on the basis of Proportional Representation. I also think that
if we consider our Municipal and our Urban
Government, and even our County Council
and Rural Council work, especially during
the trying period we are going through now,
we will agree that it has reached a very high
standard of efficiency, and we must remember
3 JULY 1922
Local Government Bill-

Second Reading.
duced is one which will be received with
acclamation by the very great majority of
the population of our area. In fact, the only
point on which I think we ought for a
moment to concentrate examination is consideration of whether the abolition of this
system of Proportional Representation with
transferable voting is absolutely fair to the
minority. In studying the return of elections
held under this system, I must confess I
cannot detect anything in the results which
has in fact pointed to special success on the
part of the minority, but if what I may call
the permanent and really large minority in
the Six Counties is uneasy over this matter,
it seems to me that those of us who are
prepared to abolish this system
that those Councils have been put in the
position they occupy on the basis of Proportional Representation. I have seen it
stated in the Press that the voting on this
basis is akin to a Chinese puzzle. I can only
read into that the fact that the intelligence
of the North of Ireland elector is quite capable of solving the Chinese puzzle, whatever
it may be, and in finding in Proportional Representation a very great means for expressing his views of any candidates that come
forward. Then again I have taken a small
part in elections, and I remember when I
was standing in a single member constituency
for the County Council there were occasions
while I was addressing audiences on whatever policy might be of interest at the momment when there was a certain amount of
anxiety as to whether we would be able
to get out of the building with that measure
of comfort and security we would all like
to enjoy. I think that to a very great degree
those embarrassing accompaniments of elections are done away with where one is fighting with a team instead of as an individual.
We must remember that in the history of our
Northern elections there have been occasions
when there have been very regrettable incidents when any particular causes have been
urged by an individual rather than by a team.
I have yet to learn that any member of a
team who was urging any particular cause
under this system of Proportional Representation has met with any unpleasantness
or has ever been put in any dangerous
position, and I can remember times when
under the other method of voting these
occasions did occur. However, I regret to say
I feel I have just listened to the funeral
obsequies of a principle that I must say
appealed to me a great deal.
In the Bill there are two things I can subscribe to most cordially. One is the Declaration of Allegiance. Of course, most of the
people who stood for the different bodies
mentioned in this Bill in former times took
that as understood in coming forward as
candidates, but it is no harm to have it down
in black and white. I also think it is a very
good thing that a deposit is asked for before a
candidature is properly in order. There have
been occasions in a County Council I know
very well when on the day of an election one
gentleman has been put down for seven or
eight different constituencies, and in two or
three of those an election was forced, and all
the expense incurred where, without his
candidature no election would have taken
place. With those two points I am in cordial
agreement, but I must say I am in a general
state of mourning because I believe I have
just listened to the death knell of a principle
which appeals to me.
Mr. LESLIE: There can be no doubt that
the Bill which the Noble Marquess has introare
that
fairly entitled to ask why it is
one of the Vice-Presidents of the Proportional Representation Society the hon.
Gentleman, who represents West Belfast at

well as others we might mention, do not take

com-
It is not at all clear that the system we are
abolishing has any advantages which outweigh
the disadvantages the Noble Marquess
alluded to. He took exactly the three points
that one would expect. He took first the
question of expense. I heard bitter
plaints in my own locality from Returning
Officers on that matter, and I heard still
more bitter complaints when the Bills were
presented. Again he took the question of
canvassing. I got over that difficulty when
I stood as a County Councillor by not canvassing at all. It was quite impossible
account of the size of the constituency. The constituency of which I am
one of four Members stretches from Red Bay
in County Antrim to a point three or four
miles from Kilrea on the river bank,
and includes all Co. Antrim on the North.
on
It is, for example, an absolutely unworkable
no reason to
constituency, and I have
suppose that it is any worse than many
others. The new method of election will, I
think, be found to work for good in one way
which the noble Marquess did not mention.
I think you will find that in County Council
matters the number of your contests will bo
reduced. It is on the whole probably a good
thing that county councillors and rural district councillors also, who have spent some
years in office, and who have given up a
certain amount of time in attending to their
duties, and have acquired the necessary
knowledge of their business, should not be
Local Government Bill-

THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
disturbed or threatened to be disturbed on

The question of the deposit appeals to me,
like my hon. Friend on my left (Mr. Barbour),
very strongly. I distinctly remember the
particular case to which he alluded, and I
also remember that there was a case in the
city of Belfast where one gentleman got himself nominated for four wards, and polled
rather less than thirty votes in the whole
four. That is a state of things which is intolerable. The Oath of Allegiance, or rather
the requirement of that oath, will be received
with great satisfaction everywhere. It fell
to my lot last week to be present at a rather
large meeting of people interested in matters
of this kind. That was the point received
with the greatest acclamation, and it was
more or less borne in on me that whatever
other Clauses of the Bill I might want to
circumstances should I
oppose, under no
operate in opposition to that Clause. I have
no intention of opposing it. On the contrary,
I hope the precedent set in this matter will
be followed by the Northern Parliament in
the cases of all people in receipt of public
It is
money-school teachers and others.
very much to be desired that there should be
no person in Northern Ireland in receipt of
public money who is not prepared to take the
Oath of Allegiance to His Majesty and the
Constitutional Government.
That is
And now that I have spoken entirely
in favour of the Bill, I have one very
mild suggestion to make dealing with the
return of the deposit. I venture to think
that the Government has tied up the Ministry
a little too tightly. I think in the second
section they provide for the power of the
Ministry to prescribe the conditions upon
which the deposit made by an unsuccessful
candidate may be returned, in the event of
his having received a certain proportion of
the votes. I imagine, in fact I am certain,
that the intention is to apply the principles
expressed in Section 27 of the Representation of the People Act of 1918.
the Section which provides as a condition
that a man shall have polled one-eighth of
the votes cast at that election, or, in the
case of a constituency returning several
members, he shall have polled one-eighth of
the number polled divided by the number of
seats there are to be filled. But the Act of
1918 also contained a provision under which
a candidate, who has the misfortune to die
between nomination and the day of the poll,
may have the satisfaction, if he is aware of it,
of knowing that his deposit will be returned
to his personal representatives. I venture to
think that the Ministry ought not to be
limited in their power to prescribe conditions
upon which the deposit made by an unsuccessful candidate may be returned by the condition that it must necessarily be paid upon
his receiving such proportion of the votes. I
am inclined to hope that the Government
may see their way to introduce a slight modification of that particular Clause at a later
stage. I am quite content to leave it in the
hands of the Noble Marquess, and I do that
with every confidence. In any case it is an
extremely small point dealing with a possible
difficulty which may arise in the application of
what I believe to be a thoroughly excellent
Bill.
Mr. GLENDINNING: While heartily
approving of this Bill in every other respect
I would make a very strong appeal to
the Government to place the City of Derry
in
exactly
the same position as the
Belfast.
city of
I believe it is the
only other borough in the Six Counties,
and the omission of the word "borough"
from the subsequent clauses would
eliminate the difficulty. Every argument which the Corporation of Belfast
could have put before the Government is
equally applicable to Derry. Continuity of
service is a very strong argument. Expense is another, because our experience
shows that the expense of elections occurring
every year is lower than when the members all go out at the same time. Further
the dignity of having elected Aldermen is
a very important matter, and I am sure if
that system is done away with there will
be the greatest possible discontent in the
city of Derry. I greatly regret that I had
not an opportuniy of putting these questions, and others which are considered very
important, before the Government before
they drafted this Bill, otherwise I should
have made a very strong effort to hove
my suggestions carried out. If it is not too
late I would make an exceedingly strong appeal to the Government to place the city of
Londonderry on exactly the same basis in the
Bill as the city of Belfast. With all the other
Clauses of the Bill I am heartily in agreement.
Mr. DUFFIN: I have no hesitation in giving a general assent to the principles of this
Bill, which has been brought in by the Government, and which has been so admirably
expounded by the Noble Marquess. I share
with the hon. Member (Mr Barbour) a certain
amount of regret at the abandonment of the
principle of Proportional Representation, even
in the matter of elections for Local Government, because it always appealed to me as
being machinery-not perhaps altogether the
best or most effective for securing a certain
amount of representation for minorities.
That, however, is an advantage which I
thing is entirely out-weighed by the disadvantages, which have been exceedingly
clearly put before us by the Noble Marquess.
One of the main disadvantages, it seems to
209 Local Government Bill-

3 JULY 1922
Second Reading.
me, is that it involves the constitution of
very wide electoral areas, and that deprives
the individual candidate of the advantage for
himself and the electors of his known character and ability and qualifications in the
area in which he lives. This consideration got
swamped in the wider area in which he was
not known.
There are just one or two points which I
would like to consider in detail. One is under
Section 6, under which, authority is given to
the Minister of Home Affairs to alter the
constitution of electoral areas and boundaries.
It is rather a ticklish question at the present
time in a general way, and it seems to me
that it is not desirable for Parliament to give
such a very wide discretion and authority to
any individual Minister. I would suggest to
the Government that it would be desirable
to introduce a Clause providing that such
Orders, which may be of very, very great
importance, and alter the whole complexion
of a constituency, should be laid before
Parliament during the Session, and should be
I do not propose to
open to review here.
move an amendment to that effect, but I
would ask that it be considered.
I
One other point which seems to me to be
worthy of further consideration is the Declaration which is required to be made.
quite agree that from any person going forward for any form of Local Government, or
taking any office under any local body, you
are entitled to call for an explicit declaration that he is a loyal subject of the Crown.
Further, we know that there is a very large
section of the public within our area who
our Government
deny the authority of
altogether and refuse to yield obedience to
it. It is certainly desirable that in some
form we should require them to acknowledge
the authority of the Constitutional Government and consider themselves bound in duty
to obey it. I think that is not, perhaps,
carried out by the form of declaration in the
Bill, which, I think, is not worded in
exactly the most appropriate manner.
loyalty to the Crown, of course, there can be
no exception, but I think the subsequent
phraseology might give rise to a certain
amount of misconception. I would suggest
that it might be reconsidered to see whether
such a declaration should not really imply
acknowledgment of the authority of the
law
by
Government as
Constitutional
established. I suggest that for the consideration of the Government.
To
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I should like to thank the House for the
reception they have given to the Bill which
I have had the honour of proposing to-day. I
do not think I am entitled to further trespass
upon the indulgence of hon. Members, and I
will, therefore, very briefly reply to the
suggestions which have been made. The hon.
Member (Mr. Leslie) has raised a point of
substance, and it is a point which, I can
assure him, will be gone into between this
and the Committee stage. The hon. Senator
from Derry (Mr. Glendinning)), whose presence
we welcome in this House (hon. Members:
Hear, hear) and I am extremely gratified that
he should have taken an early opportunity
of entering into a debate, and I sincerely
hope that we shall often hear his speeches,
and that he will give us that assistance
which he is so eminently capable of giving to
this House-raised a point with regard to
which I would draw his attention to Clause 3
of the Bill. I think he will find that in the
event of the city of Londonderry requiring
an alteration they have full power to do so
under the Bill as it stands at the present
moment. The hon. Member (Mr. Duffin) has
raised a point which, I regret to say, we cannot accept, but I can assure him on behalf
of the Government that no alterations other
than those which are necessary will be made,
and I think perhaps the hon. Gentleman will
find in that the assurance he requires.
Mr. DUFFIN: I referred to the clause in
the Bill which gives power to the Minister
of Home Affairs as an individual to make
these alterations-extensive alterations they
may be from time to time. We may not
always have the same Minister or the same
Cabinet. My point was that Parliament
should not part so completely and entirely
with its own power of superintending such
very extensive changes as may be made.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the hon. Gentleman need not have
these apprehensions, but, if he has them, he
is quite entitled and is quite right to put
them forward, and I assure him that between
now and the Committee stage I will gladly
go into that point, and perhaps will be able
to give him a better assurance to-morrow than
I do not feel that
I am able to do to-day.
on these points I need say anything further.
We had two interesting speeches, one of
which was particularly devoted to the principle of Proportional Representation, and I
regret that we do not seem to be carrying
the hon. Gentleman (Mr. Barbour) with us.
Local Government Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
I am speaking for the Government on this
occasion-a privilege of which I am naturally
very proud-but I would venture to say that
this is a matter which I have considered very
closely on other occasions, and I entirely fail
to see that minorities did receive the protection that they believed they received under
the specious arguments which those who
tried to convert me invariably put forward.
I cannot help feeling that the disadvantages
which the system of Proportional Representation carries with it are of such magnitude
that we are taking a wise action in repealing
a statute which certainly has not increased
in popularity in the Six Counties. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) I cannot feel it is
a system of election which is destined to hold
the future either in this country or in the
United Kingdom.
Moved: "That the Bill be now read a
Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn."
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly
at twenty-five minutes
before Six o'c¹´ck.

Local Government (Northern Ireland)
Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill. (Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.
Ordered That Clause 1 (Method of voting
and electoral divisions, etc.) stand part of
the Bill.
Clause 2 (Consent and Deposit on nomination of candidates).
(1) Each person who shall be nominated
for election as a member of a local authority shall signify in writing to the
returning officer, not later than the date
prescribed for the receipt of nomination
papers, his consent to such nomination, and
shall deposit, or cause to be deposited, with
the returning officer, in the case of an
election of members of a county or borough
council, a sum of twenty-five pounds, and
in case of an election of members of an
urban or rural district council or Board of
Guardians or of the Commissioners of a
town, the sum of ten pounds; and no person shall be deemed to have been duly
nominated unless his consent to the nomination has been signified, and the amount
of the deposit provided by this section has
been paid to the returning officer.
(2) The returning officer shall immediately after the day of election repay to each
person who has either withdrawn his candidature within the time prescribed for
that purpose, or who is elected as a member
of the local authority and has made the declarations required of such member, the
amount of his deposit.
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE.
(3) All sums received by the returning
officer from candidates in respect of deposits, and not returned by him, shall be
applied by him towards the general expenses of the election.
(4) The power of the Ministry of Home
Affairs for Northern Ireland (in this Act
referred to as "the Ministry") to frame
rules for the conduct of elections of members of a local authority shall include
power :-
(a) to prescribe conditions upon which
deposits made by an unsuccessful
candidate may be returned, in the
event of his having received such
proportion of the votes as may be
prescribed; and
(b) to prescribe the manner in which
consents may be given by candidates
to their nomination as members of
a local authority.
Moved: Line 13, leave out from "Council"
to the second "of" in line 15, and insert "or."
(Mr. Woods.)
Mr. WOODS: I wish to move the amendment standing in my name on the Paper with
regard to the £25 deposit. I do so because
I think £25 is too heavy a penalty to put
on any candidate seeking honours for the first
time on any public Board. We have quite a
number of Labour representatives on all the
public Boards in Belfast, and also on Urban
and Rural District Councils. Surely the
Government do not intend to dispense with
the services of those men, because I am
afraid that if the £25 deposit is insisted upon
the majority of them will not be able to seek
re-election. I am one of those who think
that Labour should have some representation
on the public Boards, and that we have men
who I, and the majority of the people of
Belfast, consider are doing their very best
to carry out the wishes of their constituents
in the interests of the Boards they belong
to. I consider that a £25 deposit would keep
those men off public Boards. I believe that
some deposit should be demanded in order
to prevent candidates coming forward for the
sole purpose of forcing an election. I think
a deposit of £10, which was the original proposal of the Government, would meet the
case, and if a distinction must be made
between the County Councils and the County
Borough Councils and Urban or District
Councils the deposit could be £10 and £5.
I hope the Government will see their way
to accept this amendment, because I believe
they will be taking a step in the right direction for the future democratic government
of Northern Ireland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
My hon. Friend seems to fear that the
amount named in the Bill will be an
effective deterrent to the desire on the
part of Labour candidates to take part
in public affairs. If that was to be co
I should be the last person to propose
anything which might have the effect of deterring those Labour and other representatives from contesting elections. But my hon.
Friend appears to me to dwell entirely on
the penalties, and, after all, people only suffer
penalties if they have committed some wrong.
In this Bill the non-return of the deposit
of £25 is in consequence of some very obvious
condition not having been fulfilled, or a
candidature having been brought forward in
a vexatious spirit. The effect of my hon.
Friend's amendment would be to impose a
flat rate deposit of £10 for all elections.
I may tell the hon. Member, if he is not
already aware, that the Government originally contemplated that there should be a
deposit of £5 for elections in the case of
subordinate authorities, and £10 for the
higher authorities-County Councils and
County Borough Councils. During the debate
in the other House it was made very clear
to the Government that the irregularities
which the Government fear, and which we
all fear, could not be prevented unless increased deposits were imposed. Therefore
use
the Government agreed to fix the deposits at
£10 and £25 respectively. I think my hon.
Friend will see the point I am trying to
make that the deposit is for the purpose of
checking irregularities, and it is no
having a deposit which will not check those
irregularities. The result which we wish to
bring about, namely the removal of these
irregularities, will not be achieved unless the
deposit is fixed at £25.
My hon. Friend seems to suggest that there
is going to be a very good chance for every
one to lose that £25. That is not so. I am
optimistic enough to think that the great
majority of candidates will have that £25 refunded, and only those who have come forward for purposes other than that they
should be elected-my hon. Friend knows
what I mean-for vexatious purposes, for
creating a three-cornered contest, those persons run the risk of having their deposits
forfeited. The matter is to a large extent in
the discretion of the Home Minister, and we
think that we can rely on him to see that any
bona-fide candidate, any candidate who ought
to be elected to one of those bodies, will not
be penalised by losing the amount of his deposit which he is called upon to put down.
I think my hon. Friend will realise also that
any alterations would probably mean the
wrecking of the Bill, as no doubt the House
of Commons would refuse to accept this
Amendment. It has been carefully considered, and if that is so the Bill could not
be got through in time. I hope the explanation will leave the hon. Member's mind
4 JULY 1922

except during the time spent at the Council
meeting. Like myself they are indebted to
the employers to enable them to attend the
Council meetings. These people could not
have the money. I do not wish to labour the
point. I do hope that the Committee will consider this matter carefully and vote in
favour of the deletion of the £25.
think £10 would be quite satisfactory.
easier than it was before. If he asserts that
the fear of losing the amount of the deposit
is a deterrent to Labour Members coming forward, that is a matter that I want to clear
his mind about. I cannot see the particular
occasion arising where a bona-fide Labour
candidate is likely to lose the deposit which
he puts down.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: 1 hope that
the Committee will pass this suggestion
1 am very
which Mr. Woods has made.
much interested in it. The Noble Lord has
pointed out that the £25 deposit is only to
