It is a question of vital importance to them,
because if they do not get a price that enables them to live in the country they will
have to go to some other country. Therefore,
I hope the noble Marquess and our Government will use all the influence they possess
with the Government, which we have every
right to believe is sympathetic with the
North of Ireland, in order to carry out the
Bill on the lines proposed by my hon. Friend
(Mr. Montgomery). The way we were
treated over the cattle question only shows
how absolutely essential it is for us to put
all the force we can behind any effort to
get this question settled or we may find that
we are left out in the cold again.
Mr. DICKSON: I do not think there is
much to add to the very lucid statement with
which my hon. Friend placed this question
before the House. The only point I would
like to emphasise is that the late Government, behind the back of Ireland, promised
a concession to Canada which the Imperial
Government of to-day felt it was under an
obligation to carry out. We hold that Mr.
Lloyd George also gave us a promise to carry
out the Land Purchase scheme, and that this
Government is entitled to carry out a promise that is favourable to Ireland when they
carried out one that was detrimental to this
country. The hon. Senator opposite (Col.
Perceval-Maxwell) has referred to the difficulty landlords in this country would be in
if they did not get almost the rental they
are receiving to-day. I speak on behalf of
the tenantry of Northern Ireland, and I say
they would regret to see the landlords of the
Northern area removed from that area.
There is no question about that. The
"bridge" should not be borne by the tenantry, who are not in a position to do it.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am sure that everybody will feel a certain
amount of satisfaction that this question,
which is naturally a very important one,
has been brought up to-day in this House.
Speaking for myself, I can say at once that
I feel in agreement with all the remarks
which have fallen from the various speakers.
The hon. Member who brought forward this
subject is very familiar with all the details.
He has been associated with this question
for a great many years, and I am in sympathy with him when he presses for something to be done to carry out the various
undertakings which have been given. It is
quite true that a scheme was brought
forward at the time of the Irish Convention,
and that scheme, while perhaps not fulfilling
the desires of everyone, was a measure with
which we all had a great deal of sympathy
and which we hoped would have been carried
into effect. We have to realise that in the
condition in which all Irish affairs have now
been for some considerable time all measures
of local interest have been in abeyance, but
I think we can feel a certain amount of
satisfaction that we in this part of Ireland
have settled down to our normal avocations,
and we may consequently hope the result will
be that one by one these questions will be
speedily settled.
The hon. Member (Mr. Montgomery) referred to assurances we have received, and
he quoted some of those assurances. We
have received a great number of assurances
and if good-will or assurances mean anything I think we need have no doubt as to
all our ambitions in this respect being fulfilled. The hon. Member who has just sat
down (Mr. Dickson) has agreed with the
previous speakers that this is a matter which
affects equally both landlords and tenants,
and that there is a large amount of agreement between the two as to the terms in
which this should be carried out. Hon.
Members are aware that the administration
of land purchase is a matter which is reserved to the Imperial Government under
the Government of Ireland Act, and that
is the reason I am unable to answer the
question with the authority I should like
to have in this House. I would refer hon.
Members to a question that was put by the
Speaker of the other House in the Imperial
Parliament on the 3rd March, 1920. The
text of the question was:
"Whether it is the intention of the
Government to introduce a Bill for the
completion of land purchase in Ireland
to be passed concurrently with the Government of Ireland Bill."
The answer given by the present Prime
Minister, Mr. Bonar Law, who was then the
Leader of the House of Commons, was in
the affirmative. Since then we have been
assured that the Imperial Government would
give effect to the provisions of a measure of
land purchase, but it is quite true that so
far nothing has been done beyond the fact
that an abortive Bill was presented to the
T
Land Purchase
Loans for Cottages
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
House of Commons in 1920, and I think we
may take it that the provisions of that Bill
represented the views of the Government of
that time, and I have no reason to believe
that the views of the present Government
are in any way different to the views held
by the previous Government. I am able to
tell the House that the Government of
Northern Ireland are fully aware of this
matter and are keeping it well before them.
Hon. Members will agree with me that for
the last two days we have been in a different
position to that which we occupied before.
We have formally and officially contracted
out of the Free State. Prior to doing that
the
Imperial Government, before taking
certain official action, always referred us to
the difficulty that might occur in connection
with the Free State. Now I think we are
on far better ground for presenting our undoubted claims upon the Imperial Government and for calling upon them to discharge
those obligations which we feel are due to
us. I can assure hon. Members that we
shall leave no stone unturned by way of
representation to press the Imperial Government to expedite a Bill on these lines.
was
There is the question of bonus, with which
I am not sure Senators are quite familiar.
The bonus, of course, was the bridge which
an hon. Member referred to. In Mr. George
Wyndham's words, it
the "golden
bridge," a sum of money which enabled a
landlord to sell his property at a price
which was remunerative to him and at the
same time enabled the tenant to pay a price
that was consistent with his financial position. The bonus is not in existence at the
present moment, but the new bonus will fall
on the Six Counties.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: No.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am sorry to contradict the hon. Member,
but I am afraid that is the case at the
present moment. It applies to new advances.
The Six
Mr. MONTGOMERY: At the present moment the bonuses under the old Acts fall on
the Six Counties because the Six Counties
have been given the annuities.
Counties received a present of the annuities
under the old Act, and they were then told
that, having received the annuities, they
would have to pay the bonuses. I do not
think anybody expects the British Government is going to lend us £3,000,000 and also
give us the annuities--
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon. Member
is not going to make a second speech.
Mr. DUFFIN: Arising out of the answer
of the Leader of the House, I should like
to point out that if this burden falls on
the Imperial Exehequer, that Exchequer, so
far as these matters are concerned, is our
Exchequer.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think there is some misapprehension on
this point, but we have no guarantee that
this cost will in future be borne by the
Government,
under fresh
Imperial
legislation.
even
In addition to new advances
required there are many sales pending from
the old Acts, amounting to something like
£1,000,000, on which bonuses will be chargeable.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: And we get the
annuities.
Mr. SPEAKER: I must ask the hon. Member to allow the Noble Marquess to make his
speech.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As far as the new bonus is concerned I have
every reason to believe it will fall on the
Six Counties. I assure hon. Members that
everything we can do to press our claims
and our rights on the Imperial Government will be done.
I am sorry it is
impossible for me to give a more satisfactory
answer to the question beyond the assurance
that the Prime Minister and Government
of Northern Ireland are fully cognisant of
our rights in the matter, and that every
step will be taken to see that those rights
are respected.
Loans for Cottages.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether Rural Councils, which
have purchased plots of ground for the
purpose of erecting cottages thereon and
are unable to erect such cottages at an
economic rent, will be able for this purpose
to procure loans from the Government at
a low rate of interest in the same way as
such loans were formerly provided by the
Imperial Government.
I do not think it is necessary to add
anything to the question. The Leader of
the House knows that in the Newtownards
area we have a large number of cottage
plots bought, and that formerly we erected
houses on them at a cost of £200 each.
Recently, when we went to get contracts
for the same kind of houses the contract
price was £700. Last year it was reduced
to £500, but unless we can get the money
on the same terms as formerly there is no
possibility of erecting these houses at a
rent which labourers can pay. The houses
at the present time are let at 8/6 per month,
and at the present contract price the rent
would be at least 10/- per week, which would
be beyond the ability of a labourer to pay,
unless we can get assistance from the
Government. There is a tremendous need
for these houses. When any become vacant
we always have nine or ten applicants for
them. I hope the Government will be able
to render us some assistance.
Cleaning of Main Drains. 291
Loans for Cottages
13 DECEMBER 1922.
Mr. LONG: I am very pleased that the
hon. Member (Mr. Dickson) has raised this
question, as we in the rural districts feel
that the matter is an important one. As
the hon. Member has said, there is a great
need of more houses in the rural districts.
We find it is quite impossible to build them
at a rent labourers can pay. The Council
with which I am connected was told that
the outlay for erecting houses would entail
a rent of 9/6 per week, and the Council
thought it would be quite impossible for
rural labourers to pay more than 2/6 per
week. In the circumstances I trust that
the Minister of Finance of the Northern
Government will sympathetically consider
the situation and extend all the help he
can. I should very much like to know
whether houses so erected would be eligible
to receive the unemployment grant disbursed by the Minister of Labour. Under
that scheme a free grant equivalent to 60
per cent. of the wages is given. If the
Minister of Labour would approve of such
a grant being given for the erection of
houses in the rural districts it would
simplify the problem very considerably. If
the information is not available to-day I
should like the Leader of the House to
procure it.
this
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Senator who has raised
question refers to loans which are advanced
under the Labourers (Ireland) Act, 1906.
Under it a special Loan Fund was established for financing rural districts on land
purchase terms. The administration of this
fund, as the hon. Member knows, is a reserved service, and is controlled by the Irish
Land Commission, with whom the Minister
of Home Affairs is now in correspondence
regarding the availability of further loans
to rural Councils in the Northern area. It
is hoped that an announcement will be made
to these same Councils at an early date.
The hon. Member who also spoke (Mr. Long)
referred to a very important matter, and
I do not propose at this moment to answer
off-hand the question he put to me.
The
method of utilising the Unemployment
Grant is not a very simple one, and there
are many considerations that enter into the
matter which I cannot go into at the
moment. But I can assure the hon. Member
that I will consult my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Labour, to see if it is
possible for the suggestion he has put forward to be adopted.
Cleaning of Main Drains.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether the Ministry of Agriculture will issue an order making the
cleaning of main drains through agricultural
lands compulsory, the area of charge being
the area benefited, except in cases where
rock or permanent obstruction exists, in
which cases Government assistance would
be granted.
In asking the question I have only to
suggest that it would be a great deal better
to employ some of the men who are receiving the dole in making agricultural land
productive in the way 1 suggest in the
question, rather than pay them money for
doing nothing.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I only
rise to point out that in this question my
hon. Friend (Mr. Dickson) suggests that the
area of charge should be the area that
benefits. In my experience of Local Government work the area of charge has always
been considered to be a townland, or two
townlands, and it would be very unfair in
the case of compulsory drainage that a
whole townland should be involved in the
expense. I think it should be confined to
the farms that benefit, and I am sure my
hon. Friend agrees with me.
Mr. DICKSON: I quite agree.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I merely wish to reinforce the case Mr. Dickson has put before
the House. In my part of the country
much of the land has become absolutely
water-logged owing to the neglect of these
main drains. In parts of the country where
the farms are large this condition might
not arise so easily, but where the farms
are small the drainage is stopped, and
though it may be possible in law to enforce
the cleaning of a very negligent farmer's
drain, at the same time there are great
difficulties, and people hesitate and endure
the mischief rather than become involved
in a law suit. If the cleaning of such
drains was compulsorily enforced it would
be a great benefit to the country. With
regard to the area of charge, I agree that
it ought to be the farms.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
These are very important matters, and I
am very glad indeed that the hon. Member
has brought them before the House. I feel
that the utility of our Houses of Parliament
is shown by the manner in which these
questions, which so much influence the life
of the Six Counties, are brought up for
discussion, and I need hardly say that I
sincerely hope the working of this legislative
assembly will be in the direction of expediting all these things and removing some of the
anomalies that at present exist.
I can
assure my hon. Friends that this is not
the first time this matter has been brought
up. Already several representations have
been received by the Minister of Agriculture
295 Cleaning of Main Drains.
Loans Guarantee Bill. 296
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
in regard to it, but as a matter of fact the
Ministry has no legal power to issue such
an Order.
During the war power was taken under
the Defence of the Realm Regulations to
make the cleaning of drains through agricultural lands compulsory, but these
Regulations lapsed on the termination of
the war. I feel sure hon. Members
will realise that this question of land
drainage involves very many controversial
points, as it affects the rights of mill owners,
and it also affects fishery rights. With
regard to the mill owners, in many cases
in order to secure that arable land
should be free from periodic flooding, to
which it is liable at present, it would be
necessary to either lower or remove altogether the weirs and dams that are fixed
in certain streams for the purpose of storing
the water to provide power for working the
mills. Any such interference with weirs
or dams would mean the possible total deprivation of power for working the mil
machinery, and owners of these mills woul
undoubtedly prosecute claims for very heavy
compensation.
In enforcing compliance with the Defence of the Realm Regulations on land
drainage during the war numerous difficulties
were encountered, such as those to which
I have already referred, and people have
found that it has involved them in very
serious liabilities. There have been cases
of very expensive ligitation resulting, and
also in the Department having to pay compensation and having to restore drains to
their original unsatisfactory condition. The
lowering of the weir would in many instances
interfere with the passage of the
fish up the river or stream, which would
mean a breach of the fishery laws. This
would entail costly law proceedings, and I
am sure hon. Members realise that Fisheries
at the present time are a reserved service
over which the Northern Government has
no power or control. I am only taking this
opportunity to show the manifold difficulties
that do exist in carrying out something
which on the face of it seems to be a very
beneficial reform, but I will undertake to
have a consultation with the Minister of
Agriculture to see if and how these many
and varying interests can be reconciled for
the benefit of the community at large.
Message from the Commons.
That they have passed the Appropriation
(No. 2) Bill, and the Loans Guarantee Bill,
to which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Brought from the Commons and read the
First time.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill be read the
Second time. This House will not look to
me for any word explanatory of this measure
as it is purely a Money Bill dealing with
the grant of Supply, and empowering the
Ministry of Finance to borrow by Treasury
Bills or otherwise to a total sum of £200,000.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committeed.
--(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be read the Third
time. (Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now
(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
pass.-
And the Clerk was directed to acquaint
the Commons that the Senate have agreed
to the said Bill without any amendment.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Brought from the Commons and read the
First time.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving the Second Reading of this brief
but important measure, I feel that but few
words will be needed from me as hon.
Members are already doubtless aware that
this is legislation parallel with that which
under the name of the Trades Facilities Act
has already been in force for more than a
year on the other side of the water. We
have the greater confidence in recommending
the passing of such a measure by our own
Parliament, in that the Imperial Government has found such utility to industry from
that Act that they are proposing to extend
very considerably the powers conferred on
the Treasury to guarantee loans to industry.
The policy at the root of this Bill is the
endeavour to remedy unemployment at its
source, unemployment caused by the stagnation of industry. This industrial stagnation is the result, as my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, justly
remarked in another place yesterday, to a
very great extent of the crushing incidence
of the burden of taxation, and in particular
of the Excess Profits Tax, the crippling
effects of which persist despite the fact that
this tax has now been abolished-abolished
quite possibly because a needy Chancellor of
297 Loans Guarantee Bill-

13 DECEMBER 1922.
Second Reading.
the Exchequer found that the lemon had
been squeezed dry. There is no doubt that
the greatest problem throughout the world
to-day, and particularly in Great Britain
and Ireland, is the problem of unemployment. It is plain that the solution lies not
in the application of palliatives but in such
remedies as will remove the cause of the
disease from the body politic. The first
cry of the unemployed is for work. They
are not looking for the easy money of the
so-called dole, which they hate, and justly
hate, as sapping their morale and undermining their self-respect. The way to provide the work they need is the restoration
of industry by giving back to it its elasticity,
its power of adaptaion to every change of
circumstance, and its ability to expand.
Credit is the life blood of industry and industry is the life blood of our people. The
Loans Guarantee Bill which is before the
House to-day provides a means of fostering
credit in private industry and supplies a
fresh current of energy to vitalise afresh
the failing powers-failing for very many
reasons of our private enterprise.
There is yet a further reason why we have
brought forward this measure. Not only
have State contributions to unemployment
insurance been merely in the nature of a
palliative rather than a remedy, but they
have been extraordinarily expensive.
For
the year 1922-23, the estimated amount of
State contributions to the Unemployed Insurance Fund was Two hundred and fifty-two
thousand pounds. Added to this, moreover,
there is an additional expenditure of several
thousands of pounds upon salaries and expenses of the administration of the Unemployment Benefit Scheme. Over and above
all this, during the first seven months of
the financial year a sum of Six hundred and
thirteen thousand pounds was advanced on
loan to the Unemployment Fund by our
Exchequer.
The Imperial Government, as I have
stated already, when faced with similar
difficulties, had passed, in 1921, the Trade
Facilities Act, which endeavoured to encourage industrial operations by guaranteeing
the payment of loans raised for the purpose
of carrying out capital undertakings. It
was recognised that many concerns, whose
credit in normal times was perfectly sound,
found themselves, owing to the depressed
state of the markets generally, unable to
obtain accommodation from their bankers for
the purposes of expansion. To meet this difficulty, therefore, the Trades Facilities Acts
empowered the Treasury to guarantee the
principal and interest or merely the interest
on such loans as might reasonably be expected to have the effect of diminishing
unemployment. The Imperial Government,
as I have already said, have found this
measure so useful that they are providing
for an extension of the Treasury powers of
guarantee.
After most careful consideration, we have
decided to follow the lead of the Imperial
Government in this matter. It may be true
that the opportunity has already been afforded to industrial houses in this Province
to take advantage of the Imperial Trade
Facilities Scheme. But we feel that the
guarantee of loans by our own Government
will eliminate such delays as may normally
be expected from the necessity to refer
claims to a committee in London.
I may
add that my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Finance, anticipates that the remedial
effect of this measure will be principally
felt in our shipbuilding enterprises and
will provide a stimulus to the absorption
of labour by our great shipyards.
Hon. Members will observe that our Bill
sets up an advisory committee to the Ministry of Finance,
the members being
nominated by that Minister, and gives
power to the Ministry of Finance to guarantee to the aggregate value in principal
and interest of Two million pounds such
loans as are recommended by this committee.
I may add that before recommending such
guarantee the Committee will require to be
satisfied
(1) that the undertaking could not otherwise be financed on reasonable terms
without State assistance;
(2) that adequate benefit will accrue to
the public in return for public assistance in the shape of a reduction of
unemployment in Northern Ireland,
and a general increase of the means
of production, or a reduction in costs.
In making their recommendations the
Committee will show a preference for
(1) undertakings on a large scale with
the minimum of financial risk;
(2) undertakings which will be rapidly
set on foot;
(3) undertakings under which orders will
be placed in this Province at prices
competitive with quotations from
other parts of the United Kingdom.
I must apologise to the House for having
detained hon. Members for so long, but I
am looking forward with confident hope to
a ready forgiveness when in the near future
we are able, owing to the beneficent action
of the present measure, to note a quickening
influence over the field of our great industries, together with a rise in the productive
powers of our Province and a corresponding
fall in the terrible and threatening
affliction of unemployment.
THE SENATE.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
The New Governor. 300
Reading to-day. I think that the limit of
Standing Orders is the Second Reading.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
With your leave I would like to say how
fully I sympathise with hon. Members who
think the Bill is going too rapidly through
this House. I think I shall be meeting hon.
Members if we have the Third Reading of
this measure to-morrow. They will have
had a certain amount of time to consider
its provisions. This change will only mean
that on the adjournment I will ask this
House to meet a little earlier than was
otherwise intended.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I do not
rise to oppose this Bill; but I do think that
a Bill of such importance should have been
in our hands a little sooner before we were
asked to approve of it. I did not see the
Bill at all. I am very much obliged to the
noble Marquess, the Leader of the House,
for giving such a lucid explanation of it,
but it is not right that we should not have
time to study the Bill which starts a new
principle, and one of such great importance.
There is one thing I would like to ask the
noble Marquess. Is it only certain particular
trades that get the benefit of this Bill? For
instance will agriculture get any benefit?
Will the farmer having to lay out or to
build be entitled to get any benefit from
this Bill? I am not quite clear, but am
I to understand that the shipbuilding industry, having already had the benefit of the
English Bill, is getting a sort of second
benefit from this Bill, because if I am right
in that I think it is a principle that is not
very satisfactory. We know the enormous
benefit which the shipbuilding is to Belfast,
but that they should first of all get the
benefit of the Imperial Treasury, of which
we are part, and then come down to the
local Treasury does not seem fair. I may
be wrong but I should like some information.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
If I may interrupt the hon. Member I am
asking the House to consent to the passing
of the Bill, for which I gave reasons. The
right hon. Member is merely raising Committee points. I have endeavoured to show
him that in the Bill an Advisory Committee
is set up in the Ministry of Finance which
will consider all these points that he is
now bringing before the House. What we
are asking in moving the Second Reading
of the Bill is that the principle which is
involved in the Bill should be accepted by
Members of this House. I think the right
hon. Gentleman (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell)
does not oppose the principle of the Bill?
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I accept
the principle of the Bill, but I should like
to protest against not having more time to
study it beforehand.
Mr. LESLIE: My sympathies are entirely
with the Government in doing everything
they can to reduce the amount of unemployment under which the whole Six Counties
at the moment are suffering. But, like the
right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Perceval-Maxwell) I, too, should like rather
more time. I am not prepared to go into
the Bill in detail to-day, and I really only
rose to ask how long the Government would
be able to give us to consider the measure.
I presume we cannot go beyond the Second
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill be
read a Second time," agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed." (Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
The New Governor of Ulster.
VISIT OF LORD DERBY.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, 'That this House do now adjourn." In doing so, I feel I shall be only
voicing the wishes of the Senators who constitute this House if I make some reference
to the important events which occurred yesterday, when our new Governor came to
Belfast in his official capacity. It is not
necessary for me to say anything about his
Grace the Duke of Abercorn. We all have
the advantage of his acquaintance, and most
of us the benefit of his friendship. We have
every reason to feel pride and satisfaction in
the fact that his Majesty has thought fit to
select his Grace as his representative in
Ulster. The noble Duke comes of a family
which is closely associated with this Province.
His forebears, like himself, spent their lives
in the service of the State, and I cannot
think that the choice of his Majesty could
have fallen upon a more fitting gentleman
than upon the Duke of Abercorn.
I think I may be allowed, on your behalf,
to express the great satisfaction we feel at his
appointment, and we know that in his labours
for our Province he will be ably assisted by
the Duchess, who is already very well known
amongst us, and who has never failed to
assist any undertaking for the benefit of this
Province.
Before I resume my seat I should like to
tell the House that the present Secretary of
State for War, Lord Derby, proposes to come
over to Ulster in the spring or early summer,
for the purpose of inspecting the Royal Ulster
Constabulary and the other members of the
Constabulary forces in the Six Counties. I
feel sure that this news will give great satis-
Land Purchase.
Londonderry Bridge.
13 DECEMBER 1922.
faction to those to whom we owe such a debt
of gratitude. I should also like to thank the
Prime Minister for having delayed making
reference to these two matters as a courteous
action to this House, which only met to-day
while the other House met yesterday.
Mr. SPEAKER rose to put the question.
Mr. MONTGOMERY 10se.
Mr. SPEAKER: I must point out to hon.
Members that when stand they must sit
down. I know no disrespect is meant, but if
our Standing Orders are carried out it leads
to better conduct of the business.
Question, "That this House do now
andjourn," put.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I think it will be
only right for me, holding the very stron
view I do on the Land Purchase, if I make
a few remarks about the matter. It appears
to me that the admission that the bonus is
to be paid by Northern Ireland knocks the
whole bottom out of the arrangement for
relieving us of the burden of this question.
It is entirely contrary to the pledge which
we are asking the Imperial Government to
fulfil. Mr. Lloyd George said that he proposed to bring in a Measure for the purpose
of enabling Parliament to give effect to the
recommendations of the Convention on the
subject of Land Purchase. The recommendations of the Convention deal with this business
of the bonus, and they say that the bonus
was always regarded as a free gift to Ireland
They go on to say it is certainly an Irish
service, but an Imperal obligation. The Noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, said there appeared to be a misapprehension about this. I
think there is a very serious misapprehension,
but I venture to think that it is not a misapprehension on my part, but on the part of
Ministers who are dealing with this matter.
There appears to be confusion, because when
the annuities which were being collected
under the old Acts were under some arrangement handed over to Ireland, Northern Ireland was told it would have to find the bonus.
I think that was altogether wrong. That
was contrary to this, but at the same time
whatever authority there was behind that it
was not part of the scheme to carry out the
recommendations of the Convention Committee. Since the Act to relieve Ireland, the
South has gone wrong in various respects,
but we here have not gone wrong. We have
done all that we were asked to do, and we
accepted the burden of self-government on
the distinct understanding that the Land
Question was to be cleared away. Nobody
could possibly understand that it was to be
cleared away at our expense, including a substantial bonus. That was never intended.
ever
Nobody understood it in that sense, and I
do not conceive it is possible for the British
Government to have done 60. We did
not ask them to give us a present
of these three-and-a-half millions, or whatit is. We asked them to invest
it on very good security. If they invest it
on very good security the annuities must
come into the British Exchequer and not into
our Exchequer, and the bonus must come
out of the British Exchequer and not out of
our Exchequer. I believe there has been a
very serious misapprehension about this. If
they wish to see this question properly settled
the Ministers, or whoever the Minister is who
is concerned in these things, should at once
withdraw this admission, if there has been
an admission, and show if the Government
mean to keep their pledges. They must do
it on the basis that they must pay this bonus,
which from the very beginning was always a
bonus provided by the British Exchequer.
It was to be paid not by the taxpayers of
Great Britain or of Ireland, but of the United
Kingdom as a whole. The United Kingdom
has been shorn of the Southern part of
Ireland, but that does not mean that we
have to pay
what formerly the United
Kingdom had to pay, and what the remains
of the United Kingdom should pay now.
This is a very serious misapprehension
which will destroy the whole benefit, or will
very largely destroy the benefit, of the
Measure we are asking for, and I urge
strongly that His Majesty's Ministers for
Northern Ireland should see that this misapprehension is put right and that the bonus
should be paid by the Exchequer of both
Kingdoms and not by the Exchequer of
Northern Ireland.
Mr. LONG: During the passage of the
Londonderry Commissioners Bill I failed to
catch your eye although I rose three times.
I did not rise for the purpose of opposing
the Bill in any way, but merely, as a Bridge
Commissioner, of expressing on behalf of
the Londonderry Bridge Commissioners the
obligation we feel under to the Government
of Northern Ireland and especially to the
Minister of Home Affairs and the Minister
of Finance for the very sympathetic manner
in which they have dealt with this Bill from
the moment it was introduced. I also wish
to express our obligation for the despatch
with which the Bill has been put through
all its stages. In all the circumstances it
was most creditable to the Government and
to the officials of the Government. They
straightened out, if I may say so, the red
tape and delay which very often occur in
getting a Bill promoted in the Imperial
Parliament. I think this is eloquent testimony to the value of local legislation. It
has also given great pleasure to the Members
303 Business of the House.

THE SENATE.
Adjournment.
of the North-West of Ireland, because the
ill-founded feeling existed there that this
Government had only the interest of Belfast
at heart. I think the passage of this Bill
in such a manner has gone to disprove such
an unfounded feeling. That is all I wish
to say.
Mr. LESLIE: If I may I would like to
ask the noble Marquess, the Leader of the
House, at what time to-morrow it is proposed
should
we
meet. and also if
there will be any other business for the
House in addition to the Third Reading of
the Guarantee Bill?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The Sitting on to-morrow will be taken up
with the Prorogation of Parliament. I would
ask hon. Members if it would suit the convenience of the House if we were to meet
at 2-45 p.m. instead of 3-15 p.m., in order
that we could get through the Third Reading
of the Loans Guarantee Bill. So far as I
know there is no further business.
On Question, Motion, "That this House
do now adjourn," agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes before Five o'clock.
Loans Guarantee Bill- 14 DECEMBER 1922.
Third Reading.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 14th December, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter before Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Message from the Commons.
To return the Londonderry Bridge Commissioners Bill and to acquaint this House
that the Commons have agreed to the Senate's
amendments made thereto.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Third reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move the
Third reading of the Loans Guarantee Bill,
the previous stage of which we passed yesterday. Two points arose yesterday which I
should like to answer now. The first was the
question whether agriculture could share in
the benefits conferred by this Bill. I am
advised that the fact that an industrial
organisation has agricultural interests certainly does not debar it from sharing in
these advantages if it can satisfy the Advisory Committee on the two heads which I
mentioned yesterday, namely-
(1) that the undertaking could not otherwise be financed on reasonable terms without State assistance;
(2) that adequate benefit will accrue to
the public in return for public assistance in
the shape of a reduction of unemployment
in Northern Ireland, and a general increase
of the means of production, or a reduction
in costs.
Whild, of course, the three reasons for preference must also be borne in mind.
The second point which arose was in regard
to concerns which have already had the
benefit of the Imperial guarantee under the
Trade Facilities Act of 1921. I can say at
once that the fact that a firm has made use
of the Imperial guarantee cannot be regarded
as a bar to an application to the Advisory
Committee for the guarantee provided under
the terms of the present Bill.
It will, however, be recognised that the fact
that a concern has already a loan guaranteed
by the Imperial Treasury will become known
of necessity to the Advisory Council to the
Ministry of Finance, and they, of course, as
I said yesterday, will be guided first and foremost in making selection by the amount of
financial risk, that is to say, preference will
necessarily be given to concerns whose credit
stands the highest amongst those who are
putting forward undertakings qualified under
the present scheme. I hope that that answers
the inquiries which I received from hon.
Members yesterday. The Bill is one brought
in in the interests of trade and in the hope
of mitigating the unemployment which now
exists in our midst. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: Remark was made yesterday
as to the shortness of the time at our disposal
for the consideration of this Bill.
I am
afraid I must say that a little further
thought rather strengthens the point of view
which was then stated. The measure is not
only one of novelty, but one of some magnitude and of great importance. However, the
object of the Bill, which is, as the Noble
Marquess has just said, the encouragement
of industry and particularly relief of unemployment, so commends itself to us as to
disarm criticism on that head. A similar
Bill has just been under examination at
Westminster, and an hon. Member of the
Lower House there, Mr. Clynes, pointed out
that if 10 or 10,000 men could be taken off the
streets, the Bill that was before that House
would be worth while. I observe the right
hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Finance,
stated when moving the second reading of this
Bill in another place, that in his view some
six or seven thousand men might be taken
off the streets of Belfast and given work in
the shipyards if this Bill were passed. When
you read the report of a statement like that
it necessarily makes it very difficult to put
up anything in the way of opposition to the
present Bill designed to effect that admirable
object.
Three points seem to me to arise in looking
at the Bill. The first of them is the amount
involved, the amount of the guarantee. That
amount is £2,000,000. The amount which it
appears has been taken across the water is
£25,000,000. Therefore we are guaranteeing
two-twenty-fifths, or about 8 per cent., over
here of the amount guaranteed over there.
It is a very large proportion, I think, having
regard to our resources as compared with the
resources of Great Britain. However, that
amount has been received with approval elsewhere, the Government supports
it, and the Minister of Finance has
moved the Bill on these terms. I do
not think it would be at all proper or
advisable to suggest any reduction here of
that amount. Indeed the question seems to
arise as to whether it would be within our
powers to amend such a Bill in that manner,
and, speaking subject to correction, I am inclined to think that, though the Bill is not
one which comes within the definition of a
money Bill given by Sir Erskine May, or
in our own Act of 1920, and therefore that it
is not a Bill which we are precluded from
amending should we desire to do so, still to
move an amendment here on a matter of
that kind, as it appears to me still, speak-
U
Third Reading.

Loans Guarantee Bill-
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
ing subject to correction-would be regarded
as rather an infringement of the courtesies
due elsewhere. I should be sorry to suggest
that that course should be taken, the more so
as the amount required must necessarily be
far better known to the Government than it
can possibly be to any private Member.
The next thing that I think we ought to
pay regard to is the question of the safety
of the money which it is proposed to
guarantee. In so far as the money is lent to
a public body with rate-making powers be:
hind it, practically no question of security
can arise. The position is somewhat
different when the proposal is to lend money
to further trade enterprise. I observe from
the debates in England that the question was
discussed rather carefully over there, and
that the suggestion was made that the public
interest might be protected by taking mortgages on debentures and so on. I can only
regard that, I think, as an intimation that
the financial authority is determined to protect the public interest, as, of course, the
value of the mortgage on debentures depends
primarily on the value of the debentures. At
all events there was no doubt that there was
a determination to protect the public interest.
And it is quite plain from the speech of the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Finance, in another place, as reported in the
newspapers, that he himself entertains no
doubt whatever, but that he thinks it
adequately protected, and that there is no
risk of any of the money being lost.
A
The other point that arises is one that I
confess has given me personally most concern, that is, the position of the provincial
towns and rural districts in connection with
this matter. Agriculture, as the Noble
Marquess has just said, can, under certain
circumstances, perhaps get something.
question was raised elsewhere on that point.
and an answer was given that it may be that
agriculture will get something, but I do not
think that the prospects are very hopeful—
that is, so far as agriculture is concerned
directly. A question was also asked elsewhere as to whether it would be possible to
let county councils have any share in the
guaranteed money in order that they might
rebuild some of their roads, with the object
of rendering them capable of supporting the
exceptional traffic which they are now called
upon to bear, and the reply of the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, was not
such as to encourage any hopes. I
do not think that the primary object
of this Bill is either to help agriculture or to assist county councils, and
it is not fair to bring that as a complaint
against it because it does not profess to do
either of these things. There is a point
which would help the districts and smaller
towns, and that is that special consideration
should be given to the claim of any town or
of any rural district which might want
to raise a loan to erect houses.
I know that the small town of Ballymoney,
with a population of 3,100, is very
anxious at the moment to get on with a
scheme for the provision of additional houses
in the town. I have not had time to consult
the Council as to whether any loan guaranteed under this Bill would be of any assistance to them. But it might very well be
that it would be, and that reduced interest
--a reduction amounting to perhaps one per
cent. on the capital-might be of very
considerable value.
I see again referring to the debates at
Westminster-that a colliery in Wales did
get a grant from money guaranteed towards
the erection of houses. I hope that that precedent will be borne in mind. Indeed I
would urge generally that every consideration
should be shown to the smaller towns and
rural districts in allocating this money. That
would go a long way to stifle any little complaint that may be made, and render our
task easier when we explain to our friends
in the country that in a matter of this kind
they must take the broadest possible view
and must pay the greatest regard to the difficulties of unemployment in a great city, and
that anything in the nature of a policy of
dog in the manger is absolutely fatal to the
well-being of Ulster. I have every hope that
the Bill will realise to some extent the anticipations of those responsible for it, and I can
certainly say that it has my own good wishes
and very heartiest support.
Mr. DUFFIN: I confess it is with a certain
amount of hesitation that I am satisfied to
support the Third Reading of this Measure.
However, it comes to us as a Government
Measure, and under Ministerial responsibility, and we know that the Government
ought to be in a better position than anyone
else to judge of the necessities of the case.
The Bill has been passed in another place
with remarkable unanimity. I confess, however, that it does seem to me that the underlying principle of this Bill, under which it
is proposed to assist in financing not merely
public corporations but perhaps, large companies or firms engaged in industrial work or
enterprises by granting them credit guaranteed by the Government, is in itself,
undesirable and liable to lead us into dangerous paths. In fact, I think it is only justified
as an emergency measure called for by
expediency and by the remarkably abnormal
conditions in which our great industries are
at the present time carried on. Therefore I
am satisfied to support the Bill and to regard
it as being an exceptional measure and not
one to be established as a precedent or one
to be followed. A good deal depends on the
proper administration of the Bill, and I
understand that a Committee will be formed
Land Purchase.
Land Purchase.
14 DECEMBER 1922.
upon whose advice and guidance the administration of the financial assistance that will
be given will very largely depend. I am
satisfied that the Committee appointed
under this Bill will be one that can be
depended upon and will be men of not
merely assured fidelity but of business
capacity, uninfluenced by any question of
personal interest and not susceptible to any
personal influence that may be addressed to
them. So I am satisfied that on the whole
we can look forward to the powers conferred
by the Bill being exercised to the general
advantage of the community, particularly
in the matter of assisting the solution of the
unemployment problem in our midst.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill do now
(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
pass.-
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have
agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.
Land Purchase in Northern Ireland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move "That this House do now adjourn during pleasure." I have every reason
to believe that it will re-assemble in a short
time for the purpose of hearing the Prorogation Speech. In the meantime I would
ask the indulgence of the House in order
that I might refer to a matter which we
discussed yesterday and when, I regret to
say, I failed to grasp the meaning which the
hon. Gentleman who sits opposite (Mr.
Montgomery) had in his mind. It seems to
me that
exgave him an answer which he did
not require, and that the question which
required an answer received no answer from
me, or at least one which did not satisfy
the hon. Member. With the leave of the
House, I venture to make a statement in
regard to the question of Land Purchase,
which is a matter of so much interest
at the present moment. I should like
to say a few words as to the
tent to which Land Purchase has been
carried on in Ireland up to the present.
There have been several famous Acts such
as the Ashbourne Act and the Wyndham
Act, as well as a number of ancillary Acts
which have put machinery in motion to buy
out the landlords and put the tenants in
possession of their holdings. Owing, however, to the financial provisions of each Act
becoming uneconomic as conditions changed,
a new Act was found to be necessary and
as a matter of interest it was invariably
more and more expedient for the Government to bridge the gap between the land
lords' requirements and the tenants' ability
to pay.
Under the first group of Acts, 1881-1885,
advances were made from the Exchequer
and later from the Local Loans Fund. Then
came the Acts of 1891-1896, creating Land
Stock for the financing of Land Purchase.
Under those two sets of Acts which are now
inoperative, so far as fresh advances are
concerned, approximately 24 millions changed
hands, and still it was found that only the
fringe of the problem had been touched.
This gave rise to what is recognised as the
greatest Land Purchase Act, namely the
Wyndham Act of 1903, which dealt with
Land Purchase on a large scale, giving what
were then favourable terms to the landlords
by means of a twelve per cent. bonus, while
the tenant almost invariably had to pay
less annuity than he had previously paid in
rent. These operations were financed, and are
still financed to a small extent, by an issue of
2 per cent. Stock, and subsequently, by the
Act of 1909, cash has been provided by an
issue of 3 per cent. Stock.
As you are well aware, the price on the
market of those Stocks has depreciated considerably during the past few years, with the
result that for each £100 of advances made,
considerably more than £100 Stock had to
be sold on the market in order to provide
£100 cash, let us say £120 stock. Now the
tenant's instalment only provides interest and
sinking fund on £100, so that the Government had to find the interest and sinking
fund on this excess issue of £20 Stock. In
addition to this the bonus had to be provided
for by an issue of Stock, and again the
Government had to provide interest and
sinking fund, the charge in both cases being
included in the Vote of the Irish Land
Commission.
Coming now to the effect of this on Northern Ireland, the administration of Land
Purchase is a service reserved to the authority
of the Imperial Parliament, but the cost is
deducted from the reserved taxes belonging
to Northern Ireland, with the result that
she is paying every penny of her own Land
Purchase, apart from the gift to her of the
annuities which were intended to cover the
cost of setting up services. If we consider
our Budget Statement it will be seen that
the cost of Land Purchase in Northern Ire
land is put at £240,000 for the present year.
Of this amount, it is safe to say that one
half is the cost of interest and sinking fund
on excess stock, and one-third is the cost of
bonus, leaving one-sixth only of the total
cost for the real administrative charges. It
is only commonsense to state that every
further operation under these Acts involves
further charges for excess stock and bonus.
In order to remedy this state of affairs,
hon. members will remember, a Bill
Land Purchase.
Land Purchase.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
was brought into the Imperial House
of Commons on 11th November, 1920, but has
not been proceeded with. The financing of
this Act was to be carried out by means of
5 per cent. stock and bonds, while the rate
of bonus was high, going sc far as 18 per
cent. in some cases. In addition, vendors
under the old Acts who received 3 per
cent. stock after the passing of
the new Act were to be reimbursed in part
for the loss sustained in paying off charges on
the estate by the sale of stock. Thus, no
matter what happens, I am afraid that the
charge for excess stock and bonus under the
1903 and 1909 Acts will not grow less for
many years to come, while there is every
prospect that any new legislation would require considerable subsidy in
another in order to give the landlords
reasonable price while maintaining
tenants' annuities at a reasonably low level.
Whether the Imperial Government would
undertake this fresh charge is a matter on
which I cannot express any opinion.
one way
or
a
the
Now, as to estimates of the present position
of Land Purchase, let me give a few main
figures. At the passing of the Wyndham Act,
in 1903, it was estimated, I believe, that 50
millions would clear Land Purchase. Since
then, over 90 millions have been advanced,
and I understand, as a rough estimate, that
one-third of the agricultural land in Ireland
remains to be sold to the tenants. There
would, therefore, remain about 50 millions to
be advanced, of which 10 millions have already
been agreed. Taking one-fifth as the proportion of land subject to Land Purchase Acts
in Northern Ireland, it will be seen that the
capital value of land still unsold in our area
is about 8 millions. As against this figure I
have the opinion of people well qualified to
know, that the amount outstanding is only
5 millions, but from experience we know those
estimates have a pernicious habit of expanding. To bring the matter to a head, I consider as a rough forecast that the completion
of Land Purchase in Northern Ireland would
cost an additional £80,000 per annum, but as
I have already said I cannot anticipate
against whom this liability will lie.
I trust this somewhat lengthy explanation
of Land Purchase will make clear the present
position of Northern Ireland in the matter,
and show that every circumstance must be
viewed from all aspects before being urged
or adopted. I ventured to say yesterday
that the Prime Minister and the Government
have this question very much before them at
the present moment. I do not anticipate
that the hon. Gentleman need fear that we
should be behindhand in making the same
representations as he would make himself were
he in our position.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I wish to take this
opportunity of apologising for what I am
afraid was disorderly conduct yesterday, I
hope your Lordship will realise that I am
very deaf and do not always hear
what is said. I am very much
obliged to the Noble Lord, the Leader
of the House, for having taken the trouble
to inquire into the matter we were discussing
yesterday, and for having made such an
interesting statement. With regard to the
amount that has to be dealt with in connection with unsold land in Ulster, he has
given the House certain figures which do not
correspond with the figures I have been
given by what I believe to be good authority,
but I am not in a position to check those
figures or to argue about them. The difficulty that arose yesterday was this: I asked
for a
in
guarantee that
the new Act
to be brought in in compliance with
the pledge to complete Land Purchase
in Ireland
on the lines of the report
of the Irish Convention, that that pledge
should be interpreted in its fullest sense;
that it should be a measure dealing
not with Irish land-because the Free State
somehow or other got out of this thing
and the pledge of the late Government has
been greatly confused by what has happened
in Southern Ireland-but with Northern
Ireland, because I am perfectly satisfied that
we can claim from the Imperial Government
the complete fulfilment of their pledge. I
tried to show yesterday that the complete
fulfilment of that pledge was to bring in a
Bill which would deal with Northern Ireland
as the Wyndham Act dealt with all Ireland,
that is to say, that the advances should be on
the lines of the Irish Convention report, and
the necessary bonus and other expenses should
be an Imperial charge, as I hold it would be
useless to bring in a Bill and throw these
charges on the Northern Government.
There still appears to be some confusion
between the arrangements made as regards
the existing Acts and the arrangements that
were asked to be made as regards the new
Bill to be introduced in fulfilment of these
pledges. They are two entirely different
things. The noble Lord has mentioned that
the bonus and various other charges in connection with the administration of the existing Acts falls on the Northern Ireland Exchequer, and he also mentioned that on the
other hand Northern Ireland has received a
present of the annuities payable as interest
and sinking fund on a large sum of money
advanced by the British Treasury. That is a
distinct gift to us, and what I want to call
attention to is the fact that that makes all the
difference to the question of whether any of
the burden of the bonus and other charges is
to be borne by us or not. In the case of the
existing Acts we have received a gift of the
annuities out of which we should pay these
Land Purchase.
Prorogation.
14 DECEMBER 1922.
expenses, and in the fulfilment of the pledge
in the new Bill we do not expect that we
shall get a present of the annuities. We
believe that if the Government carries out
its pledges they will say, "Here is the
£3,000,000 or £3,250,000 required to fulfil
our bargain with that part of Ireland that
has kept its bargain with us." That is a
perfectly safe investment. By lending this
£3,000,000 to the tenants of Northern Ireland
to buy out their holdings the British Exchequer will not lose one penny. That is a very
different thing from making us a present of
the annuities. They will go to the Treasury,
and what I wanted to point out when I was
dealing with the fulfilment of the pledge was
that the new Bill is a distinctly different
matter from the carrying out of the existing
Acts. As far as I can see, if you apply the
same rule to two different transactions, the
transaction under which we get the annuities
and the transaction under which we will
not get the annuities, you will merely be
falling in with some admirable arrangement
with the British Treasury to feed the Ulster
dog with joints of its own tail. That is what
it amounts to. If it is properly handled, I
cannot imagine that such a transaction as
that can be put forward as a fulfilment of
the pledges which the noble Lord quoted,
and which I could have quoted at much
greater length, but I did not wish to take
up the time of the House unnecessarily.
I understood that the noble Lord's statement was this. I asked for a guarantee that
under this new Bill, under which presumably
all the annuities will go to the British
Exchequer, that Exchequer would bear the
burden of the bonus. His answer was so
worded as to convey the meaning that he
could give no such assurance, and that, on
the contrary, we should have to bear the
burden. I now understand from the noble
Marquess that that is not what he intended
to say, and I expect he was only giving us
a perfectly truthful account of what was
happening in connection with the administration of the old Acts. I suppose I may
therefore take it that he was unable to give
me the assurance I asked for in regard to
the new Act, as nothing was said on that
point. May I take that as the upshot of
his remarks yesterday, and the interesting
statement he has made to-day? I am sorry
the noble Lord could not go further than
that, but I am very well satisfied to have it
explained that in saying that the burden must
fall on Northern Ireland, his Lordship was
only referring to the old Acts, and not to
the new Bill we are asking for. I trust the
Northern Gvernment will take this matter
into serious consideration, and will see that
this imposition is not put upon us, and that
the fulfilment of the pledge should be in
such a form as I indicated in my statement
yesterday. I hope we may have some information to lead us to suppose that in any
negotiations in regard to the new Bill this
matter will be taken up and insisted upon
by our Government.
There will be no fulfilment of the pledge
if the British Government bring in a Bill
merely making an excellent investment of
£3,000,000, and burdening us with a charge
of £350,000, or whatever it may be. I trust
the Northern Government will see that
the pledge is fulfilled, not in this deceptive
manner, but in a manner that anybody
reading the report of the Irish Convention
would consider it ought to be fulfilled. Unless I get some information of a satisfactory
kind that the question is being taken up
in this sense, and that there will be no
more yielding to the attempts of the British
Treasury to make this new Bill a barren
gift to Ulster, I will raise the question again
on the re-assembly of this House.
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps the hon. Member
will allow me to thank him for his few remarks at the beginning of his speech, but I
cannot allow him to accuse himself of disorderliness. He was in no way disorderly.
Naturally our rules are at present not very
well known to all Members, and when they
are broken it is my duty to call attention
to them.
The Royal Assent.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am authorised by the Prime Minister on
his behalf to inform the Senate that His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland, in
the name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his assent to
the following Bills:-
1. Appropriation (No. 2).
2. Loans Guarantee.
3. Londonderry Bridge Commissioners.
PROROGATION OF PARLIAMENT.
Moved: That the Gentleman Usher of the
Black Rod be commanded to inform the
Commons that the Speech of his Grace, the
Governor of Northern Ireland at the Prorogation of Parliament is to be communicated
to the Senate forthwith, and that this House
invite the Commons to attend in order to
hear the same. (Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
HIS GRACE'S SPEECH.
And afterwards his Grace's Speech was delivered to both Houses of Parliament by the
Speaker of the Senate (in pursuance of his
Grace's command) as followeth :-
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons:
The Second Session of your Parliament,
which closes to-day, will be memorable in
the history of Northern Ireland; for dur-
His Grace's Speech.
His Grace's Speech.
THE SENATE.
ing that Session you have exercised, in
accordance with the powers conferred
upon you by the Imperial Parliament, the
right to remain outside the Irish Free
State.
By taking this step you have retained
the representation of Northern Ireland in
the Imperial Parliament, which, therefore, remains responsible for safeguarding your interests in regard to all
Imperial questions.
Consequent upon the severance of the
Irish Free State from the United Kingdom
the office of Lord Lieutenant for Ireland
has ceased to exist, and a separate
Governor has had to be appointed for
Northern Ireland. His Majesty has conferred upon me the honour of being your
first Governor, a position which I highly
appreciate as an Ulsterman and as one
who has been closely associated with you
all my life.
I am gratified that the measures adopted
for restoring law and order in the Province have been crowned with success, in
achieving which both the Royal Ulster
Constabulary and the Special Constabulary have to be congratulated; they have
won the confidence of all classes of the
community.
I am pleased to learn that unemployment in Northern Ireland, although still
serious, is gradually becoming somewhat
less acute. Its full effects have been
mitigated by the legislation which you
have passed, in common with the Imperial Parliament, enabling you to provide, in co-operation with public bodies in
Belfast and Northern Ireland, relief
schemes for a considerable number of ex-
Servicemen, as well as others. Further,
the Government, by providing credit
facilities, hope to encourage commercial
undertakings with a view to unemployed
persons being absorbed in industries as
quickly as possible.
Happily there are indications of a
steady, though slow, improvement in
trade, which, beginning in the shipyards,
is extending to other staple industries.
Not only have active steps been taken by
the Government to assist this revival in
trade, but it is satisfactory to observe that
many misconceptions in Great Britain
and abroad regarding the high commercial standing of Northern Ireland have
been removed.
I trust that the great operations in connection with the provision of the parliamentary and other buildings for the Province, which have just been started, will
make rapid progress.
Northern Ireland has been faced with
exceptional difficulties in regard to the
agricultural industry, difficulties which
affect a very large proportion of the community. The Act which has been passed
to endeavour to preserve a high standard
in cattle-breeding will, I have no doubt,
prove beneficial to your farmers by maintaining the prestige which Ulster has
acquired in Great Britain by the excellence of her live stock, and of her other
agricultural produce.
During the past Session you have passed
24 Acts of Parliament, some designed to
bring your laws into conformity with those
prevailing in Great Britain, from which
they formerly differed, and all of importance in making effective the administration of government in Northern Ireland.
Members of the House of Commons:
I thank you for the provision of supplies for the public services, and I
congratulate you on the fact that your
financial position is so sound, in spite of
many adverse factors.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons:
I am glad to learn that the necessary
preliminary investigations in regard to
education and licensing reform are now
so far advanced that it is hoped it may be
possible to introduce legislation on these
subjects during the next Session.
I desire to express the earnest hope that
the time may not be far distant when the
Executive of the Irish Free State and the
End of Session.
Proclamation

14 DECEMBER 1922.
Executive of Northern Ireland may meet
to settle in amicable conference those outstanding questions which affect the welfare of both areas.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your labours.
Then the Proclamation for proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland was
read by the Speaker as followeth :-
WHEREAS it is provided in effect by the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as modified
by and in pursuance of the Irish Free State
(Consequential Provisions) Act, 1922, as from
the date of the presentation of such Address
as is referred to in the last mentioned Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in His Majesty's name, summon, prorogue, and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that this
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
should be prorogued to the Twenty-seventh
day of February, One Thousand Nine
Hundred and Twenty-three:
NOW I JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, Governor
of
Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation
do publish and declare His Majesty's
Pleasure that the said Parliament be
as from the reading of this Proclamation
in the Parliament of Northern Ireland prorogued to Tuesday, the Twenty-seventh day
of February, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-three:
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the said
Twenty-seventh day of February, One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Twenty-three
assemble and be holden for the despatch
of divers urgent and important affairs, and
the Senators and Members of the House
of Commons of Northern Ireland are hereby
required to give their attendance accordingly.
Given at Belfast, this Twelfth day of
December, 1922.
(Signed) ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the Second Session of the First
Parliament of Northern ireland, in the
Thirteenth year of the Reign of His Majesty,
King George the Fifth.

YD 08451
ON-CIRCULATING BOOK

Tuesday, 14th March, 1922.
The Senate met at Twelve noon, Mr. Speaker
in the Chair.
THE KING'S SPEECH.
The SPEAKER: The Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod will now inform the
Commons that the Speech of His Excellency
the Lord Lieutenant General and General
Governor of Ireland is to be communicated
in the Senate forthwith and will invite
them to attend in order to hear the said
Speech.
The Commons having attended with their
Speaker at the Bar of the Senate.
The Speaker read His Majesty's Speech
as follows:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons —
some
Your Parliament has been established for
months, and the legal process of
transferring services to the control of the
Government of Northern Ireland has now
been completed. I regret that the transfer
of staff and the due allocation of property
and records to the Departments of Government have not proceeded as expeditiously
as might have been desired, and I trust
that means may shortly be devised for removing the causes of delay and enabling
your Departments to cope with the difficult
administrative problems which necessarily
confront a new Government.
I congratulate you on the manner in
which, in spite of these obstacles, the Departments of Government have been
brought so rapidly into smooth working
order.
I regret that Northern Ireland is still
faced with the serious problem of unemployment, but am confident that the
Ministry of Labour is giving the matter
constant and sympathetic consideration.
The spirit of conciliation shown in Northern Ireland in the settlement of industrial disputes is tending towards a more
satisfactory state of affairs, and I trust
that spirit will continue to animate all
classes of the community.
I am glad to know that, acting in conformity with a decision arrived at by the
British Cabinet, the Cabinet of Northern
Ireland has decided to grant an additional
six weeks' benefit during the present
period of special distress.
Members of the House of Commons-
The estimates of expenditure on the
Services for the ensuing financial year will
be laid before you in due course, but owing
to the fact that your finance is largely
dependent upon that of the Imperial Par-
A
ت

King's Speech.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE.
liament, it will only be possible to make
partial and tentative estimates until the
Imperial Budget for the coming year is
available. Every effort has been made by
the Government to reduce expenditure to
the lowest possible limit consistent with
the security of the Province and the efficiency of its public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons-
It is hoped to lay before you measures
dealing with the following matters :-
The conferring of such additional powers
on the Government of Northern Ireland
as are necessary for the prevention of
crime, preservation of peace, and the
maintenance of order.
The continuance of the issue of Savings
Certificates.
The amalgamation of the General Nursing Council with the Central Midwives'
Board for Northern Ireland.
The amendment of the Intermediate
Education (Ireland) Acts.
The Breeding of Cattle.
The Imperial Government is introducing
legislation for making permanent the
principle of Daylight Saving. It will be
for you to consider whether in this matter
you will remain in conformity with the
Mother Country.
It is hoped that the Committees dealing
with the improvement of Education, the
establishment of the Constabulary, the
system of Trade Boards and the Employment Exchanges in Northern Ireland will
shortly complete their labours. In that
event it may be found possible to introduce
for giving effect to their
measures
recommendations.
I pray that Almighty God may bestow
His abundant blessing upon your deliberations.
Senate adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed at Half-past Two of
the Clock.
MINISTRY OF COMMERCE.
The minute of the Minister of Commerce
assigning Powers and Duties of Departmental
Officers under the Ministries of Northern
Ireland Act, 1921, was laid before the House
(pursuant to Act), and ordered to lie on the
Table.
LOCAL BILL.
Mr. Speaker acquainted the House that the
Clerk of the Senate had laid upon the Table
the certificate from the Examiner that the
Standing Orders applicable to the following
Bill have been complied with:-
Belfast Harbour.
Mr. Speaker having read the King's
Speech,
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move that a humble
Address be presented to His Excellency in
the following words:-
-
Your Excellency, we, His Majesty's most
dutiful and loyal subjects, the Senate of
the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to your
Excellency for the Speech which your
Excellency has addressed to both Houses
of Parliament.
His Majesty, in the beginning of his
Speech, congratulated the Government of
Northern Ireland on the manner in which,
in spite of many obstacles, the departments
of Government had been brought so rapidly
into smooth working order. Everyone who
knows, as we Members of both Houses do,
the serious and almost insuperable difficulties which the Prime Minister and his
Government encountered on proceeding to
take up the reins of Government of Northern
Ireland under the Act of 1920, will join
heartily in these congratulations and will
have admiration as well for their courage
in facing the difficulties in the way they
have done, as for the patience and per
tinacity with which they have already
accomplished so much of their task, unfortunately not yet completed. It is to
be fervently hoped that some means may
be found of removing those obstacles that
still remain, so that our Parliament may
have a fair chance of carrying out successfully the important and responsible duties
that have been imposed upon it by the
Imperial Parliament. Under any ordinary
manufacturing undertaking it would be
taken for granted that, before any working
or manufacturing is begun, there would at
any rate be a factory built and equipped
and all the necessary machinery completed
down to the very last bolt, but when the
Northern Parliament was set in being our
Prime Minister and his colleagues had to
set to work first to provide suitable buildings
in which to hold meetings and accommodate
Members of Parliament. They had also to
create the various departments of State and
group these departments under several Ministers. They then had to arrange for the
transfer of staff to these departments and
I gather from the reference in the King's
Speech to that matter that it has been found
to be full of difficulty. I may say I am
not surprised, because the success of a department depends very largely on the kind
5 Debate on Address.
14 MARCH 1922.

2388 Debate on Address.
LILLARY

IT OF ILLIWOR
way
will she be able to go forward on her
with confidence and self respect.
mented in the King's Speech. I gather
I observe that the Daylight Saving Bill is

from what is stated here that an Act of
Parliament will be passed by the Imperial
Government in England which will deal
with this question, and will settle the question for Ulster unless the Parliament of
Ulster chooses to make some other Act of
Parliament altering or amending it. This
is a question on which there will, of
course, be a sharp divergence of opinion between agriculturalists and persons engaged
in other industries, or to put it more briefly,
between town and country. For my part, I
hope that if the Imperial Parliament should
bring in a Daylight Saving Bill on the lines
of previous Bills it will be accepted in
Ulster as well as in England. It would mean
a very serious dislocation if the time in England and Scotland differed from that in Ulster.
I hope the result will be that the town will
take a deeper interest in the country, to the
great advantage of both. There is mention
made in the King's Speech of the continuance of the system of Savings Certificates. I
do not quite know what is meant by that,
but I presume that what used to be known
as War Savings Certificates are now to be
issued in slightly different form and to be
just Savings Certificates, and I think it is
an excellent idea. From a considerable experience of those Saving Certificates I know
they were an excellent means of inducing
the working classes and small shopkeepers
to save. In connection with a centre in
Kensington I noticed a very large number
of people were very glad to come and purchase their 15s 6d certificates. A considerable amount of revenue was raised in that
way. It was then used for war purposes,
but I understand the revenue will now be
used for the general public services of
Ulster. I would hail with great satisfaction
the passing of that measure.
of staff they have got to work it. And when
one wants to take away some members of
the staff from another department, the other
department is not likely to give them the
best men or with any great gush of welcome.
So I can see quite well how difficulties may
have arisen. The staff themselves may have
wished to have some say in the matter
whether they should come to Northern Ireland or stay where they were. Therefore,
we can all see how difficult the matter is
to arrange. For my own part, I think the
selections of the staffs have been most
fortunate up to the present time.
addition to the matter of staff they have
had to arrange for the transfer to Northern
Ireland of the documents and records which
have been kept in another place.
that must take time. And then they had to
provide for the departments, such as the
establishment of Courts of Justice and so
forth, and other matters too numerous to
mention. But at the same time they
had to steer the ship of State safely
and securely through more dangerous seas
than have ever been encountered in all the
previous history of this province. For
those achievements I certainly think the
Government deserves to get our most profound congratulations. The serious problem
of industrial unrest consequent on unemployment which is looming so largely throughout
the world at the present time, and from
which, unfortunately, Ulster is not exempt,
is also referred to in the gracious Speech,
and we have every confidence that the
Members of our Cabinet who are intimately
acquainted with the situation as it exists
here among us, are approaching the
matter already in a spirit of conciliation
and fair play all round. I have every hope
that they may discover some satisfactory
and permanent solution of the question in
Ulster. I say satisfactory and permanent,
but I think unless it is permanent it would
not really be satisfactory. Dangers and
difficulties arising every two or three years
would be a very bad thing indeed. So I
hope we may be able to discover some permanent and satisfactory solution of the
question. I am glad to say that in the
meantime, as stated in the gracious Speech,
they have arranged to extend the period of
benefit grant for six weeks owing to the
special distress which is being experienced
at the present time. I observe that the
gracious Speech holds out the prospect of a
wide field of legislation during the coming
Session or in the near future, and by far the
most important measure to come before us
is one to confer additional powers on the
Government of Northern Ireland for the
prevention of crime. Ulster can never
prosper until respect for law and order,
security of person and property are absolutely established throughout the entire area.
To ensure this, it must be definitely brought
home to every malefactor that punishment,
sure, swift, and adequate, will inevitably
follow the commission of crime that there
will be no longer divided responsibility in
carrying out the law, and the punishment
of criminals, and that the taking of human
life unjustifiably is a crime that can in no
case be condoned. In my opinion then, and
then only, will crime cease in Ulster, and
The last subject to which I wish to refer
is that of educational reform, one of the
most important in the Government's programme. Many changes, chiefly in administration, are urgently required, and we are
much gratified to And that the matter is being taken in hand by the Noble Marquess,
the Leader of the House, who will, I am
quite certain, go into it in a statesmanlike
way. A man of his experience is sure to find
