On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have
agreed to the said Bill with some amendments, to which they desire the concurrence
of the Commons.
Arson in the City of Belfast.
Mr. LESLIE, I rise to ask the Noble
Marquess a question of which I have given
private notice. My question is: whether the
Leader of the House can give the number
of cases of arson in the City of Belfast during
the months of April, May, and June of this
year. I am asking the question because I
have seen it stated or suggested that the
number of fires in the city during the three
months I have mentioned is something over
400, and it is suggested that not less
than nine out of ten of these fires
have been due to arson, or as it is
called in Scotlland, fire raising, and in Scotland at present the death penalty is still
in force in these cases, although I believe it
has not been enforced for a great many years.
The figures in themselves are very disquieting
-407 fires in three months, that is approximately one fire for every 1,000 inhabitants
of Belfast. If that be true, and if it be true
that such a large proportion is due to deliberate and wanton arson, an index of the
ferocity and the recklessness of those opposed
to law and order is afforded which should not
be overlooked by those who watch the course
of events in Northern Ireland. Happily
there is a brighter side to the picture. That
a larger number of the inhabitants of the
City have not been rendered homeless and a
larger proportion of the factories and magazines of commerce have not been destroyed
can only be due to the vigilance of the wardens of our peace and to the extraordinary
efforts which must have been made by the
Belfast Fire Brigade. It is obvious, having
regard to the limited resources of those responsible for the Brigade, that the method
of organisation and discipline must be beyond
praise. In a city like Belfast fire once
started, can easily spread, and must be very
hard to subdue, and we owe all the greater
thanks to those to whose efforts it is due
that greater damage has not been effected.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
(Leader of the House): I am much obliged
to the Hon. Member for having given notice
of the question which he was desirous of
asking, although I regret he did not give me
notice of the information which he was anxious to gain on this point. I am happy to
be able to give him the figures which he
asks for with regard to the number of cases
of arson in the City of Belfast during the
months of April, May, and June. In the
month of April there were 11 cases; in the
month of May there were 39; and in the
month of June there were 32, which make
82 cases in all. That I am glad to say
answers that part of the question, in which
it was stated there was an idea that there
had been something like 400 cases of arson
throughout the City of Belfast. He (Mr.
Leslie) went on to speak of the general state
of law and order in the City of Belfast and
mentioned the Scottish law, of which I have
no doubt he is as great an exponent as he is
of the law under which we live.
Arson in Belfast.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
It affords me great pleasure to answer
my hon. friend, inasmuch as the question
gives me the opportunity of voicing the
general recognition that exists of the admirable work which has been accomplished
in combating the dastardly attacks of the
fire raisers, not only in the city of Belfast,
but throughout the whole Northern Area.
This campaign of arson has naturally been
most intensive in the great city of Belfast
where the means of effecting such outrage
are nearer to the hand of the miscreant, the
prospects of extensive damage more encouraging to him, and the avenues of escape more
easy.
I certainly am at a loss to find words of
praise adequate to the magnificent work in
countering the organised arson of the
enemies of this province performed by our
Police Force and our Firemen.
The numbers of the cases of arson would
have been much greater but for the unceasing activities of the Police, as, according
to documents which have been seized, there
was an organised plan for wholesale burnings in the city.
With regard to the work of those engaged
in actual combat with the flames, the
courage and devotion to duty of all ranks
of the Fire Brigade have been unsurpassed.
By the wakeful and unceasing efforts of the
Firemen, under their Chief, Mr. James
Stafford, property of enormous value in the
aggregate has been saved from destruction.
We owe them a debt of gratitude, and I
take this, the first opportunity that has
arisen, and I am grateful to my hon. Friend
for having given me the opportunity, to
acknowledge the fact and set it on record.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I would move that this House adjourn
during pleasure for the purpose of waiting
until such time as we receive a message
from the Commons agreeing to the Bills
which we have just passed down to them.
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Three
of the Clock, during pleasure.
The Senate resumed at twenty minutes after
Three of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
Message from the Commons.
That they have agreed to the amendments
made by the Senate to the Live Stock Breeding Bill and the Local Government Bill without amendment.
Royal Assent.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I have been authorised by the Prime Minister on his behalf to inform the Senate that
his Excellency the Lord Lieutenant in the
name and on behalf of his Majesty the
King has been pleased to give his assent to
the following Bills :-
Live Stock Breeding.
Church Temporalities Fund.
Appropriation.
National Health Insurance.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn, I think I should be acting with the
agreement of the Members of this House if
I make the suggestion that we adjourn until
such time as you, Mr. Speaker, call us together again
Mr. LESLIE : I want to take advantage
of this opportunity of making two small
suggestions of which I have not given the
Noble Marquess notice and therefore I do not
ask or expect an immediate answer. I want
to ask whether it would not be possible to get
the Debates printed and issued rather more
quickly than is done at present. I also want
to suggest that some means might be found
of indicating on a Bill when it comes to us
from another place if the Bill is a money
Bill. It will be within the recollection of
the House that we have this week dealt with
three Bills, one of which was the Appropriation Bill, which was obviously a money
Bill, another, the Church Temporalities Bill,
which was held to be a money Bill, but with
regard to which we might perhaps have been
pardoned for not at first apprehending its
nature, and the other a Bill which had a
money clause. I venture to suggest that it
might be convenient to us if some indication
appeared on the outside of the Bill, on the
back, which would give us a sort of warning
that the Bill in question was one which ought
not to be amended.
THE MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I can only speak again by leave of the House.
I have naturally noted what the hon. Member
has said with regard to the two questions
he has brought forward. In regard to the
first one I can assure him I will make such
representations in the proper quarter as to
whether the printing of the Debates can be
expedited in any way. As regards the second
point I do not feel competent at this moment
to deal with it because I have had no notice.
I am not aware what steps we can take to
satisfy his wishes. I can assure him I will
consult the proper authorities.
233 Malicious Injury Claims.
5 JULY 1922 Malicious Injury Claims. 234
Malicious Injuries Compensation Claims.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In answer to the question raised
by my hon. Friend to my right (Mr. Long),
I would like to inform him that the Government were not given any notice of this
question, which is one we cannot possibly
answer on the spur of the moment. But I
can assure the hon. Gentleman that this
question of compensation for malicious injuries, which is a very big one and a very important one, continues to have the attention
of the Prime Minister and his Government,
and is in fact engaging his attention very
seriously at the moment. Beyond that I cannot at present give my hon. Friend any further answer to the point.
Mr. LONG: I wish to call the attention of
the Noble Lord the Leader of the House to
the enormous sums claimed and awarded in
malicious injury cases in the Six Counties.
The prevalent idea is that the Imperial Government is justly entitled to pay not only
the 60 per cent which they have promised
to do, but that they are as well entitled
to pay the entire sum. Now it would tend
to allay the grave concern that the ratepayers
feel in the serious situation in which they
are placed if the Noble Marquess would
undertake to make representations to the
authorities at Westminster that we feel that
they should come to our aid in this matter.
If he with his great influence and that of the
Northern Parliament, would do so I feel that
it would relieve the feelings of the ratepayers
who are greatly concerned. It is imperatively
necessary that this should be done, I feel,
if we are to live and thrive in the land of
our birth. I should like if he could give
this matter his attention.
On Question, Motion (That this House do
now adjourn) agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Half after
Three of the Clock, until such day
as the Speaker may appoint.
Adjournment.
Royal Assent.

THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 17th October, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
ROYAL ASSENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am authorised by the Prime Minister, on his
behalf, to inform the Senate that His
Excellency the Lord Lieutenant in the name
and on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the following Bill:
Local Government.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg leave to present a Bill to deal with
certain expiring laws with respect to
Northern Ireland, making some of them
permanent, repealing others, and continuing
the remainder for a limited period. I beg
to move that this Bill be now read a First
time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
Perhaps for the convenience of the House
I might say that it is proposed to take the
Second Reading of the Expiring Laws Bill
on Thursday, and the remaining stages on
Friday. I do not know whether hon. Members would desire to know the proceedings
of this House after that date, but I expect
we shall find it necessary to sit on the first
four days of next week to get through the
business that will come up to us from
another place. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
Minutes after Three of the Clock.
Education Report.
Expiring Laws Bill
19 OCTOBER 1922
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 19th October, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
Message from the Commons.
Message from the Commons: That they
have passed the Solicitors Bill and the
Finance Bill to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
The Solicitors Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First

The Finance Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

EDUCATION COMMITTEE INTERIM
REPORT.
Mr. GREER: I beg to ask the Leader of the
House whether an opportunity will be given
to the House to discuss the Interim Report
of the Departmental Committee on the
Educational Services before the Introduction of a Bill dealing with these matters;
and whether it is contemplated that such
Bill will be introduced during this Session?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): In view of the
necessary curtailment of the present
Session the time at the disposal of the
Government is very restricted, and perhaps
under the circumstances I may ask my hon.
Friend to assist us by withdrawing his
present request. I can assure the hon.
Member that the Government has no desire
to burke discussion, and ample opportunity
will be afforded for debate when our legislative proposals for Education are produced.
I think personally that a discussion upon
concrete proposals will be
infinitely
preferable and more fruitful than any
academic debate upon the Report. Meanwhile, if the hon. Member still wishes for
this discussion, I will make the best
arrangement I am able, in view of the very
restricted time at our disposal, to meet his
wishes.
The answer to the second part of the
question is in the negative. The Bill will
be introduced at the earliest possible date
during the Session in the New Year.
Mr. GREER: I am quite satisfied with the
answer which I have received to my
question. I should like to make it clear
that I had no idea in my mind of forcing a
discussion when they were not prepared. I
did not mean anything of that kind in the
question which I put. I think that it
would be very desirable that we should
have a full debate, and I am sure that the
noble Lord will be very glad to give us an
opportunity at the proper time. I understand it is considered that the proper time
would be when the Bill is introduced into
the House rather than upon the Interim
Report. I am perfectly satisfied.

Bill.

Expiring Laws Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In rising to move the Second reading of the
Expiring Laws
Bill, I will ask the
indulgence of the House if I detain it for
a little in explaining a measure over which
there is usually no discussion whatsoever.
The Bill, as its title clearly shows, deals in
varying manner with the various measures
which, were it not for a continuing Bill of
this nature, would cease to have effect.
A legislature often finds that it is
necessary to pass measures of a temporary
character. Hon. Members will realise the
necessity for this. An Act may be passed
to deal with an occurrence such as war with
a foreign State or civil commotion within
the State itself, occurrences which are
mercifully of
temporary and not a
permanent nature, and which, therefore,
demand legislation of
like passing
Character. Again, a law may introduce a
new departure or involve a drastic change
of policy, such as should in the first
a
a
Expiring Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
instance only be tried experimentally. In
this way there has arisen the necessity for
keeping a register of laws of this temporary
nature in order that provision may be made
from time to time for the continuance of
such laws, either in whole or in part, as
should not be allowed to expire, whether by
reason of the continuance of a state of
affairs which gave rise to their inception
or to the proven nature of their utility.
The adoption of this system-at any rate for
a time by the Northern Ireland Parliament
is unavoidable. This is so, in the first place,
because the Government of Ireland Act provided in general terms that all existing laws
in Northern Ireland-that is, both the common law and the statutes of the United Kingdom Parliament should continue in force.
Amongst these laws is included an entire
series of temporary laws expiring on various
dates.
In the second place the Parliament of
Northern Ireland has itself enacted several
temporary statutes to deal with what I may
call emergency conditions. Thus, the bestowal of special powers upon the Minister
of Home Affairs with respect to law and
order, and the power to enable recalcitrant
local authorities to be dissolved. The vast
majority of the temporary laws in force in
Northern Ireland will expire either at the
end of the present year or shortly afterwards, and it is therefore necessary to deal
with them at the present sittings of
Parliament.
'The Bill which is now before the House
divides these temporary laws into three
main groups, and deals with them
accordingly:-
Clause 1 takes a group of Acts which have
-so far as can be seen found a place in
the permanent administration of the
country, such as the Employers' Liability
Act, 1880, and the Motor Car Act, 1903, and
instead of providing for their further
temporary continuance they are now to be
made permanent. This course has already
been adopted in respect of these particular
Acts, so far as they apply to Great Britain.
Hon. Members will see this if they refer to
the first Schedule.
Clause 2 deals with certain enactments
which are temporary in their essence, for
example, a provision for bringing into operation the National Insurance Act, 1911, and a
provision for constituting
temporary
court which has
become operative.
These appear in the Second and in the
Fourth Schedules.
never
a
Clause 3 continues temporarily certain
enactments which may be regarded as being
still experimental or liable to revision. Two
dates only have been adopted for
tinuance, viz., 31st December, 1923, and 31st
March, 1924. The former date has been
conadopted to secure uniformity, as far as
possible, with what has been done for Great
Britain by the recent Expiring Laws Act
passed there. The latter date has been
adopted in order to allow ample time for
consideration of two groups of measures :-
First of all two statutes under which a
Government Department can deal with
charges payable by the public in respect of
services such as tramways, harbours, etc.,
which could otherwise only be dealt with
by Parliament itself. These are referred to
in the Third Schedule. Secondly, Two
Northern Ireland emergency statutes which
would otherwise expire respectively in
December and April next. These appear in
the Fifth Schedule.
It may be noted that the Acts mentioned
in Part Five of the 3rd Schedule have
technically lapsed during the Parliamentary
recess, and sub-section (3) of Clause 3
provides respectively for their continuance as from the dates upon which they
expired. The Acts which are being continued will be found in the various parts
of the 3rd and 5th Schedules.
Clause 4 is inserted in order to ensure
that all amending enactments are to follow
the course of the temporary enactments
which they amend.
Clause 5 deals with the construction of
the measure. Sub-section (1) contains the
Short Title. Sub-section (2) ensures that
where United Kingdom Statutes are
referred to, the reference includes any
adaptations of those statutes which have
been made in pursuance of the powers comtained in the Government of Ireland Act.
Sub-section (3) deals with cases where an
Act relates in part to matters within the
powers of the Northern Ireland Parliament,
and in part to matters with respect to which
the Parliament of the United Kingdom retains the power. It has been framed so as
to fit in with the co-ordinate provision in
the recent English Act, under which that
Act only applies to Northern Ireland, to
use the words of the Act, "in so far as it
deals with any enactment relating to a subject with respect to which the Parliament
of Northern Ireland has not power to make
laws."
The two measures, taken together, deal
with all temporary laws applicable to
Northern Ireland, according as they are
within the power of one or other of the two
Parliaments. I will not further detain the
House by a particular reference to each
separate
mentioned in the five
Schedules to this Bill, but if hon. Members desire to raise any question in regard
to any of these measures in the course of
debate, I shall be glad to furnish all th
information and assistance that I have at
my disposal.
measure
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
Message from the Commons.

have passed the Unemployment Insurance

(Elections and Constitution) Bill, to which
they desire the concurrence of this House.
Unemployment Insurance (Amendment) Bill.

time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Local Authorities (Elections and
Constitution) Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving that this House do now adjourn
I feel hon. Members will remember that at
our
last meeting, in the forecast of the
course of future business which I gave on
that occasion I stated that it would be
necessary for this House to meet to-morrow,
Friday, and on Monday, Tuesday and
Wednesday of next week. We endeavour,
as hon. Members are aware, to make a rule
with regard to the convenience of Members
of this House that we should sit, if it was
possible to arrange SO, on Tuesdays,
Wednesdays, and Thursdays, and not on
Friday or on Monday. By arrangement of
the work I am able to announce, if it suits
the convenience of hon. Members, that we
do not now find it necessary to sit tomorrow, though it will be necessary for the
House to sit on Monday.
We propose, therefore, to transfer the remaining stages of the Bill, of which I have
just had the privilege of moving the Second
reading, to Monday, and to take this Bill
then together with any other measures
which may come before us from another
place. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty-five
minutes before Four of the Clock
until Monday, 23rd October.
Expiring Laws Bill. 214
Oral Answer.
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Monday, 23rd October, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
MINISTRY OF AGRICULTURE AND
STORMONT.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I rise to
ask the Leader of the House whether it has
been decided to locate the staff of the Ministry of Agriculture at Stormont, and if so
whether, in view of the great inconvenience
which will be caused thereby to farmers
having business to transact with the Department, the Government will reconsider their
decision, and provide accommodation for the
Ministry of Agriculture in the city of Belfast.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD
(for the Marquess of Londonderry): In the
unavoidable absence of my noble Friend the
Leader of the House, who, as hon. Members
are aware, has gone over to London to attend
a very important political meeting, I will
answer the question put by the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman opposite. The answer
is as follows:-It has been decided that, in
common with other Ministries, with the exception of the Ministry of Commerce, the
headquarters staff of the Ministry of Agriculture shall be located at Stormont; but
that it shall be open to the Ministry of
Agriculture to take advantage of the facilities afforded to the other Ministries for
having accommodation in the city. In order,
therefore, to meet the convenience of farmers
coming to Belfast on official business,
arrangements have been made to provide
Conference and Committee Rooms in the
centre of the city.
Expiring Laws Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to

The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair.

be made permanent); Clause 2 (Certain Acts
to be repealed); Clause 3 (Certain Acts to be
continued temporarily); Clause 4 (Amending
Enactments); Clause 5 (Short Title and Construction), stand part of the Bill."
Ordered: "That the First Schedule (Enactments of the United Kingdom Parliament
made permanent), the Second Schedule
(Enactments of the United Kingdom Parliament repealed), the Third Schedule (Enactments of the United Kingdom Parliament
continued in their duration), the Fourth
Schedule (Enactments of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland repealed), and the Fifth
Schedule (Enactments of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland continued in their
duration), be the Schedules of the Bill."
Ordered: "That the Preamble and Title
be the Preamble and Title of the Bill."
Ordered: "That this Bill be reported to
the House without amendments."
Ordered: "That this House do now resume."
The House resumed accordingly, and the
Expiring Laws Bill was reported to the House
without amendment.
Ordered: "That this Report be now
received."
SUSPENSION OF STANDING ORDER.

and Ferrard.)

Solicitors Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In rising to move the Second reading of the
Solicitors Bill, it will only be necessary for
me to detain the House for a short time, as
it is evident that the present proposals are a
necessary corollary to the Government of
Ireland Act of 1920 which separated the
Courts of Justice of Northern and Southern
Ireland. Like the medical profession, the
legal profession is regarded as the custodian
of its own honour, and this present measure
Solicitors Bill-
Second Reading.
23 OCTOBER 1922
implements the Royal Charter granted on the
10th of July last to the Incorporated Law
Society of Northern Ireland the representative body of the profession in this Province.
This society, therefore, now takes the place
in Northern Ireland of the Incorporated
Law Society of Ireland, the body which has
functioned hitherto for the whole of Ireland.
It will be agreed, I am confident, that it
would be impossible for a State possessing its
own Judiciary to leave the training and
discipline of a body of men so indispensable
to the administration of justice in the hands
of any society extern to the Province.
I will not trouble the House by speaking
upon the Clauses in their order, for it is
clear that, with a few modifications for local
needs, this Bill is merely a re-enactment of
the Solicitors (Ireland) Act of 1898. But I
shall be pleased to give such assistance as
lies within my power if, in the course of
debate, any hon. Member desires to raise a
point or requires an explanation. I beg
to move.
Mr. GREER: As this Bill vitally affects
the interests of the profession to which I
belong I should like to support it in a few
words. Before the passing of the Act of 1920
the whole profession of solicitors in Northern
Ireland was governed by the Incorporated
Law Society of Ireland. They acted under
a Royal Charter given by Act of Parliament
in 1859, which conferred upon them statutory
powers to exercise certain functions with
regard to bringing apprentices into the profession and dealing with any cases that might
arise from time to time in reference to
solicitors and other matters. After the
passing of the Act it became impossible for
the Incorporated Law Society of Ireland to
exercise any jurisdiction over the solicitors
in the Northern part of Ireland, and accordingly it was necessary for a similar body
to be established with similar functions for
Northern Ireland. I may say, in passing,
that the Incorporated Law Society of Ireland
acted through an Executive Committee which
was called the Council of the Incorporated
Law Society, and for many years past I
have been a member of that Council as a
delegate annually elected to represent the
Province of Ulster; and it is with feelings
of no little regret that I have now come to
the parting of the ways and know that it is
likely that I shall no longer be able to represent that Society. They have done very
good work in the past, and I wish them
success in the future.
To come to our Northern Province, the
whole Judiciary has been re-arranged, and it
is absolutely necessary that some body should
be established to make arrangements for
bringing young men into the profession, for
having lectures given in law and practice and
so forth, for seeing that they attain a certain
amount of proficiency in law before they are
admitted as solicitors, and for various other
matters in connection with solicitors, and the
profession has now obtained a Royal Charter
to take charge of these matters under the
name of the Incorporated Law Society of
Northern Ireland. Other institutions of
more importance in the country have had a
Government thrown upon them which they
did not wish to have. They are now trying
to do their best to govern. We are in exactly
the same position. We have had this duty
cast upon us against our will. Now, it lies
upon us to do the best we can to arrange in
the interests of Northern Ireland matters
concerning our profession.
The only thing I would like to say before
sitting down is just this: The Incorporated
Law Society of Ireland was in possession of
very fine buildings with an Examination
Hall, Lecture rooms, and a very excellent
Law Library, a number of rooms required
for the Secretariat, and various consultation
rooms, and last but not least a very fine
refreshment room. Now, we have lost all
these things, and we shall require to set up
buildings, and examination rooms and so on
for ourselves. I think as we have been deprived of the benefit of these establishments
by the act of the British Government, carried out for purposes unconnected with our
profession, we have a very good claim against
the British Government for a grant to enable
us to provide ourselves with the necessary
buildings. I do hope that when the time
arises the Government of Northern Ireland
will give us all the assistance they can in
endeavouring to bring our case before the
British Government for compensation.
Lord MASSERTENE: I hardly think it is
necessary for me to say anything in this
House after the gratifying speech of the hon.
Member who has just sat down. As regards
the question of compensation, doubtless that
will be brought before the Government in
due course, and I have no doubt it will
receive a syn pathetic hearing.
On Question, Motion "That the Bill be
read
Second time," agreed to.
a
Local Authorities (Elections and
Constitution) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Bill which I now have the
honour to present to this House for a Second
Reading contains the provisions which the
Government feel to be consequential to the
delay which took place before the Local
Government Bill which we passed at our last
meeting received the Royal Assent, and so
took its place upon our Statute Book. And
247 Local Authorities Bill-

THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
in reading the present Bill I must ask hon.
Members to bear in their minds the provisions
of that Local Government Act.
As my hon. Friend, the Parliamentary
Secretary to the Ministry of Home Affairs,
said in his lucid exposition of this Bill on
the Second Reading Debate in another place,
there were ihree alteratives before the
Government in view of the situation that
arose owing to our Local Government proposals becoming law on the 11th of September
last, instead of at the end of June or early in
July. We might have proceeded with the
elections on the basis of the old Electoral
Divisions in existence before the Proportional
Representation Act. We might have asked
Parliament to allow us to proceed with Proportional Representation for another year;
or we might have taken the course which we
are taking now, and proposed legislation
postponing these elections so far as County
Councils and Rural Districts are concerned.
The first course we refused to adopt, because we have no intention of employing
again a systera of distribution which was laid
down some eighty years ago. It would only
be confusing the issue were we to abandon
Proportional Representation in one election,
and in the next make yet another change.
Against the second course there was the very
decided judgment of the House upon the
system of Proportional Representation. It
Lad been tried upon the dog, and the dog was
sick of it. And, therefore, we ask the House
to allow us to adopt the third course, namely.
a postponerient of these elections.
The chief argument for this course is the
urgency of redistribution. It will be remembered that when the Local Government
Bill was introduced, it was evident on examination that the existing system of
District Electoral Divisions required alteration, and the Local Government Act provides
that in reverting to the old County Electoral
Divisions the Ministry of Home Affairs should
have regard to population and valuation,
and although there was no similar provision
regarding District Electoral Divisions, I am
sure that this House will agree that it is a
cardinal and unavoidable principle that the
District Electoral Divisions in Northern
Ireland should, as far as possible, be made
uniform. Let me cite a few instances in
proof of the need of redistribution.
We find on examination of the last Census
Returns that Armagh Rural Electoral
Killeen Division has only 869. Similarly in
Killeen Divison has only 869. Similarly in
the Newry No. 2 Rural District the population of Camlough Division is 3,935 as against
the population of 969 in Tullyhappy.
County Down we find the same inequalities.
One electoral division, Crossgar, in Ban-
In
bridge Rural District, had only 675 inhabitants as against 3,282 in Tullylish, and the
same inequalities obtain in practically every
Rural District in that County.
A more glaring inequality in Belleek Rural
District in Fermanagh County is Mallybreen
District Electoral Division, whch has only
143 inhabitants, and had the same representation on the Rural District Council as Inishmacsaint with 2,036 inhabitants. Similarly
in Lisnaskea Rural District, Kilturk Division
has 165 inhabitants as against Rosslea with
2,158. In Londonderry we find the Six Towns
Division, with a population of 629, having the
same representation on the Magherafelt
Council as the Castledawson Division with a
population of 2,786. In every District Electoral Division in Tyrone we find the same
inequalities in each Rural District.
With these facts before them the Government, in sub-section 3 of Section 7 of the
Local Government Act, extended the date
for altering the District Electoral Divisions
to the 15th of November, 1922, thus relieving
itself of the disability placed upon it by subsection 6 of Section 68 of the Local Government Act of 1898, which made the 1st of May
the last date in any year for such alterations
in respect of such elections in that year. But
the delay over which we had no control, and
for which we were not responsible in placing
the Act upon the 'Statute Book, made it impossible for the redistribution to be ready
by the date mentioned in the Act, namely,
November 15th. The same cause which militates for the postponement of the Rural
District elections, namely, the necessity for
redistribution of the District Electoral
Divisions makes for the necessary postponement of County Council elections, as the
County Electoral Divisions are based primarily upon the District Electoral Divisions.
We are not postponing the elections in the
Municipal areas, because there are not the
same difficulties. There are not the same
anomalies here, and our general principle is
to re-establish the wards and other electoral
areas which existed prior to 1919, and examine
any special anomalies in time to effect necessary alterations before the Municipal elections in January next.
The Bill is a very short one. Clause 1 provides for the postponement. Clause 2, subsection 1, facilitates the application of the
provisions of this Bill, while the 2nd and 3rd
sub-sections are merely re-enactments of the
provisions of the Local Government Act of
1898 in regard to the dates of annual meetings of Local Authorities. Clause 3 is purely
interpretative.
Before I resume my seat I should like to
remind the House that there can be nothing
oppressive, at least from the point of view
of those persons who are so urgent against the
249 Local Authorities Bill-
Second Reading.
23 ОСТОВER 1922

abandonment of Proportional Representation
in order to secure what they deem is a just
representation of minorities, if by passing
this Bill we secure the existence for a further
year of conditions which are the results of
elections carried through upon the very basis
of Proportional Representation which they
uphold. I beg to move.
Sir JAMES JOHNSTON: In the Committee
stage of this Bill it is my intention to move
a provision under Clause 1: That this Section
shall apply to the Belfast City and District
Water Commissioners and to the existing
members of that body, and for the purpose
of this Section said Commissioners be deemed
to be a Local Authority.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which has been
brought before us in so lucid a manner by
the noble Viscount is not one which I think
is at all likely to occasion anything approaching controversy in this House. None of us
can by any possibility desire to perpetuate
the inequalities to which the noble Viscount
has alluded. On the contrary many of us
have had experience in our own areas of a
certain amount of discontent among our constituents on the part of those who consider
themselves inadequately represented, or
rather who consider that they have an inadequate share of representation on their
particular body. On the whole it is probable
that the existing members of the rural and
county councils will be glad of the fact
that the troubles of an election are put
off
for another year and that the
burdened ratepayer will also probably be
glad that the expense which is unavoidable
if the elections have to be held is postponed. Every thing is, I think, in favour
of a postponement. The only points that
occur to me that should be safeguarded are
the interests of the members of the council
who, having volunteered to serve for three
years, will now be required to serve for four.
As the ordinary law stands, a law introduced
by Privy Council Order of December, 1898,
it is impossible to retire from the office of
county councillor without paying a fine, that
fine being such fine not exceeding £50, as
may be provided by the standing orders of
the particular council, or in default of any
such provision, £25. It is on the face of it
apparently unjust to require a man to extend
his service, but I think the unfairness is
really apparent and will not be found to
exist in fact. I believe that in the case of
some county councils in this area penalties
on retirement have not been imposed. Therefore I think it will be found on examination
that the Ministry possesses powers conferred
upon it by the principal Act, the Local
Government Act, 1902, to which the noble
Viscount referred-I think it will be found
the powers conferred upon the Ministry are
sufficient to prevent any injustice actually
occurring, if indeed there should be any
member of any council who may desire to
vacate his seat after 31st May, at which
period his office naturally expires. I am
sure that having mentioned the matter 1
may rely on the most favourable consideration being given to it by the Government
in order that no possible sense of unfairness
may be left in the minds of anyone. There
is a precedent for extending the term of
office of our Local Authorities. During the
late war under the provisions of an Order
made under one of the Defence of the Realm
Regulations elections were postponed more
than once. I think some question of the
possibility of a fine being incurred must have
arisen then and been settled in some way
or another by the Ministry. I think, therefore, that there is not the smallest doubt
that ample means will be found to get over
the difficulties. I do not think it will be
necessary to move anything in the nature
of a formal amendment. I prefer to leave
the matter in the hands of the noble Lord.
On the whole I think there will be satisfaction throughout the area.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: Under present circumstances it is quite clear that this Bill
is necessary in the interests of many of the
supporters of the Government and his
Majesty's loyal subjects with regard to Local
Government in the future. I think, however, it is well to point out that many
of these loyal subjects, by whom most of
the rates are paid, are very much disappointed, and they feel that they are injured
by the necessity having arisen for the postponement of these elections. When the Local
Government Act of this year was passed they
had hoped that by the exercise of powers
given to the Ministry of Home Affairs under
that Act matters would be so arranged that
elections would be held at the usual time,
and that the grievances that they feel and
suffer under now would be redressed. In so
far as postponement has been made necessary
by the delay of the Royal Assent to this Act, I
desire to associate myself with the protests
that were made in another place against what
seems to us an altogether improper use of
the Royal prerogative, which in a matter of
this sort obviously should only be used in
compliance with the advice of his Majesty's
Ministers for Northern Ireland. Some
doubt has been felt as to whether, in
spite of this delay, the Ministry of
Home Affairs might not have used their
powers within the enlarged period provided
in the Act, but those of us who have gone
into the matter think it most desirable
that these powers should not be exercised in
a hurried manner, that they should be exercised with full time to see that they are
exercised in the very best form. Therefore
I think that although we greatly regretthose of His Majesty's subjects who are
Adjournment-
Offices of Agriculture
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Montgomery]
aggrieved that though we are disappointed
that the change cannot take place next year
we feel that on the whole the Government
is taking the proper step in postponing these
elections, so as to carry out their proposed
action under these powers in the most effective and complete manner. Under the circumstances I have great pleasure in supporting the motion.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I think I am right in saying that this is the
first time we have had the pleasure of hearing
the hon. Senator who has just sat down
speaking in this House, and if I may do so,
I should like to congratulate the hon. Member on his speech. As regards the question
raised by the right hon. Gentleman opposite
me (Sir James Johnston), I do not anticipate
that the Government will raise any objection
to the Amendment which he proposes to introduce in the Committee stage to-morrow.
With referenco to what the hon. Gentleman
from County Antrim (Mr. Leslie) stated, I
can assure him that under Standing Orders
the Ministry of Home Affairs can waive the
right to exact any penalty from a Member
retiring before his period expires. I have no
doubt that the Ministry of Home Affairs will
exercise that authority and waive the penalty.
On Question, Motion "That the Bill be now
read a Second time," agreed to.
Adjournment.
OFFICES OF THE MINISTRY OF
AGRICULTURE.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn
till 3-15 to-morrow." (Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard.)
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I rise for
a few minutes to express what I believe will
be the universal opinion of the agricultural
community-regret at the unsatisfactory reply
which the Government has thought it necessary to give to the question for which I was
responsible. The Prime Minister the other
day said that he wanted it to be clearly
understood that the Government was the
Government for the Six Counties, and was
not in any way more favourable to Belfast
interests than to country interests. I think
that an ounce of practice is worth many
pounds of precept. This is an occasion, I
think, where the Government might have
met agricultural interests The Ministry of
Commerce and the Ministry of Agriculture
are under the same Minister. The Ministry
of Commerce has the whole of its staff, the
whole of its personnel, located in Belfast.
The Ministry of Commerce deals with men
who are constantly in Belfast, and, as a rule,
are better off than the farming community.
The farming community is under the same
Minister, and yet the headquarters are put
out to Stormont. It is true that for the convenience of farmers coming to Belfast on
official business arrangements have been
made to provide conference and committee
rooms. But, when a farmer comes to Belfast,
he is not always able to make an appointment
beforehand. A man comes up with a load of
hay. He wants, perhaps, to get a permit to
bring a ram from Scotland. It is a terrible
thing to send him out to Stormont. As far
as this reply is concerned, it does not seem to
me that there is any arrangement made for
even one official in the city of Belfast, unless
a previous appointment has been made. As
to the room for committee meetings, the
Ulster Farmers' Union have a splendid room,
which would be available at any time, so that
all the Government proposes to do for the
farming community does not mean anything.
I cannot help hoping that, when they consider this more fully, they will see that it is
a hardship for a man coming from Tyrone,
or from any long distance, probably on business to Robson's, to be told, "Oh, you have
got to go out to Stormont." We are all delighted to think and hope that Stormont
will be a hapy home for the Prime Minister
for many years, but we do not want it to
become a white elephant. It is a paradise we
all look forward to from a distance, but it is
like another paradise to which we all hope to
go, but to which we all avoid going as long as
we possibly can. I am afraid the farming
community will not think the reply of the
noble Viscount on behalf of the Government
at all satisfactory. I sympathise with him
because I know he is in no way responsible
for it.
Mr. DICKSON: I just rise to support the
protest of my right hon. and gallant Friend
(Colonel Perceval-Maxwell) against the
establishment of all the Ministry's offices at
Stormont, with the exception of a conference
room in the City of Belfast. It means that
when men come up to town without an appointment they will not be able to get home
again if they have to go out there. The
Ministry of Agriculture is an educational
establishment, and agricultural men coming
to town want to go there for advice, and
they cannot go away out to Stormont merely
to ask a question. I agree with the right
hon, and gallant Gentleman in the protest
he has made, and I hope the Ministry will
consider the matter in a more favourable
light.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I also want to support what the right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell) has said.
I think it is most important that farmers
and their representatives should be able to
find the representatives of the headquarters
staff of the Ministry of Agriculture in a convenient place when they come to Belfast.
There are various members of that staff whom
they may want to interview on various sub-
Adjournment-
Offices of Agriculture,
23 OCTOBER 1922
jects. An interview would probably save
very troublesome correspondence, and, at any
rate, the farmer, who is entitled to all the
help he can get from this Ministry, is not
as a rule very good at writing letters and
carrying on a correspondence. I think if the
Ministry of Agriculture is to do good in the
country, as I am sure it desires to do good,
and as we all desire it should, it should be
more accessible to the farmer than it can
possibly be if the headquarters staff is
established out at Stormont.
Mr. GREER: I rise also to support the
protest which has been made by my right
hon. and gallant Friend (Colonel Perceval-
Maxwell). I have been asked by the Ulster
Farmers' Union to do so. I do not understand from them that they wish to have an
expensive set of offices set up in Belfast for
this Department, but what they wish is to
have some office in the city where there would
be a permanent clerk in attendance, who
could give farmers coming into the city information on various points that might arise,
or get various permits signed if necessary.
I think that could very conveniently be arranged by the Minister of Commerce. When
he is preparing the Department which he
is setting up in Belfast for that Ministry, I
think it ought to be very easy for him to set
aside a room in which not only deputations
might be received, but in which a permanent
clerk could attend for the purpose of doing
business with the farmers.
was
an
Mr. BARBOUR: While I have every
sympathy with trying to meet every reasonable demand of the farmers, I do feel that
some expression of sympathy with the
Government in this difficulty should be
made. As I remember it when the broad
question of the Government buildings was
raised, the Government spared no pains to
try to consult everybody, and to get the
benefit of the advice of local people who
should be in a position to know the very
best position for all these buildings; and
there
almost, unanimous vote in
favour of putting them out at Stormont.
With that in my mind I must say I am still
in sympathy with that choice, although
personally I could have wished it in another
place. Still the matter having been put in
the hands of experts to advise, I am perfectly willing to be guided by the advice of
those experts. I would like to appeal to this
House to realise that there is a great deal
to be said in favour of having the Government buildings in a place by themselves, and
in an atmosphere of their own. I have not
travelled a great deal, but I must say that
anyone who visits Ottawa must be impressed
by the fact that there is a town given up to
the government of Canada. The Government
buildings might very well have been placed
in Montreal, or Winnipeg, or Toronto, but
Ottawa was chosen, and I think everyone is
impressed by the atmosphere which prevails
in that town. Again, the largest Englishspeaking nation in the world-the United
States of America-find that they can run
their affairs very well in a town that is not
a very important commercial town, but one
which is almost set apart for Government
work. Those of us who hope that our Parliament is a permanent one must look forward,
and I think one could impress this generation and generations to come much more
effectively if when people go out to
visit their Parliament they find simple
buildings, nicely equipped,
and well
laid out. To my mind to reverse the
policy and to give to all sections of the community the boon now asked for on the part of
the farmer would mean that the visitor to
Belfast would run the risk of being more
impressed by the evidence of city government
than of Ulster government. It would be very
hard to-day for even our Government to contemplate the outlay necessary for a building
like the City Hall, and I think we can avoid
any competition with the city authorities by
removing out to Stormont. I am not sure
that the arrangement as set out by the Prime
Minister has failed, it is a step towards meeting the farmer, and before we embark on any
large buildings in Belfast I would be inclined
to try the experiment suggested here, and see
really if it does not go a very long way to
meet the farmers. We must also remember
that in a particular case where the Government had to go along and practically take
over a very large office building it caused a
great deal of inconvenience and has led to a
rather serious outery on the part of the
tenants who have been dispossessed. Therefore, I would venture to say that before we
bring too much pressure on the Government
to embark on any serious step in this
direction we might give a reasonable chance
to the experiment which has been suggested
by the Prime Minister.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
There has undoubtedly been a good deal of
criticism of the fact that the headquarters
of the Ministry of Agiculture is not to remain
in the city of Belfast. I should like to remind
hon. Members that Parliament decided at one
of its earliest meetings that the offices for the
Government of Northern Ireland should be at
Stormont, and it would be rather going back
on that decision if we were to have the headquarters of the various Ministries back again
in the city. I entirely associate myself with
what the hon. Member (Mr. Barbour) has
just said. I think it would undoubtedly give
the impression that the Government of Northern Ireland existed primarily for the city
of Belfast if all our offices were in
that city. I would also like humbly to
inform hon. Members that this office
Offices of Agriculture. 256
Adjournment-
THE SENATE.
a
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
of the Ministry of Agriculture in the
city will be controlled by responsible
officer, and surely it should not be
past the wit of man to devise an arrangement by which anyone who wishes to see
some responsible official can do so. After
all, we have a telephone system in this
country. However, though I cannot myself,
speaking on behalf of the Government, hold
out any hope that the suggestions of the
right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Perceval-Maxwell) and the other Members
who have spoken will be carried out, I will
bring their views very strongly before the
Minister of Agriculture, and perhaps the
question will be raised again at some future
date.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before I put the motion
for adjournment, I would like to say that I
think in future it would be more convenient
if hon. Members did not put a star opposite
their questions, and then they could debate
the question at the time it is raised instead
of having to raise it again on the motion for
the adjournment.
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn," put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
Minutes after Four O'Clock.
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
24 OCTOBER 1922
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 24th October, 1922.
'The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move that
Standing Order No. 25 be suspended for the
remainder of this week.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Finance Bill
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: In
rising to move the Second reading of the
Finance Bill I venture to draw the attention
of the House to the fact that the general purpose of this Bill is to assimilate certain
enactments in regard to Entertainments Duty,
Licence Duty for Mechanically-Propellea
Vehicles, certain Stamp Duties, and Estate
Duty to those now in force in Great Britain.
I do not think it is necessary for me to labour
the point that being, as we claim, an integral
part of the United Kingdom, it is essential
that the basis of our taxation should
nearly as possible be identical with the basis
of taxation prevailing across the water. That,
as I say, hon. Members will find is a principle that has guided my right hon. Friena
the Minister of Finance in framing the present measure.
as
By Sub-section (1), Clause 1, all payments
made in respect of the Entertainments Tax,
whether the tickets purchased beforehand for
an entertainment be subsequently used or no,
will go to swell the balance in our Treasury,
and not, as now, the balance of the promoter
of the entertainment.
Sub-section (2) of this Clause removes the
exemption from the Entertainments Tax
hitherto enjoyed by the spectators of certain
specific forms of entertainment, an exemption
found in Section 1, Sub-section (5) para. (d)
of Finance New Duties Act of 1916.
Clause 2 (a) provides that in cases where
Charitable Entertainments are given by the
proprietors of theatres, picture houses, and
sports clubs, these proprietors shall be
obliged, as in point of fact they do now as a
matter of honour without obligation, to pay
over to the charity for which the entertainment is given the sums of money which are
refunded to them as proprietors of a charitable entertainment.
Clause 2 (b) exactly follows the lead of the
Imperial Government in extending the re
payment of Duty in the case of Charitable
Entertainments to cases where the whole of
the expenses do not exceed 30 per cent. of
the receipts. The primary object of the
public in patronising these entertainments
is the benefit of the Charity concerned, and
the new Clause will assist charitable and
philanthropic organisations to
larger
extent than has hitherto been the case by
admitting those entertainments whose expenses exceed 20 per cent. of the
receipts to the privilege of repayment of this
tax. Clause 3 follows the British Finance
Act of 1922, and removes certain ambiguities
which have existed in regard to the existing
law. That ends Part I of this Bill.
a
to
Part II deals with the Licence Duty for
Mechanically-Propelled Vehicles. Clause 4
deals with vehicles which have been
diverted from commercial purposes
purposes of pleasure. Hon. Members know
that the rate of Duty in the latter case is
higher than that in the former. Clause 5 extends the privilege at present accorded to
Motor Agents of paying a single Licence Duty
to cover the use of all cars which they employ
upon their business, to Motor Car Repairers
who, in the great majority of cases, are also
Agents or Dealers.
Part III of this Bill deals with Stamp
Duty payable by Alkali Works under the
Act of 1906. the Duty being devoted to the
payment of the Government expenses involved in control and inspection. The
present fees have been found inadequate
owing to changes in money value, and are
now increased. In Northern Ireland
there are one Alkali Works and
twenty-six other works which fall within
the scope of the Act. This is not a serious
increase, as the total is estimated to amount
to an addition of some £80. Clause 7 grants
specific exemption from Stamp Duty in respect of the documents referred to in the
Clause. This merely puts an end to the practice whereby, in the execution of certain official instruments, the Government simply
changes its money from one pocket to another. No loss to the revenue is involved.
Clause 8 brings us to Part IV., which deals
with the assimilation of our law in regard to
Estate Duty to the law in Great Britain, as
established by Section 45 of the Finance Act
of 1922. In the Finance Act of 1894 provision
was made to avoid the payment of double
Death Duties in respect of property situate in
British possessions passing on the death of a
deceased. The present Clause grants to certain of the Malay States the status of a
British possession for Estate Duty purposes.
It is not anticipated that many cases will
arise in Northern Ireland in which the Clause
R
Unemployment Bill-
THE
Second Reading.
SENATE.
will be applicable; but for the sake of uniformity in legislation it is proposed to follow
the lead of the Imperial Government.
The House will, I hope, forgive me for
occupying so much time, but I felt that, although we have no power to amend the provisions of this Bill, at any rate an explanation of their policy showing the changes contemplated in the system of our taxation, small
though they be, is due to and willingly accorded by the Government to this House. I
beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill. (The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: "That the Finance Bill be now
read a Third time."
Mr. DICKSON: I should like to ask the
Leader of the House before this Bill is finally
passed if he could tell us if entertainments
which are of an educational value, such as
agricultural shows, will be exempt from the
entertainment tax.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the entertainments referred to in the
question which the hon. Gentleman has
asked me come under the heading of the
entertainments which are comprised in this
Bill under Clause 2a.
Ordered: That this Bill be now passed.
Unemployment Insurance Amendment Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
My right hon. Friend in a most lucid and
eloquent speech when he introduced the
Second reading of this Measure in another
place, laid particular stress upon the
necessity of keeping our Unemployment
Insurance legislation upon parallel lines
with those which are being followed
across the Irish Sea.
He adduced, firstly,
the very powerful argument that we
are part and parcel with Great Britain, and
that, therefore, it is only just that the conditions under which His Majesty's loyal
subjects live in Northern Ireland should be,
as far as human wit can make them, no less
favourable than those which they would find
were they to remove themselves across
the water. That is not to pledge us to follow
any wild course of public extravagance which
might conceivably follow the establishment of
a Socialist Government in Westminster, but
that so far as they are reasonable lines, lines
which are consonant with public economy we
will most certainly follow them.
Secondly, my right hon. Friend called attention to the necessity that Unemployment
Insurance legislation here should not give
more favourable terms than legislation of this
class at Westminster. We are all well aware
that we have our own proper burden already
-a very heavy burden it is and it would be
suicidal were we to attract, as such legislation
would undoubtedly attract, numbers of those
who lack employment from Great Britain or
the Irish Free State.
دو
“
"
For a proper understanding of the purport
of the present Measure I would venture to
refer the House to the Unemployment Insurance Act which we passed in July last, the
provisions of which were to come into operation on the 20th of July. That Act in sub-
Section 2 of Section 3 provided two Special
Periods
for the purpose of Unemployment
Insurance-two new
Special Periods"
termed the Third and Fourth Periods. The
Third Special Period commencing on the 6th
of April and terminating on the 1st of November, 1922, consisted of 30 weeks, and by sub-
Section 1 of Section 4 of the Act a definite
maximum number of 15 weeks' benefit was
laid down, divided by "gaps" of five weeks
each, during which no benefit should be payable. This Third Special Period has been so
arranged to create similar conditions to
those in Great Britain for our unemployed,
whose unemployment is no whit less
misfortune or less incidental to conditions
entirely beyond the control of its victims
than is that across the water. But a fortnight after our adjournment in July the
Imperial Parliament passed an amending Act
which, firstly, increased the maximum number
of weeks' benefit payable in this Third Special
Period to 22 weeks, and, secondly, reduced
the length of intervening periods when no
benefits should be payable from 5 weeks to
one.
a
Moreover, those persons who had stamp
contributions to their credit could, by our
Unemployment Insurance Act of July last,
draw benefits without any break, and the
result was that persons of this category
having exhausted their authorised period of
15 weeks' benefit on or about the 13th of
September were now faced with a period of
7 weeks during which they could draw no
further benefit. This contingency has been
met largely in Great Britain by the payment
of Outdoor Relief by the Poor Law Authorities. For instance, in Birmingham over
£14,000 weekly is distributed in such relief.
We may compare this with £120 a week
distributed in Belfast on the same basis. It
was, therefore, necessary for the Government
to deal with this anomaly and we decided to
bring forward legislation to sanction payment to persons in this class during the
Third Special Period of a portion of the unemployment benefit which is payable in the
Unemployment Bill-
24 OCTOBER 1922
Second Reading.

disposal, amounting in all to £208,587. Those
are my right hon. Friend's figures up to the
middle of the present month.
Fourth Special Period. We shall recoup the
fund by introducing into the Fourth Special
Period for these persons an interval of a
week without benefit for each week's benefit
paid in advance during the Third Special
Period now running to its end. Thus, all
that this concession will cost to the fund is
the amount of interest payable for the advance of moneys to meet the accelerated expenditure-accelerated, not increased.
It will be observed that this Bill gives us
power to vary the terms of benefit for the
Fourth Special Period, which is at present
occupying the earnest attention of the Government in consultation with the Imperial
Government.
Before I resume my seat I think the House
will agree with me in congratulating my
right hon. Friend upon two heads. Firstly,
upon a fall in the percentage of unemployment and short-time in this Province, not
only in comparison with our own figures nine
months ago, but when we compare them with
the figures in Great Britain. These figures
are: On January 31st, 1922, the percentage
of unemployment in Northern Ireland was
26.39. On September the 25th the percentage
was 20.22-a drop of over 6 per cent. In
Great Britain the figure for January 31st
was 17.4, showing a difference of 8.99 per
cent. against this Province. The figure for
September 25th in Great Britain was 12.2
per cent., and this compared with the 20.22
per cent. for Northern Ireland for the same
date shows a difference, still unfortunately
against us, of 8.02 per cent. That means
that unemployment in Northern Ireland has
been decreasing nearly one per cent. more
rapidly than in other parts of the United
Kingdom. I congratulate my right hon.
Friend upon that, and I think that considerable credit is also due, as he so generously
acknowledged in another place, to my right
hon. Friend who presides over the Ministry
of Commerce.
The second reason for which I find room for
congratulation is one wherein I think there
will be agreement with me generally in this
House, and throughout the Province, that
my right hon. Friend has shown that he
knows how best to grapple with the situation.
I refer to the wise and statesmanlike use
which he has made of the opportunities
provided to him by the grants of £500,000
from the Imperial Treasury and £50,000 from
our own Treasury for the provision of employment. By a system of subsidy to various
public bodies and local authorities he has
secured the promotion of schemes of the
total value of not less than £940,980 as a
result of grants out of the funds at his
By these schemes of relief he is ministering
not only to the physical need, the want of
bread and butter in which our unemployed
stand. But he is also ministering to their
moral need. These men and women are not
beggars. They want money certainly, for
they and their children must live: but,
before money, they desire the opportunity to
earn it.
They are persons of self-respect.
Many of them, both men and women, helped
their country in its hour of need. To those
men who actually fought and bled for us we
owe a debt which we can never repay-only
the smallest part of which is measurable in
terms of money. I am confident that this
House will march step for step with the
Government, though the road be never so
long and never so stony, though it lie uphill
all the way, the road which leads to the
restoration of the ancient prosperity of our
hard-working Province and the recovery of
conditions whereunder there is provided a
decent opportunity to every individual citizen to earn a competence and to maintain
his independence.
Moved: That the Unemployment Insurance
Bill be now read a Second time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That this Bill be now read a
Third time.
Ordered: That this Bill do now pass.
The Solicitors Bill.

read.

itself into Committee on the said Bill.

accordingly, Mr.

House.

Ordered: That the Bill be now read the
Third time.
Ordered: That the Bill do now pass.
And a Message was sent to the House of

have agreed to the said Bill without

Local Authorities (Elections and
Constitution) Bill.
Sir JAMES JOHNSTON: I beg to move
the Amendment standing in my name, and
in doing so I understand that the noble
Marquess, the Leader of the House, who
represents the Government does not offer any
opposition to the proposed amendment. The
next elections of the Belfast Water Commissioners are due to be held on the second
Tuesday in March, 1923, to fill the places of
the present fifteen elective Commissioners,
who will go out of office on that date. The
Commissioners are elected on the local government franchise, one Commissioner to
represent each of the fifteen Wards into
which the city is divided, and these elective
members, together with the Lord Mayor of
the City of Belfast and the chairman of the
Belfast Harbour Commissioners, as ex-officio
members, constitute the Water Board.
The elections of the Commissioners are
conducted under provisions of the Belfast
Water Aots and the Election Rules in force,
as confirmed by the Local Government Board
for Ireland, in pursuance of these Acts. It
is desirable that the provisions of the Local
Government Act, 1922, Sections 2 and 5,
which must now be observed at the elections
of all public bodies in Northern Ireland
elected on local government franchise, should
also be observed at the next elections of
Commissioners, who are elected on the same
franchise, and these provisions are to the
effect that nominated candidates must consent in writing, and deposit a specified
amount. The provisions also deal with the
return of deposit to elected candidates, and
candidates whose nominations have been
withdrawn, also conditions upon which such
deposits may be returned to unsuccessful
candidates, and the application of deposits
which may not be returned. There are also
provisions as to how consents may be given,
and for declaration of allegiance to the King
and the Parliament of Northern Ireland by
Members and Officers of the body.
To make these provisions applicable to the
Commissioners elections they have decided to
promote a Bill in the next Session of Parliament, but it will be impossible for it to
become law in sufficient time to apply to
their first elections on the second Tuesday
in March next, and the matter is so important as to call for a postponement of
their elections for one year, by which time
their new Act would be in force, and their
first elections would be held subject to the
provisions before mentioned. As the Government do not offer any opposition to this
Amendment, and as it is of such importance,
I hope the House will agree to it.
Moved: That it be an instruction to the
Committee on the said Bill that they have
power to extend the scope of the Bill with
regard to the postponing of elections to the
Belfast City and District Water Commissioners. (Sir James Johnston.)
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am quite willing to accept the right hon.
Gentleman's Amendment.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Order of the Day for the House to be put
into Committee read.
Ordered: That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee.
House in Committee accordingly.
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair.
Clause 1 (Postponement of next elections of
County Councillors, Rural District Councillors, and Guardians).
(1) The next triennial elections of county
councillors, rural district councillors and
guardians in urban districts, instead of
being held on the ordinary day of election
in the year nineteen hundred and twentythree, shall be postponed until the ordinary
day of election in the year nineteen hundred
and twenty-four, and the term of office of
each existing councillor and guardian shall
accordingly be extended so as to expire on
the day next after such day of election.
(2) Any provisions of any Act, order or
regulations relating to county councillors,
rural district councillors or guardians, or
to chairmen, vice-chairmen, deputy vicechairmen or committees of those bodies,
shall be construed as if they were modified
in such manner as to give full effect to
the provisions of this section.
265 Local Authorities Bill.

deemed to be a Local Authority. (Sir James
Johnston.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That Clausel 1, as amended, stand
part of the Bill.
Ordered: That Clause 2 (Removal of difficulties, etc.) stand part of the Bill.
Clause 3 (Interpretation, short title, and
construction).
(1) In this Act-The expression "existing
councillor and guardian" means al councillor or guardian holding office on the
ordinary day of election in the year nineteen
hundred and twenty-three:
The expressions "Local Authority" and
"members of a Local Authority" have the
same meaning as in the Local Government
Act (Northern Ireland), 1922.
(2) This Act may be cited as the Local
Authorities (Elections and Constitution)
Act (Northern Ireland), 1922, and shall be
construed as one with the Local Government Acts (Northern Ireland), 1898 to 1922.

quently these words have no meaning in this
Bill.

Wednesday, 25th October, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
