Londonderry Bridge Commissioners
The following report was made from the
Joint Committee on the Bill, viz., "That
they had considered the said Bill and
examined the allegations thereof, which
were found to be true, and that the Committee had gone through the Bill and made
some amendments thereto."
Ordered: That the report do lie on the
Table.
Ordnance Maps.
Mr. LESLIE: In asking the question which
stands in my name whether the preparation
and revision of Ordnance Survey Maps for
Northern Ireland will be continued-I would
like to say that these maps are regarded as
of great value by the people of Northern
Ireland, and that there was a widespread
desire that nothing should prevent the
continuance of the work that was being
done. I hope the Noble Marquess will be
able to assure us that the changed conditions
in this Island will not in any way interfere
with the continuance of this work.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): The answer to the
question which the hon. Gentleman has just
asked is in the affirmative. Since the transfer to the Ministry of Agriculture of the
Ordnance Survey Services, so far as they relate to Northern Ireland, the revision of the
25-inch scale plans of County Antrim has
been practically completed, and it is hoped
to commence a similar revision of County
Londonderry next month. In addition, the
preparation of a new combined map of Belfast on the 6-inch scale on a single sheet has
been undertaken, and it is hoped, in this
connection, that the plans will be in the
hands of the printers at an early date.
Uniformity of Laws Bill.
Order of the Day for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The object of the Bill which I have the
honour to bring before the House for a
Second reading this afternoon appears to
me to be sufficiently described in its own
long title, namely, "to apply to Northern
Ireland certain Acts passed by the Parliament of the United Kingdom in the present
Session thereof." The Bill consists of 2
Clauses and 5 Schedules. The First Clause
is the enacting Clause whereby the several
Acts of the Imperial Parliament set forth
in the Schedule are adopted with necessary
adaptations as Statutes having force in Northern Ireland. The Second Clause merely
recites the short title of the Bill.
The House will, therefore, naturally turn
its chief attention to the 5 Schedules and
examine closely the 5 Acts therein named.
The First Schedule contains the Infanticide
Act of 1922. The Government has no hesitation in asking this House to pass an Act the
provisions of which will relieve our legal
authorities from the terrible dilemma of proceeding upon a charge of murder in these
very pitiable cases, or of consenting to the
raising of the far less serious and often inadequate charge of concealment of birth.
At the same time, it will protect the
miserable objects of these charges from
the possibility of suffering in the dock,
in addition to the sufferings through which
they have already passed, the ordeal of the
sentence of the penalty of death which
everyone but the unfortunate and unhappy
prisoner is absolutely certain will never be
carried out. This is a measure which, I am
sure, will receive the approval of this
House.
The effect of the Gaming Act in the Second
Schedule is to repeal Section 2 of the Gaming
Act of 1835. That Act provided that money
paid to the indorsee or holder of a cheque,
bill or other security given as payment of a
"
gaming transaction" which will probably
be known to hon. Members under the name
of a bet-such money being paid for an
illegal consideration, could be recovered
from the person to whom the cheque, bill
or other security had been given. This was
intended to prevent what is known as credit
betting, but cash betting was not affected,
and money paid on a cash bet could not be
recovered. The effect of the provision making money paid for credit betting recoverable imposed on trustees the very unwelcome obligation to sue for its recovery as
being part of the assets of the estate. The
Bill, therefore, makes such money no longer
recoverable, and relieves trustees of the obligation to sue for recovery of money so paid
or which may hereafter be so paid.
In the Third Schedule is found the British
Bread Acts Amendment, the application of
which to Northern Ireland will empower my
right hon. Friend the Minister for Home
Affairs, as the authority for our Public
269 Uniformity of Laws Bill-
25 OCTOBER 1922
Second Reading.

Health, to issue regulations for the prevention of the adulteration of the ingredients
for bread.

With regard to the Sale of Tea Act, 1922,
which is the Fourth Schedule to this Bill,
I feel that the House will agree with the
principle of any legislation which ensures
that a purchaser of a packet of tea shall obtain a certain specified weight consisting of
tea alone, and not tea with a make-weight
of lead foil or other packing material.
The Act specified in the Fifth Schedule
does not refer to cinemas, but to places in
which the raw material or the finished film
is stored.
There may be other measures which hon.
Members may think might well be included
as a Schedule to the present Bill, but I can
assure the House that my right hon. Friend
has considered most carefully the course
which it is advisable to pursue in regard to
enactments of the Imperial Parliament. It
is generally recognised by the Government
that wherever possible, local considerations
being taken into account, we should follow
the course of Imperial legislation closely,
and maintain generally the same conditions
here as those on the other side of the Irish
Channel. But now, just as often was the case
before the Act of 1920, that there was certain
legislation with special application to England, Ireland, Scotland or Wales, so it by no
means follows that every Act of the Imperial
Parliament can be adopted in whole or in
part for this Province. Certain enactments,
such as the Fifth Schedule to the present
Bill, may be adopted as a whole; others
would need special legislation on parallel
lines by the Parliament of Northern Ireland;
others again might not be applicable at all
to our conditions, and the House may, I
think, confidently leave it to the Government
in consultation with its very able Attorney-
General to deal in the most appropriate way
with legislation of the Imperial Parliament.
I beg to move.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
Second time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
a
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that we are all
quite prepared to receive
Bill of this
description for keeping our laws on parallel
lines with those which may be made in the
United Kingdom, and I imagine we may look
forward to a Laws Uniformity Bill annually.
I am sure we shall always be given such
time as circumstances may permit to consider the Bill, but such a Bill in the nature
of things must be introduced towards the
close of the Session. The Schedule of the
Bill that appeals to me most amongst the
five is undoubtedly the first, and I cannot
pass from that first Schedule without expressing my congratulations to the right hon.
Gentleman, the Attorney-General, in having
the privilege of introducing such a Measure
in another place. This Act will enable our
legal authorities to do by statute what for
half-a-century has had to be done in a
round-about way. The noble Marquess
alluded to that state of things. It has been
known for years and years that no jury,
if it could possibly avoid it, would convict
a woman, under such circumstances as are
adumbrated in the Schedule, of the offence
with which she has been charged. We are
taking in adopting this Act a great step
forward on lines of humanity, and I hope
that it will be observed outside, because,
as we know, there has been too much disposition in uninstructed circles to attack
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister
of Home Affairs, and the right hon.
Gentleman, the Attorney-General. Both
these Gentlemen have been attacked as if
their one desire was to coerce and oppress
their fellow-subjects. The Attorney-General
in particular has been held up to something
approaching opprobrium, as if, when he
appears for the prosecution, he was a kind of
persecutor, instead of being, as the fact is, a
semi-judicial officer engaged in the service of
the Crown, and in the administration of
that prerogative of justice which the Crown
enjoys for the protection and not the
oppression of the people.
Passing from the First Schedule to the
Second, which is an Act which we can alternatively describe as a short measure for the
further promotion of common honesty. It has
the additional advantage of relieving honest
trustees. The next Act scheduled is really a
further step to prevent adulteration. The
Fourth Schedule would appear to be aimed
principally at the class of person satirised
some
"
years ago in the well-known play,
Pilkington's Peerage." That is the sort of
person who sold the wrapper with the tea,
and such persons will enjoy no sympathy
in Northern Ireland. This particular Schedule exemplifies a rather curious draftsmanship; in fact at first sight I thought a
word had lost its way in the printing press.
It was only after the attention of the officials
had been drawn to the matter that I saw
what was really meant, "This Act shall
come into operation on the first day of blank,
nineteen hundred and twenty-two." It
appears that there was really no other way
of dealing with the matter. What happened
in England when the Bill was before Parliament was that the House of Lords introduced
an Amendment making the Bill run from
the 1st September of this year, and the Bill
as a matter of fact received the Royal Assent
on the 11th July. The Amendment was
introduced by the House of Lords in order
to prevent the Measure becoming retrospective, and to give time before the Measure
became effective. The effect here of our
Uniformity of Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Letslie]
not specifying a date in the future will be that
this provision will begin to operate from
the date when this Bill receives the Royal
Assent. I am quite sure we may safely
leave it to the Government to cause to be
exercised ordinary discretion in refraining
from prosecutions without giving a man due
warning. It is common practice when a
new Act is introduced for the police authorities charged with its execution to give
warning before proceedings are taken, and
I am sure that will be done in this case.
The remaining Schedule will only provoke
the comment that life is as precious in
Northern Ireland as anywhere else, and that
it is very necessary that the public should
be protected. On the whole, I am sure this
is a Bill that will commend itself to all
of us, and provoke no controversy either
in this House or outside.
Mr. DUFFIN': I have pleasure in rising to
support the Second reading of this Bill. It
has always been too much the custom to
differentiate between the law of Ireland and
the law of Great Britain. Of course there
will be certain subjects of legislation which
do not directly apply to conditions in Ireland
in the same way as to Scotland, but I think
the familiar clause, "This Act shall not apply
to Ireland," has been far too frequently appearing in the Statute Book and Ireland has
been excluded from certain social and legal
reforms, perhaps from a want of knowledge
on the part of our legislators as to how far
they can be applied to Ireland, although 1
think it is more likely it has been regarded
as a desirable way of smoothing its passage
through the House by conciliating the opposition of the Irish Nationalist Members.
I think we may take it, as indicated by the
noble Marquess, that this Bill, together with
others such as we passed only the other day,
is an earnest of the desire and of the intention of our Parliament to harmonise, as
far as it is possible, our legislation with that
of Great Britain, and it is satisfactory to
know that the Government will march forward on parallel lines with the Government
of Great Britain.
As has been indicated by the noble Marquess, the position with reference to this
legislation has been considerably changed by
the Act of 1920, giving us the Constitution
under which we now govern. It is likely to
be still further changed by the legislation
which I suppose will take place before the
end of this year to give effect to the conditions of the Treaty of 6th December. After
that date Parliament will not in fact-although it may be in name be the Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great
Britain and Ireland. This familiar clause,
"This Act shall not apply to Ireland," will
no longer be required. It will be out of
place just as much as would be a declaration
that this Act shall not apply to the Dominion
of Canada or to any other of our selfgoverning Dominions. I think we should
realise the change which is coming and what
we have to look forward to now is not a
Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great
Britain and Ireland-
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt
the hon. Member, but he is rather wandering away from the subject of the Bill.
Mr. DUFFIN: I bow to your ruling. I
merely wanted to indicate some of the
changes that will be brought about before
very long. I did not intend to express any
views with regard to the desirability or
otherwise of these changes. I beg to support
the Second reading.
On Question, Motion: "That this Bill be
now read the Second time," agreed to.

Lord Londonderry and Ulster.
APPRECIATION BY SENATORS.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move. "That this House do now adjourn." In doing so, I would venture to
make a small correction to a statement I
made yesterday which I regret to say was
made in error in reply to a question by the
25 OCTOBER 1922
Adjournment--
Lord Londonderry and Ulster. 274
has shown us that he is endowed with a
greater gift than any of these, the priceless
gift of the power to renounce.
hon. Member for Derry (Senator Long). I
am sorry to see he is not in his place to-day.
He asked me a question as to whether agricultural shows were exempt from the Entertainment Tax and in correction of what I
said yesterday I should like to inform the
House that agricultural shows are exempt
under Section 7 of the Finance Act, 1921,
if they are purely agricultural and without
any extraneous attractions, such as a band,
sports, horse jumping, or riding competitions.
If these extraneous entertainments are connected with the agricultural show it means
that the agricultural show is not exempt
from the Entertainment Tax. I should like
to make this clear to the House and to
apologise to the House for having incorrectly
answered the hon. Member's question yesterday.
Mr. MDOWELL: It was Senator Dickson
who asked the question.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
thank you for reminding me that it was
Senator Dickson who asked the question. In
moving that this House do now adjourn, I
think it will meet the wishes of hon. Members
if the House adjourns to such date as Mr.
Speaker may appoint. I assume it will be
necessary for us to meet again in the course
of the next few weeks. The House of Commons is adjourning from next Friday until
12th December. Consequently, I think hon.
Members will realise that we shall have to
meet about that time, and I hope they will
be able to make arrangements which will
be convenient to their attendance in this
House at that time.
Mr. LESLIE: I think we cannot separate
to-day-and I know that on this point I am
not speaking for myself-without expressing
our gratification at knowing that when we
meet again we shall still have the inestimable advantage of the Leadership of the
noble Marquess. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) We profoundly appreciate the value
of his guidance. Day by day we are more
and more impressed by the thoroughness
with which he does everything he takes in
hand, by his sagacity, and the kindly and
courteous consideration he extends to us all.
We feel that this Chamber, which I think
we are learning to love, would not be the
same to us if the noble Marquess were not
here. We are delighted and thankful to
know that our Leader has decided that his
duty still remains with us. The noble Marquess is, if I may say so, of those to whom
much belongs by right of birth. He is an
hereditary Councillor of the Crown, he has
the armorial achievement appropriate to
the three distinguished families which he
represents, and he has great possessions.
But to-day, and not for the first time, he
To give up or to decline a seat in the
Ministry of the United Kingdom is an act
of renunciation which is not often found.
Very rarely indeed have we had experience of
such an act, but that great act of renunciation will, I believe, bring to the noble Marquess a rich and well and truly earned
reward both now and in the years to come.
The reward will lie in the esteem felt for
him personally and in the heartfelt gratitude of every right thinking person in this
Province.
Mr. DUFFIN: I am sure we will all
willingly endorse the sentiments given expression to by the hon. Member who has
just sat down. I recollect that at the time
of the formation of our Northern Government we were all well aware that his
Lordship's position, experience, and ability
entitled him to claim a position of importtance in any Government, and it was with
great satisfaction we learned he had determined to cast in his lot with Ulster, and
had taken an important and leading position
in the Government of Sir James Craig. It
was with a certain amount of apprehension
that we saw the other day an announcement
that the noble Marquess had been offered
a very leading position in the Government
of the United Kingdom, a position which
might be envied by anyone and one which
his Lordship was entitled to accept and fill,
and it was a relief to us to sce the further
announcement that he had decided to remain
with Ulster and was not to be tempted to
take a place amongst the seats of the mighty,
and to know that we will still have him
with us to fill the important position he
occupies so admirably. We fully appreciate
the greatness of the sacrifice he has made
in remaining with us here, and I desire to
express the deep sense of gratitude we owe
to him for the position he has taken up, and
to assure him it will not be forgotten in
Ulster.
Mr. WOODS: As a representative of the
working classes I would like to identify
myself with the remarks of the hon.
Senators. I can assure you that when 1
read the statement that an offer had been
made to the noble Marquess to take up a
position in the Imperial Cabinet I wondered
how we would fill the vacancy that would
occur in the Northern Government if he
saw his way to accept that post. However,
I was pleased to see the statement in this
morning's newspapers that his Lordship had
decided to remain with us. We appreciate
very highly indeed the fact that the Imperial
Cabinet recognises that we have a man of
such ability, able to fill such an important
{
S
Lord Londonderry and Ulster. 276
Adjournment-
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Woods]
position as that of Air Minister in Great
Britain, but we appreciate far more the fact
that his Lordship has decided to remain
with us and assist us to build up the
Government of Northern Ireland, more
especially as we expect shortly to have a
Bill introduced in which he has taken a
great interest. I refer to the Education
Bill, and I am sure that is one of the main
reasons that has induced his Lordship to
retain his present position in the Government of Northern Ireland. He has taken
such a deep interest in Education since he
was appointed Minister of Education that
I am sure he would not like to go away
until the Bill has finally passed through both
Houses. I am pleased, indeed, to be here
to-day to identify myself with the remarks
made by Senators Leslie and Duffin.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I would ask the indulgence of the House
to allow me to reply to the far too flattering
words which have fallen from my hon.
Friends. I can assure them that I feel
so deeply touched by their kind expressions
with regard to myself that I have great
difficulty in finding words to give adequate
expression to my feelings. I need hardly
say that the fact that my acceptance of
office in this Government some time ago
now was greeted with so much enthusiasm, if
I may use the expression, was indeed
reward to me, and I feel, and I hope, that
from that time I have done everything that
lay in my power to further the interests
of our Province, of which we are all so
justly proud, and to do my humble best in
tiding it over the many difficulties with
which it has been confronted.
a
I should be scarcely human if I did not
feel a deep sense of satisfaction and gratification in the fact that the Prime Minister
of Great Britain has thought fit to offer
me an honourable post in his Government.
I do feel intensely grateful for the honour
which he has done me, and I feel that it is
not only an honour to myself but also to the
community to which I have the honour to
belong. But, my lord, if we take part in
public affairs, we have all to decide at
different times of our lives as to the line
which is most consistent with our duty,
and also as to how the course which we
take will benefit those with whom our duty
lies. I need hardly say that I considered the
case very carefully, and I felt that my duty
lay with the Province of Ulster, and that
what little I could do would be best devoted
to assisting our Government, and also our
fellow-citizens in passing through the difficulties with which we are assailed. 1
sincerely hope I have made the right choice,
but I need hardly say that the choice has
been made without any regrets, and the fact
that Mr. Bonar Law has thought fit to offer
me a high post in his Majesty's Government
in England is only a further incentive to me
to do my utmost to bring to a successful
conclusion the plans and the policy in which
we in this Province are now engaged.
I value your kindly expressions with regard
to myself, and I should like to take this
opportunity of saying that I appreciate to
its fullest extent the great assistance which
I have had not only from the Members of
this House, but also from so many throughout the Six Counties who, I know, are as
deeply anxious for the welfare of this Province as I am myself. We all owe Sir James
Craig a deep debt of gratitude, and it is the
duty of every one of us to lighten the burden
which rests on his shoulders. I have the
feeling that by standing by his side I can
in some small measure relieve that burden,
and I do feel also that if all the people in
the Six Counties would realise that they
have a share also in bearing the burden, we
shall tide over the difficulties with which we
are confronted, and sail forward to the calm
waters of peace and prosperity in this Province. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
On Question, Motion, "That this House
do now adjourn," agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Four
of the Clock till such date as
Mr. Speaker may appoint.
277 Northern Ireland and Free State. 7 DECEMBER 1922 Address to His Majesty. 278
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 7th December, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the Chair.
Royal Assent.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I am authorised by the
Prime Minister on his behalf to inform the
Senate that his Excellency the Lord Lieutenant in the name of and on behalf of His
Majesty the King has been pleased to give his
assent to the following Bills:
Unemployment Insurance (Amendment).
Finance.
Solicitors.
Uniformity of Laws.
Expiring Laws.
Local Authorities (Elections and Constitution).
SITTING SUSPENDED.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
May I suggest that this House do now adjourn during pleasure until a message which
we are expecting shall arrive here from
another place. I beg to move that this
House do now adjourn.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
Minutes after Three of the
Clock during pleasure.
The House resumed at Twenty-five minutes
after Three of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the
Chair.
Northern Ireland and Free State
Address to His Majesty.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
A Message was received from the Commons
that they have agreed to the following
Address to be presented to His Majesty, in
which they desire the concurrence of this
House:
MOST GRACIOUS SOVEREIGN,
WE, your Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Commons of Northern
Ireland in Parliament assembled, having
learnt of the passing of the Irish Free
State Constitution Act, 1922, being the Act
of Parliament for the ratification of the
Articles of Agreement for a Treaty between
Great Britain and Ireland, do, by this
humble Address, pray Your Majesty that
the powers of the Parliament and Government of the Irish Free State shall no
longer extend to Northern Ireland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
It is my duty to ask this House to pass
this Address which has come to us from
another place. I can hardly feel that there
is any necessity for me either to explain
its provisions or to urge upon the Members
of the Senate to pass it as it stands. The
Address embodies in its phraseology the
sentiments which have been in our minds
ever since the 1920 Act, which created this
Assembly and called upon us to discharge
duties which up to that time had been discharged by the Parliament of the United
Kingdom, a state of affairs which we hoped
would continue for all time. We know other
counsels prevailed, and we have been called
upon to discharge those duties and to carry
on as a separate State under the authority
of the Crown.
I do not feel that we need look back upon
the last two years with any misgivings. I
think that we can agree that we have discharged those duties to the very best of
our abilities, and the future for us looks
comparatively bright. To-day we are establishing the fact that the Six Counties come
under the Northern Government, and we
are in process of dissociating ourselves from
the Government of what is known as the
Free State.
I do not feel that in this Assembly there will be any dissentient voice.
The fact that we are practically in control
of our own destinies in the Six Counties does
in no sense militate against the sentiment
uppermost in all our minds, and that is
that we are still united with Great Britain.
I feel sure you will all agree with me when
I say that our successful future depends upon
that union, and I sincerely hope nothing will
occur that will damage that connection to
which we all attach so much importance. I
feel that it is not my duty to say anything
more in regard to the Address, and I venture
to make the Motion.
Moved: That this House do agree with the
House of Commons in the Address to His
Majesty and do insert the words "Senators
and" before the word "Commons" ("loyal
subjects the Commons of Northern") in the
said Address.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have agreed
to the said Address, and have inserted the
words "Senators and" before the word "Commons" in the said Address.
Ordered: That the Marquess of Londonderry do present the Address on the part of
this House.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Half
after Three of the Clock.
Reply to Address
Londonderry Bridge
THE SENATE.
THE
SENATE.

Wednesday, 13th December, 1922.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPERS PRESENTED TO PARLIAMENT.
1.-MINISTRY OF HOME AFFAIRS.
Northern Ireland Winter Assizes Order,
1922.
2.-MINISTRY OF EDUCATION.
(1) Rules made by the Ministry, with the
approval of the Ministry of Finance, for the
payment of grants to Secondary Schools.
(2) Teachers' Salaries Grant Rules (Statutory Rules and Orders of Northern Ireland,
1922, No. 53).
Presented by Command, and ordered to lie
on the Table.
3.-MINISTRY OF LABOUR.
(1) Order to vary or amend the provisions of
the Insurance Industry (Unemployment Insurance) Special Scheme, and to provide for
Consequential Amendments as to rates of contributions and otherwise.
(2) Regulations to amend and vary subparagraph (4) of paragraph 4 of Unemployment Insurance (Directions to Committee)
Regulations, 1921, in so far as those Regulations apply to Northern Ireland.
(3) Regulations made by the National Health
Insurance Joint Committee as to the valuation
deficiencies of small societies in Northern Ireland.
(4) Regulations amending the National
Health Insurance (Approved Societies) Consolidated Regulations, 1918 to 1920, as regards
the expenses of Insurance Committees for the
administration of Sanatorium benefit methods
of dealing with any deficiency in a Society's
administration, accounts, etc.
(5) Provisional Regulations made by the National Health Insurance Joint Committee
amending previous Arrears Regulations as regards the time within which employed contributors may pay arrears.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
His Majesty's Reply to Address.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to read the reply
to the Address to His Majesty which His
Majesty has sent to this House:
I have received the Address presented to
me by both Houses of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland in pursuance of Article
XII. of the Articles of Agreement set forth
in the Schedule to the Irish Free State
Agreement Act, 1922, and of Section 5 of the
Irish Free State Constitution Act, 1922, and
I have caused my Ministers and the Irish
Free State Government to be so informed.
Londonderry Bridge Commissioners
Bill.
Order for the Report of Amendments read.
Mr. GREER: The Bill now before the House
has been submitted by the Londonderry
Bridge Commissioners. It is entitled an Act
to authorise the Londonderry Bridge Commissioners to borrow money for, amongst other
purposes, the painting and strengthening and
repair of the Londonderry Bridge, and for
other purposes. This Bill is an amending
Bill, amending two previous Acts under which
the Londonderry Bridge Commissioners were
incorporated and were empowered to construct and maintain a bridge across the Foyle,
in the City of Derry, and they are empowered
to expend sums not exceeding the amount
specified in the Bill in maintaining the bridge
and to raise that money by levying a rate on
certain contributory areas, the City of Londonderry being one, the County of Londonderry being another, and the County of
Tyrone, or parts of the Counties of Tyrone and
Londonderry being also contributory areas.
These sums that they were authorised to expend were quite sufficient to maintain the
bridge and ferry that is worked in connection
with it in good order until recently. But for
some time past it has been quite insufficient,
and the bridge has not been properly maintained, and as a consequence it has got into
bad order and is dangerous and unsafe to use.
A considerable amount of debt has been incurred in connection with maintaining the
bridge and ferry. The Bill now before the
House seeks to get power to raise a larger
sum off these contributory areas a sum which
would be sufficient to keep the bridge in
proper repair. The Bill was opposed by the
Londonderry County Council, who raised four
or five points in Committee against the Bill
but during the hearing all the points were
agreed on, with the exception of one, and the
Committee heard evidence on that point and
came to a decision upon it, and that decision
was accepted by both parties. There were
also some amendments or suggestions that
were made by the Ministry of Home Affairs
which they thought would improve the Bill,
and these also came before the Committee,
the promoters, and the petitioners, and they
were all agreed to. The Bill now before the
House carries out that decision of the Committee and the agreements that were entered
into by the parties at the hearing, and I do
not think it is necessary for me to go through
the variations in the Bill that were made in
Committee. They are in the present printed
Londonderry Bridge
Standing Orders
13 DECEMBER 1922.
Bill. I do not think it is the practice to go
into them seriatim. I now beg to move that
the amendments made to this Bill by the
Joint Committee be agreed to.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Suspension of Standing Orders.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that the Standing Order No. 25
be considered in order to its being dispensed
with in respect of this Bill, in order that this
Bill may be read a Third time to-day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Standing Order be
dispensed with in respect of said Bill.
Londonderry Bridge Commissioners
Bill.
Mr. GREER: I should now like to move the
Third reading of this Bill. I am glad that
the Standing Order has been dispensed with,
and that we can take the Third reading today, because it really is a matter of great
urgency that this Bill should be passed as
soon as possible. I should say that that was
abundantly proved by the evidence that was
given as to the condition of the bridge by the
Civil Engineer in charge. I beg to move that
this Bill be now read a Third time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
"
دو
Mr. GREER: There are one or two formal
amendments I should like to move now. They
are amendments that were suggested by the
Ministry of Home Affairs, and have all been
agreed to by the promoters of the Bill. They
are purely formal. For example, to change
the word "Minister" of Home Affairs into
the word Ministry of Home Affairs, and
in another place where it refers to high water
to insert after the words "high water" the
words "of ordinary spring tides." I understand it is not usual in Westminster, either
in the House of Commons or in the House of
Lords, to go through these formal amendments seriatim, and therefore I beg to move
that they be agreed to.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move that this Bill
do now pass.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons to
acquaint them that the Senate have agreed to
the said Bill, with some Amendments to
which they desire the concurrence of the
Commons.
Standing Orders Relative to Local
Bills.
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA (Chairman of Committees): As Chairman
of Committees, in moving the amendments
which appear on the paper I should like to
give the House a short resume of their purport.
No. 38. This Standing Order, which it is
proposed should take the place of the old
one, has been arranged after consultation with
various Government Departments in London.
In order that Imperial interests may be safeguarded, the new Standing Order provides
that Bills must be deposited at the Treasury,
General Post Office, and the Office of Woods,
and in some cases at the Board of Trade, also
at the Home Office. Hon. Members will see
that on the paper the Office of Home Affairs
is described as the Irish Office, but as the
Irish Office has ceased to exist it is now known
as the Home Office. Then, of course, they are
still to be deposited at the Government Departments of Northern Ireland.
No. 73. Instead of a fixed date (the 31st
January) it is considered advisable to take
power to fix the date every year for the examination of proofs of compliance with the
Standing Orders. The 31st January is the
Westminster date, but the keeping of a fixed
date might mean delay in starting the
Private Bill Legislation of the Session.
Standing Order No. 74 provides that a week's
notice must be given of such date, and, therefore, it will be no hardship on promoters to
alter the fixed date of the 31st January to
such date as the Speaker of the House of
Commons may appoint.
No. 97. Here again it has been considered
advisable to change the fixed date for the
lodging of petitions against a Bill to a date
which will be fixed from the First Reading
of such Bill. In cases where Parliament
meets late, the fixing of this date (12th February) means that the petitions must be
lodged much earlier than is necessary; that is,
that petitions must be drawn and lodged
where in some cases they need not have been
drawn or lodged at all. Negotiations between
parties may render such petitions unnecessary
and expense may be saved if further time be
granted. In other cases, when Parliament
meets early Bil's may be delayed from going
into Committee because the petitions have not
to be lodged until this fixed date. By the
proposed amendment in the Standing Order
the petitions will have to be lodged within
seven days of the First Reading of any Bill,
which will ensure that the petition is lodged
before the Bill can possibly be ready for the
Committee Stagel
No. 98. This amendment is merely consequential on the previous amendment.
Standing Orders
THE SENATE.
Land Purchase
[The Marquess of Dufferin and Aval
A year's experience in Local Bill Legislation has shown that with the shorter Sessions
of this Parliament as compared with those at
Westminster it is important that therel
should be no delay in dealing with Local Bills.
No alteration has been made in any of the
Standing Orders which protect private interests, but these amendments are proposed
in order to lessen the danger of insufficient
time to get the Bills through Parliament.
The only alterations are in Standing Order
38, Sub-section 2, after "General Post Office"
to insert "Home Office," and after "Irish
Office" to insert " so long as it shall continue
to exist."
Moved: To leave out No. 38 and insert as a
new Standing Order:-
38. "On or before the Twenty-first Day of
December a printed copy shall be deposited-
(1) Of every Local Bill, at the Ministries
of Finance, Home Affairs, Labour, Education. Agriculture, and Commerce in Belfast.
(2) Of every Local Bill, at the Office of
His Majesty's Treasury, at the General Post
Office, at the Irish Office, and at the Office
of Woods in London.
(3) Of every Local Bill affecting any dock,
harbour navigation, pier or port, at the
Board of Trade in London."
No. 73. Leave out "the 31st day of January,"
and insert "such day."
No. 97. Leave out " in the case of Local Bills
on or before the twelfth day of February, and
in the case of Provisional Order Confirmation
Bills or of Local Bills not presented within
the required time, on or before the tenth,"
and insert "on or before the seventh."
No. 98. Leave out "other than Petitions required to be presented on or before the
twelfth day of February."
(The Marquess of Dufferin and Ava.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That said Standing Orders, as so
amended, be printed.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that Standing Order No. 25 be
considered in order to its being dispensed
with, in order that the Appropriation No. 2
Bill and the Loans Guarantee Bill, if received
from the Commons, may receive First and
Second readings.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That said Standing Order be suspended for this day's sitting.
Land Purchase in Ireland.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: With your leave I
should like to offer a few remarks in explanation of the question that is on the paper. I
do not propose to weary the House by giving
a long string of quotations from the utterances of various Members of the late Government to show how deeply they are pledged to
take up the subject mentioned in the paper.
The Senate is no doubt fully aware of the
letter that was written by Mr. Lloyd George
to the Irish Convention on the 25th February,
1918, in which he said that if the report of
the Land Committee of that Convention commended itself to the Convention the Government would be prepared to introduca in Parliament as part of the plan of settlement, and
simultaneously with the Bill amending the
Government of Ireland Act of 1914, a measure
for the purpose of enabling Parliament to
give effect to the recommendations of the
Convention on the subject of Land Purchase.
That covers the whole ground. That is the
whole of these recommendations. I think I
may also remind the Senate that Lord Salisbury, on the 9th of March of this year, said
in the House of Lords that
The Government are pledged up to the
eyes to pass an Act for the completion of
land purchase in Ireland, which was to
have accompanied the Home Rule Act of
1920, and which is very much overdue. Although that obligation is primarily an
obligation of His Majesty's Government, it
is also an obligation of Parliament.
Viscount Peel, in his reply, said:
As to the completion of land purchase,
that in Northern Ireland will require an
Act of the Imperial Parliament. The completion in Southern Ireland will be a matter
with which the Free State Parliament will
have to deal.
A deputation afterwards waited on Mr.
Austen Chamberlain, Mr. Winston Churchill,
and Viscount Peel, and these statements
were repeated, and Mr. Winston Churchill
said that he did not go into details because
he did not like to affront the Free State
Parliament, who would have to deal with
the matter in the Free State. He went on:
As regards Northern Ireland, the completion of Land Purchase in the Six
Counties would be carried out by an Act of
the Imperial Parliament.
I am informed that the Duke of Devonshire. who has succeeded Mr. Winston
Churchill, said that he fully accepts all the
obligations and pledges of his predecessor.
I venture to hope that the Noble Lord in
answering my question will be able to give
us some assurance that the British Government is going on with the Bill for Northern
Land Purchase
13 DECEMBER 1922.
Land Purchase
Ireland without waiting till something
is done in the Free State. Now that we have
legally established our position completely,
there is no excuse for delay. There may be
great delays with regard to the South. There
are undoubtdly great difficulties, and it is
most unfair to the tenants, who are looking
for relief, and to the landlords, who are locking for delivery from certain embarrassments
and troubles, and still more so to this Parliament. who will have this question on its
hands unless promptly settled by an Act of
the Imperial Parliament, that we should be
kept waiting for the fulfilment of this overdue pledge, because there are difficulties in
arranging for the completion of land purchase
in the Free State. It is not at all likely that
the process of carrying out the completion
of land purchase in the Free State, whatever
shape it may take, will yield any useful result that will in any way make it easier to
complete purchase in Northern Ireland,
where the problem is comparatively simple.
On the other hand it may very likely improve the prospects in the Free State if the
question here is satisfactorily settled. I am
sure that we all should be very much pleased
if that were so, for none of us can fail to
have the warmest sympathy with the un
fortunate position of the landlords in the
Free State.
The duty of the British Government really
was at first (by Mr. Lloyd George) to complete
the operation begun by the Wyndham Act
before unloading its responsibilities as regards
the government of Ireland on the Northern
and Southern Parliaments. Having failed
to deal with the question as it ought to have
been dealt with at the proper time, they
cught undoubtedly to deal with it as regards
the Six Counties without any further delay
whatever. I hope the Noble Lord will
be able to give us an assurance that
the Government accepts responsibility for
the pledges given to the full extent,
and that they will deal with this
question by a measure to be passed in the
Imperial Parliament and financed completely, including bonus and all expenses, by
the Imperial Exchequer. In consequence of
certain transactions I have heard of, it is
very necessary to emphasise that point.
also hope that they will be able to give us
assurances that this transaction will be kept
distinct and apart from the negotiations
about reserved services that are being transferred to the Northern Government and Parliament. It is entirely a separate transaction
that ought to have been completed before this
Parliament was set up and which ought not
now to be mixed up with any sort of giveand-take over matters in which they are
bound to give this Government assistance.
I
Lastly I hope the Noble Lord will be able
a
to assure us that the principle of the Irish
Convention Land Committee will be adhered
to. That is, roughly speaking, that the
tenants are to be placed in as good a position as their neighbours who bought under
the Wyndham Act, that the landlords should
be assured their present net income, or an
income not less than about 90 per cent. of
their third term rents, or 80 per cent. of
their first term rents, and that
bonus
should be provided to the amount necessary
to enable the landlords and tenants to get
the benefit of these terms. The bonus is a
distinct element in this matter. The Northern farmers have no desire to confiscate
the landlords' property, or to see the landlords so impoverished as not to be able to
live in their mansion houses. On the other
hand, they are not prepared to saddle their
holdings with a liability for an annuity
which they cannot see their way clearly to
be paid by themselves and their successors
for a period of 50, 60, or 70 years. In order
to meet these two points there is a gap
which must be bridged. Mr. George Wyndham was a man of great vision, and he saw
that the position into which legislation, or
circumstances, had landed the Irish land
question made what he called, I think, a
golden bridge absolutely necessary in order
to produce a settlement. He insisted on the
necessity of a free gift in case the Act did
operate successfully to a very large extent,
of £12,000,000. The necessity for this bonus
in any land measure has been acknowledged
by all parties dealing with the question
since. It is admitted in the Land Act of
1909 and in some respects in the Bill of 1920.
It is most distinctly included in the pledge
given by the late Government which the
present Government acknowledges it is
bound by. The Irish Convention Land
Committee say it is to be paid not
by the taxpayer of Great Britain, but
by the United Kingdom as
whole.
Further, it is an Imperial obligation. If the
Government dealings with Southern Ireland
have made it impossible to get their share of
this taxation, that is no reason why we should
be burdened with it. I hope that the Noble
Lord will be able to assure us that no
attempt is being made to cast upon Northern
Ireland any part of the obligation admitted
by the British Government in this matter.
a
Now, if I may say so, I have been concerned
with a great many Land Purchase Bills and
Acts for the last 30 years. I was even consulted in the framing of two of them, and I,
and those with whom I have been in the
habit of working-though there are features
in the Bill of 1920, which follows the Irish
Convention Report, that we are not very
much pleased with are entirely agreed that
this Bill, subject to certain amendments, is
the best both for the landlords and the
tenants that can possibly be hoped for under
Land Purchase
Land Purchase
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Montgomery]
present circumstances, and that it is most
important to secure its passing at once.
Therefore I hope the Noble Lord will be able
to assure us that the lines of the Bill that
was drawn in July last year the lines off the
Irish Convention report-will be stuck to. 1
need hardly point out that we are asking no
very great boon from the British Government
and Exchequer in insisting on the fulfilment
of this pledge. The completion of land purchase in the Six Counties cannot be regarded
as a very big undertaking from the point cf
view of the British Treasury-(Hon. Members: Hear, hear)-though it would be exceedingly embarrassing and troublesome for this
Parliament to have to deal with. The best
information I have been able to obtain shows
that the rental of unbought holdings which
are subject to the provisions of the Land
Acts in the Six Counties is something over
£155,000 a year. The report upon which
this figure is based I believe to be imperfect,
and, therefore, very likely it may amount
to £200,000. The advance necessary, according to the Land Commission scheme, will
be less than £3,500,000, and this is an investment that will be absolutely secure.
Northern tenants can be absolutely relied
upon to pay their instalments regularly,
as they have been in the habit of paying
their rents, and those who have bought out
have paid their instalments.
The amount of the bonus cannot be fixed
at present, owing to certain fluctuations in
the value of money. It will not amount to
more than £350,000. This the British
Treasury is bound in honour to provide in
order to fulfil its bargain with this Parliament. We accepted the duties allotted to us
under the Government of Ireland Act, 1920,
with the promise before us that this land
question would be settled, and this understanding, this undertaking, should now be
fulfilled. I should like to mention, before
asking the question, that there was a large
meeting held this afternoon at the Reform
Club at which this resolution was unanimously passed :-
"That we earnestly appeal to the Imperial Government to carry through Parliament a Bill for the completion of land
purchase in Northern Ireland in pursuance
of the undertaking given by the late Prime
Minister, Mr. Lloyd George, that
measure for the completion of land purchase would be passed along with the Bill
for Self-Government."
a
I beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether he is able to give any information
with regard to the steps to be taken by the
British Government in fulfilment of their
pledge to transfer the remaining property of
the owners of lands in Northern Ireland subject to the Land Acts to the occupiers on
terms fair to both parties, and on the basis
agreed to by the Land Committee of the
Irish Convention of 1917-1918.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: After the very full
and exhaustive explanation my hon. Friend
(Mr. Montgomery) has given, I think it
would be unnecessary for me to say much,
but I should like to dwell upon the point
that this is likely to be an agreed measure.
There were consultations between the representatives of the landlords and the tenants
before the Bill of 1920 was introduced, and I
believe perfect harmony was arrived at.
When I say it was an agreed measure I
mean that in all essentials the tenants and
landlords were agreed, although there might
be certain amendments of no great importance which might be left for discussion
afterwards. Another point I wish also to
emphasise is that this should be treated as
a distinct measure apart from what may
happen in Southern Ireland. Certain representatives of those interested in land in
Southern Ireland lately got into touch with
the Minister of Agriculture, and his views
are rather different from ours on this question. His opinion is that the question of
future land purchase cannot be dealt with
alone.
It is intimately connected with the
arrangements to be made between the two
Governments with regard to land stock already issued, and indeed the general financial settlement between the two countries.
I think that if we in Northern Ireland have
to wait until a general financial settlement
is arrived at between the two countries the
youngest of us will have died of old age,
and therefore I think it is essential that the
matter should be dealt with distinct from
anything that may happen in the South of
Ireland. The landlords and tenants are
agreed, and I cannot conceive anything that
would tend to promote harmony and good
feeling and a sense of security throughout
the North of Ireland more than would the
final settlement of this question.
I do not think that either landlords or
tenants view this measure with enthusiasm
because owing to the change in the value of
money it is impossible for either one or the
other to expect anything like the terms they
were given under the Wyndham Act, when
money could be borrowed at 24 per cent.,
but at the same time we are both prepared
to submit to some suffering and disadvantage
in the interests of the peace, security, and
contentment of the people. Therefore, I
hope the Noble Lord will be able to give an
assurance such as is sought by my hon.
Friend (Mr. Montgomery).
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I rise to
support what my hon. Friend on my right
(Mr. Montgomery) and my hon. Friend
opposite (Mr. Armstrong) have said with
regard to the urgency of this question. After
Land Purchase
Land Purchase

13 DECEMBER 1922.
all, it is not a very big question. It really
represents £350,000 and bonus. The other
money is very well invested, and that is not
a very large sum, as my hon. Friend on the
right has said. Such a measure would have
two effects. It will relieve our Government
of having to do this themselves, and it will
also be a boon not only to the tenants who
are supporters of our Government but to
the tenants of all creeds and classes throughout the Six Counties. I should like to urge
on the Government the importance of the
fact that nothing less than securing to the
Ulster landlords all their present rentals,
or nearly their present rentals, will save
either this generation or the next generation from having to leave their homes.
It is a question of vital importance to them,
because if they do not get a price that enables them to live in the country they will
have to go to some other country. Therefore,
I hope the noble Marquess and our Government will use all the influence they possess
with the Government, which we have every
right to believe is sympathetic with the
North of Ireland, in order to carry out the
Bill on the lines proposed by my hon. Friend
(Mr. Montgomery). The way we were
treated over the cattle question only shows
how absolutely essential it is for us to put
all the force we can behind any effort to
get this question settled or we may find that
we are left out in the cold again.
Mr. DICKSON: I do not think there is
much to add to the very lucid statement with
which my hon. Friend placed this question
before the House. The only point I would
like to emphasise is that the late Government, behind the back of Ireland, promised
a concession to Canada which the Imperial
Government of to-day felt it was under an
obligation to carry out. We hold that Mr.
Lloyd George also gave us a promise to carry
out the Land Purchase scheme, and that this
Government is entitled to carry out a promise that is favourable to Ireland when they
carried out one that was detrimental to this
country. The hon. Senator opposite (Col.
Perceval-Maxwell) has referred to the difficulty landlords in this country would be in
if they did not get almost the rental they
are receiving to-day. I speak on behalf of
the tenantry of Northern Ireland, and I say
they would regret to see the landlords of the
Northern area removed from that area.
There is no question about that.
"bridge" should not be borne by the tenantry, who are not in a position to do it.
The
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am sure that everybody will feel a certain
amount of satisfaction that this question,
which is naturally a very important one,
has been brought up to-day in this House.
Speaking for myself, I can say at once that
a
I feel in agreement with all the remarks
which have fallen from the various speakers.
The hon. Member who brought forward this
subject is very familiar with all the details.
He has been associated with this question
for a great many years, and I am in sympathy with him when he presses for something to be done to carry out the various
undertakings which have been given. It is
quite true that
scheme was brought
forward at the time of the Irish Convention,
and that scheme, while perhaps not fulfilling
the desires of everyone, was a measure with
which we all had a great deal of sympathy
and which we hoped would have been carried
into effect. We have to realise that in the
condition in which all Irish affairs have now
been for some considerable time all measures
of local interest have been in abeyance, but
I think we can feel a certain amount of
satisfaction that we in this part of Ireland
have settled down to our normal avocations,
and we may consequently hope the result will
be that one by one these questions will be
speedily settled.
The hon. Member (Mr. Montgomery) referred to assurances we have received, and
he quoted some of those assurances. We
have received a great number of assurances
and if good-will or assurances mean anything I think we need have no doubt as to
all our ambitions in this respect being fulfilled. The hon. Member who has just sat
down (Mr. Dickson) has agreed with the
previous speakers that this is a matter which
affects equally both landlords and tenants,
and that there is a large amount of agreement between the two as to the terms in
which this should be carried out. Hon.
Members are aware that the administration
of land purchase is a matter which is reserved to the Imperial Government under
the Government of Ireland Act, and that
is the reason I am unable to answer the
question with the authority I should like
to have in this House. I would refer hon.
Members to a question that was put by the
Speaker of the other House in the Imperial
Parliament on the 3rd March, 1920. The
text of the question was:
"Whether it is the intention of the
Government to introduce a Bill for the
completion of land purchase in Ireland
to be passed concurrently with the Government of Ireland Bill."
The answer given by the present Prime
Minister, Mr. Bonar Law, who was then the
Leader of the House of Commons, was in
the affirmative. Since then we have been
assured that the Imperial Government would
give effect to the provisions of a measure of
land purchase, but it is quite true that so
far nothing has been done beyond the fact
that an abortive Bill was presented to the
T
Loans for Cottages
Land Purchase

THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
House of Commons in 1920, and I think we
may take it that the provisions of that Bill
represented the views of the Government of
that time, and I have no reason to believe
that the views of the present Government
are in any way different to the views held
by the previous Government. I am able to
tell the House that the Government of
Northern Ireland are fully aware of this
matter and are keeping it well before them.
Hon. Members will agree with me that for
the last two days we have been in a different
position to that which we occupied before.
We have formally and officially contracted
out of the Free State. Prior to doing that
the Imperial Government, before taking
certain official action, always referred us to
the difficulty that might occur in connection
with the Free State. Now I think we are
on far better ground for presenting our undoubted claims upon the Imperial Government and for calling upon them to discharge
those obligations which we feel are due to
us. I can assure hon. Members that we
shall leave no stone unturned by way of
representation to press the Imperial Government to expedite a Bill on these lines.
There is the question of bonus, with which
I am not sure Senators are quite familiar,
The bonus, of course, was the bridge which
an hon. Member referred to. In Mr. George
Wyndham's words, it was the "golden
bridge," a sum of money which enabled a
landlord to sell his property at a price
which was remunerative to him and at the
same time enabled the tenant to pay a price
that was consistent with his financial position. The bonus is not in existence at the
present moment, but the new bonus will fall
on the Six Counties.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: No.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I am sorry to contradict the hon. Member,
but I am afraid that is the case at the
present moment. It applies to new advances.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: At the present moment the bonuses under the old Acts fall on
the Six Counties because the Six Counties
have been given the annuities. The Six
Counties received a present of the annuities
under the old Act, and they were then told
that, having received the annuities, they
would have to pay the bonuses. I do not
think anybody expects the British Government is going to lend us £3,000,000 and also
give us the annuities-
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon. Member
is not going to make a second speech.
Mr. DUFFIN: Arising out of the answer
of the Leader of the House, I should like
to point out that if this burden falls on
the Imperial Exehequer, that Exchequer, so
far as these matters are concerned, is our
Exchequer.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think there is some misapprehension on
this point, but we have no guarantee that
this cost will in future be borne by the
Imperial Government, even under fresh
legislation. In addition to new advances
required there are many sales pending from
the old Acts, amounting to something like
£1,000,000, on which bonuses will be chargeable.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: And we get the
annuities.
Mr. SPEAKER: I must ask the hon. Member to allow the Noble Marquess to make his
speech.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As far as the new bonus is concerned I have
every reason to believe it will fall on the
Six Counties. I assure hon. Members that
everything we can do to press our claims
and our rights on the Imperial Government will be done. I am sorry it is
impossible for me to give a more satisfactory
answer to the question beyond the assurance
that the Prime Minister and Government
of Northern Ireland are fully cognisant of
our rights in the matter, and that every
step will be taken to see that those rights
are respected.
Loans for Cottages.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether Rural Councils, which
have purchased plots of ground for the
purpose of erecting cottages thereon and
are unable to erect such cottages at an
economic rent, will be able for this purpose
to procure loans from the Government at
a low rate of interest in the same way as
such loans were formerly provided by the
Imperial Government.
I do not think it is necessary to add
anything to the question. The Leader of
the House knows that in the Newtownards
area we have a large number of cottage
plots bought, and that formerly we erected
houses on them at a cost of £200 each.
Recently, when we went to get contracts
for the same kind of houses the contract
price was £700. Last year it was reduced
to £500, but unless we can get the money
on the same terms as formerly there is no
possibility of erecting these houses at a
rent which labourers can pay. The houses
at the present time are let at 8/6 per month,
and at the present contract price the rent
would be at least 10/- per week, which would
be beyond the ability of a labourer to pay,
unless we can get assistance from the
Government. There is a tremendous need
for these houses. When any become vacant
we always have nine or ten applicants for
them. I hope the Government will be able
to render us some assistance.
293 Loans for Cottages
Cleaning of Main Drains. 294
13 DECEMBER 1922.
Mr. LONG: I am very pleased that the
hon. Member (Mr. Dickson) has raised this
question, as we in the rural districts feel
that the matter is an important one. As
the hon. Member has said, there is a great
need of more houses in the rural districts.
We find it is quite impossible to build them
at a rent labourers can pay.
The Council
with which I am connected was told that
the outlay for erecting houses would entail
a rent of 9/6 per week, and the Council
thought it would be quite impossible for
rural labourers to pay more than 2/6 per
week. In the circumstances I trust that
the Minister of Finance of the Northern
Government will sympathetically consider
the situation and extend all the help he
can. I should very much like to know
whether houses so erected would be eligible
to receive the unemployment grant disbursed by the Minister of Labour. Under
that scheme a free grant equivalent to 60
per cent. of the wages is given. If the
Minister of Labour would approve of such
a grant being given for the erection of
houses in the rural districts it would
simplify the problem very considerably. If
the information is not available to-day I
should like the Leader of the House to
procure it.
It
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Senator who has raised this
question refers to loans which are advanced
under the Labourers (Ireland) Act, 1906.
Under it a special Loan Fund was established for financing rural districts on land
purchase terms. The administration of this
fund, as the hon. Member knows, is a reserved service, and is controlled by the Irish
Land Commission, with whom the Minister
of Home Affairs is now in correspondence
regarding the availability of further loans
to rural Councils in the Northern area.
is hoped that an announcement will be made
to these same Councils at an early date.
The hon. Member who also spoke (Mr. Long)
referred to a very important matter, and
I do not propose at this moment to answer
off-hand the question he put to me.
method of utilising the Unemployment
Grant is not a very simple one, and there
are many considerations that enter into the
matter which I cannot go into at the
moment. But I can assure the hon. Member
that I will consult my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Labour, to see if it is
possible for the suggestion he has put forward to be adopted.
Cleaning of Main Drains.
The
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether the Ministry of Agriculture will issue an order making the
cleaning of main drains through agricultural
lands compulsory, the area of charge being
the area benefited, except in cases where
rock or permanent obstruction exists, in
which cases Government assistance would
be granted.
In asking the question I have only to
suggest that it would be a great deal better
to employ some of the men who are receiving the dole in making agricultural land
productive in the way 1 suggest in the
question, rather than pay them money for
doing nothing.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I only
rise to point out that in this question my
hon. Friend (Mr. Dickson) suggests that the
area of charge should be the area that
benefits. In my experience of Local Government work the area of charge has always
been considered to be a townland, or two
townlands, and it would be very unfair in
the case of compulsory drainage that
whole townland should be involved in the
expense. I think it should be confined to
the farms that benefit, and I am sure my
hon. Friend agrees with me.
Mr. DICKSON: I quite agree.
a
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I merely wish to reinforce the case Mr. Dickson has put before
the House. In my part of the country
much of the land has become absolutely
water-logged owing to the neglect of these
main drains. In parts of the country where
the farms are large this condition might
not arise so easily, but where the farms
are small the drainage is stopped, and
though it may be possible in law to enforce
the cleaning of a very negligent farmer's
drain, at the same time there are great
difficulties, and people hesitate and endure
the mischief rather than become involved
in a law suit. If the cleaning of such
drains was compulsorily enforced it would
be a great benefit to the country. With
regard to the area of charge, I agree that
it ought to be the farms.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
These are very important matters, and I
am very glad indeed that the hon. Member
has brought them before the House. I feel
that the utility of our Houses of Parliament
is shown by the manner in which these
questions, which so much influence the life
of the Six Counties, are brought up for
discussion, and I need hardly say that I
sincerely hope the working of this legislative
assembly will be in the direction of expediting all these things and removing some of the
anomalies that at present exist. I can
assure my hon. Friends that this is not
the first time this matter has been brought
Already several representations have
been received by the Minister of Agriculture
up.
Loans Guarantee Bill. 296
Cleaning of Main Drains.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
in regard to it, but as a matter of fact the
Ministry has no legal power to issue such
an Order.
sure
During the war power was taken under
the Defence of the Realm Regulations to
make the cleaning of drains through agricultural lands compulsory, but these
Regulations lapsed on the termination of
the war. I feel
hon. Members
will realise that this question of land
drainage involves very many controversial
points, as it affects the rights of mill owners,
and it also affects fishery rights. With
regard to the mill owners, in many cases
in order to secure that arable land
should be free from periodic flooding, to
which it is liable at present, it would be
necessary to either lower or remove altogether the weirs and dams that are fixed
in certain streams for the purpose of storing
the water to provide power for working the
mills. Any such interference with weirs
or dams would mean the possible total deprivation of power for working the mil
machinery, and owners of these mills would
undoubtedly prosecute claims for very heavy
compensation.
In enforcing compliance with the Defence of the Realm Regulations on land
drainage during the war numerous difficulties
were encountered, such as those to which
I have already referred, and people have
found that it has involved them in very
There have been cases
serious liabilities.
of very expensive ligitation resulting, and
also in the Department having to pay compensation and having to restore drains to
their original unsatisfactory condition. The
lowering of the weir would in many instances
interfere with the passage of the
fish up the river or stream, which would
mean a breach of the fishery laws. This
would entail costly law proceedings, and I
am sure hon. Members realise that Fisheries
at the present time are a reserved service
over which the Northern Government has
no power or control. I am only taking this
opportunity to show the manifold difficulties
that do exist in carrying out, (something
which on the face of it seems to be a very
beneficial reform, but I will undertake to
have a consultation with the Minister of
Agriculture to see if and how these many
and varying interests can be reconciled for
the benefit of the community at large.
Message from the Commons.
That they have passed the Appropriation
(No. 2) Bill, and the Loans Guarantee Bill,
to which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Brought from the Commons and read the
First time.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill be read the
Second time. This House will not look to
me for any word explanatory of this measure
as it is purely a Money Bill dealing with
the grant of Supply, and empowering the
Ministry of Finance to borrow by Treasury
Bills or otherwise to a total sum of £200,000.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committeed.
-(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.

time. (Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-
(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And the Clerk was directed to acquaint
